KTs in the cutoff is a strong hand to open with. Two players have already folded. I have position on the blinds and decent stack. Standard 2.5x raise to take initiative and potentially win the blinds or play in position postflop.

Ts2c is near the bottom of the button defending range versus a 2.5bb cutoff open. It’s heavily dominated, plays poorly postflop, and we also have the small blind (short stack) and big blind still to act who can squeeze or come along, worsening our realization. With plenty of better hands to continue and no need to take thin -EV spots, we simply fold.

Current stack is ~21.75 BB. A5o is a marginal hand to defend from the Small Blind against a Cutoff open, especially against a large-stacked opponent (Claude) who has shown aggression in recent hands. Calling invites a squeeze from the Big Blind and leaves me playing a dominated hand out of position. A 3-bet shove is slightly too deep (21+ BB) for A5o to be a standard jam. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve the stack for a better spot to double up.

Call to 250 total. Qc3c is marginal but suited Q has playability and backdoor flush/straight potential, and facing a CO open in 6-max his range is fairly wide. The pot is 400 and I only need to call 150 (breakeven ≈21%), so defending the big blind cheaply is correct. Raising here with a weak hand would commit OOP and be unnecessarily aggressive; folding would be too tight given the price. Plan: see the flop and continue on a club/queen/connected board; fold to heavy aggression if I miss and have little equity.

I have Qc3c on 3h 8h 8d — that’s two pair (eights and threes) with a Q kicker. This is a very strong, made hand on this paired flop and is ahead of most of Cutoff’s opening range except when he holds an 8, 3 (or A3/33). I want to extract value from worse hands (weaker pairs, A3/K3-type hands, occasional bluffs) and deny free cards that could turn trips/full houses or improve hidden combos. A half-to-two-thirds pot bet is appropriate to charge calling hands and protect. I will bet ~2/3 pot (350 into 550). If Claude raises big (pot-sized or shove) I’ll assume he has a boat/trips and fold or re-evaluate; I’ll call small raise-sized bets and continue to the turn to reassess.

I have KTs with a flush draw (Th) on 3h8h8d. Minni's donk bet of 350 into 600 could be an 8, a small pair, or a probe. I have 9 flush outs plus potentially 6 overcard outs. Getting 3.6:1 on a call (350 to win 1250) with good implied odds if I hit. Position advantage lets me control the pot on later streets. Calling is the best play here to realize my equity.

I have Qc3c on 3h 8h 8d 9c — two pair (8s and 3s). This is a very strong, made hand and is ahead of the bulk of Claude’s calling range (single pairs, floats and heart/straight draws) after he called my flop bet. The turn 9c does create some hands that now beat me (combinations containing 9+8 or 9+3), but those combos are relatively rare compared to the worse hands I can still get value from. I want to extract value and deny a free river card, so I’ll continue betting for value and protection. Bet ~2/3 pot (800 into the 1250 pot). If Claude raises a small-to-medium amount I’ll call and re-evaluate on the river; if he comes over with a pot-sized shove or very large raise I will assume a full house/quads and fold.

I have KTs with a flush draw (Th). Board is 3h8h8d9c. Pot is 2050, need to call 800 for 3.5:1 odds. With 9 flush outs, I have ~18% equity to hit on river. Pot odds need ~22%, but implied odds are decent - Minni has ~3500 behind. Their continued aggression could mean an 8, overpair, or a draw. Given my position and implied odds if I hit the flush, calling remains correct.

I check. I hold Qc3c for two pair (8s and 3s) on 3h‑8h‑8d‑9c‑6d — a strong but very vulnerable hand. Claude called my 350 flop and 800 turn but has shown no aggression, so his continuing range is polarized toward medium-strength hands and occasional slow‑played very strong hands (8x, 3x, 9x, rare 88/33). The river 6d changes very little: hands that now beat me (any 8, any 3, many 9x two‑pair combos) are present in his calling range, while there are few worse hands that will profitably call a sizable river bet. Betting here risks folding out hands I beat and getting called (or raised) by hands that already crush me. With pot = 2850 and an effective stack of 4650, pot‑control is the prudent EV‑maximizing line — check to showdown and avoid bloating the pot into a spot where I can be exploited. If Claude now leads a small bet I’ll call; I’ll fold to a large shove that polarizes to boats/quads.

Missed my flush draw. Board: 3h8h8d9c6d. Minni bet flop and turn aggressively, then checked river - likely a missed draw or weak showdown hand. With K-high I beat missed draws but lose to any pair. Bluffing is risky since Minni will call with any pair. Checking back to showdown K-high is optimal - I might win against other missed draws and save money against made hands.

I am in the UTG position with Jack-Ten offsuit. In a 6-max game, this is generally considered too weak to open from the earliest position, as I will likely be out of position post-flop and the hand lacks the playability of its suited counterpart. Given the stack sizes and the tendency of players like Claude and Minni to be active, I will play conservatively and fold to find a better spot.

J8 suited in middle position is a playable hand with flush and straight potential. I'm deep stacked at 170bb. A standard 2.5x open raise to 250 allows me to take initiative and potentially win the blinds or play postflop with a hand that has decent equity when called.

Claude’s 2.5x open from middle position is a reasonably strong range, and 9♣6♠ is an offsuit, dominated hand with poor playability. Calling invites pressure from the short-stacked Button (who can jam) and from the blinds, and a 3-bet bluff is unattractive here because we have no blockers and a hand that realizes equity badly when called. Best EV is to fold and preserve stack for better spots in position.

I am on the Button with King-Eight offsuit (K8o) and a stack of 21.75 BB. Claude, an active player with a large stack, has opened to 2.5 BB from Middle Position. K8o is generally dominated by a standard MP opening range (which includes better Kings, pairs, and strong Aces). Calling is marginally negative EV given my stack depth; I am short enough that I should avoid bleeding chips with dominated hands but deep enough that I don't need to take desperate risks yet. A 3-bet bluff is risky against a large stack who may call wide. The most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot to enter the pot.

I have K♥7♦ in the small blind facing a 250 MP open. This hand is marginal — often dominated by the raiser’s Kx and by his broadway/pair range — and I would be out of position postflop (I’ll be first to act), giving me a tough time extracting value or controlling pots. Calling costs 200 into a 400 pot (requires ~33% equity) and a light 3-bet with K7o is thin versus a likely strong open; Elon (BB) could also complicate the pot. Conserving chips and waiting for a better spot is the best play here, so I fold.

Preflop in the big blind facing an isolated raise to 250 from Claude in middle position after multiple folds. My hand is 3c5d, which is a complete trash hand: offsuit, unconnected low cards with minimal playability or equity against a typical raising range. Pot odds to call 150 into 400 (roughly 2.7:1) are insufficient for such a weak hand, as it will be dominated postflop most of the time and struggle to realize equity. With a shortish stack of 4950 (~50BB), preserving chips for better spots is key rather than defending wide. Folding is the disciplined, stack-maximizing play.
9s4h is a very weak offsuit hand with no connectivity or high card value. From UTG position, I need a much stronger hand to open. Easy fold.

Td7h offsuit is too weak to open or limp from middle position in a 6-handed game, especially with a short-stacked cutoff behind who can reshove and blinds that will defend/3-bet. Limping gives up initiative and raises are unlikely to realize equity profitably. Preserve stack for better spots.

I am in the Cutoff with [Jd Qc] and a stack of 21.75 BB. Action has folded to me. QJo is a strong enough hand to open-raise from late position in an unopened pot to steal the blinds and antes. My stack is slightly too deep for an optimal open-shove (which would risk ~22BB to win 1.5BB), so a standard raise is preferable. This allows me to fold if faced with a 3-bet shove from the large stacks behind me, preserving my tournament life. I will raise to 250 (2.5 BB), consistent with the standard opening size at this table, to apply pressure on the Button and Blinds.

I’m on the button with 8♥6♠ facing a CO open to 250. 8-6o is marginal but playable in position — calling preserves my stack, keeps the pot manageable against a shortish opener (Pro ~19bb) and lets me use positional advantage post‑flop. Pot odds: calling 250 into a 400 pot (~38.5% equity needed) is reasonable against a wide late‑position opening range. I don’t want to 3‑bet light and get committed out of position or immediately face a shove; I also don’t want to fold a playable button hand. Plan: call 250, see a flop and play aggressively on favourable boards, fold to significant preflop isolation or shove pressure if ranges clearly dominate.

Preflop in small blind with 4d3h offsuit, one of the worst possible hands. Pro raised to 250 from cutoff (short stack ~19BB, potentially wide), Minni called from button. Pot 650, to call 200 gives ~3.25:1 odds, but 34o has negligible equity against any reasonable raising range and is unplayable postflop OOP, especially multiway with BB to act. Folding preserves stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 2s 3d, which is nearly the weakest starting hand possible. Pro raised to 250 from the cutoff, and Minni called on the button. While I have excellent pot odds to call (150 to call into a 650 pot), I will be out of position against two opponents with a hand that almost never flops well and is easily dominated. Conserving chips for better spots is more important than taking a high-variance, likely negative-EV call with garbage cards.

I am out of position on the Cutoff against the Button with a stack of roughly 19 big blinds. The board is Ace-high (A-9-6), which favors my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor, but also connects with the Button's flat-calling range (A-x, sets, 9-x). My hand (QJo) has no immediate value or strong draws. While a continuation bet could fold out some underpairs or floats, betting into a shallow stack-to-pot ratio out of position is risky if called or raised. Checking allows me to control the pot size, see a free turn if the opponent checks back, or easily fold to aggression without wasting chips.

Flop: 6h 9c As. I hold 8h6s — middle pair with a weak kicker on an ace‑high board. Pro opened CO to 250 preflop and checked the flop, which shows some weakness (or a slowplay), and he’s relatively short (≈19bb). In position I want to take the initiative with a small value/protection bet: I beat pure overcards and some busted hands, deny free cards to backdoor draws, and can fold to heavy action from a likely ace/strong hand. I’ll make a modest bet (~1/3–1/2 pot) to extract value while keeping the pot controllable. If he check‑raises or shoves, I’ll assume strong and fold this vulnerable second pair; if called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn.

I hold QJo on a board of As-9c-6h. I checked the flop, and the opponent on the button bet 250 into a pot of 650. I have Queen-high with no immediate draws and very little equity against a range that includes any Ace, pair, or better high card. Given my stack size of ~19BB, I cannot afford to float out of position with air. The pot odds are decent, but my realization of equity is poor and my hand is likely dominated or drawing very thin. Preserving my stack for a better spot is the correct play.

9d6s offsuit is too weak to open from UTG in a 6-handed lineup: it plays poorly multiway, gets dominated when it makes a pair, and has limited high-card/straight potential compared to suited connectors. Limping is also unattractive with several shorter stacks behind who can isolate and put us in tough spots out of position. Best EV is to release and preserve our stack for better open/3-bet spots.

I am in Middle Position with a short stack of roughly 19 big blinds (1925 chips). My hand is 7c 8h, an off-suit connector. Given my stack depth and position, this hand is far too weak to open raise profitably. I need to preserve my stack for high-equity hands or better shove spots. Opening here risks bleeding chips against larger stacks who can easily apply pressure. The correct play is to fold.

9♣6♥ is marginal but openable from the cutoff with UTG and MP folded. I want to apply pressure to the button and blinds, gain fold equity, and avoid a limp-heavy multiway pot. Claude in the big blind is very deep and can defend/3‑bet, so I size to a standard 3x (300) rather than the minimum 200 to discourage callers and keep pot control. Plan: if I get single callers I’ll continuation bet favorable dry flops and proceed cautiously on connected/wet boards; fold or release to a large, committed 3‑bet given this hand’s weak showdown value.

K3o is a marginal offsuit hand with poor playability and high likelihood of being dominated by better kings or any pair. Facing a standard 3x raise from Minni in cutoff, who has been active in previous hands. On button with decent stack (49BB), but calling commits 6% of stack for unfavorable pot odds (~1.5:1) and weak equity vs raising range (~20-25% vs typical CO open). 3-betting has little fold equity with this hand and risks playing OOP postflop. Best to fold and wait for stronger spots in early tournament phase to build stack.

I am holding Jack of Hearts and Trey of Diamonds in the Small Blind. Minni has raised to 300 from the Cutoff. J3 offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position, even against an active raiser. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit chips to the pot.

86 offsuit is a weak holding. While I'm getting ~2.25:1 pot odds (200 to win 450), this hand plays poorly postflop - not suited, not well connected. Against a cutoff raise, this is a clear fold even with BB discount.

Current stack is 19.25 BBs. KJs is a strong broadway hand that falls well within the opening range from UTG in a 6-handed game. Open-shoving 19 BBs is slightly excessive and isolates against a stronger range, whereas a standard raise allows me to play post-flop or realize equity if called by worse hands. Consistent with my previous behavior (Hand #1, Hand #19), I will open to 2.5 BBs.

8♣5♥ offsuit is a very weak hand to play cold against an UTG open. I’m out of position with multiple players yet to act (including big stacks on my left and a short-stacked opener who may shove), so calling 250 is a speculative, low-expected-value play with poor equity and limited implied odds. 3-betting with this hand is out of the question. Preserve my 8,000 stack and fold here.

Preflop, facing an UTG raise from Pro to 250 into a 400 pot (blinds 50/100 + raise). My hand Th3s is a trash offsuit hand with low cards, no connectivity, and dominated by any reasonable UTG range (pairs, broadways, suited connectors). Even in cutoff position with 49BB stack, calling commits chips to a hand with poor equity and playability postflop. Pro's stack is 1675 after raise (~14BB effective), but no need to speculate. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I am on the button with 6c 4s, which is an extremely weak hand. Pro, who is short-stacked with only 1,675 chips, raised to 250 from UTG. This raise represents a significant portion of his stack, suggesting he is likely playing a strong range or is willing to commit. Given my hand's lack of equity and the fact that I have a healthy stack, there is no reason to get involved in this pot. I will fold.

Ad8d is a decent suited ace in the SB facing a UTG raise from short-stacked Pro (1675 chips). Calling 200 more to see a flop is reasonable - the hand plays well postflop with nut flush potential. 3-betting could work but Pro is already fairly committed with their stack. Calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me see how the board develops.

We’re in the BB facing an UTG open (tight range, and UTG is also short ~16–17bb) plus a small blind cold-call, meaning we’ll often go multiway and out of position. Q6o has poor playability and very weak equity realization here: we’ll make dominated top pairs (Qx) and struggle to continue versus pressure on many runouts. Although the pot odds (150 into ~600) look tempting, the reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage versus stronger ranges make this a clear low-EV defend. No good squeeze option either—isolating a short UTG with Q6o is not a profitable value/bluff candidate. Fold and preserve stack for better spots.

Flopped top pair on A-3-2 rainbow board. Pro is short-stacked (1425 behind) and opened UTG, so they likely have a decent hand. My A8d is strong here - I should lead out for value. A bet of around 300-400 puts pressure on Pro and extracts value from worse aces, pocket pairs, and hands that might float. If Pro shoves their remaining 1425, I'm calling.

I hold Kh Jh and missed the flop of 3d 2s Ah. The opponent (Claude) leads out (donk bets) for 350 into a pot of 600. Although Claude has shown significant aggression and bluffs in previous hands (e.g., Hand #16), leading into the pre-flop raiser on an Ace-high board is often a sign of a made hand like a weak Ace preventing a check-back, or a pair. With only King-high and a stack of 16.75 BBs remaining, I do not have enough equity to float, and shoving as a bluff is too high-risk given that the Ace hits the SB calling range well. Preserving my stack is the optimal play.

UTG with 4♦6♣ — a weak, unsuited, low-connected hand. Out of position against five opponents and with several deep stacks to my left, this hand is unlikely to realize equity profitably and is often dominated. Calling 100 to play a multiway pot OOP is unnecessary; a raise to 200 to try and steal would be spewy from UTG with this holding. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot. Folding is the prudent play.

Preflop, first action after UTG fold in middle position with As6s, a strong suited ace hand with good playability, nut flush potential, and ace blocker. Standard raise to 2.5x BB (250 total) to build pot, take initiative, and potentially isolate blinds or get value/call from worse aces/suited hands. My 49BB stack supports it. Limping weak, folding too tight given position and range. History shows frequent 250 raises, opponents call light but we have fold equity.

K6o is a weak hand to play against a raise from middle position. Even though I am in the cutoff, this hand lacks the necessary strength, connectivity, or suitedness to profitably call or 3-bet against Elon's open. Folding is the standard play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.
82o is a trash hand. Even on the button with position, calling 250 with 8-2 offsuit against a raise is -EV. This hand has no playability and very little potential to make strong hands. Easy fold.

Elon opened from middle position and we’re in the small blind with Q4o, a dominated, low playability hand that performs poorly out of position. Although the price is tempting, calling invites reverse implied odds (Qx dominated by better queens) and we can’t profitably 3-bet bluff with this holding against an opener who can continue or jam effectively. Best is to cut losses and fold the small blind.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s2c, a very weak off-suit hand. Facing a 2.5x raise from Elon in Middle Position, I am getting pot odds of roughly 27% (calling 150 to win 550 total). However, 92o has poor equity against a standard opening range and extremely poor playability out of position, especially with a shallow stack of roughly 16 big blinds. I cannot profitably call or re-raise here. Preserving my stack for a better opportunity is the correct strategic move.

T9o is a marginal speculative hand offsuit from UTG in a 6-handed game. Standard UTG opening range is tighter: pairs 66+, AJo+, KQo+, suited connectors like 87s+, but offsuit T9o is too weak and prone to domination. Limping invites multiway pots where it plays poorly. Raising risks playing OOP postflop with a mediocre hand. With 50BB stack and tournament lasting 150 hands, better to wait for premium spots to build stack without variance. Opponents' stacks deep except Pro; no exploitative reason to enter. Folding preserves stack.

I am in the Hijack position with Ace-Nine offsuit. This is a solid opening hand in a 6-max game after the UTG player has folded. I will raise to 3x the big blind (300) to take control of the pot and narrow the field.

J9o is a marginal hand facing a middle position raise. While I'm in position relative to Flash, this hand doesn't play well enough to call profitably - it's dominated by better Jx hands and doesn't have great straight/flush potential. There are still players behind me who could squeeze. Standard fold here.

Flash opened to 3bb from middle position and we’re on the Button with 6♦4♥ offsuit. This hand is too weak to profitably call (poor playability, dominated, hard to realize equity even in position) and not a good 3-bet candidate given the short-stacked SB who can jam and the BB who can defend/4-bet, increasing our variance with minimal upside. Best is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

Current stack is 15.25 BBs. Holding 33 in the Small Blind facing a raise from Middle Position (Flash) to 3 BBs. 

1.  **Call**: Calling 250 to win a pot that will be around 700-800 offers poor implied odds for set mining. I would be committing ~16% of my stack. Without a set on the flop (which happens ~88% of the time), I will likely have to fold to a c-bet out of position, bleeding chips unnecessarily.
2.  **Raise (Shove)**: Shoving 1525 chips might generate some fold equity, but Flash opened from MP, indicating a stronger range than a button open. With a large stack, Flash can easily call the remaining 1225. Against a calling range of higher pairs (which dominate 33) and overcards (flips), 33 performs poorly. 
3.  **Fold**: Preserving the 15 BB stack for a better spot (like opening myself or shoving over a late position raise with better equity) is the highest EV play. 33 is simply too weak to play against an MP open with this stack depth.

Facing a 300 open from middle position while I'm in the big blind with 2♦ T♠. This hand is offsuit, poorly connected and frequently dominated by the raiser’s range (A/K/Tx and broadways). Out of position with ~79bb effective stacks, a light 3-bet is a weak bluff and a call commits me to many difficult postflop spots against c-bets. I’ll fold preflop and wait for a better, more playable opportunity.

I am UTG with King-Jack suited, which is a strong opening hand in a 6-max game. I have a healthy stack of over 130 big blinds. A standard raise of 3x the big blind (300 chips) is appropriate here to take initiative and build a pot with a hand that has high playability and nut potential.

A6o is a marginal hand facing an UTG raise. With players still to act behind me and being out of position against the raiser, this hand doesn't play well enough to call. A6o is dominated by better aces and doesn't have good playability postflop. Folding is the disciplined play here.

Flash opens 3bb UTG in a 6-handed lineup; that’s typically a reasonably strong range. Ts7h offsuit is dominated by his value opens (AT+, KTs+, broadways, pairs) and plays poorly even in position. Flat-calling also invites the short-stacked Button (15bb) to jam or squeeze spots from the blinds, putting us in tough decisions with a hand that can’t continue. No profitable bluff-3bet candidate here given positions and stack dynamics. Best is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am on the Button with J4o, a very weak hand. I have a short stack of 1525 (approx 15 BB). The UTG player (Flash) has opened for a raise to 300. Given the strength of a UTG opening range and my poor hand equity, I cannot profitably call or raise. I must fold to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

2♠4♣ is a very weak hand. Facing an UTG open to 300 from early position and being out of position in the small blind, the 250 to call doesn't offer good pot or implied odds and will often put me in tough multiway or postflop spots. A raise would be a light bluff into several deep stacks and a shove is unnecessary with this stack and hand. Preserve chips for better opportunities — fold.

Preflop in BB facing an UTG raise to 300 from Flash (total pot 450, to call 200). Hand 8s3s is a weak suited connector with poor playability OOP. Equity against a typical UTG range (top 15-20%) is around 30%, but postflop OOP dynamics make it unprofitable. Pot odds require ~23.5% equity after rake/OOP adjustment, but this hand plays poorly postflop. Standard BB defense folds this vs early position open. Preserve stack for better spots.
6-2 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker. From UTG position with 5 players behind me, this is a clear fold.

Claude has folded and it’s effectively first-in from middle position. J7o is too weak and dominated to profitably open here (and limping invites squeezes/iso from the cutoff/button and puts us in marginal postflop spots versus the blinds). With plenty of playable stack and better steal candidates, we pass on this spot.

Current stack is 15.25 BBs (1525 chips) with blinds at 50/100 and no ante. While K7s is a suited King, it is a marginal hand to open-shove from the Cutoff without antes, as the risk of 15 BBs to win 1.5 BBs offers poor risk/reward. The hand is easily dominated by better Kings and Aces which opponents in the blinds or button will likely call with. Given I am not yet in critical push/fold territory (<10 BBs), I can afford to wait for a stronger holding (pairs, Ax, better broadways) to commit my stack.

I’m on the button with T9o in a 6-max game and everyone folded to me — a clear spot to open. T9o has good post‑flop playability in position and reasonable blocker value; I want to apply pressure to the blinds rather than limp. SB (Elon) has shown some aggression but also folds to pressure; BB (Flash) defends but is deep. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x the big blind) — larger than the minimum to maximize fold equity and discourage limp‑calls while keeping the sizing reasonable relative to our ~78bb stack. If I face a large 3‑bet I’ll fold; if called I’ll use my positional advantage to play the flop aggressively or pot‑control on unfavourable boards.

Preflop in SB facing a button raise to 300 (3x) from Minni, who opens frequently based on history. My AcTh is a strong 3-bet candidate OOP vs a wide button range (likely 30-50% hands): it blocks AA/AK/AQ, has good equity vs broadways/dominates Ax worse, and straight potential. SB defends wide, but flat-calling OOP with AToo plays poorly postflop (hard to realize equity). 3-betting isolates, applies pressure, and with my 49BB stack, a standard 3x sizing of 900 puts good odds on Minni while retaining fold equity. BB likely folds or calls wide but we have blockers. Folding too tight for tournament stack building.

I am holding 5d 3s in the Big Blind. Minni opened from the button for 300, and Elon 3-bet from the small blind to 900. My hand is extremely weak and does not play well against a 3-bet range. Given the action and my poor hand strength, the only reasonable move is to fold and preserve my stack.

Call 600 more (900 total). I opened to 300 on the button with T9o for position and post‑flop playability. Elon's small 3‑bet to 900 from the SB is likely a fairly wide/inconsistent range; with position and a hand that connects well with many flops I prefer to see a flop rather than 4‑bet bluff or fold. The call is inexpensive (≈600 into a ~1300 pot, ~31% break‑even) and lets me use position to realize equity, c‑bet or pot‑control depending on texture. If Elon shows heavy further aggression (large 4‑bet or shove) I’ll fold this offsuit combo; otherwise I’ll proceed post‑flop looking to exploit position.

Flop of 2c9dQh is dry and rainbow, ideal for continuation betting my SB 3-bet range OOP vs BTN calling range. AcTh blocks AA, AK, AQ (top pairs/draws), improving my nut equity and fold equity against villain's likely holdings like dominated Ax, KQs+, pocket pairs below QQ/99 (unlikely), or weaker broadways. Ace-high has solid showdown value against villain's bluffs/marginal calls and decent equity (~25-35%) vs polarized range. Low SPR (~2.1) means we can bet for value/thin value and pot control, denying equity to any backdoor draws. History shows Minni capable of folding post-flop (e.g., Hand 15, 16). Standard 2/3 pot sizing extracts max fold equity while keeping range balanced with Qx, sets. Betting builds stack aggressively in tournament spot without overcommitting.

Board 2c 9d Qh — I hold 9c Ts: middle pair with a decent kicker in position. Pot is 3200 and Elon has bet 1300 (I must call 1300, min‑raise to 2600 available). Elon 3‑bet preflop and now c‑bet small; his range will include strong top‑pair/overpair hands (AQ/QQ/AK, some pairs) but also bluffs/floaters from the SB. Calling is the best play: the call costs 1300 to win ~4500 (≈28.9% break‑even), and my pair has reasonable equity and lots of post‑flop playability with position. Raising here would bloat the pot and often isolate me against hands that dominate me (AQ/QQ/KK/AA) or simply induce an all‑in from a short stacked opponent; it would also fold out weaker hands I can extract value from. Plan: call 1300, use position to evaluate the turn — fold to clear, strong shove/raise if the action indicates I’m well behind, but call a short‑stack shove if pot odds and reads justify it.

Turn board 5h is a relatively blank card for my perceived range advantage, adding a weak heart draw that villain might rep with a call preflop and flop. Ace-high continues to block villain's strongest Ax and AQ hands, maintaining decent showdown value against her likely Qx, 99, JTs-type floats, or weaker pairs/draws from her BTN calling range vs SB 3bet. With SPR ~1.2 (2750 into 4500 pot), aggressive betting risks getting called by better while thin value doesn't justify variance in tournament spot aiming for stack maximization over 150 hands. Checking OOP allows pot control, induces bluffs from missed draws (e.g., backdoor flush/straight chasers), and retains calling range flexibility on river (e.g., vs half-pot bet with fold equity history from Minni). History shows Minni capable of aggressive lines but also check-back/check-fold spots (Hands 2,12,17); betting all-in commits without sufficient value edge, better to realize equity passively here for ICM/ survival.

Elon (SB) 3‑bet preflop and c‑bet the flop, then checked the turn. I have middle pair (9cTs) with position on a 2c‑9d‑Qh‑5h board. Pot is 4,500 and Elon only has ~2,750 behind, so betting risks getting shoved on by hands that dominate me (AQ/QQ/overpairs) and will also fold out worse pairs and bluffs I can extract value from. I prefer pot control and to keep his weaker hands and bluffs in the pot — check behind, see the river, and then decide (call small bets, call a shove if pot odds/reads justify it, or thin‑value bet if he checks). Checking preserves stack and gives me the best chance to realize my equity from position.

River Jc completes some straight draws (e.g., KT, 87 for lower, but notably improves JT to a straight or pair Js, and AJ/KJ to top pair), which were part of villain's calling range vs my 3bet (suited connectors, broadway). It also adds a third club (2c Jc), potentially completing weak flush draws, but more importantly, it's a card that hits villain's perceived range harder than mine while bricking my AcTh completely (ace-high no pair/draw). After checking turn for pot control OOP with ~1.2 SPR, shoving 2750 into 4500 as a bluff leverages fold equity against Minni's history of folding to aggression (e.g., Hands 8,10,15 river folds), as she may have Qx, 99, missed draws, or marginal pairs without improving. AcTh blocks her top value (AQ/AK/AJ), T blocks QT/JT straights, making this a strong semi-bluff catcher turned bluff. Value hands like sets/AQ would likely bet turn; her check-back suggests capped range ripe for exploitation. Tournament stack-building favors aggressive lines with blockers in low-SPR spot vs frequent button caller. Shoving maximizes fold equity while denying her free showdown with worse.

Elon (SB) has shoved his last 2,750 after 3‑betting preflop and c‑betting the flop. The pot is 7,250; calling 2,750 would make the final pot 10,000, so I need ≈27.5% equity to break even. I hold middle pair (9s) with a T kicker. Elon's line (3‑bet preflop → small flop c‑bet → turn check → river shove) is heavily weighted to value hands here (AQ/KQ/QJ, QQ/JJ/TT, maybe sets or two‑pair/turned J), all of which beat my pair. He can have missed AK/Ax or heart draws as bluffs, but they are unlikely to be frequent enough to push my equity above the break‑even point. Calling would commit almost half my stack in a marginal spot and leave me short‑stacked post‑call; preserving chips for better +EV spots is preferable. Fold.

We’re UTG 6-max with 7♠6♣ (offsuit), which is too weak to open profitably from the earliest position. It plays poorly multiway, is easily dominated when we make a pair, and will face frequent 3-bets from several active opponents behind (Elon/Minni/Flash/Claude). With no dead money beyond blinds and no need to force thin edges this deep, the highest-EV line is to fold and preserve our positional advantage for stronger opens.

I am sitting in Middle Position with a short stack of roughly 15 Big Blinds (1525 chips). My hand, Queen-Four offsuit, is extremely weak and plays poorly post-flop. With players left to act behind me, including the aggressive big stacks, this is a trivial fold. I need to preserve my stack for a better spot to shove or open.

Cutoff with 6♣7♦ (offsuit). Pot is 150, to call 100, min-raise to 200. 6-7o is a marginal holding but in late position it's a reasonable steal attempt — I have position on the button and the blinds. Calling 100 often leads to a multiway pot where this hand's equity collapses; folding is too tight. I will open with a small raise to apply pressure and pick up the blinds or play postflop in position if called. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet (hand is not strong enough to fight off-position), otherwise proceed cautiously postflop — c‑bet selectively when favorable and play for pot control.

J5o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against a cutoff raiser's range, which includes strong hands and broadways. Facing a 2bb raise (200 into 350 pot), the pot odds are about 2.75:1, but my hand performs poorly multiway or heads-up, especially OOP for blinds. Position on button is nice for speculative hands, but J5o isn't suited or connected enough. Early in tournament (hand 26/150), better to fold and wait for stronger spots to build stack without spew. Minni's stack (5450) allows postflop play, but no need to engage with trash.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Ten offsuit. Minni, who has been quite active in opening pots, raised to 200 from the Cutoff. This is a small 2x open. Given the pot odds (calling 150 to win 350) and the fact that JTo has reasonable playability against a wide opening range from the CO, I will call. However, I must be cautious of Claude in the Big Blind, who might squeeze, and recognize that I will be out of position for the rest of the hand.
Holding 65 suited in the BB with 100 to call into a 500 pot. Getting 5:1 odds with a suited connector that has good playability (straight and flush potential). Easy defend spot.

I have missed this flop completely with Jack-Ten offsuit. The board is King-high, which typically hits the pre-flop raiser's range better than mine. I am out of position against both the Big Blind (Claude) and the original raiser (Minni). Checking is the standard and most disciplined play here to see how the other players react to the board.

I flopped an open-ended straight draw with 65dd - any 3 or 7 gives me the nuts. Board is 2c4sKs. Minni opened preflop and likely has something. With 8 outs (~32% by river), I could semi-bluff, but taking a free card in position seems optimal here. No need to bloat the pot when I can see a turn for free.

I raised preflop but on this dry K♠-4♠-2♣ flop my 6♥7♦ has no pair and only very thin runner-runner straight outs. Both blinds checked, which can be weakness but this is effectively a multiway situation and my bluff equity is low — betting here often gets called by Kx/pocket pairs and can put me in a tough spot out of hand. I have position, so I prefer pot control: check and see a free turn card. I will fold to a large bet and only continue to very small probes that offer good pot odds; if checked through I’ll reassess on the turn (look for a 5/3 or pairing card to improve equity).

On the turn, the Queen of Clubs has improved my hand significantly as I now have an open-ended straight draw (any Ace or Nine gives me a straight). Since the flop was checked around by everyone, including the pre-flop aggressor (Minni), it's likely that the current ranges are relatively weak or capped. However, I am still out of position against two opponents. Checking is the most solid play here to realize my equity. It allows me to see the river for free if both opponents check behind, or evaluate the pot odds if someone decides to bet. I'm not looking to bloat the pot yet without a made hand.

Still have my open-ended straight draw (3 or 7 completes). Turn Qc doesn't help but doesn't hurt either. Flash checked, I should check behind to see a free river. No reason to bluff into a dry pot when I can take a free card with 8 outs.

Check. The turn (Qc) didn’t help my 6♥7♦ — I have no pair, no flush draw and with only one card to come I can’t make a meaningful straight (only 6 simple pair-outs: three 6s + three 7s). The board (2♣-4♠-K♠-Q♣) is fairly dry and both SB and BB have checked the flop and turn, showing passivity; in a multiway pot my fold equity is minimal and a bluff-sized turn bet is unlikely to take this down and will just bloat the pot out of position. I raised preflop so checking here preserves chips and lets me see a cheap river. Plan: check and fold to any sizable bet this street; if checked through I’ll re-evaluate the river and only continue with a pair or if a very small probe gives correct pot odds.

On the river, the Ace of Diamonds completes my straight (Ten through Ace), which is the absolute nuts on this board. No flushes or full houses are possible. After the flop and turn were checked through by all players, it is likely that ranges are weak; however, the Ace is a 'scare card' that may have improved someone to a pair or two pair, or it may induce a call from a King or Queen. I will bet 400 into the 600 pot to extract maximum value from any Ax, Kx, or Qx hands that might have been waiting for the river to improve.

My straight draw missed - needed a 3 or 7 and got an Ace. I have 6-high with zero showdown value. Flash bet 400 into a 600 pot on a board of A-K-Q-4-2. This board hits a lot of calling ranges (any broadway cards, pairs). Bluffing here makes no sense - Flash likely has at least a pair to bet this runout, and Minni is still behind me. Easy fold with no equity.

Board: 2♣ 4♠ K♠ Q♣ A♦. My hand: 6♥7♦ — no pair, no draw on the river. Pot is 1000, Flash bet 400; calling 400 would win 1400, so I would need ~28.6% equity to justify a call. On the river there are no outs — my only chance is that Flash is bluffing. His line (called my preflop raise, checked flop and turn, then a smallish 400 river bet) is much more consistent with thin value or a blocking bet with A/K/Qx or a turned pair than with a pure bluff. Given my lack of showdown value, the low frequency of plausible bluffs in his range, and the fact raising as a bluff here is out of line and likely to be called by better hands, folding preserves chips and avoids a negative expectation call. With a healthy stack and many hands left, I fold.

I am in UTG position with a very short stack of 15.25 BB. My hand, J2o, is extremely weak and unplayable from this position. I need to preserve my chips for a spot where I can shove with reasonable equity or steal the blinds from late position. Folding is the only correct play here.

Ts3h is a very weak, poorly connected offsuit hand in middle position. There are multiple players left to act behind me (Cutoff, Button, SB, BB) who have shown aggression in earlier hands, so limp-calling 100 risks getting raised and facing a multiway pot with very low equity. Calling requires ~40% equity to be +EV here and T3o rarely meets that against one or more realistic ranges. With a healthy 54bb stack I should avoid marginal, multiway spots early in the tournament and wait for a better opportunity. Fold and preserve chips for a stronger hand or a better positional spot.

Preflop in cutoff with 9c3d offsuit, a complete trash hand with no high card value, connectivity, or suited potential. Facing standard blinds, no prior action other than folds from UTG and MP. Folding is mandatory to avoid playing speculative hands out of position postflop. Preserving stack for better spots in a long tournament (hand 27/150). No steal incentive as button and blinds have deep stacks and will defend wide, but equity is negligible.

I am on the button with 2d 3s, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Although I am in a prime position to attempt a steal, the blinds (Claude and FiveTwo) have shown enough activity and aggression in previous hands that a bluff here is unlikely to be profitable. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play with this hand.

Everyone folded to me in the SB with Q6o. This is a decent spot to raise and try to take down the blinds. Q6o is playable heads up, and a standard raise to 250-300 puts pressure on the BB. Folding is too tight here.

Claude is opening very wide from the small blind, and we’re getting a good price: 150 to win a pot that will be 500 if we call (~30% required). Even with a weak hand like 54o, we have enough equity versus a typical SB opening range, plus we’ll have position postflop (SB acts first). 3-betting this hand doesn’t have great blockers and can get us into tough spots versus a continue/4-bet range, so the best play is to defend by calling and realize our equity.

Flopped middle pair (6s) on Kc 2h 6h board. I have the Qh for a backdoor flush draw. With just middle pair on a K-high board, I shouldn't c-bet for value - a king is definitely in villain's calling range from the BB. Better to check and control pot size with this marginal hand. If they check back, I can get to showdown; if they bet, I can evaluate.

Claude is opening very wide from the SB and then checked this K♣-2♥-6♥ flop, which caps him away from a chunk of his strong Kx/overpair range some of the time. With 5♦4♠ we have essentially no showdown value but we do have a gutshot to the 3 (2-3-4-5-6), so turning this into a small stab is higher EV than checking back: we can fold out his whiffs (QJ/JT/etc.), deny equity to hands like A3/A4/34, and occasionally pick up the pot immediately. I’ll use a small sizing to risk little while pressuring his air and marginal pairs.

I have middle pair (6s) on Kc 2h 6h. FiveTwo's 200 bet into 500 is small - about 40% pot. With a pair of 6s, I have showdown value and beat bluffs/draws. The Qh gives me backdoor flush equity. Calling is standard here - folding is too weak with a made hand, and raising accomplishes little as I'd only get called by better hands. Will call and reassess on turn.

Turn brought Th, completing three hearts on board. I have Qh for a flush draw (9 outs) plus my pair of 6s. FiveTwo checked after betting flop, which could mean: 1) giving up with air, 2) trapping with a king or flush, or 3) pot control with a medium hand. With my pair + flush draw, I have decent equity but checking is smart here - I have showdown value, get a free card to potentially hit my flush, and avoid a check-raise that would put me in a tough spot. No need to inflate the pot out of position with a marginal made hand.

Claude’s flop check/call versus a small stab keeps his range very wide: some Kx, some 6x/2x/pairs, and a lot of floats (random overcards, hands with/without a heart). The T♥ is a strong barreling card for our range: it completes the front-door flush and adds an overcard that we can credibly represent (made flushes, strong Kx with a heart, Tx, etc.). With 5♦4♠ we have essentially no showdown value (only a gutshot to the 3), so checking back mostly just concedes the pot. I’ll apply pressure with a sizable turn bet aimed at folding out his air and many marginal pairs/weak one-pair holdings. If he check-raises, we simply fold.

I have middle pair (6s) + Qh flush draw on a three-heart board. FiveTwo bet 700 into 900, about 78% pot - larger than their flop sizing. I need ~30% equity to call. My flush draw gives ~18% to hit the river, plus my pair of 6s might be good against bluffs. If they have a made flush I'm in bad shape, but they could be semi-bluffing with a single heart or value betting a king. Combined equity from flush draw + showdown value justifies a call.

River Ac completed the board: Kc 2h 6h Th Ac. My flush draw with Qh missed - there are only 3 hearts on board. I'm left with just middle pair (6s). The board is scary with potential flushes, two broadway cards (K, A), and possible straights. My pair of 6s has minimal showdown value - it loses to any K, A, T, flush, or two pair. Betting would only fold out worse hands and get called by better. Checking makes sense to potentially catch a bluff or reach showdown cheaply against a missed draw.

Claude’s check/call on the flop followed by a call of our large turn barrel when the front-door hearts flush completed strongly condenses his range toward made hands that can continue on that texture (a lot of heart holdings/flushes, some Kx with a heart, sets/two-pair type hands). The river A♣ is a decent scare card in theory, but we have zero relevant blockers (no heart, no ace), so our bluff is poorly positioned into a range that contains many hands that simply won’t fold at a profitable frequency. With 5♦4♠ we have no showdown value, but firing again here risks spewing into a range that is too strong after the turn call. Best play is to give up and check back.

Qs Jh (QJ offsuit) is a raisable UTG hand in a 6-max game — good playability and decent equity. I want to open for value and to avoid a multiway pot; limping or a min-raise is too passive and invites callers/3-bets. A standard 3bb open to 300 builds the pot, provides fold equity, and leaves me plenty of stack (≈54bb) to play postflop. If I face a shove from the very short BB I’ll call; vs a larger 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on stack-to-pot and shove sizing. Plan: C-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously when I encounter heavy resistance.

Pocket eights is a strong hand preflop, strong enough to 3-bet an UTG open from Minni. Her range is decently strong (TT+, AQ+ likely), but 88 has good equity against it (~40-45% heads-up) and performs well postflop. 3-betting builds the pot when ahead, denies equity to worse hands, and pressures shorter stacks behind like Pro (1425). Size to 900 (3x her raise) is standard, leaving room for 4-bets while committing ~12% of my stack. Calling risks multiway pot OOP; folding is too tight. With 7250 stack and Minni's 5150 effective, this spot is profitable long-term.

I am holding 3h 2c, which is the weakest starting hand in poker. There has been an initial raise from Minni in UTG and a 3-bet from Elon in Middle position. Given my position and the strength of my hand, this is an automatic fold. There is no strategic value in calling or attempting a 4-bet bluff against two early-position raisers with such a marginal holding.
9h4c is a very weak offsuit hand with no connectivity or high card value. Facing a raise and 3-bet, I have terrible equity here. Easy fold.

We’re in the small blind with 7♣4♠ facing an UTG open and a sizable 3-bet to 900. This hand is too weak to defend out of position versus strong ranges, and calling invites an awkward multiway pot (UTG can continue, BB can jam short) where we’ll realize equity poorly and be dominated frequently. No profitable 4-bet bluff candidate either. Best is to cut losses and preserve stack.

I am holding Q9o in the Big Blind with a stack of roughly 14 BBs. The action involves a UTG open from Minni and a 3-bet to 900 from Elon in Middle Position. This action represents significant strength. My hand is heavily dominated by the ranges of both opponents (likely pairs, stronger broadways, or premiums). Calling would commit more than half my remaining stack with a weak hand out of position, and shoving has zero fold equity against the 3-bettor. This is a trivial fold to preserve my stack for a better spot.

QsJh is a reasonable UTG opener but too marginal to 4-bet as a bluff here. Elon’s 3-bet to 900 is a standard size — I have good playability and equity with QJ offsuit and don’t want to fold after opening. Calling 600 (total 900) keeps the pot controllable with ~45bb effective behind and lets me realize equity postflop. Plan: play OOP for pot control — c-bet only on favorable textures (Q/J-high, strong draws), check and fold to heavy turn/river aggression on Ace/King-high boards where I’m likely dominated.

Elon 3-bet pre so his range is weighted to strong pairs and broadway/Ax hands. The flop 6♣ 5♥ 5♣ is a dry, low paired texture that does not favor Q♠J♥ — I have only two overcards and no club backdoor. Out of position versus a 3-bettor I want pot control and to realize equity rather than fire a marginal C-bet that will often be called or raised by better hands. I will check. Plan: call a small probe bet (~1/3 pot ≈650) to see a turn, fold to a sizable bet or a check-raise, and only start betting/raising if I pair up (Q or J) or hit a clear draw on a later street.

Heads-up vs Minni after my 3-bet and her call preflop. Flop 6c-5h-5c is excellent for 88: overpair to the paired board, blocks sets somewhat (no 8 on board), strong against her UTG 3-bet calling range (TT+, AQ+, AKo). Board has club flush draw and possible straight draws (e.g., 43s, 74s), but her range hits value poorly except 55 (quads), 65/56 (two pair/straight), or 66 (set). She checks, which could be weakness or trap, but standard to c-bet for value from worse pairs/overpairs (77-44, AT etc. that flop pair/draw) and protection vs draws. Pot 1950, bet ~2/3 pot (1300) builds pot OOP while leaving room for turn play; effective stacks allow it (me 6350, her 4550). Checking risks giving free cards to draws/improves and misses value.

I opened Q♠J♥ UTG and flat-called Elon's preflop 3‑bet to keep the pot manageable. The flop 6♣5♥5♣ is a dry, low paired texture that does almost nothing for my hand: I only have two overcards and minimal backdoor equity (one heart backdoor, no club backdoor). Elon is the 3‑bettor so his range is value‑heavy (overpairs, top pairs, sets) and his 1,300 into ~1,950 (~2/3 pot) is a large continuation bet. Calling 1,300 gives me ≈28.5% equity requirement to break even — my realistic equity vs his c‑betting/3‑betting range is well below that. I have no good semi‑bluff (no meaningful draws) and raising would be risking a big portion of my stack OOP with poor equity and limited fold equity. Following my preflop plan to control the pot and fold to heavy aggression on unfavorable textures, I fold and preserve chips for better spots (I’ll re‑evaluate if I pair up or pick up clean equity on a later street).

UTG with 3c4c suited connector is a standard fold in a 6-handed tournament. This hand has poor playability out of position, low equity multiway, and doesn't justify entering the pot early when better spots await with my 83BB stack. Folding preserves chips for stronger hands.

I am in middle position with 8c 7d, a marginal hand that doesn't play well against the stacks behind me. Pro is very short in the small blind (approx. 13.75 BB), which increases the likelihood of a shove if I open. Standard strategy dictates folding 87o from this position to avoid playing a weak hand out of position or against aggressive short-stack jams.

64 suited is a weak hand. While I'm in late position (cutoff), this isn't strong enough to open-raise with. Pro is short-stacked in the SB and could be looking to shove, and FiveTwo on button is active. Not worth the risk with this marginal holding.

Folds to us on the button and both blinds are incentivized to play fairly straightforward (SB is very short and BB has shown some raise/fold tendencies). 97o is comfortably within a button opening range in 6-max, and raising leverages position + fold equity to pick up the blinds. I’ll use a standard small steal sizing that still pressures the blinds without over-risking chips; if the SB jams, I’m not committed and can fold this hand.

I am in the Small Blind with a short stack of 13.75 BB. Facing a standard open from the Button, my hand (J4 offsuit) is far too weak to defend by calling or 3-bet jamming. The hand has poor equity and playability out of position. Preserving my remaining stack for a better spot is the optimal strategy.

Button opened to 250 (2.5x). I'm the big blind with Qh9d — a hand with decent equity and good post-flop playability (Q-high, backdoor hearts/straights). The pot is 400 and I must call 150, giving ~27% pot odds, which is attractive versus a wide button opening range. A min-3-bet here would be thin and leaves me out of position with a marginal holding, so I prefer to defend and see a flop. Plan: continue with top pair or strong draws, control size or fold to heavy aggression when missed. Total commit including my 100 BB posted: 250.

I’m out of position with Q♥9♦ on A♥ 2♠ 2♦ — I completely missed (no pair, no realistic backdoor flush or straight). The button’s opening range contains a lot of A-x and pocket pairs that do well on this flop, and any value hands will bet for value. I want to keep the pot small and avoid committing with air, so I’ll check and control size. If he fires a small probe (cheap enough), I may call to see a turn given pot odds and possible later bluff opportunities, but I will fold to meaningful pressure. If he checks back I get to see a free turn and re-evaluate.

This A22 board is significantly better for my button-raising range than for the BB flatting range, and the paired deuce makes it harder for BB to have many strong hands beyond Ax. With 97o I have no made hand, but a small c-bet should generate a lot of immediate folds from unpaired broadways/air, while keeping my risk low when BB continues with any Ace or a sticky pocket pair. I’ll use a small, range-style sizing and be prepared to mostly shut down versus resistance on later streets.

I’m OOP with Q♥9♦ on A♥ 2♠ 2♦ and I checked to control size. Button fires a small probe bet (200 into a ~750 pot) — this sizing is consistent with a wide value/bluffing range. I have no realistic draws (only one heart; cannot make a 5‑card flush by river) and only six thin pair outs (3 Qs, 3 9s) that often will still be behind A‑x or pocket twos. However the price is cheap: calling 200 to win ~950 is ~21% breakeven, so it’s correct to defend and see a turn given the button’s wide opening range and the small bet. Plan: call the flop, re-evaluate on the turn — fold to significant turn pressure if I still have no pair, continue cautiously if I pair or the opponent slows down. Total committed after this call = 450 chips.

Check. I'm out of position with Q♥9♦ on A♥ 2♠ 2♦ 4♦ and I haven't improved — only six thin pair outs that are often still behind A‑x or pocket twos. Button's line (open pre, bet flop) is consistent with many A‑x and pocket pairs that continue to value-bet; I have very little fold equity and don't want to bloat the pot OOP with air. Checking controls the pot (pot = 950, stack = 4100) and keeps my options: call a small turn stab (roughly ≤ ~1/3 pot) to see a cheap river and re-evaluate, but fold to significant turn pressure. If the button checks back I get a free river to try a bluff with better blockers or to showdown if I pair.

Flop c-bet got called on A22, which tightens Minni’s range a lot toward Ax, some 2x, pocket pairs, and some floats/backdoors. On the 4d turn I have essentially zero equity and no useful blockers (no diamond, no A/2), so a second barrel is likely to get looked up too often by any Ace, any pair (now including some 4x), and diamond backdoors. Checking keeps the pot smaller, preserves my ability to bluff selectively on many rivers if she checks, and aligns with the plan to shut down versus resistance.

I never improved with Q♥9♦ on 2♠ 2♦ A♥ 4♦ K♦ — I have Q-high with only 6 thin pair outs (three Qs, three 9s) that are mostly irrelevant now. Villain (Button) opened pre and fired a small c-bet on the flop, then checked turn and checked river — a very pot‑control/weakness line consistent with A‑x, medium pocket pairs or missed bluffs. Bluffing the river here has poor fold equity vs a preflop raiser who called the flop; many A‑high and pair hands will simply call a reasonable river bluff, and I block almost no meaningful combos (one diamond only). Pot is only 950 and my stack is 4100 — there’s no need to risk chips with air. The lowest-variance, most +EV play is to check and take my showdown (or fold if villain suddenly jams). Action: check.

Minni’s flop call on A22 heavily weights her toward Ax (and some 2x/pocket pairs). After the turn checks through, the river K doesn’t give me useful blockers (I don’t block A, K, or any key continues), and my perceived value is basically just Ax—so a river bluff is likely to get looked up too often by any Ace and sometimes even by stubborn pocket pairs. With low fold equity and no showdown value, the best EV is to take the free showdown and avoid lighting chips on fire.

I am in the Under the Gun (UTG) position with Jack of Spades and Four of Diamonds (J4o). This is a very weak hand with poor equity and post-flop playability. In a 6-max game, opening such a wide range from UTG is unprofitable, especially with a healthy stack of 13,600 chips. There is no reason to commit chips with this hand.
Q8 suited in middle position is a decent opening hand. I'm the first to act after UTG folded. With a healthy stack of 18375, I can open raise with this suited queen. Standard 2.5x open to 250.

Claude’s 2.5bb open is a great spot to build a pot in position with the top of range. Flatting can induce squeezes, but with a very short-stacked Button (Pro) behind and blinds that can defend, I prefer a value 3-bet that also leaves room for Pro to jam and for Claude to continue with worse. Sizing to 850 (about 3.4x) pressures the opener, denies cheap equity, and sets up straightforward stacks on later streets while still being small enough to keep dominated hands and short-stack jams in.

I am sitting on the Button with a short stack of 13.75 BBs holding Queen-Ten offsuit. The action involves an open raise from Middle Position and a large 3-bet to 8.5 BBs from the Cutoff. My hand is easily dominated by the 3-bettor's range (AQ, KQ, AT, pairs) and the original raiser's range. With such a shallow stack, I cannot profitably call or shove over a 3-bet with this marginal holding. Preserving my stack for a first-in shove or a better spot is the only correct play.

Claude opened to 250 (MP) and FiveTwo 3-bet to 850 from the cutoff. I’m in the small blind with K♣9♣. The pot is 1250 and it costs me 800 to call. K9s is marginal here: out of position postflop, likely behind a CO 3-bet range, and my heads-up equity is probably below the ~39% required (800/(1250+800)) to make a pure call +EV. A 4-bet would be overplaying this holding. With 50bb effective and several aggressive players left to act, folding and preserving my stack for a better spot is the correct play.

Preflop in the big blind with 5c7s offsuit, facing a raise from Claude (middle position) to 250 and a 3-bet from FiveTwo (cutoff) to 850. Pot is 1250, to call 750 for pot odds of about 2.66:1, but 5-7o has very poor equity against two strong ranges from raisers (likely high cards, pairs, suited connectors from aggressive positions). Multiway pot postflop would be disastrous with this weak, uncoordinated hand. No fold equity in calling. Standard fold to preserve stack early in tournament.

Q8s is too weak to continue against a 3-bet. FiveTwo raised to 850, and I'd need to call 600 more. I'll be out of position postflop against the 3-bettor. Q8 suited doesn't play well against a typical 3-bet range, and I'm getting only about 2:1 pot odds. This is a clear fold.

K9o from UTG is a marginal hand. While it has some playability, it's generally too weak to open from under the gun in a 6-handed game. There are 5 players left to act behind me, and K9o doesn't play well against calling/3-betting ranges. Better to fold and wait for a stronger hand or better position.

Unopened pot folded to us in middle position; Tc2s is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability and dominated top-pair potential. With multiple players behind (including active blinds) and no need to force thin steals, best EV is to preserve chips and wait for better open/defend spots.

I am in the Cutoff with a very short stack of 13.75 BBs. My hand, J3o, is extremely weak. While I am in a push/fold mode generally, this hand is at the very bottom of my range and is not suitable for a steal attempt or an all-in shove. There are still three players left to act behind me. Conserving my remaining chips for a better spot is the correct play.

On the button with pocket sixes (6s6h) and only the blinds to act, this is a standard spot to take the initiative. 66 is a profitable hand to open from the button: it has decent showdown value, good set-mining implied odds, and I want to avoid a multiway pot.  A 3x BB open (300 total) gives me solid fold equity vs the blinds, keeps the pot manageable if called, and leaves me ~47 BB behind to play postflop.  If I get a small 3-bet I can call and play for a set or evaluate on the flop; I will fold to a large 3-bet that commits too large a portion of my stack.  Raising also isolates the blinds and capitalizes on their tendency to defend/passively — overall best EV play here is to raise to 300.

7c3d is an extremely weak offsuit hand with no playability, low cards, and no connectivity or suited potential. Facing a button raise to 300 after multiple folds (UTG, middle, cutoff), the range is wide but still far ahead of my hand. I'm OOP in SB postflop, which amplifies the disadvantage. Pot odds to call 250 into 450 offer ~2.8:1 but equity is negligible (<20% vs typical button range). Standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots in a tournament with 120 hands left and 8150 stack (81.5BB effective).

I am in the Big Blind with King-five offsuit. Minni, on the Button, has raised to 300. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win a total pot of 450), K5o is a weak hand that performs poorly out of position. Minni has been very active, but a 3x open raise usually suggests a range that dominates my King-low kicker. Given my healthy stack and the early stage of the tournament, I prefer to fold and wait for a better spot rather than potentially putting myself in a difficult post-flop situation out of position.

We’re first to act in a 6-max configuration with T9 offsuit. This is too thin to open from UTG because it’s dominated by the continuing ranges (AT+/KT+/QT+/better Txs and most 9x that continue), plays poorly out of position, and will face squeezes/3-bets from the Button and blinds. With a short stack (Pro) behind who can reshove over opens, raising also increases variance for a marginal hand. Best EV is to pass and preserve stack for stronger UTG opens.

Current stack is 13.75 BB, which is entering push/fold territory. The hand 5c 4h is an unsuited low connector, extremely weak from Middle Position. With four players left to act, including deep stacks in the blinds who can easily call a shove, there is zero merit in playing this hand. The goal is to preserve the short stack for a higher equity spot (like an Ace, pair, or broadway cards) to shove or open. Folding is the only standard and correct play here.

Cutoff with 6♥4♦ — a weak, unsuited, and often-dominated hand. Pot is only 150 and the cost to see a flop is 100 (the big blind), but there are three players left to act (Button, SB, BB) who are deep and defend/3-bet frequently. Open-raising here risks a multi-way or 3‑bet pot out of position where this hand has very poor playability and little fold equity. Limping hands this weak just creates difficult multiway flops. With ~51 BB I can wait for a much better stealing or postflop hand; preserving my stack and folding is the +EV line.

Folded to me on the button pre-flop with K7h suited. Excellent position with a speculative hand that plays well post-flop due to suitedness, king high, and potential for straights/flushes. Standard play is to raise for value/thin value and to steal blinds. Blinds are deep but have shown loose tendencies in history. Raising 3x BB to 300 total, which is a good size given the pot of 150 and no action ahead.

I am in the Small Blind with Q9s. Elon has opened for 300 (3x) from the Button. While Q9s is a decent speculative hand that usually warrants a call or a 3-bet in a 6-max game, I am out of position against Elon, who has shown recent aggression. Additionally, Claude is in the Big Blind with a massive stack and may squeeze or call, putting me in a difficult spot post-flop in a multi-way pot. Given the tournament context and my healthy stack, I will choose a conservative fold here to avoid a high-variance, out-of-position struggle with a marginal hand.
T4o is a weak hand to defend from the BB against a button raise. Even with decent pot odds (2.25:1), this hand plays poorly postflop with no connectivity or high cards. Easy fold.

I am in UTG position with a short stack of 13.75 BB (1375 chips). The hand is ATo (Ace-Ten offsuit). In a 6-handed game, ATo is a marginal hand from early position. With less than 15 BB, I am in a push/fold mode, but ATo is generally considered slightly -EV or break-even to shove from UTG with this stack depth, as it is often dominated by calling ranges (AJ+, TT+) and flips against lower pairs. Given that I have enough chips to wait for a better spot (better hand or better position like CO/BTN), preserving my stack is preferable to taking a high-variance marginal spot from the worst position at the table. Therefore, I will fold.

Holding Q♠7♠ in middle position with four players to act behind (CO, BTN, SB, BB). This is a marginal suited Q and is easily dominated by many hands that will continue (Qx, better spade A/K/Qx, pairs). With ~51 BB effective and aggressive, deep stacks behind who defend and 3‑bet fairly often (observed tendencies), opening here invites multiway pots out of position or a 3‑bet I don’t want to face with a weak kicker. Limping (call 100) gives poor fold equity and lets position/initiative be taken away. Better to preserve chips for a cleaner spot later—folding is the least costly, +EV play from MP with this hand.

Folds from UTG and MP, leaving dead money in the pot (150 chips). Qc9s is a playable hand from the cutoff in 6-handed play with position on the blinds. It has decent equity, queen blocker, and good playability postflop. Raising steals blinds frequently against passive tendencies observed in opponents like Claude and FiveTwo, who often check/call rather than 3-bet light. Standard sizing of 3x BB builds the pot while maintaining fold equity. Deep stacks (83BB) allow for postflop maneuvering.

I am on the button with 63 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. Elon raised to 300 from the cutoff. Even with positional advantage, there is no strategic justification for calling or raising with such a low-equity hand. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack.
T2o is a trash hand. Facing a raise from the cutoff, I'm out of position with a hand that has no playability. Easy fold.

CO opens to 3bb and we’re getting a good price in the BB (200 to win a pot that will be 650 if we call, needing ~31%). Js8o is a reasonable BB defend versus a CO range: it has decent realization (top pair potential, some straight potential) and we keep the pot smaller with an offsuit hand that can be dominated when we make a jack. A 3-bet bluff here is possible, but Js8o isn’t a great candidate (poor blockers and postflop playability when called), so the higher-EV line is to defend by calling and play postflop in position disadvantage with a manageable SPR.

Heads-up BB vs CO on A♠ Q♥ 4♣, our J♠8♥ has no pair, no meaningful straight draw, and only a thin backdoor heart draw (needs runner-runner). This board strongly favors the preflop raiser’s range (many Ax/Qx), and leading would mostly bloat the pot with a hand that can’t comfortably continue versus pressure. Best is to check our range and evaluate versus sizing/frequency if Elon c-bets.

Flopped top pair queens with a 9 kicker on an ace-high board in position heads-up against FiveTwo's BB calling range, which is likely wide including Ax, weaker queens, pairs below, and draws. The board is As Qh 4c, somewhat coordinated but my Q blocker reduces QQ likelihood. Betting for thin value against worse made hands and floats, while providing protection against overcards or weak aces that might have called pre. FiveTwo has shown passive check/call tendencies in prior hands. Half-pot sizing builds the pot without overcommitting deep stacks (~80BB), maintaining balance and fold equity against pure air.

On A♠ Q♥ 4♣ versus a small 35% c-bet, we do get a decent price, but J♠8♥ has very poor equity and realization: no pair, no overcards, no real straight draw, and only a thin runner-runner heart possibility. Calling would mostly be a float OOP with too few good turns to continue profitably, and we’ll be forced to fold to most turn barrels. This is too low in our range to defend here, so we conserve chips.

AQs (A♦Q♦) is a clear UTG open in 6-max. With ~51 BB I want to take the initiative, get value from worse Ax/broadways and fold out some speculative hands. The pot is 150 with 100 to call and the min-raise is 200, so I size up to 300 (about 3x BB) — big enough to reduce multiway action and gain fold equity, but not so large that I overcommit versus a potential 3-bet or a short-stack shove. If I face a shove from the short-stacked BB I can still call and evaluate; versus deep 3-bets I will proceed cautiously. Postflop plan: continuation-bet dry boards, pot-control or check back when I face heavy aggression or an Ace-high board where I may be dominated.

K9 suited is a strong playable hand with good post-flop potential, especially suited for flush draws and straight potential. Minni's UTG raise to 300 is standard sizing, but from history, Minni raises frequently and appears loose-aggressive (multiple opens and calls in prior hands). Game is loose overall with frequent calls and limps. Pot odds are decent (calling 300 into 450 pot pre-action from others), and I have position on Minni. Short stack Pro in BB (12BB) may shove wide, but K9s holds up well against short-stack shoving ranges. Post-flop playability is strong in a multiway pot potential. 3-betting is possible but risks bloating pot OOP with marginal strength; calling keeps pot smaller and leverages suited connectivity. Folding is too tight given opponent tendencies and stack depths (86BB effective). Plan to call and play aggressively post-flop if hits or as semi-bluff.

I am in the Cutoff with Jc 5d, which is a very weak hand. Minni raised from UTG and Elon called in middle position. Given the early position strength shown and the poor quality of my hole cards, there is no reason to play this hand. Folding is the most prudent action.

45o is a weak hand even on the button. While I have position, this offsuit connector doesn't play well multiway and there's still action behind me (two blinds could squeeze). Not worth 300 chips to see a flop with this marginal holding.

We’re in the small blind facing an UTG 3bb open and a flat call, which heavily reduces fold equity for a squeeze and also creates a multiway, out-of-position spot. Q6o is dominated by both players’ continuing ranges and has poor playability (reverse implied odds) if we call. The big blind is very short and can come along/jam frequently, making any bluff squeeze even worse. Best EV is to preserve stack and pass on this marginal spot.

I am in the Big Blind with Pocket Tens (Tc Th) and a stack of 1275 chips (12.75 BB). Facing a UTG raise to 300 and a flat call from MP, there is 750 chips in the pot. My hand is very strong relative to my stack size. Just calling would leave me with an SPR of ~1 playing out of position multi-way, where many flops with overcards could put me in a tough spot. A squeeze shove here is the standard and optimal play. It maximizes fold equity to potentially take down the dead money (increasing my stack by ~60% without showdown) and ensures I realize my full equity if called, as I am likely ahead of MP's calling range and flipping or ahead of much of UTG's range. I will move all-in.

I have A♦Q♦ — a strong but not premium hand. Pro’s 3-bet to 1,275 puts 975 more to call into a 1,925 pot. A 4‑bet to the minimum (2,250) would commit a huge chunk of my stack (~1,950 more) and is unnecessarily bloating the pot with Elon already cold-calling; 4‑bet bluffs are less effective multiway and I don’t want to commit vs a likely strong 3‑bet range. Calling keeps the pot controlled, preserves postflop maneuverability, and lets me realize equity (and call a small short-stack shove from Pro if they commit the last ~100). Plan: call to 1,275 total, see the flop, continuation-bet dry boards, and pot-control or check back on Ace-high boards where I may be dominated.

K9 suited clubs is a strong hand with excellent playability, straight and flush potential. Already invested 300 pre-action. Pro's 3-bet to 1275 from BB with only 100 behind indicates a short-stack shove range, likely wide including Ax, pairs, suited connectors where K9s has good equity (dominates lower kings, Ax, etc.). Minni's UTG flat-call of 3-bet suggests a strong but not monster hand like QQ-JJ, AK, AQ. Pot odds are outstanding: calling 975 into 2900 pot (~3:1, needing ~25% equity), and vs two ranges I have well over 30-40% equity. Raising to isolate Pro risks facing Minni's strong range OOP with shallow effective stacks (Minni 3875). Folding is too tight given history of loose play and my hand's strength. Call, realize equity, and play post-flop aggressively if improves or as bluff.

I have only 100 chips left (1 BB) in a massive pot of 3,875 after my pre-flop squeeze was called by two players. The flop [Ah 3d Ks] is disastrous for my Pocket Tens, as it hits the UTG and MP ranges very hard (containing AA, KK, AK, AQ). I have zero fold equity. Leading out for 100 chips accomplishes nothing. My best play is to check. This allows the other two players to contest a side pot, potentially causing one to fold and isolating me against a single opponent, or allowing the action to check through so I can realize my slim equity for free. If there is a bet, I am pot-committed to call given the extreme odds, but checking is the correct initial action.

Preflop I called a big 3-bet and we’re now multiway on A♥ 3♦ K♠. I have top pair with the best kicker I can reasonably hope for (A♦Q♦), but Pro’s large 3‑bet range is heavily weighted toward AK/AA/QQ+ — hands that beat me (AK and AA in particular). With two opponents and a very large pot relative to my stack, I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginally vulnerable top pair. Checking controls the pot size, preserves showdown value, and lets me see how the others act — I can call a small continuation bet but will fold to a sizable check‑raise which will usually represent two pair/trips from the 3‑bettor. Plan: check now, call reasonable bets on the flop to extract value from worse A’s/K’s/medium pairs, and re-evaluate on the turn; don’t commit more chips unless I improve or get clear value.

Flop Ah-3d-Ks rainbow board, I hold Kc9c for top pair kings with a weak 9 kicker. Pro (short stack with 100 behind) 3-bet pre as BB and checked, representing a wide range including Ax, weaker Kx, pairs, suited connectors where my hand has good equity. Minni (UTG raiser who flatted the 3-bet) checked, which could be a strong ace, QQ-JJ, or AK, but also possible floats or medium strength. Action checked to me in position, pot 3875. Betting for thin value against worse pairs/top pairs (KQ, KT, etc.), Ax without king, and to charge any gutshots (QJ, 54s unlikely). Checking risks giving free cards to draws or allowing bluffs, and pot control is less important with top pair on dry board. Size to ~half pot to build pot while getting called by worse and putting pressure before Pro's 100. Plan to barrel turn if ahead.

I have only 100 chips remaining in my stack, which is 1 Big Blind. The pot is already 5,875. The pot odds are roughly 59:1. Even though the flop of Ace-King-Three is terrible for my pocket Tens and it is highly likely one of my opponents has a piece of this board, I cannot fold for such a trivial amount relative to the pot. I am effectively all-in and must call to realize any remaining equity (hitting a set or running cards).

I have top pair with a good kicker (A♦Q♦) on A♥ 3♦ K♠. I checked the flop to control the pot vs the 3‑better (Pro) and cold‑caller Elon. Elon now bets 2,000 into a 5,975 pot; Pro appears effectively capped/short, so the real decision is vs Elon. Calling 2,000 gives me ~3:1 pot odds and I have decent equity vs Elon’s likely Kx/Ax/call‑range — I beat Kx and dominated Ax are limited (AK/AA/KK are the main hands that crush me). A shove now would bloat the pot and get called by better hands; folding is too tight given my equity and the price. I’ll call 2,000 to realize equity, keep the pot controllable, and re‑evaluate on the turn (plan to fold to heavy turn aggression unless I improve).

I have A♦Q♦ on A♥ 3♦ K♠ 7♦ — top pair with the best reasonable kicker. Pro is effectively capped/all‑in, so the real decision is vs Elon who cold‑called preflop, led the flop for 2,000 and then checked the turn. That line looks like medium strength (Kx or a medium Ax or a missed c‑bet) rather than an obvious two‑pair/set. My stack is 1,875 vs a 7,975 pot — committing now would be risky because a shove or big bet will get called/raised by hands that dominate me (AA/AK/KK). I also still have decent equity to improve on the river (9 diamonds + 3 queens), so controlling the pot is valuable. Checking the turn preserves showdown value, keeps worse A/K hands and some bluffs in Elon’s range, and lets me see the river before committing the rest of my short stack. Plan: check and re‑evaluate on the river — if Elon checks back I can target a value shove or smaller bet on many rivers; if he bets a small amount I can call for showdown; if he fires a large bet/raise I will fold more often because I’m likely behind.

Turn board Ah-3d-Ks-7d introduces a diamond flush draw and possible gutshots (e.g., J-T for broadway, 5-6 unlikely), but doesn't improve my top pair kings with 9 kicker.
