If he bets, I can re-evaluate based on the pot odds provided, knowing I still have roughly 20% equity to hit the nut flush on the river, plus potential outs for an Ace or a Four.

Turn paired the board with Jh. I still have a monster draw: flush draw (9 outs) plus straight draw with A or 9 (about 6 more clean outs). Flash has been check-calling, showing some showdown value but not aggression. With ~15 outs and position, I should continue betting as a semi-bluff. If Flash has a Jack, they'd likely lead or raise. Betting around 65% pot to apply pressure and build the pot for when I hit.

On the turn, the Jack of Hearts pairs the board, which is a significant card given Claude's pre-flop 3-bet and aggressive post-flop action. My hand, Ace-Four of diamonds, gives me a pair of fours and the nut flush draw. The pot is currently 7,350 and I need to call 2,900 to see the river, providing me with pot odds of approximately 2.5:1 (or 28%). With 9 diamond outs remaining, my equity for hitting the nut flush is roughly 20%. While the board pairing makes it possible that Claude has a full house (with hands like TT, AJ, or JT), he could also be betting trips, an overpair (AA, KK, QQ), or semi-bluffing with high diamonds like AKd or KQd. Given the substantial implied odds if I hit the nut flush against the tournament chip leader, and the fact that I still have a pair that could occasionally win at showdown, calling is the most profitable decision.

On the river, the board is Td-4c-Jd-Jh-2s. I have missed my nut flush draw, leaving me with two pair (Jacks and Fours) with an Ace kicker. I am out of position against Claude, who has been the aggressor throughout the hand (3-betting pre-flop and betting both the flop and turn). Given Claude's line, he likely holds a strong range including trip Jacks, overpairs (AA, KK, QQ), or potentially a full house if he held JJ, TT, or JT. There is very little value in betting here, as better hands will call and worse hands (like missed draws) will likely fold. By checking, I hope to reach a showdown cheaply if Claude checks behind with a hand like Ace-high or a medium pair, or I can evaluate his action if he decides to bet.

I missed my flush and straight draws completely. I have K-high with no showdown value. Flash has check-called two large bets on flop and turn, showing they want to see showdown with likely a ten or middle pair. Bluffing here is unlikely to work - they're somewhat pot-committed and have shown call-down tendencies. Best to check back and accept the loss rather than spew more chips.

I am UTG in a 5-max game with K9s. This hand is a standard open in this position to exert pressure and pick up the blinds, or play a pot with initiative. Given my stack size and the recent table dynamics where Claude has been quite active and aggressive, I want to continue playing a solid range. A 3x raise to 300 is consistent with my previous opening sizes.

Ah8h is a solid suited ace in UTG+1. Facing a 3x open from Flash, I have good playability with nut flush potential. With my healthy stack of 18775, calling here to see a flop is profitable - if I hit the flush draw or ace, I can extract value. 3-betting is also an option but calling keeps the pot manageable.

Facing a 3bb UTG open plus a call, 9♦4♣ offsuit is far too weak to continue. It has poor playability, gets dominated when we make a pair, and performs badly multiway even with position. This is also not a good 3-bet squeeze candidate (no blockers, no suitedness), so the highest-EV play is to preserve chips and wait for better spots.

I have Qc6d in the small blind facing an UTG open to 300 with one caller (Claude). This will be a multiway pot and I’ll be out of position vs at least the raiser and caller. Q6o has poor equity and post‑flop playability, is often dominated, and has very little fold equity if I try a 3‑bet. Calling 250 to play OOP in a 3‑way pot with a weak hand is a losing long‑term line; folding preserves a healthy stack for better spots. I fold.

KsJd is a strong hand with good equity and playability multi-way. Facing an UTG raise from Flash, who raises frequently based on history, and a call from Claude. In the BB, we get excellent pot odds: calling 200 more into a 750 pot (3.75:1 implied odds, plus positionally closing the action). Folding is too tight; 3-betting risks playing out of position with a marginal offsuit hand against two opponents. Calling keeps the pot multi-way where KJ performs well and allows post-flop flexibility with our 58BB stack.

Flop 7c Td Jc gives me top pair top kicker (Jd pairs the Jc, Ks kicker) with a gutshot to the nut straight (Q makes Broadway). Strong hand multi-way against Flash's UTG range and Claude's calling range. Board has flush draw potential (two clubs) and straight draws (e.g., 89, Q9), so betting protects against draws, extracts value from worse pairs/draws, and builds the pot with my ~58BB stack. First to act, betting ~2/3 pot denies equity and takes initiative. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or check-raises from strong hands. History shows Flash and Claude call bets with marginal holdings.

I have an open-ended straight draw (needing an 8 or a Queen) on a board of 7-10-J. Although Elon has led out into me (the pre-flop aggressor), the price to call is 650 to win a total pot of 2250 (roughly 3.5:1), which is acceptable for a 17% chance to hit on the turn and roughly 32% by the river. Since Claude is still behind me, calling is safer than raising, as it keeps the pot manageable and allows me to see the turn before committing more chips. If Claude raises behind me, I will re-evaluate based on the new pot odds.

I have Ah8h on a 7c-Td-Jc board. This is a complete whiff - no pair, no flush draw (board is clubs, I have hearts), and only a weak backdoor straight possibility. Two opponents are already involved with Elon betting and Flash calling. With just ace-high and no real equity, this is a clear fold.

Heads-up on the turn OOP with top pair top kicker (Jd pairing Jc with Ks kicker) on a 7c-Td-Jc-4s board. The board is somewhat coordinated with flush draw (clubs), gutshot straight draws (e.g., 89, Q9), and possible two-pair or better combos, but the 4s turn is relatively neutral and doesn't complete many strong draws. Preflop, Flash opened UTG (tight position), got called by Claude and me (BB defense with good odds), and then check-called my flop c-bet of 650 into ~800 (solid value/protection bet with TPTK + gutshot). His call suggests a range including Jx (AJ+, KJ, QJ, JT), Tx (AT, KT, QT), sets (77, TT, JJ unlikely pre), combo draws, or floats. Folding Claude improved our equity multi-way. Pot is 2250, my stack 4950 (~50BB), Flash deep at 16100. Betting ~2/3 pot (1500) extracts value from worse pairs (Qx, Tx), draws, and Ax-high, protects against runner-runner draws or equity realization, and denies him a free river card. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or facing a turn bet where we'd have to call OOP, and while check-call/check-fold is playable, continued aggression with a premium hand builds the pot and leverages our perceived strength from the flop lead. History shows Flash calls stations (e.g., multiple check-calls in prior hands), so thin value likely. No strong reason to check; bet sizing 1500 balances value/protection without overcommitting our stack (leaves ~3450 behind).

On the turn, the board is 7c-Td-Jc-4s. I have an open-ended straight draw (8s and Qs). Elon, who led out on the flop (a donk bet into two players), has now fired a second barrel of 1500 into a pot of 2250. The current pot is 3750 and it costs 1500 to call. This gives me pot odds of approximately 28.5%. With 8 outs to a straight, my chances of hitting on the river are only about 17.4%. Even considering implied odds (Elon has roughly 1300-1900 remaining), the call is mathematically incorrect. Furthermore, the 8c and Qc might complete a flush if Elon is holding clubs, potentially tainting my outs. Given Elon's strength leading into the pre-flop aggressor and his commitment to the pot, it is highly likely he holds a strong made hand like two pair or a set. Chasing here is -EV, so I will fold and preserve my healthy stack.
J4o from UTG is a clear fold. This hand is far too weak to open from early position - it's not in any reasonable UTG opening range. I'll wait for a better spot.

We’re first in with a very weak offsuit connector (4♦3♣) and no positional advantage. Limping invites isolation from the button/blinds and creates multiway pots where this hand realizes equity poorly; opening would be overly loose and unnecessary with ~56 BB. Best EV is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

Pocket tens on the button with a 12625 stack and only the SB and BB to act — strong, value hand and we have position. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to call; a min-raise to 200 is too cheap and gives the blinds good odds to limp/call. I want fold equity, to deny speculative hands equity, and to build the pot with a hand that plays well postflop, so I open to 3x the big blind (300). Postflop I’ll c‑bet favorable textures and use position to navigate; if I face a sizeable 3‑bet from the shorter SB I’ll re-evaluate (more inclined to fold to a large isolation 3‑bet, but can call smaller 3‑bets and play postflop).

Preflop in SB with A7o facing a button isolation raise to 300 after two folds. Button ranges are wide, and A7o is a strong 3-bet candidate here due to the ace blocker, fold equity against Minni's likely wide range, and good playability postflop. Pot is 450, calling 250 OOP isn't ideal with position disadvantage and multiway potential if BB calls. Folding is too tight given the spot. 3-betting to 900 applies pressure, isolates the button, and sizes up appropriately (3x their raise). Minni's history shows aggression but also folds to 3-bets. BB may fold wide behind. Effective stacks are deep enough for postflop play if called.

I am holding the worst possible starting hand (2-3 offsuit) in the Big Blind. Minni opened from the button and Elon 3-bet from the Small Blind. There is no strategic justification for calling or raising with this hand, especially out of position against two active opponents. Folding is the only viable move.

I opened to 300 on the button with TT and the SB (Elon) 3‑bet to 900 — a relatively small 3‑bet from the shorter SB. TT is strong and plays very well in position, so I prefer to call and realize my equity rather than 4‑bet commit with a hand that can extract value postflop. Calling 600 more (900 total) gives ~31.6% break‑even equity on the pot and keeps his range wide (pairs, broadways, some suited connectors and bluffs) that I often dominate or can outplay on later streets. Plan: call to 900, see the flop, bet for value on dry/low boards and exercise pot control or fold to heavy aggression on Ace/King‑heavy boards; re‑evaluate if he jams or makes a large, polarizing action.

Flop board is 6d Ks 6s, a paired board with a king. My A7o completely misses: no pair, no draw, ace-high is weak OOP heads-up vs button who called my 3-bet. My 3-bet range includes value like AK, KK, AQs+, sets possible postflop, but this is a clear check. Pot is 1900, my stack 6300 effective vs Minni's 11725. No reason to barrel bluff here with no equity; board favors her calling range (Kx, 65s, 76s, pocket pairs). Check to induce bluffs or realize equity if she checks back, then fold to reasonable bets. Avoids bloating pot OOP with air.

I have pocket tens (T T) on a 6♦ K♠ 6♠ flop after calling Elon’s small SB 3‑bet preflop. I’m ahead of a lot of his calling/3‑betting combos (smaller pairs, many broadways/bluffs) but behind any K and crushed by 6‑6. Elon checked the flop, which shows some caution/neutrality. This is a fairly dry board with little draw equity, so a half‑pot c‑bet extracts value from worse pairs and some draws, denies free cards, and preserves pot control vs his shorter stack. I’ll bet ~1,000 (≈50–55% pot). Plan: fold to a large check‑raise (he’s likely very strong with 66 or Kx if he raises big); if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn — value‑bet blanks, pot‑control or fold to heavy action on K/A/6 turns/river.

Facing a 1000 bet into ~1900 pot on a 6d Ks 6s flop after checking my missed A7o (ace-high, no pair/draw). My preflop 3-bet range is polarized, but this hand has zero equity against Minni's calling range, which includes KK, 66, AK, KQs, 76s/65s that connect well. Board is paired and favors her range heavily; donk bet suggests strength or semi-bluff, but nothing induces her to fold worse than my ace-high OOP. Calling commits ~1/3 stack OOP with no fold equity later and poor playability. Raising is a pure bluff into a strong range on a dry board. Pot odds (~2.9:1) don't justify a call given <20% equity. Standard check-fold as planned preflop, preserving stack for better spots.

5-handed UTG, pocket sixes are comfortably in an opening range. Raising builds the pot for when we flop a set, and also gives us fold equity to just take blinds/deny multiway realization versus overcards. With my stack (~56bb) I don’t want to limp and invite an isolation from the deeper stacks in position. I’ll use a standard small open that still pressures the blinds.

Five-handed, UTG opened to 250. I have Ad7c in UTG+1 — a playable ace in short-handed play but vulnerable out of position. A 3-bet bluff here is thin (UTG opener is likely still reasonably strong and there are two deep stacks behind who can squeeze), while folding is too tight with deep stacks and a reasonable chance to flop an A or steal the pot. Pot is 400 and the price to see a flop is 250 (calling gives ~38% break-even), so I’ll just call to keep the pot manageable and evaluate the flop. Plan: fold to a sizable 3-bet, proceed only with top pair, strong draw, or a favorable board texture.

5s7d is a speculative offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against an UTG raise and call from Minni. UTG ranges are strong, and even on the button, calling multiway risks difficult postflop spots with reverse implied odds. Pot odds are ~2.6:1 effective (250 to win 650), but hand doesn't justify it. Preserve stack for premium situations in mid-tournament with 63BB effective.

I am in the Small Blind with 3h 5c. FiveTwo raised from UTG and Minni called. This is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position against two opponents. Even though it's only 200 more to call, 3-5 offsuit is a clear fold to preserve my stack for stronger holdings.

I have 3s8c - a very weak offsuit hand with no connectivity or high card value. Even getting ~5:1 pot odds (call 150 into 800), this hand plays poorly multiway and out of position. I'll fold and save chips for better spots.

This flop (K-T-9, fairly connected) hits the caller’s UTG+1 flatting range hard (KQ/KJ, QJ, JT, TT/99), and our 66 has very poor equity and little ability to barrel turns. Betting small will often just get called and put us in tough spots versus pressure, while checking keeps the pot controlled and protects our overall range (we can still have strong hands when we check). Plan: check, and versus most bets we’ll just fold.

I called preflop to see a flop, but on K-T-9 rainbow I only have Ace-high with no backdoor flush or straight. UTG open range contains a lot of hands that beat or are very close to me here (Kx, Tx, 9x, pairs and QJ already makes the Broadway straight), so I don't have the initiative or a hand worth building the pot with OOP. My preflop plan was to continue only with top pair, strong draws, or a clearly favorable texture — this flop gives none of those. I’ll control the pot and check. If FiveTwo leads I’ll fold to meaningful pressure (only calling very small probes if I believe they’re bluffing often); if they check back I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and only continue with an Ace or a favorable runout.

Turn is a complete brick and the flop checked through, which caps Villain’s range somewhat (fewer immediate value bets like strong Kx/sets/2p, though some traps exist). With 66 we have very low showdown value on K-T-9 and don’t want to face a polarized river stab. A small delayed c-bet works as a profitable probe: it can fold out a chunk of ace-high/underpair floats, charges QJ/J8 type hands that took a free card, and functions as a blocker to keep the pot manageable. If Minni raises, our hand can’t continue and we comfortably fold; if called, we’ll usually check river and proceed cautiously.

I hold Ad7c on Kc Th 9d 2h. This is heads-up vs the UTG opener who checked the flop and now makes a small turn probe of 250 into a 900 pot. I have no pair — only Ace-high — but the bet is cheap. Calling 250 would require ~18% equity (250 to win ~1400), which is well within a realistic bluff/value mix of his line. Raising OOP with nothing is too thin and folding surrenders too much equity for a small stake. Plan: call the 250 to see the river; if I pair the Ace I can extract value, if I miss I'll fold to significant river aggression but can call small river probes.

River pairs the deuce (K-T-9-2-2), which doesn’t meaningfully change equities. After flop checks through and my small turn probe got called, Minni’s range is now weighted toward made hands that beat 66 (Kx/Tx/9x, some underpairs like 77/88, occasional slowplays like QJ), with relatively few natural bluffs available. Betting would mostly get called by better and fold out the few hands we actually beat (A-high), so it’s a poor value/bluff spot. Best play is to check and try to realize whatever thin showdown value we have while avoiding getting put in a bigger pot unnecessarily.

Board: T♥ K♣ 9♦ 2♥ 2♠. I hold A♦7♣ — Ace-high with no pair and no meaningful showdown equity vs an UTG opener’s range (Kx, Tx, 9x, pocket pairs). Villain checked the flop, led a small turn probe (250) which I called; that line can be thin but also consistent with many hands that currently beat me. On the river he checked; betting as a bluff here has poor fold equity against an opener and will be called by most value hands, so it’s a negative-EV shove or small bluff. With zero to call and the pot already 1,150, I minimize further risk and go to showdown: check.

UTG with A♦2♣ in a 5‑hand game is worth an open. I want the initiative and to avoid multiway pots where this weak ace is easily dominated, so I’ll raise rather than limp. A standard 3xBB to 300 is slightly above the min and fits the table sizing history — it gives fold equity against the two shorter stacks behind and keeps stronger players honest. Plan: c‑bet favorable flops; fold to a large 3‑bet from the big stacks, but consider calling small 3‑bets depending on odds and opponent tendencies.

Facing a raise to 300 from UTG (Minni), who has shown aggression in early position with a reasonably strong range including pairs, broadways, and suited aces. My 9sTh is a suited connector with some playability, but from UTG+1, I'll be OOP postflop against their range, which hits flops better than mine. Pot odds are about 1.5:1 (300 to call into 450), but implied odds are reduced OOP and against a tight-ish UTG opener. Not a great 3-bet candidate as it doesn't block their value range well. Folding to preserve stack for better positions and hands in this multi-way tournament spot.

I am on the button with Ace-Four suited, a hand with strong post-flop potential due to its nut-flush and wheel-straight possibilities. Minni, who has been quite active in opening pots, raised to 300 from the hijack/UTG position. Given my position and the hand's playability, calling is a standard and effective play. This allows me to see a flop in position without inflating the pot too early, while also keeping my range wide and protected.

4h5s is a weak offsuit hand. While I'm getting decent pot odds (250 to win 750), I'll be out of position post-flop against two opponents. This hand has limited playability - no flush potential and weak straight draws. Folding is the disciplined play.

We’re getting a good immediate price (200 more to win a pot that will be 950), but K3o performs terribly multiway and out of position versus an UTG opening range plus a button flat. We’re frequently dominated (Kx, better kickers) and have significant reverse implied odds when we make a pair, while having poor playability on most flops. A squeeze is too spewy with this holding given UTG strength and deep stacks behind. Best is to relinquish the blind and preserve stack for better spots.

I opened UTG to 300 and we’re heads-up on a Kc 8h 4c flop with a 750-chip pot. As the preflop aggressor I should continuation-bet most of my range — this board is relatively dry (K-high with two clubs) and folds out a lot of the button’s calling range (weak broadways, missed connectors, some small pairs). My hand Ad2c has decent showdown value (ace-high) and a small backdoor club possibility, but it’s not strong enough to check and give the button free cards very often. I’ll bet ~2/3 pot (500) to get fold equity, charge any club draws, and keep control of the hand. If I get a large raise I’ll fold (my equity vs a raise is poor); if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn — calling small bets with A-high or barreling only on safe turn cards (A or blank/non-club) depending on the action.

On a board of Kc-8h-4c, I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker. Minni has led out with a continuation bet of 500 into a pot of 1250. Given that I am in position and have a piece of the board along with the best possible non-paired kicker, folding would be too tight. I am getting roughly 2.5-to-1 on a call. Raising is unnecessary as it would only force out hands I beat and get called by hands that have me dominated (like a King or a higher pocket pair). Calling allows me to see how the turn develops and potentially catch an Ace or another Four, while also keeping her potential bluffs in the hand.

Turn is 5♣ giving K♣ 8♥ 4♣ 5♣. I have A♦2♣ — Ace‑high plus a four‑card club draw (9 club outs) and two non‑club aces as additional clean outs (≈11 clean outs, ~24% to make a pair or flush; ~30% if you include pairing the 2). I was the preflop and flop aggressor and this is a classic semi‑bluffable spot, but the turn is NOT a “safe” blank — it actually improves club equity for the caller’s range (and helps hands with a club or Kx). My earlier plan was to barrel only on safe turns (A or blank/non‑club); this 5♣ strengthens the caller’s range and makes betting riskier. I’ll check to control the pot and realize my draw equity cheaply. If Flash bets a small‑to‑medium probe (roughly ≤ ~1,000–1,200) I’ll call for the correct pot odds and implied odds; I’ll fold to a large raise or shove that represents a strong made hand.

The turn card 5c completes a potential flush and several straight draws. Minni, who led the flop, has now checked. While my bottom pair of fours has some showdown value, it is extremely vulnerable on this board. Betting here doesn't achieve much; better hands like a King or any club will likely call, and I'm not looking to turn my pair into a bluff yet. By checking behind, I control the size of the pot, see a free river, and hope to reach a cheap showdown or potentially improve to two pair or trips if an Ace or Four lands on the river.

I opened UTG and c-bet the Kc 8h 4c flop; Flash called. The turn 5c completed some club combos and strengthened his range, so I checked to control the pot and realize equity. The river 8d is a non‑club blank — my actual hand is just Ace‑high (Ad2c) with no pair. Flash checked the turn and hasn’t shown aggression, so his remaining range contains a lot of missed draws, weaker overcards and some medium pairs. Checking here surrenders the pot to any Kx or pair he slow‑plays; a moderate sized bluff gives decent fold equity. I’ll make a half‑pot river bet (875) to fold out missed clubs/overcards and steal the 1750 pot. If called I’ll muck — this is a thin bluff attempt sized to balance fold equity vs risk given my stack.

On the river, the board is 8h-Kc-4c-5c-8d. My hand is As-4s, which gives me two pair: Eights and Fours with an Ace kicker. Minni's line (raise pre-flop, bet flop, check turn, bet river) is often indicative of a marginal hand that checked the turn when the flush completed and is now betting for thin value or a bluff. However, my pair of fours is extremely weak on this board. Any King (making 8s and Ks), any Eight (making trips), any pocket pair higher than 44 (like 66, 77, 99, etc.), and any 5 (making 8s and 5s) all beat my hand. Furthermore, any flush made on the turn also beats me. While I am getting 3:1 odds to call, there are very few hands Minni would bet for value that I actually beat, and her range is heavily weighted towards hands that have me crushed. Folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

Preflop UTG with 67d suited in a 5-handed game at 63BB effective stack. 67s is a marginal speculative hand with good playability multiway but poor position if raised or called. Facing deep-stacked opponents behind, including active raisers like Flash next. Standard play is to fold to avoid difficult OOP decisions postflop and preserve stack for better spots. No strong history indicating loose opens behind to exploit.

Ace-Five offsuit is a weak hand to open with from early position (UTG+1) even in a five-handed game. With aggressive players like Claude on the button and Minni in the big blind, raising here risks being dominated post-flop or facing a 3-bet from the shorter stacks (Elon/FiveTwo). Folding is the standard disciplined play to preserve my stack.

3s4d is a very weak offsuit hand. Even on the button with only blinds to act, this hand has poor playability and limited value. While I have position and a healthy stack, opening with 34o is too loose and likely -EV. Better to wait for a stronger hand.

Folds to us in the small blind, so this is a pure heads-up blind battle where raising has strong EV. J9s plays well with initiative (can make strong top pairs/straights/flushes) and we benefit a lot from fold equity versus the big blind’s wide defend range. Completing invites the BB to realize equity for cheap and puts us in tougher postflop spots out of position. I’ll raise to a standard ~3bb size to pressure Minni’s range while keeping the pot manageable if called.

I’m in the big blind with J♥8♠ facing a 300 open from the small blind. The pot is 400 and I need 200 more to call (total 300), giving ~3:1 immediate pot odds. J8o is marginal but playable — it has reasonable showdown equity and some post‑flop playability versus a likely wide SB opening. With deep stacks I prefer to call and see a flop rather than 3‑bet out of position (which would bloat the pot and leave me first-to-act postflop) or fold and surrender good pot odds. Plan: call to 300, evaluate the flop (continue with a pair, strong draws, or favorable textures; fold to heavy aggression if I miss and face strong pressure).

Paired A88 is a very good board for the preflop raiser: we have plenty of strong Ax and overpairs, while the BB has a lot of unpaired junk that can’t continue versus any pressure. With J9 we have essentially pure air (no diamond, only backdoor clubs), so our best line is a small range c-bet to pick up the pot immediately at high frequency and deny equity to hands like KQ/QT/76 that peeled pre. Size small because the board is static and we don’t need protection; we also keep our risk low and can comfortably fold to a check-raise.

I hold J♥8♠ on an 8♦ A♦ 8♥ flop — I have trips (three eights), which is almost certainly the best hand here except for the tiny combos that make quads (the last 8) or A8 (full house). FiveTwo’s flop stab is tiny (175 into a 775 pot) and looks like a standard wide SB c‑bet or probe. This is a textbook spot to raise for value and protection: I want to charge A‑x hands and two‑diamond flush draws, and extract from pocket pairs and weaker holdings that will call a meaningful raise. I don’t need to jam — that would be unnecessarily polarizing and could fold out some calling hands — but a sizable raise makes draws and top‑pair hands pay to continue. I therefore raise to 1000 total on the flop (adding 825 chips). If called I will re‑evaluate on the turn and continue to build the pot on safe cards or protect/settle on dangerous diamond/ace/8 runouts.

We c-bet small on A88 because it’s a high-frequency range bet, but Minni’s check-raise to 1000 is a significant show of strength on a board where continuing ranges are heavily weighted to Ax and 8x. With Jc9c we have essentially no equity (no pair, no diamond backdoor, no meaningful straight draws) and calling needs ~32% immediate equity plus realization, which we won’t have facing turn pressure at a low SPR. Turning this into a bluff 3-bet/jam is also poor: we don’t block Ax/8x, and we’d need an unrealistically high fold rate. Best play is to cut losses and preserve stack.

K2o is a very weak hand to open from under-the-gun (UTG) in a 5-handed game. The King has some high-card value, but the deuce kicker provides no connectivity and very little showdown value if a King does not hit. Opening with such a wide and weak range from early position puts me at a significant disadvantage against the remaining four players, especially with Claude and Minni having large stacks. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.
A5o in UTG+1 is marginal but playable. With a healthy stack of 183BB, I can open raise here. The ace has some equity and I can represent strength on ace-high boards. Standard open to 2.5x.

Claude opens to 2.5bb from UTG+1 in a 5-handed game. Even on the Button, J4 offsuit is too dominated (by Jx, broadways, and many suited connectors that outplay us) to defend profitably. A 3-bet bluff with this hand is also poor since it has very weak equity and bad playability when called, and Claude has shown willingness to continue. Preserve the ~57bb stack for better spots in position.

Claude opened UTG+1 to 250 — an early-position raise representing a fairly strong range. I have K♦8♣ in the small blind: marginal and often dominated vs an EP opener, but it has reasonable showdown value and the potential to make top pair. Calling 200 more (total 250) is cheap relative to my 14,500 stack and gives good pot odds to see a flop; 3-betting out of position as a bluff is unattractive because I’d be OOP against both Claude and likely the big blind. Plan: call to see the flop. If the big blind folds and it’s heads-up vs Claude I’ll proceed only when I hit (K or 8 or a favorable draw) and otherwise control the pot or fold to strong aggression. If the big blind 3-bets, I will fold K8o to a substantive 3-bet.

Preflop in the big blind with 4s2c, one of the worst possible hands. Facing a raise to 250 from Claude in UTG+1 (tightish early position) and a flat call from Minni in SB. My hand has no high card value, no suited connectors for speculation, and minimal straight potential. Pot odds are about 4:1 (150 to call into 600 pot), but equity against raiser's range (likely strong) and caller's range is too low, especially multiway OOP postflop. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this long tournament.

Claude opened UTG+1 and then checked the flop to me. I flopped top pair (K♦8♣ on 6♠7♦K♥) with a mediocre kicker — ahead of a lot of his calling/continuation range but vulnerable to better Ks, sets and rare two-pairs. Heads‑up this is a clear spot to bet for value and protection: a half‑pot bet charges any straight draws (e.g. 8‑9 combos), gets called by worse Kx and underpairs, and keeps the pot manageable if he shows aggression. I will size modestly to 300 into 600; if he check‑raises big I’ll give credit to a very strong range and fold or re‑evaluate depending on sizing, if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn.

I completely whiffed this flop with just Ah 5d on a K-7-6 board. I have ace high with no pair and no meaningful draw. Minni's small donk bet of 300 into 500 could be a weak king, a draw like 89/45, or testing with a medium pair. With no equity besides a backdoor gutshot, continuing here would be purely speculative. Folding is the disciplined play.
AKs is a premium hand, one of the best starting hands in poker. From UTG position, I should raise for value. Standard opening raise of 2.5x BB to 250.

Claude is opening from UTG in a 5-handed game to 2.5bb. 6♣3♥ offsuit has very poor playability and realization versus an early-position range, and calling would put us in a dominated, reverse-implied-odds spot with a medium stack (~57bb). There’s no profitable 3-bet bluff profile here either given position and blockers. Best decision is to release and preserve stack for better steal/defend spots.

Claude opened UTG to 250. I’m on the button with 5h4h — a suited, connected speculative hand. With 149 BB effective and position on Claude, this is a clear flat-call spot: good implied odds and post‑flop playability outweigh trying a thin 3‑bet. There are two players to act (SB and BB) so a small 3‑bet (to 400) would often get called or 4‑bet and put me in difficult spots against an UTG range. Calling 250 keeps the pot manageable, preserves fold equity post‑flop, and lets me realize equity with position.

Preflop in SB with Kc9c, facing a UTG raise to 250 from Claude (tight/aggressive range) and a button call from Minni. Pot is 650, calling 200 more for ~23% equity needed, but K9s plays poorly multiway OOP against strong ranges. No position advantage postflop, better to fold and wait for better spots to build stack over tournament.

I am in the Big Blind holding Qd 9d, which is a strong suited hand with good playability. Claude opened from UTG for 250 and Minni called on the Button. I only need to call 150 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 800 chips. These pot odds (over 4:1) are too good to pass up with a hand that flops well in multi-way pots. While I will be out of position, the speculative value of my hand justifies the small investment.

I have flopped a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten) and have two overcards to the board's middle and low cards. In a three-way pot with the pre-flop raiser still to act, checking is the most prudent move to see how my opponents react to this relatively dry board.

I have AKs but completely whiffed this Js-2h-8c flop. I have two overcards and a backdoor spade flush draw. Against two opponents, a c-bet with just air is risky - this board hits a lot of calling ranges (Jx, 8x, pocket pairs). Checking and reevaluating on the turn is the better play here. I can catch an A or K for top pair, or potentially bluff later if checked to again.

Check. On J♠-2♥-8♣ my 5♥4♥ has no pair and only remote runner-runner equity (backdoor hearts/straights). Claude opened UTG so his range is relatively strong; both opponents checked to me, so there's no clear fold equity to profitably fire a bluff into two players with deep stacks. In position I prefer pot control and to see a free turn — I can float a small turn bet to try and take it away on the river or fold to significant aggression. Plan: check now, call a small continuation bet to use position and potentially bluff later, but fold to a large bet.

The turn 6s doesn't significantly change the board texture. I still hold a gutshot straight draw with my Nine and have two overcards that might be clean, though unlikely. After the flop checked around, it's clear no one has a powerhouse hand, but there's no reason to bloat the pot out of position with minimal equity. Checking is the most standard play here to try and see a free river and potentially realize my draw.

The 6s on the turn gives me the nut flush draw (9 outs) plus 6 overcards to the A or K. With ~15 outs and position to act, this is a great spot to semi-bluff. Betting around 50% pot applies pressure and builds the pot for when I hit. If called, I still have strong equity to improve on the river.

Call 400. Claude's 400 into a ~1200 pot is a small c-bet (~1/3 pot). I have 5♥4♥ with no pair — only 4 clean outs to a 7 for a straight and 6 thin outs to single pair — roughly ~18–20% raw river equity. The call costs 400 to win ~1600 (about 4:1 pot odds), so this is an inexpensive way to realize equity and use position on the river. Raising as a bluff multiway is too risky without clean draws; folding here would be too tight given the price. Plan: call to see the river, then re-evaluate — fold to heavy action if I miss, or look to apply pressure only if both opponents show weakness.

I am in the Big Blind with Qd 9d. The board is Js 2h 8c 6s. On the turn, after checking, Claude (UTG) bets 400 and Minni (Button) calls. The pot currently stands at 1600, and it costs me 400 to call, meaning I am getting 4:1 pot odds (or need 20% equity to break even). My primary draw is a gutshot to a Queen-high straight (requiring a Ten). With only 4 outs, my chances of hitting the straight on the river are approximately 8.7%. While I also have a Queen as an overcard, it is unlikely to be a 'clean' out given the pre-flop action and the turn bet/call. Since I lack the immediate pot odds to call and my implied odds are not significant enough to justify chasing a gutshot out of position, the most professional decision is to fold and preserve my stack.

River brought the Jc, pairing the board. My flush draw bricked - I have just A-high. The pot is 1600 and it's checked to me. Minni called my turn bet, suggesting they have something - likely a pair (8x, 6x, pocket pair) or possibly a jack. Bluffing here is risky since my story is weak and A-high actually has some showdown value against busted draws. Checking back is the smart play - I might occasionally win at showdown and avoid losing more chips when called.

I missed — 5♥4♥ has no pair and the backdoor hearts didn’t hit. Claude opened UTG and then led the turn for a small c‑bet; that line looks like a relatively strong/value-heavy range (and river pairing J makes trips or strong pairs plausible). With only two players, my bluff has very limited fold equity (I don’t block J or premium pairs) and the pot is small relative to our stacks, so risking chips here is negative EV. Plan: keep the pot small, check and fold to any river bet — preserve the stack for better, more profitable spots.

5-handed from UTG (effectively middle position), 7♦6♦ is a suited connector that plays well in raised pots with enough stack depth (~56bb). Limping invites multiway pots where we’re out of position and makes it harder to realize equity; folding is too tight given short-handed dynamics. I’ll open-raise to a standard 2.5bb size to apply pressure, deny blinds a cheap look, and keep my range uncapped while still leaving room to maneuver versus a 3-bet.

Facing an UTG open to 250 (pot 400) from FiveTwo with Kc5c in UTG+1. K5s is a marginal, often dominated hand versus an early-position opener and is tough to play out of position if called. Calling 250 invites multiway action and leaves me OOP with poor kicker equity; 3-betting as a bluff here risks unnecessary chips and will often be called or 4-bet by players with deeper stacks. Given table dynamics and my stack depth, the correct short-term EV play is to conserve chips and fold to the preflop raise.

On the button with Ks7s facing a UTG raise to 250 (2.5BB) into a 4BB pot, getting excellent pot odds of about 2.2:1 to call 250. K7s is a strong suited hand with king high, good playability, straight/flush potential, and dominates many weaker king hands. FiveTwo's UTG raise range is decent but history shows FiveTwo plays a wide range, often calling stations postflop. Position allows me to realize equity well, control pot, and bluff effectively. Folding is too tight; 3-betting risks isolation with a speculative hand against a shorter stack (FiveTwo ~54BB), better to call and play postflop. Stack depths support calling (my 61BB effective with FiveTwo's 54BB provides good implied odds). Aim to maximize EV over tournament.

Holding Ten-Nine offsuit in the Small Blind, I am facing a 2.5x open from FiveTwo (UTG) and a call from Elon (Button). While the pot odds are roughly 4:1 to call the additional 200 chips, T9o is a marginal hand that performs poorly out of position, especially against an early-position raiser. My stack is healthy at nearly 150 big blinds, and I prefer to avoid multi-way pots out of position with easily dominated hands. Folding is the most disciplined and standard professional play in this scenario.

Holding 4h5s offsuit in the BB facing a UTG raise and a call. Getting about 4.3:1 pot odds (650:150), but 45o is a weak hand that plays poorly multiway and out of position. Even though the pot odds are decent, this hand rarely makes strong holdings and will be difficult to play postflop. Folding is the disciplined play here.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser, I retain a strong perceived range advantage on T♠ J♣ 2♠ (overpairs, AJ/KJ/QJ, TT/JJ), while Elon’s button flatting range contains plenty of medium strength hands and some whiffed suited connectors that will fold to pressure. My 7♦6♦ has essentially no immediate equity (no flush draw, no straight draw), so the best way to realize EV is a small range c-bet that leverages fold equity without risking too much when called. A ~1/3 pot sizing also keeps my range protected and leaves room to barrel selectively on good turns (A/K/Q, spades, or cards that improve my equity).

Flop of Ts2sJc gives me the nut flush draw with Ks7s (9 outs, ~35% equity heads-up) plus king-high overcard, backdoor straight potential, and position. FiveTwo's UTG open + small donk bet (225 into 875, ~25% pot) into a draw-heavy board suggests weakness: possibly Jx, weak pairs, or weaker spade draws, as strong hands like sets or two-pair might bet larger for protection/value. His history shows loose-aggressive tendencies and calling station traits postflop, but shorter stack (52BB effective) and my position favor a semi-bluff raise to deny equity, build pot with strong draw, and take initiative. Calling realizes equity well but risks multiway if turn bricks; raising folds out ~50-60% of his range while getting called by worse (e.g., weaker flush draws, Jx). Raising to 700 (~3x his bet, ~2/3 pot) balances value/protection/bluffing frequencies. Pot odds excellent even if called (~4:1 immediate). EV+ over tournament.

Elon’s raise versus a small c-bet on T♠ J♣ 2♠ is a strong signal: value (Jx/Tx, sets) and plenty of semi-bluffs (spade draws, Q9) that have meaningful equity. With 7♦6♦ we have essentially no backdoors (no spades, no gutter), no relevant blockers to his value range, and we’re out of position—so continuing requires turning this into a high-frequency multi-street bluff with poor prospects. Calling 475 to chase near-zero equity is burning chips, and 3-bet bluffing here would risk a big chunk of our ~52bb stack without good blockers. Best play is to abandon the c-bet and preserve stack for better spots.

Pocket tens UTG in a 5‑handed game is a strong, clear open — don’t limp. I want to build the pot and take away equity from speculative hands (suited connectors, small pairs) while keeping worse hands in. A standard 3x open to 300 (table norm here) gives good fold equity, keeps the field manageable, and leaves plenty of room to play post‑flop with 14250 behind. Plan: if I get a single caller proceed with a c‑bet on favorable boards; if I face a 3‑bet I’ll re‑evaluate vs sizing and opponent (call or 4‑bet selectively, fold to oversized 4‑bets).

Preflop, UTG raise to 300 from Minni, who has been active in raising pots throughout history but UTG opens are typically strong (TT+, AQ+, AK). ATo offsuit is a marginal hand in early position (UTG+1), facing OOP postflop. Equity against a strong UTG range is around 35-40%, but positionally disadvantaged with multiple players behind including deep stacks on button and blinds. Pot odds are decent (450:300), but multiway pots OOP reduce playability. My stack is 67BB, no need to speculate with a non-premium Ace. Folding preserves stack for better spots in tournament with 150 hands remaining.

I am on the button with Qd 8h. Minni raised to 300 from UTG. In a 5-handed game, Q8o is a weak hand to play against an early position raise, even with the advantage of position. It doesn't have much post-flop playability and is likely dominated by Minni's opening range. Given my healthy stack, there is no reason to take a high-variance line or call with a subpar hand. I will fold.
53 offsuit is a very weak hand. Facing a UTG raise from Minni, this is a clear fold. No playability or equity against a UTG opening range.

We’re in the BB facing a 3x UTG open in a 5-handed lineup, and action has folded to us so we’ll be heads-up out of position. Although the direct price (200 to win 450) looks tempting, 7♥4♥ is a very low-quality defender versus an UTG range: poor high-card value, weak playability OOP, and we’ll realize equity badly against a tighter opening range. With a 51bb stack, this is a spot to conserve chips and avoid getting dragged into tough postflop situations with dominated/low-equity holdings.

5-handed NLHE, UTG position with Jd Ts offsuit. This is a weak offsuit broadway hand, not in a standard UTG opening range (typically 66+, AJo+, KQo+, suited aces/broadways, strong suited connectors). With 68BB stack and multiple players behind (including deep stacks like Claude on button), raising risks playing OOP postflop with a marginal hand. Limping is passive and invites multiway pots. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities, aligning with tight-aggressive strategy to maximize long-term EV in tournament.

Holding Ace-Deuce of Hearts, a suited Ace with nut flush potential, I am the first to act after Elon folded. In a 5-handed game, this hand is strong enough to open. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard play to take control of the pot and potentially take down the blinds or build a pot with a hand that plays well post-flop.

9s4d is a weak offsuit hand. Even with position on the button, calling a 3x raise from UTG+1 with this hand is -EV. The hand has poor playability and limited potential to make strong hands. Easy fold.

Facing a 3x open from Flash in the CO, we’re in the small blind and will be out of position the whole hand with Q6o, a hand that is frequently dominated by Flash’s continuing range (Qx, better broadways, suited holdings). Flat-calling creates a tough multiway spot with the BB still to act, and 3-bet bluffing this specific offsuit combo performs poorly when called. Best EV is to fold and preserve stack for better SB 3-bet/call candidates.

I’m in the big blind with A♦6♠ facing a 3bb open to 300 from Flash (UTG+1) in a 5-handed game. A6o is marginal but worth defending heads-up — it blocks many stronger A-x combos and has reasonable equity versus a wide open range. Folding is too tight given pot odds and my stack; a 3-bet OOP is feasible but unnecessary and often gets called or 4-bet, which puts me in tough spots with a weak kicker. Pot is 450 and I need to call 200 more (300 total committed) — after calling the pot will be 650, so I need ~30.8% equity to break even, which A6o can achieve vs a typical opener here. I’ll call to keep the pot controlled, see a flop, and proceed cautiously unless I hit a strong draw or top pair.

Heads-up vs Flash (preflop raiser). I hold A♦6♠ on Q♣-2♣-3♥: ace-high with no club and no pair — some showdown value but easily dominated by Qx, pocket pairs, and vulnerable to club draws. I’m out of position and there’s no reason to inflate the pot with a marginal hand; checking controls the pot, keeps worse hands in, and lets me see how he reacts. Plan: check and call small c-bets to realize equity, fold to large pressure, and only start betting when I improve (an Ace or a safe dry card) or pick up better reads.

I hit bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a board that generally favors my pre-flop opening range. As the aggressor, I will place a continuation bet to represent a strong Queen or overpair, which forces out overcards like KJ or AT and charges potential club flush draws. This bet also helps me define where I stand in the hand if Minni chooses to continue.

Preflop I defended the BB with A♦6♠ vs a 3bb open — fine heads-up. Flop Q♣‑2♣‑3♥: I have Ace‑high, no club draw and no pair, but I block many Ax combos and retain decent showdown equity. Flash (preflop raiser) c‑bets half‑pot 325. Calling requires ~25% equity (325/(975+325)=25%); against a typical 5‑hand c‑bet range (Qx, small pairs, some Ax, club draws and bluffs) A6o has enough equity to continue. I’m out of position and have no meaningful draw, so bluff‑raising OOP is unattractive — I don’t have fold equity and would be in tough spots if called. Calling is cheap relative to my 14.1k stack (~2.3%) and keeps worse hands and floats in, letting me realize equity and re‑evaluate on the turn. Plan: call the 325, see a turn, fold to significant turn/river aggression unless I improve to a value hand (Ace) or the action and read justify further investment.

I checked the flop and didn’t improve on the turn — I have only Ace-high (A♦6♠) on Q♣‑2♣‑3♥‑T♥. I’m out of position vs the pre‑flop raiser (Flash), have no club/heart draw, and very little showdown value beyond blocking some A‑x combos. Betting OOP here with a marginal hand would bloat the pot and put me in awkward spots if called or raised; I have poor fold equity and no real value. I’ll check to control the pot, allow him to bluff or show weakness, and only call small/medium bets to realize my (small) showdown equity — folding to large river aggression unless I pair my Ace or pick up a clear read to proceed.

On the turn, the Ten of Hearts does not improve my bottom pair, although it provides a backdoor heart flush draw. Minni has called both the pre-flop raise and the flop continuation bet, suggesting she has a piece of the board (like a Queen or a pocket pair) or a draw (like clubs). By checking behind here, I control the size of the pot, avoid getting check-raised off my equity, and take a free card to potentially improve to trips, two pair, or a backdoor flush on the river. My pair of deuces also has some showdown value against missed club draws.

I’ll check. I have only Ace‑high (A♦6♠) on Qc‑2c‑3h‑Th‑Kd and didn’t improve. Flash was the preflop raiser, c‑bet the flop (I called) then checked the turn — a line consistent with Qx, small pocket pairs, or a missed c‑bet bluff. There’s no bet to call on the river, and a value bet here would be very thin (most hands that would call beat me; only pure bluffs fold). With pot control and stack preservation in a 5‑handed spot, checking for showdown is the +EV play — we realize our showdown equity without risking extra chips.

On the river, the board is Qc-2c-3h-Th-Kd. I have a pair of deuces with an Ace kicker. After Minni called my pre-flop raise and flop continuation bet, the turn went check-check. Minni has checked again on the river. My pair of deuces has very limited showdown value but might beat a missed club draw. Betting here is unlikely to get a better hand to fold, nor is it likely to be called by a worse hand. Checking behind is the most prudent play to reach showdown and minimize losses if Minni has a marginal hand like a pocket pair or a weak Ten.

I am holding Jack-Four offsuit (Jh 4s) in the Under The Gun (UTG) position. This is a very weak hand with poor playability and low equity against standard opening ranges, even in a five-handed game. My stack is currently very healthy at over 150 big blinds, so there is no reason to take unnecessary risks with marginal or poor hands. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

A9o in UTG+1 with Flash already folded. This is a reasonable opening hand from this position. With a healthy stack of ~189 BBs, I should open raise to 250 (2.5x) to take initiative and potentially steal the blinds or play in position against callers.

Claude is opening to 2.5bb from an early-ish position in a 5-handed game, but his history shows he’s reasonably active and not just nutted. K8o is marginal yet has acceptable raw equity versus a wider 5-handed opening range, and we have position to realize it. 3-betting as a bluff with K8o risks bloating the pot with a dominated, offsuit hand versus a big-stack opener who can defend; folding is a bit too tight given pot odds and button advantage. I’ll flat and play postflop cautiously, keeping pots smaller when we make a one-pair hand and using position to control the hand.

I’m in the small blind with 2h3h facing an UTG+1 open to 250 and a button call. 2-3 suited is a speculative hand with decent flush/straight potential and good implied odds multiway, and calling 200 more (250 total committed) is cheap relative to my 13.7k stack and the 650 pot. Folding is too tight here and a 3-bet bluff out of position is unnecessary and high-variance. I’ll call to see a flop, then proceed cautiously post-flop — fold to strong action unless I hit a meaningful draw or made hand, and try to extract value when I improve.

Preflop in the big blind with QJo offsuit. Facing a raise to 250 from Claude (UTG+1, aggressive raiser based on history), a call from FiveTwo on the button, and a call from Minni in the small blind. Pot is 850, and calling adds 150 for ~5.7:1 odds. QJo is a marginal hand that plays poorly multiway out of position post-flop, with limited straight potential and dominated by many better hands in callers' ranges. No strong reason to 3-bet as it's offsuit and not premium. Folding preserves stack for better spots in a tournament setting, avoiding speculative multiway pots OOP.

Flop: 5s-4d-9c with 2h3h. I have an 8‑out open‑ended straight draw (all 6s and Aces) and backdoor heart potential, but no pair and I’m out of position against the UTG+1 raiser and the button caller. Pot is 850 and my stack is deep enough to realise equity, so I’ll control the pot and see a turn rather than fire OOP as a bluff. Plan: check and call a reasonable continuation bet to chase the draw; fold to an over‑aggressive raise or very large donk that gives poor pot odds.

Hit top pair with top kicker (A9) on a 5-4-9 board. This is a good value spot - I should bet to get value from worse 9x hands, pocket pairs below 9, and protect against straight draws (67, 78). Betting around 60% pot.

Multiway flop spot versus the preflop raiser with K8o on 9-5-4 rainbow. Claude’s small c-bet can be wide, but our hand has very little immediate equity (no straight draw, only one true overcard) and, more importantly, poor realization: if we improve with a K or 8 we’re often still dominated or facing heavy action, while continuing without improving invites getting barreled off in a bloated pot. With SB still to act behind and our stack only 48bb, this is a low-EV float. Preserve chips and wait for better spots.

Flop 5s-4d-9c with 2h3h: I have an 8‑out open‑ended straight draw (any 6 or any A) — ~17% to hit on the turn, ~31.5% by the river — plus backdoor hearts. Claude (UTG+1) bets 500 into a 1,350 pot; calling 500 gives ~27% immediate pot odds (500 / (1,350+500) ≈ 27%). My two‑street equity (~31.5%) plus deep stacks and implied odds make a call +EV here. I’m out of position and Claude opened from early position (a fairly strong range), so a raise OOP with only a draw is high variance and likely to either get called (inflating the pot when behind) or face a reraise I don’t want to gamble with. Plan: call to see a turn, fold to a large raise, and extract value if I complete the straight or pick up extra equity.

I have 2♥3♥ on 5♠4♦9♣7♥. I still only have an OESD (A or 6 = 8 outs) — ~17.4% to hit the river. Pot is 1,850 and I’m out of position vs Claude (UTG+1), who opened preflop and has shown aggression. Betting OOP with only a draw is high-variance and will usually get called by better hands, so I should preserve my equity and keep the pot controllable. Check and take a free river (or induce a bluff); on the river I’ll value-bet if I complete the straight, call small bets (break-even call vs ~17% equity is about 390 chips into this pot) and fold to large barrels.

I have top pair top kicker (A9) on a 5-4-9-7 board. The 7h completes 86 for a straight, but most of Minni's check-calling range is likely worse 9x hands, pocket pairs, or draws. I should continue betting for value and protection against remaining draws. Betting around 65% pot.

Fold. I have 2♥3♥ on 5♠-4♦-9♣-7♥ — my only clean outs to a made straight are Aces and 6s (8 outs ≈ 17.4% to hit on the river). Claude (UTG+1) opened preflop, c‑bet the flop and now barrels the turn to 1,200 into a 3,050 pot. Calling 1,200 gives me pot odds of roughly 1,200 / (3,050+1,200) ≈ 28.2%, which is well above my clean equity. Including pairing 2s/3s as “dirty” outs pushes the raw % up (~30.4%), but those are weak outs and often won’t be good versus Claude’s line. I’m out of position, raising OOP with only a draw is high variance, and calling to see a river will frequently force a fold to a large river bet. Preserve my stack and wait for a better, cleaner spot — fold here.
2c 6d is a very weak hand - unsuited, low cards, no connectivity. From UTG position, I need to play a tight range. This is an easy fold.

5-handed with Claude folding UTG, we can profitably open a wider range. J9o is not premium and can be dominated, but it has reasonable playability and benefits a lot from taking the initiative rather than limping. With 48bb, I’ll raise small-to-standard to apply pressure to the blinds and avoid multiway limped pots; if we face a 3-bet from the stronger ranges behind, we can comfortably fold.

FiveTwo opened UTG+1 to 250 — an early-position open suggests a fairly strong range. Q7s on the button is a playable, suited hand with good post‑flop potential and a big positional advantage, but it’s not strong enough to 3‑bet for value. The cost to see a flop (250) is small relative to my 130BB stack and gives me the initiative of acting last post‑flop. I’ll call and use position to evaluate the flop: proceed aggressively if I hit strong equity (pair, good spade draw) or use positional pressure to steal later; fold or exercise pot control if I face heavy resistance (large 3‑bet or big flop raise).

9cTc is a strong suited connector from the small blind. Pot odds are excellent: 200 to call into a 650 pot (3.25:1), with potential for multiway action and strong implied odds given the deep stacks behind (Minni and Flash) and FiveTwo's shorter stack. Preflop position on raiser and caller if Flash folds or checks. Folding is too tight; raising risks isolating with a speculative hand preflop out of position postflop if called. Calling keeps the pot multiway-friendly and realizes equity well.

I am in the Big Blind with 8s2h, which is a very weak hand. FiveTwo opened to 250 and received two callers, making it a four-way pot. Although I am getting generous pot odds to call the remaining 150, 82o has terrible playability and equity realization, especially out of position against three opponents. It is better to fold and preserve my stack for more profitable situations.

Post-flop on Jd7d6s with 9cTc from SB, first to act. Multiway pot (FiveTwo IP as preflop raiser UTG+1 with 4550 stack, Minni button caller deep). Hand has a weak gutshot straight draw to the 8 (~4 outs, ~8% equity standalone) and backdoor club flush draw, but no pair, no strong draw on this diamond-heavy board ripe for flush draws and possible made hands like sets/pairs. Pot 850 is decent size already. Betting risks getting check-raised by draws or value, and fold equity is low OOP multiway vs aggressive opponents seen in history (FiveTwo often cbets, Minni calls loosely). Checking realizes equity for free, invites a cbet from FiveTwo's range which I can fold to cheaply if big, or check-fold if check-around. No reason to bluff here with marginal draw OOP; preserve stack for better spots in tournament with 90 hands left.

We raised preflop and flop comes J♦7♦6♠ giving us top pair with a middling kicker (J9). In a 3-way pot this isn’t a hand that wants to build a huge pot, but it does benefit from denying equity: there are many overcards+draws, diamond draws, and straight draws (T8/98/54 type hands) that we want to charge. A small-to-medium c-bet also gets value from worse Jx and some 7x/6x that will peel once. I’ll bet with a clear bet/fold plan versus significant aggression, since a raise multiway on this texture is heavily weighted to strong made hands/strong draws.

I have Q7s on the button — a vulnerable but playable middle pair with a decent queen kicker and a backdoor spade/straight equity. FiveTwo opened UTG+1 preflop (a fairly strong range) but then made only a small flop C-bet (300 into a ~1150 pot). With position on both opponents and a multiway dynamic (Elon still in the hand), raising here would be thin — I’m often behind to Jx/strong pairs and can get outdrawn or face a reraise. Calling keeps the pot under control, lets me realize my equity, and lets me use positional advantage to apply pressure on later streets or extract value when I improve. Plan: call the 300, reevaluate on the turn — value-raise if I pair my Q or hit two-pair/trips, bluff/float if the opponent shows weakness, fold to heavy aggression if the turn card and action make my hand unlikely to be best.

Facing a flop bet from FiveTwo (preflop raiser with 42BB stack) and a call from Minni (deep button caller) into a 1450 pot, to call 300 OOP multiway with T9cc on Jd7d6s. Hand holds only a weak gutshot straight draw to the 8 (4 outs, ~8% equity standalone) plus backdoor club flush draw, no showdown value, on a diamond-heavy board favoring strong flush draws, sets (66/77/JJ), two-pair combos, and overpairs from raiser's range. FiveTwo's UTG+1 raise and small cbet fits polarized range (value + bluffs/draws), Minni's call suggests medium strength or draw. Calling OOP invites turn barrels from better hands/draws while denying my equity realization; implied odds limited by FiveTwo's stack and multiway dynamic. Raising as semi-bluff risky OOP vs two opponents likely to continue with better. Folding preserves stack for superior spots with 90 hands remaining in tournament; speculative preflop call realized minimal dead money, now correctly releasing to coordinated board and action.

Turn A♠ is a major range-shift card that improves a lot of Minni’s flop-calling range (suited Ax, AJo/AQo, etc.) and makes our J9 top pair much thinner. While it’s a decent barrel card for our range, it’s not a great one for this specific hand because getting called often puts us behind, and getting raised is miserable at this SPR. Checking keeps the pot controlled, preserves our showdown value, and lets us respond to sizing (we can bluff-catch some smaller bets from draws/float lines, but comfortably fold to bigger value bets).

Turn decision: I have Qs7s on Jd 7d 6s As — currently second pair (7s) with a strong spade draw. FiveTwo opened UTG+1 and made a small flop c-bet (300) that I called; he then checked the turn. I count ~9 spade outs (≈19.6% to hit the river) plus 2 sevens and 3 queens for additional two‑pair/trips outs (≈30% total to improve). The Ace on the turn helps a lot of hands in his UTG+1 range (Ax, AJ, Kx) and his check is more indicative of pot control/medium strength than weakness that I can reliably fold out with a bluff. Betting now would be a thin bluff — many hands that continue will still be ahead and I’d be bloating the pot with only one card to improve.

Plan: keep the pot controlled and use position to see the river for cheap. If FiveTwo bets the river I can re‑evaluate (fold to polarized large bets, call small/medium bets with my equity, or value-bet when I improve). If the river completes my draw or gives me two pair, I’ll extract value. So I check the turn.

River pairs the J giving us trips (JJJ with A and 9 kickers). After Minni flats the flop and checks back the A♠ turn, her range is still heavy on Ax, 7x/6x, and some diamond/straight draws that mostly missed. Trips is now comfortably ahead of all one-pair Ax and two-pair type hands, but we don’t want to bloat the pot because she can still have better Jx (KJ/QJ/JT) or the occasional boat (AJ/J7/J6). A small value/blocker bet targets calls from Ax and worse pairs while keeping the price down and letting us fold to a raise, which will be very value-heavy here.

I have Qs7s on Jd 7d 6s As Jh — second pair (sevens) with a decent queen kicker, missed the backdoor spade. FiveTwo opened UTG+1 (fairly strong range), made a small flop c‑bet and then checked the turn; the small 500 river sizing looks like a thin value/block bet or a probe rather than a polarizing shove. Calling 500 into a 1950 pot gives ~20% break‑even equity; against a capped/medium-strength river range (Ax that checked turn, some missed diamond draws, weaker pairs, and occasional bluffs) my hand has enough realistic showdown equity to justify a call. Raising would be a thin bluff with poor blockers and would commit me too often against Jx/Ax/sets that already beat me. I will call and see the showdown — fold to strong aggression beyond this small river bet.

5-handed UTG we can open a bit wider, but 9♣6♥ offsuit is still a low-equity, easily dominated hand that plays poorly out of position versus the remaining field and especially versus big stacks in the blinds who can 3-bet/punish limps. With ~62 BB, preserving stack and avoiding marginal multiway spots is higher EV than limping or opening this combo.

A♥J♠ UTG+1 in a 5-handed game is a clear open. I want initiative, fold equity and to discourage multiway action against deep-stacked blinds. I will raise to 300 (3x BB) — big enough to deny cheap odds to callers and make squeezes less profitable from Claude/Flash, but a standard sizing with ~120bb effective stacks. Plan: if I get single-called I’ll play aggressively on favorable flops; if faced with a small 3-bet I’ll consider calling/4-betting depending on sizing and player; vs a large 3‑bet I’ll re-evaluate (fold more often).

82o is one of the worst possible hands: disconnected, offsuit, low cards with poor playability postflop. Minni's early position raise (UTG+1) indicates a strong range. Even on the button, calling 300 into a 450 pot offers no equity realization or fold equity if raising. Deep stack (64BB), better to preserve chips for premium spots rather than speculate with trash. Standard fold.

I am holding King-Queen offsuit in the Small Blind. Minni has opened to 300 from UTG+1. KQo is a strong hand, but it is difficult to play out of position if I only call, especially with Claude (the chip leader) still to act in the Big Blind. By 3-betting to 1000, I take the initiative, put pressure on Minni's opening range, and make it difficult for Claude to enter the pot with a marginal hand. If Minni 4-bets, I can easily fold, and if she calls, I have the lead heading into the flop.

Facing a 3-bet from Flash (1000) after Minni opened to 300. I have KJo which is a decent but not premium hand. With 900 to call into a pot of 1400, I'm getting ~1.5:1 odds. KJo plays reasonably well postflop and I have a deep stack (19875). 4-betting would be too aggressive with this hand OOP. Folding seems too tight given pot odds and hand strength. Calling to see a flop is the best line - I can hit top pair with good kicker or pick up draws.

I opened A♥J♠ to 300 and Flash (SB) 3‑bet to 1000 with Claude (BB) cold‑calling. That makes it a three‑way pot — 4‑betting here loses a lot of its value because I’ll often face multiple players and be OOP; the min‑raise to 1700 is too small to meaningfully isolate and a larger 4‑bet would commit too much out of position. AJs is too good to fold but I prefer to keep the pot controllable and use postflop playability. I’ll call the additional 700 (1000 total) to see a flop, plan to continue aggressively on favorable A/J‑high or draw‑heavy boards, and re‑evaluate/fold to heavy action on dry boards where I miss.

I am in a three-way pot after 3-betting pre-flop with King-Queen offsuit. The flop of 8d-6c-7s is very coordinated and favors the calling ranges of the Big Blind (Claude) and the original raiser (Minni) much more than my high-card heavy 3-betting range. Since I have completely missed this board and am out of position against two opponents, a continuation bet is unlikely to succeed and would only serve to bloat the pot with a weak hand. Checking is the most prudent play to see how the other players react. If there is significant action, I will likely have to fold, but checking allows me to potentially see a free turn card if everyone else checks behind.

Missed this coordinated 8-7-6 board completely with KJo. I have two overcards but no pair and no real draw. Flash (the 3-bettor) checked, and Minni is behind me. On this connected board that likely hits calling/3-betting ranges, betting with air is risky. Best to check and see a free card - maybe hit a K or J on the turn.

Check. I opened and called the SB 3‑bet with A♥J♠ to see a flop, but 8♦6♣7♠ completely misses my hand — no pair and no realistic draw — and this is now a multiway pot with both the 3‑better and a cold‑caller still involved. The board is reasonably coordinated, so a continuation bet here is thin: it will be called by many hands with equity and can get me raised out of the pot. Both opponents checked, showing weakness or pot‑control; I want to keep the pot small and preserve fold equity for later. Plan: check to the turn, and only start betting/raising if I improve (pair an A or J) or if a very favorable runout appears; otherwise be prepared to check‑call small probes and fold to large aggression.

On the turn, the King of Hearts is a very favorable card for my hand as I have now hit top pair with a strong Queen kicker. The flop checked around, which reduces the likelihood that either opponent is holding a monster like a set or a made straight, as they would typically want to protect those hands on such a coordinated board. Since the King hits my pre-flop 3-betting range perfectly, I should bet for value to get called by pocket pairs (99-JJ), middle pairs, or straight draws. A bet of 1500 (half the pot) is a standard size that allows me to build the pot while still being able to fold if I face a significant raise from the chip leader.

The King on the turn gives me top pair with Jack kicker. Flash checked the flop on the coordinated 8-7-6 board but now bets 1500 when K hits. This could be: (1) Flash has AK/KQ and hit, (2) Flash is bluffing using the scare card, or (3) Flash has a made straight (T9/54). My TPJK is strong enough to continue but not strong enough to raise on this draw-heavy board. Calling to see the river is correct - folding is too weak, raising is too aggressive given straight possibilities.

Call 1500. I opened A♥J♠ and we’re now in a three-way pot after Flash 3‑bet and Claude cold‑call. The flop (8♦6♣7♠) missed my hand and I checked to keep the pot controllable. Flash’s turn 1500 into a 6000 pot is a relatively small probe — it could be a value bet with a King or a delayed c‑bet/bluff. Folding here would concede a lot given the cheap price; raising from out of position with no pair would be over‑aggressive and likely punished by stronger hands. Calling costs ~13.7% of my stack and shows the best mix of pot control and equity realization. Plan: see the river and re‑evaluate — fold to large river aggression if I still have only Ace‑high, and if I pair my A or J on the river consider extracting value or turning the hand into a bluff‑catch/raise depending on sizing and reads.

On the river, the 2nd is a complete brick that does not change the board state. My top pair with a Queen kicker is a strong hand, but having two opponents call my turn bet is concerning. One of them could be holding AK, a slow-played set, or a straight from the coordinated flop, although the flop checking around makes a monster hand slightly less likely. By checking, I am exercising pot control in a three-way pot. This allows me to see a showdown if it checks through, or evaluate a bet from either opponent based on their sizing. Betting again might only get value from a few weaker Kings while risking a large loss if I am raised by a better hand.

River 2d is a blank. Flash bet turn when K hit but now checks river - this looks like either a give-up on a bluff or a medium-strength hand. With top pair jack kicker, I should value bet targeting worse Kx hands (K9, KT, K8). Minni is behind but also checked flop and just called turn, likely doesn't have a monster. A ~1/3 pot bet extracts thin value while controlling the size.

Pot is 10,000 and Claude’s river bet is 2,500, so calling costs 2,500 to win 12,500 (pot after call) — ~20% equity required to break even. I hold A♥J♠ and missed this connected board (8♦6♣7♠ K♥ 2♦), so I only have Ace‑high. Flash (the preflop 3‑better) bet the turn then checked the river — that looks like pot control with Kx or weakness. Claude cold‑called preflop, called the turn and now makes a small ~25%‑pot river shove/bet — stylistically this is consistent with thin value (Kx, 8x/7x) but also allows some light bluffs. Raising to 5,000 here would be a large, out‑of‑position bluff that’s unlikely to fold enough value hands and would overcommit a big chunk of my stack. Folding surrenders too much equity given the cheap price. Conclusion: call 2,500 — I get excellent pot odds to catch the bluffs/weaker hands and will fold to any sizeable raise behind.

I am holding top pair with a strong Queen kicker on a board of 8-6-7-K-2. After I checked the river for pot control, Claude (the big blind and chip leader) bet 2500 into a 7500 pot, which is a 1/3 pot-sized bet. Minni called that bet. Minni's call is a significant sign of strength, as she is calling into both the original turn bettor (me) and the river bettor (Claude). However, the pot is now 12500 and it only costs me 2500 to call. These 5:1 pot odds are extremely favorable. While it is possible one of them has a set, a straight (like T9), or a better King (AK), Claude's small sizing could also represent a weaker King or even a medium pocket pair. Given the math and the strength of my hand relative to a 3-bet pot where the flop was checked around, a call is the most disciplined play.

UTG with 3♦4♣ (offsuit) — a very weak, low offsuit connector with poor playability out of position. Pot is 150 (blinds 50/100), to call 100 or min-raise to 200. Opening here would frequently face callers and 3-bets from big stacks (Flash 25k+, Claude 14k) and I have no fold equity or blockers; limping just invites aggression. With ~70bb effective and plenty of hands to wait for in better position, the +EV play is to preserve my stack and fold.

UTG+1 position with K2s (suited but very weak hand with poor playability and dominated by better kings). No limpers yet, pot has blinds only. Standard play is to fold trash like this from early position in a 5-handed game to avoid difficult postflop spots OOP with a marginal hand. My stack is deep (64BB), no need to speculate with this. Preserve stack for better spots.

I am on the button with 7s 5h. While the button is an ideal position to steal the blinds, especially as the chip leader, 75o is a very weak hand with poor post-flop playability. Given that Claude (SB) and FiveTwo (BB) have shown willingness to defend or play back in previous hands, I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack advantage.

Folded to me in the SB with Q9o. This is a solid hand heads-up against the BB. I should raise to try to take down the pot or play postflop with position relative to my opponent. Standard 2.5x raise to 250.

Small blind open in 5-handed is very wide, so big blind should defend a lot. With 9c3c we’re not strong enough to 3-bet bluff profitably at a good frequency versus an unknown/competent SB, but we do have suitedness, decent pot odds (150 to win 350), and position postflop (we act first but can still realize equity vs a wide range). Calling keeps the pot manageable and allows us to continue on favorable flops (pair/flush draws/straight potential) while avoiding bloating the pot with a dominated hand.

I flopped trip queens with Q9 - an incredibly strong hand. Board is Qc Qd 8c with a flush draw possible. I should bet for value to charge draws and build the pot. A small bet around half pot should get called by pairs, draws, and random floats. Checking risks missing value if FiveTwo checks back with air.

Claude’s 1/3-pot c-bet (250 into 750) is consistent with a very high-frequency range bet from the SB on a paired Q-high flop. With 9c3c we have a clean flush draw (9 outs) and are getting excellent immediate odds: 250 to win 1000 (~25%), while our raw equity to the river is ~36% even when he has a made hand. Raising here would bloat the pot OOP with a middling/dominated club draw (many Ac/Kc/Jc/Tc combos in his range) and invites a jam/3-bet from Qx/overpairs, so calling realizes our equity cheaply and keeps our range wide to apply pressure on favorable turns (club, 9/3, or scare cards).

I have trip Queens with a 9 kicker - a monster hand. The turn 6d is a blank that doesn't complete any draws. FiveTwo called my flop bet so they have some equity - likely a flush draw, pocket pair, or maybe an 8. I should continue betting for value. A bet around 65-70% pot (~700) will extract value from draws and weaker hands while building the pot for a river shove if called.

Claude’s turn barrel is 700 into 1000 (now 1700 in the middle), a much more committed sizing than the flop and typically skews his range toward Qx/value and strong continues. Calling 700 to win 2400 requires ~29% immediate equity, but with 9c3c we’re basically on a small flush draw only: ~19.6% to hit a club, and even that is slightly discounted (6c is bad vs Qx making a boat; plus our 9-high flush is sometimes dominated when he’s barreling big with higher two-club combos). With us out of position and likely facing another tough river decision, the implied odds don’t compensate. Raising is also unattractive at this stack depth because his continuing range (Qx/boats/overpairs/strong draws) won’t fold enough and we risk getting jammed off our equity. Best play is to cut losses here.

UTG in a 5-handed game with Qh2c offsuit, a very weak hand with poor playability, low equity, and high likelihood of being dominated. Standard fold from early position to preserve stack and wait for better spots. No reads from history justify playing this trash hand.

I am holding Queen-Ten offsuit in UTG+1 in a 5-handed game. With the chip lead and having seen a relatively active table, opening this hand is standard to apply pressure on the shorter stacks (FiveTwo, Minni, and Elon). QTo is a solid semi-Broadway hand that plays well enough post-flop if called, especially given my positional advantage over the blinds. I will open for my standard 3x raise to 300.

JTo on the button facing a 3x open from UTG+1. This is a solid calling hand with position - good playability postflop with straight potential. Not strong enough to 3-bet here, but definitely too good to fold on the button. Calling to see a flop in position.

A2s is a playable defend in the small blind getting good immediate price (250 to win 750) and strong implied odds when we flop nut-flush draws/wheel equity. With Flash opening and Claude already calling, a squeeze would need a lot of fold equity to show a profit, and both opponents look capable of continuing, leaving us in a bloated 3-bet pot out of position with a dominated-ace candidate. Calling keeps the pot manageable, realizes our suited ace equity, and we can proceed cautiously postflop when we miss.

Flash opened to 300 UTG+1 and both Button and SB have called, making this a 4‑way pot. Pot is 1000 and I need to add 200 (total 300) to call.
