That gives me ~16.7% break‑even equity (200 / (1000+200)). Q♦4♠ is a weak, often dominated hand, but at this price with deep stacks it's worth seeing a flop occasionally. A raise/squeeze here is unattractive — I have no hand to play post‑flop heads‑up against an aggressive opener (Flash) and risk a 3‑bet. Folding is too tight given the pot odds. I will call 300 to realize equity and look to fold to significant post‑flop aggression unless I pair my queen or pick up strong draws. If I miss the flop and face heavy betting, I will release the hand to preserve my stack for better spots.

Four-way pot on a paired J55 rainbow flop. Our A♠2♠ is just ace-high with no immediate draw (only very thin backdoor spades/wheel runouts), and multiway dynamics greatly reduce profitable bluffing frequency. Donk-leading risks getting called/raised by any Jx, 5x, pocket pairs, and even diamond floats, putting us in tough spots out of position with little equity. Best is to check and evaluate—mostly check-fold versus meaningful action, and happily take a free card if it checks through.

Flop: 5♦ 5♣ J♦. I hold Q♦4♠ in a 4‑way pot (Flash opened, Button and SB called). I have no pair — only a weak backdoor diamond possibility and the chance to pair my queen. Betting here as a bluff into three opponents is unprofitable (very little fold equity) and a raise/squeeze is out of the question. Pot‑control and cheap equity realization are correct: check and see what the preflop aggressor does. Plan: if faced with a small probe bet (a light C‑bet ≤ ~300–400) I can consider a call to chase my modest outs; fold to any sizable bet (~50% pot or larger, ≳600–800) since my equity is minimal. If checked around I’ll see a turn for free and reassess (bet only if I pair or pick up real draw).

In a 4-way pot on a paired board (5-5-J), the effectiveness of a continuation bet is significantly reduced. While I was the pre-flop aggressor, my hand (QT) has no current showdown value and only marginal backdoor equity. With three opponents including the button still to act, checking is the most disciplined play to control the pot size and see if the turn improves my hand or provides a better opportunity to bluff.

Flopped top pair with Jh on a 5d5cJd board. Everyone checked to me in position. This is a clear value betting spot - I have the best hand most of the time and want to extract value from weaker jacks, pocket pairs, and diamond draws. Betting around 1/2 pot for value and protection.

Claude’s 1/3-pot stab into a 4-way, paired J55 board after everyone checks is a spot where his value range (any Jx, 5x, overpairs/medium pairs) and protection bets show up a lot, and we’re out of position with two players still to act behind us. With A♠2♠ we have only ace-high with no real draw (just very thin backdoors), so we won’t realize anywhere near the ~25% equity needed to justify a call, and calling also opens us up to getting squeezed by Flash/Minni. Preserving stack and avoiding dominated, low-equity floats multiway is best here.

Fold. The pot is 1800 and Claude’s raise is 600, so I need 600/(1800+600)=25% equity to justify a call. With Q♦4♠ on 5♦-5♣-J♦ I have no pair, only a very weak backdoor diamond (need both turn+river) and ~12.5% to hit a queen by river — far below the 25% threshold. This is a multiway pot (Flash, the preflop raiser, is still involved) which further reduces my realized equity. Claude’s sizable button raise into a checked pot looks value‑oriented (Jx, 5x, sets, overpairs) and I have almost no fold equity or meaningful blockers. Squeezing or calling OOP with air is a losing line here; folding preserves my ~6675 stack for better spots.

On a paired board of J-5-5 in a 4-way pot, my Queen-high has very little equity. Claude (the Button) is betting 600 into 1200 after it was checked around to him. While he could be making a positional play to take down the pot, I have no piece of the board and no significant draws. Continuing out of position against the chip leader with two other players still to act is a losing play. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am currently the chip leader with 25,125 chips, more than 8,000 chips ahead of the next largest stack (Claude). In this 5-handed game, I am acting from under-the-gun (UTG). Ace-Four offsuit is a marginal hand that typically falls outside a standard opening range from this position, even in short-handed play. Given my active image in recent hands (opening Hand #64 and 3-betting Hand #62), folding here serves two purposes: it preserves my stack against the aggressive Claude (who is seated directly to my left and has the chips to challenge me) and it helps balance my image by showing I am not playing every hand. There are better opportunities to use my big stack to pressure the shorter stacks (FiveTwo, Minni, and Elon) from later positions.
73o is a trash hand in early position. Easy fold - no playability, no high card value, not suited, not connected.

On the Button with Ah4s, we’re first in after two folds and have maximum positional advantage against only the blinds. This hand is a standard late-position open: it blocks strong Ax holdings, has some wheel/straight potential, and benefits a lot from winning preflop. With stacks ~50–65bb, we don’t need to bloat the pot; a normal 2.5x open pressures the blinds while keeping our risk controlled. Plan: fold to a sizable 3-bet, play in position if called.

I'm in the small blind (66bb) with T5 suited hearts facing a Button open to 250. T5s is a marginal but defendable hand — suited with backdoor straight/flush potential — so I prefer to defend cheaply rather than inflate the pot out of position. A tiny 3-bet to 400 won’t fold out many hands and just bloats the pot and puts me OOP with a hand that can be easily dominated. Call the 200 to see a flop and play post‑flop based on texture (probe/c-bet or check/fold as appropriate).

In the big blind facing a button raise to 250 (2.5BB) and a small blind call, with K5o (offsuit king-five). This hand has poor post-flop playability, is easily dominated by better kings, and lacks strong equity against a wide button range or calling range. Pot odds are decent (600 pot, 150 to call), but implied odds are low due to multiway pot and weak hand. FiveTwo raises frequently from button as seen in history (e.g., Hands 5,9,29,47,52), often with marginal holdings, but folding here preserves stack for better spots. No need to defend wide multiway. Standard fold to avoid tough post-flop decisions.

Check. I have T♠5♥ (Ten and Five of hearts) on 3♥‑7♠‑4♥ — a very strong drawing hand: a four‑card heart flush draw (9 hearts left) plus a Ten overcard. That’s roughly 12 “clean” outs (9 hearts + 3 tens), ~25.5% to improve on the turn and ~45% to hit by the river. Out of position and facing the preflop raiser, I want to realize equity without bloating the pot. Checking lets me call a standard c‑bet and see the turn cheaply, keeps worse hands and loose calls in, and preserves fold equity for later. Plan: call a small‑to‑medium c‑bet (I’ll continue with the draw vs. ~≤2/3 pot bets and even larger sizes if pot odds are decent), fold or re‑evaluate vs an extreme overbet/all‑in depending on exact sizing and reads. If the button checks back I’ll take a free turn to barrel or bet for value on favorable cards (heart or Ten).

We’re heads-up in position after our button open. This low, somewhat dynamic flop (3h-7s-4h) interacts with the SB calling range, but as the preflop raiser we can still apply pressure with a small c-bet. With Ah4s we have a pair plus some backdoor potential (notably the Ah for runout heart turns) and we benefit from denying equity to two-overcard hands while also getting thin value from worse pairs/draws. A small sizing keeps our risk controlled and lets us comfortably fold if Minni shows significant strength via a check-raise.

Board is 3h‑7s‑4h and I hold Th5h — a four‑card heart flush draw plus a Ten overcard (about 12 clean outs). That’s ~25.5% to hit the turn and ~45% to hit by the river. Villain’s bet is tiny (200 into a 800 pot), so the break‑even equity to call is only ~20% (200/(800+200)). Calling is +EV: it realizes my strong equity, keeps worse hands and draws in, and preserves fold equity on later streets. I’m out of position so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal semi‑bluff and risk a shove or big 3‑bet; a raise here is unnecessary given the price. Plan: call the 200 to see the turn — continue/raise if I hit (flush or pair of tens), call moderate barrels with correct odds, fold or re‑evaluate to extreme overbets or shoves depending on sizing and reads.

The 6♦ on the turn completed my hand: with T♥5♥ on 3♥‑7♠‑4♥‑6♦ I now have a made straight (3‑4‑5‑6‑7) plus 4‑card heart backdoor potential. Button (FiveTwo) led small on the flop and then checked the turn, so his range is wide — pairs (7x/4x/6x/sets), single‑heart and two‑heart combos, missed overcards and bluffs. I’m out of position and the pot is 1000. Betting for value/protection is the right play: I want to charge draws and worse pairs, deny a free river heart that could change the showdown dynamics, and extract thin value from hands that will call (top pairs, second pairs, some draws, and weaker showdown hands). A half‑pot bet is a clean sizing here — big enough to charge equity but not so large that I fold out all worse hands or overcommit if I meet resistance. If FiveTwo raises heavily I’ll re‑evaluate (he could have a disguised value or a two‑card heart combo), but given his line a 500 chip bet should be +EV. Action: bet 500 into 1000.

Minni’s turn donk for 1/2 pot after check/calling flop is consistent with a lot of one-pair/protection hands (7x/6x/44x), some draws, and occasional slowplayed strong hands. With Ah4s we have a pair plus a gutshot to the 5 (4 clean outs) and we’re in position with decent pot odds: 500 to win 1500 needs ~25% equity, which we can realize via improving rivers and by occasionally winning at showdown when this is a probe/bluff. Raising as a semi-bluff is higher variance and risks getting jammed off our equity by stronger made hands. Best is to call and re-evaluate river based on sizing/action.

I’ve made the 3-4-5-6-7 straight (T♥5♥ with board 3♥-7♠-4♥-6♦-Q♣). Almost nothing in villain’s range can beat this — the only hands that tie are other 5x combos; full houses/higher straights are impossible on this runout. Villain’s line (button open, small flop c-bet, check turn, call my 500) looks like a wide/medium pair or a draw that didn’t improve; the Q on the river likely gives some Qx hands a pair and those are precisely the hands I can extract value from. Checking surrenders value to those pair hands. I’ll make a thin-value river bet sized to keep worse pairs and Qx in the pot but not fold them all off: bet ~700 into 2000 (~35%). If I get a reasonable raise I’ll re-evaluate (a huge shove is unusual here and would require careful consideration, but most raises will be from hands that I still beat or tie), so plan is bet 700 to get called by top pairs and worse showdown hands.

We’re facing a small river bet (700 into 2700, ~21% to call) with only a very thin bluff-catcher: one pair of 4s on a board that heavily favors Minni’s turn-leading range. After check/calling flop, her turn donk on the 6d credibly represents value hands that improved or want protection (any 5x straight, 76/64/74 two-pair, strong 7x/6x), and the river Qc is a card many of those hands can still value/block-bet. Importantly, holding the Ah reduces the density of missed-heart bluff combos, making her river betting range more value-heavy. With our hand near the bottom of our range and limited unblockers to her value, the best stack-preserving, high-EV decision is to fold.
As9s is a solid suited ace from UTG. With 164 BBs, I have a comfortable stack. This hand plays well postflop with nut flush potential and decent high card strength. Standard 2.5x open to 250.

Facing an UTG open from Claude, Q8o is too often dominated by his value/strong Broadway range (QJ+, KQ, AQ) and has weak playability out of position with 4 players left to act. Calling invites tough postflop spots and reverse implied odds, and it’s not a good 3-bet candidate given blockers/equity. Preserve 44.5bb and fold.

Claude opened UTG to 250 — an early-position raise that represents a fairly strong range. On the Button with T♥2♥ I have a weak, often dominated hand with poor equity. Calling 250 into a 400 pot will likely put me in multiway pots facing the SB/BB and leaves me in tough postflop spots; a light 3-bet (min to 400) has little fold equity versus an UTG opener and is a poor risk/reward. Preserve the stack and fold.

Jh7s is a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity and dominated by any reasonable raising range from UTG Claude, who has shown aggression in previous hands. Facing an early position raise, closing the action effectively with Minni folded behind, but as SB we have poor postflop position and playability. Pot odds are about 2:1 but equity against Claude's range is insufficient (~20-25% vs typical UTG open). Folding preserves stack for better spots in this multi-table dynamic.

Claude is opening from UTG, which normally signifies strength, but previous hands (like Hand #63 where he raised with 75o) show he has a very wide and aggressive opening range. Despite the attractive pot odds (150 to call into a pot of 400), 54o is a very weak hand to play out of position. I have the chip lead and there's no reason to voluntarily enter a pot with such a low-equity hand against an aggressive opponent who will likely make post-flop play difficult. I will wait for a better spot.

5-handed (effectively 6-max), KTo is strong enough to open from UTG for initiative and fold equity. Limping invites the big stacks to isolate and puts us in tougher multiway spots. I’ll use a standard, non-committal open size that can win the blinds often and still allows an easy fold versus a sizeable 3-bet from the aggressive stacks behind.

UTG+1 with Tc3s — a weak, easily dominated hand. FiveTwo opened from UTG (stronger range) and there are still the button and both blinds to act, so I'd be out of position in any raised pot. Pot odds and hand equity don't justify a speculative call or a light 3-bet here. Preserve my 7,725 stack for a better spot — fold.

Qh6c is a marginal offsuit hand with queen-high and no connectivity or suited potential for strong draws. Facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo (stack 4200), their range is strong (pairs, broadway, suited aces/connectors). Even on the button, calling 250 into 400 pot commits poorly with weak equity and postflop playability issues, especially if check-raised or multiway. Folding preserves stack for superior spots in a tournament with 83 hands left, where patience maximizes EV.

I am in the Small Blind with 6s 3h, which is a very weak starting hand. FiveTwo has opened from under-the-gun with a raise to 2.5x the big blind. Given the strength of an UTG opening range and my poor position for the remainder of the hand, there is no reason to continue with such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my large chip lead.

Facing a UTG raise with Q2o in the BB. This is a weak hand that doesn't play well out of position. Even getting ~2.7:1 pot odds, Q2o lacks the playability needed to profitably defend against an UTG opening range. Easy fold.

UTG with 4♦5♣ — a weak, unsuited, low connector in first-to-act position. Calling 100 into a 150 pot gives only ~40% pot odds, and this hand’s equity multiway (and out of position) is well below that. Raising to 200 as a steal is poor here — I’ll face aggression from deeper stacks (Flash, Claude) and a capable short stack (FiveTwo) who can three-bet or defend. Preserve my ~77BB stack for better spots (pocket pairs, suited connectors or strong Axs/broadways in position). Fold.

Ks8c is a weak offsuit king from UTG+1 in a 5-handed game. It has poor playability postflop with a weak kicker and no suited potential. Facing the blinds with effective stacks around 45-250BB, folding preserves my 62BB stack for better spots. No strong history read justifies a limp or raise here; standard is to fold marginal hands early.

I am on the Button with Eight of Spades and Deuce of Hearts (82o), which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. While I am the current chip leader and in a good position to steal the blinds, 82o has virtually no post-flop equity and is not worth the risk of an open-raise or a limp. I will fold and wait for a more favorable hand to maintain my chip lead.

Heads up against the BB with 65o. While not a strong hand, it's connected and playable in a steal situation. FiveTwo has only 4500 chips, so they may be protective. I'll raise to put pressure and try to take down the blinds.

Claude is opening from the small blind to 2.5x and it’s heads-up to me in the big blind. 97o is not strong enough to 3-bet for value, but it realizes equity well in positionless blind-vs-blind spots and has decent playability (pair/straight potential). With 150 to call into a pot that will be 500 after I call, I only need ~30% equity, which 97o will generally have versus a typical SB opening range. Folding would be too tight; 3-betting is unnecessary at this stack depth with this hand. I’ll defend by calling and play postflop.

Completely whiffed this flop with 56o. Board is Ts Qc 7c - I have no pair, no flush draw (only 6c), and just a weak gutshot to 8-9. This board texture connects well with FiveTwo's calling range (broadways, middle pairs). With no equity and facing a short-stacked opponent who might check-raise, checking and giving up is the prudent play.

Claude (preflop raiser) checks on a fairly dynamic T♠Q♣7♣ board. With 9♥7♦ I have a weak made hand (bottom pair) that’s ahead of a lot of his give-up range (A-high/K-high, some underpairs) but is very vulnerable to overcards and club/straight equity. In position, a small stab accomplishes a lot: it can take the pot down immediately, folds out many hands with 6 outs vs me, and sets a cheap price to continue if called. I’ll size small to keep myself from getting punished by a check-raise; if he check-raises to a meaningful size, I can comfortably fold this hand.
I have 5h6c on Ts Qc 7c. Only a gutshot to 8 (4-5-6-7-8). FiveTwo bet 175 into 500, giving me 3.8:1 odds. Need ~26% equity but gutshot only gives ~8%. Clear fold - no odds to chase.

Qd2h is a trash hand offsuit with poor playability and low equity. UTG in a 5-handed game, first to act preflop, standard play is to fold marginal hands like this to avoid difficult postflop situations out of position. Pot is small (blinds only), no need to limp or raise a weak queen high. Preserve stack for better spots.

In a 5-handed game, A5o is a standard opening hand from the Hijack/UTG+1 position. As the chip leader with 250BB, I want to maintain an aggressive profile and put pressure on the shorter stacks in the blinds (FiveTwo and Minni). While A5o is at the bottom of my opening range, its value as an 'Ace-blocker' makes it a good candidate for a raise. I will fold if faced with significant resistance (a large 3-bet) from the shorter stacks.

5h6h suited connector on the button facing a 3x raise. With deep stacks (161BB effective) and excellent position, this hand plays well post-flop for implied odds. Can make straights, flushes, and two pairs. Flash's UTG+1 range is likely wide enough to justify a call. If the blinds squeeze, I can fold.

We’re in the small blind facing a 3x open from Flash (5-handed, effectively early/mid position) and a flat from Claude on the button. Q6o plays very poorly multiway and out of position: when we flop a queen we’re often dominated by better Qx, and the hand doesn’t realize equity well without position. Even though the price to complete is tempting, this is a classic spot to avoid leaking chips with a dominated, low-connectivity offsuit hand. No squeeze consideration with this holding.

I’m in the big blind with Tc6c facing an UTG+1 raise to 300 and a button call. Multiway pot reduces raw equity, but calling 200 into a 750 pot gives ~21% pot odds — reasonable to defend with a suited Ten that has flush/straight potential and good implied odds with deep stacks. I don’t want to 3-bet (hand is too weak to isolate UTG raiser and can get into trouble vs a 4-bet), and folding here is too tight given the price. Plan: call to see a flop and proceed only with strong pair, nut/strong club draws, or favorable textures; fold to heavy aggression without improvement.

I hold Tc6c on Kc 9s 2s. I missed a pair and only have a backdoor club draw (need both turn and river to make a flush) and very little showdown value. This is a 3‑way pot with the UTG+1 preflop raiser and a button caller — c‑bet bluff equity is low and I’ll get called by Kx, 9x, spade draws and most single‑pair hands. Pot control and seeing a free turn is the right line. I’ll check and evaluate the turn: if I see a club or I pair my Ten/six I can take a more aggressive line; against a meaningful bet or raise I’ll fold unless I’ve improved or face a very small probe (<= ~1/3 pot) which I can call to realize equity.

On this King-high board in a three-way pot, my Ace-high has little equity. While I was the pre-flop aggressor, betting into two opponents on a board that likely connects with their calling ranges (Kx, pocket pairs, or spade draws) is risky. I will check to see how the action develops behind me and hope to see a free turn card. If faced with a bet from Claude on the button, I will likely fold.

Completely missed this flop with 5h6h - no pair, no draw (spades on board, I have hearts). Both opponents checked showing weakness, but bluffing into two players with zero equity is risky. K-high board favors Flash's preflop raising range. Best to check and give up.

I defended preflop with Tc6c for price and backdoor club potential. I missed the flop and only had the backdoor club; everyone checked so I exercised pot control. The turn 5♦ doesn't change that — no club and no pair — so my hand has almost no showdown value in a multiway pot against the UTG+1 raiser and the button caller. Bluffing here is unlikely to work and I don’t want to bloat the pot with a weak holding. I’ll check to see the river and only continue if I improve (pair or club) or face a very small probe-bet I can call to realize equity; otherwise I’ll fold to meaningful aggression.

On the turn, I have improved to bottom pair (fives) with an Ace kicker. The flop checked around, which suggests that a King is unlikely to be out against two opponents, or at least that no one is currently playing it aggressively. Minni has checked again from the Big Blind. While I have improved, my hand is still marginal. By checking, I can control the pot size and see how Claude reacts on the button. If Claude checks as well, I am happy to take a free river card or head towards a relatively cheap showdown. If he bets, I will evaluate based on his sizing, but with a pair and an Ace overcard, I have some equity to continue depending on the price.

I hit bottom pair with the 5d on the turn. Both Minni and Flash have checked twice showing weakness. With position and a made hand now, I could bet for thin value, but bottom pair is vulnerable. A check allows me to see a free river and potentially improve or showdown cheaply. If I bet and get check-raised, I'm in a tough spot with just 5s.

I never improved — Tc6c missed the turn and river (no club, no pair). The river paired the board with a second King, which makes Kx/trips (and even K+side-card full houses/quads in rare cases) very plausible. This was a multiway pot and both opponents checked down, so my bluff equity is very low and my Ten‑high has essentially no showdown value. Don’t bloat the pot: check to see showdown and fold to any meaningful river bet (only consider calling a tiny, obvious probe bluff).

On the river, the board pairs with a second King. Given that the flop and turn were checked by all three players, it is highly unlikely that anyone is holding a King, as they would typically have bet for value earlier or to protect against spade draws. My hand has improved slightly relative to the board's structure, but it remains a marginal showdown-value hand (KK55A). Betting here likely only gets called by better hands (like a 9 or a slow-played King) or forces out missed draws that I already beat. By checking, I control the pot and get to a cheap showdown. If Claude checks behind on the button, my pair of fives is frequently going to be the best hand. If Claude bets, I will have to evaluate based on his sizing, but a check is the most prudent move to realize my equity in this passive pot.

River paired the Kings. I have a pair of 5s which is weak showdown value. Both opponents checked three streets showing significant weakness - likely missed draws or complete air. Betting for thin value is risky since any 9, any pocket pair 66+, or any Kx beats me. The hands I beat (missed draws, A-high) will mostly fold to a bet anyway. Better to check and win at showdown against busted draws.

I am holding J5 offsuit in the Under The Gun (UTG) position. This is a very weak hand to open with in early position, even in a 5-handed game. Given my large stack and the fact that there are aggressive players behind me, folding is the standard and most profitable long-term play here. I want to avoid entering pots with marginal hands out of position.

KJo in UTG+1 is a solid opening hand. With a healthy stack of ~159BB, I should open raise to 250 (2.5x) as I've been doing with my standard opens. This hand plays well postflop and has good equity against calling ranges.

Facing a 2.5x open from Claude (effectively cutoff in 5-handed), 8♠2♣ offsuit is too weak to continue profitably. Flat-calling creates poor playability and invites squeezes from the blinds; a 3-bet bluff with this hand is also low quality given our stack depth and Claude’s tendency to continue. Preserve stack and wait for better spots on the button.

Claude opened to 250 from UTG+1. In the small blind with A♣T♦ I have a playable but marginal hand out of position. A min 3-bet to 400 is thin here — it commits more chips OOP and likely won't fold out a wide opener or deter the big blind. Folding is too tight given the hand and stacks. I'll call the 200 more (250 total committed) to keep the pot manageable, see a flop, and play postflop based on texture (value/float with A-high or fold to heavy action). If I face a sizable 4-bet, I will re-evaluate and likely fold.

62h suited is a speculative hand at best, but facing a raise from Claude in UTG+1 (early position, typically strong range) and a call from Minni in the small blind, defending out of position multiway is unprofitable. Pot odds require about 20% equity (150 to call into 750 pot), but 62s has poor equity against typical raising/call ranges and terrible post-flop playability OOP with two opponents likely to continue betting strong hands. Previous hands show Claude raises aggressively but EP raises still warrant respect. Preserve stack (6175, medium size) for premium spots to maximize long-term EV in tournament.

I have A♣T♦ on Q♥8♣K♥ — only Ace‑high with no heart and no pair. Claude opened UTG+1, so his range is weighted toward Kx/Qx and strong broadways that currently beat me. I'm out of position and don't want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand, so I will check for pot control. Plan: if Claude fires a small c‑bet (roughly ≤ ~300, ~1/3–1/2 pot) I'll call as a bluff‑catcher to see the turn for possible A/T improvements; fold to large bets or any raise. If he checks back I can take a free card and re‑evaluate on the turn (bet if I pair up or get a clear improvement, otherwise proceed cautiously).

I flopped top pair with a jack kicker on Qh 8c Kh. The board has two hearts creating a flush draw. Minni checked to me - I should bet for value and to charge draws. With top pair good kicker, a standard c-bet of ~60% pot is appropriate here.

I have A♣T♦ on Q♥‑8♣‑K♥ — only Ace‑high with no flush draw and a one‑card (J) straight draw. Claude opened UTG+1 (a fairly strong, K/Q/broadway‑heavy range) and has c‑bet 350 into the ~600 pot (~1/2 pot). Folding here is too nitty given the price and my live equity (I block many A‑combos and can hit a J or pair), and raising OOP as a pure bluff is unattractive vs an opener’s range. Calling 350 is a small fraction of my 7,175 stack (≈4.9%) and lets me see a turn for the ~4 J outs plus any pair‑up or showdown value with Ace‑high. Plan: call now, re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to a large raise or heavy turn shove unless I improve (J or pair/clear equity); if I hit the J or pair I can start extracting value.

Check. The turn 3d is a blank for my A♣T♦ — I only have Ace‑high and am out of position versus an UTG+1 opener who c‑bet the flop (range weighted to Kx/Qx/broadways). With no bet to face, checking controls the pot and preserves fold equity on a future river; betting OOP as a bluff is thin and unnecessarily bloats the pot with a marginal hand. I still have live outs (Js for a Broadway straight, plus A/T pairs) so I want to see a free river. Plan: check to the river, then value‑bet if I improve (A/T or J) or fold to significant aggression on a blank river unless the price is right.

I have top pair with a good kicker (KJ) on Qh8cKh3d. Turn was a blank 3d. Minni checked again. I should continue betting for value - I likely have the best hand and need to charge the flush draw still out there. Betting around 65-70% pot (~850) is appropriate to extract value from Qx hands and protect against draws.

Call 850. Claude is UTG+1 and has a fairly strong continuing range after open → flop c-bet → turn barrel (Kx/Qx/strong broadways, some sets/two-pair). I’m out of position with A♣T♦ and only ace-high, but the pot odds justify a call: calling 850 into a 2150 pot (3000 final if I call) gives ~3.5:1 (need ≈22–23% equity). I have ~4 clean J outs to make the nut straight plus 3 Aces and 3 Tens (roughly 10 raw outs ≈22%), and I block some of Claude’s A combos — so my equity is around the breakeven point. Folding is too tight given the price; raising OOP is too thin and will overcommit against his value-heavy range. I’ll call, see the river, and then: fold to large river aggression on a blank, call reasonable bets if I pair or hit the J (or raise for value only if I make the straight and get the right look).

The river A♠? (Ah on board) pairs my A♣, so I have top pair A‑T but I’m out of position to Claude (UTG+1) who opened and barreled flop → turn. His line is value‑heavy (AK/AQ, strong K/Q broadways, some sets) — many of those beat my one pair (AK/AQ => two pair, sets/trips). Betting for thin value is unattractive: a small bet will often be called by worse hands I beat rarely and by better hands that already beat me; a larger bet risks a check‑raise and bloats the pot with a marginal holding. With pot control and showdown value in mind, I’ll check back to avoid committing more and go to showdown.

The Ah river is a terrible card for me. It completes the heart flush draw and puts an overcard to my Kings on the board. My KJ is now second pair and likely behind any Ace or flush. Minni has been check-calling - possibly drawing. Betting gets called only by better hands (flushes, Ax, two pair) and folds out worse. Easy check back for showdown value.
J2o is a trash hand from UTG. Easy fold - way too weak to open from early position.

9♣5♦ is a weak, offsuit, disconnected hand with poor playability and reverse-implied-odds issues. From UTG+1 (5-handed) I still need to maintain a reasonably strong opening range; limping invites tough multiway spots versus position and the big blind, and raising just bloats the pot with a hand that struggles to realize equity. With ~48bb, preserving stack and avoiding marginal punts is best.

Button with 7♣8♣ and ~90BB effective — a very playable, speculative hand in position. Both UTG players folded so I’m facing only the blinds; opening here both gains fold equity and gives me position post‑flop. Standard open size of 3x the big blind (300 total) applies pressure on the blinds while keeping the pot manageable if called. Plan: if I get only calls, use positional advantage to C‑bet appropriate boards and realize equity; fold to a large, committed 3‑bet, consider calling smaller 3‑bets for implied odds.

Preflop in SB facing a button raise to 300 (250 to call into 450 pot). KTo offsuit is a strong enough hand to defend SB vs button range, which is wide. Good pot odds (about 1.8:1), playability with king high, broadway potential, and straight draws possible postflop. Folding too tight; 3-betting not ideal as it's offsuit and not premium. BB acts after but deep stack doesn't change calling here. Call to see flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Five offsuit. The Button (Minni) raised to 300 and the Small Blind (Elon) called. Although I am getting 4.5:1 pot odds to call (200 more to win a pot of 900), J5o is an extremely weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Given my massive chip lead (over 25,000 chips), there is no need to enter a multi-way pot with a dominated hand. I will preserve my stack and wait for a more advantageous situation.

Flop board 9s Jh 3c is fairly dry with no flush draws and limited straight draws, but coordinates somewhat with J-9 possible and gutshots. My Kc Td gives king-high with a gutshot straight draw to broadway (4 outs to Q). Equity against Minni's button raising range (wide: broadway cards, Ax, suited connectors, pairs) is around 30-40% depending on exact range, but I'm OOP acting first with marginal hand. No strong reason to lead as value or bluff; her range hits this board decently, and I have showdown value with king. Best to check, allow her to c-bet her wide range, and decide based on sizing/action. Folding to big bets, calling small cbets, or check-raising strong hands (which this isn't). Pot control and realizing equity postflop maximizes EV long-term.

I raised pre‑flop from the button and it checked through to me on 9♠ J♥ 3♣ with only the SB (Elon) in. I have 7♣8♣ — an open‑ended straight draw (6 or T completes, ~8 outs) and backdoor club potential, plus the positional advantage. The flop is fairly dry so a continuation bet is a standard semi‑bluff: it folds out many missed hands in his calling range (overcards, weak A‑x, some small pairs) while building the pot when I have decent equity to improve. I’ll size to about half the pot to retain fold equity without overcommitting (pot = 700, bet = 350). Plan: if called I continue with my draw and evaluate the turn; fold to a large check‑raise but call small raises given the pot odds and implied odds.

Facing a 1/3 pot c-bet (350 into 1050) from Minni's wide button range on a dry 9s-Jh-3c flop. KTo has king-high with a gutshot straight draw (4 outs to broadway), decent blockers (K blocks top of her range), and plays well multiway but heads-up OOP. Pot odds are excellent (~4:1, needing ~20% equity), and king-high has ~25-35% vs her polarized cbet range (Jx/9x value + air bluffs). Folding is too tight preflop defense realization; raising is overplay as semi-bluff OOP with marginal equity and poor post-turn playability if called. Check-call controls pot, realizes equity on turns that improve (Q, runner-runner), and induces bluffs/checks from missed draws/value. Consistent with preflop plan, exploits her aggression long-term.

Turn board 9s Jh 3c 6h adds minimal new draws (possible wheel gutshots or heart flush draw starts, but still fairly disconnected). Kc Td remains king-high with now-missed gutshot (no Q), providing showdown value against Minni's wide button c-bet range (Jx/9x value, Ax, broadways, bluffs/air). Preflop SB defense and flop check-call were for pot control and equity realization OOP heads-up. First to act on turn, betting is suboptimal: thin value bets risk getting called by Jx/sets/draws while folding out worse; bluffs are poor OOP with reverse implied odds and her aggression history (frequent cbets, turn bets when checked to). Checking maximizes EV by: inducing her continuation bets/bluffs (exploiting wide range), allowing check-behind from missed value, controlling pot size, and preserving stack for river decisions. Pot odds irrelevant as acting first; consistent with controlled, exploitative line vs aggressive button. Fold equity unnecessary here.

I raised preflop and c-bet the flop; Elon called both. The turn (6♥) did not complete a club flush but it did give me an open‑ended straight draw (5 or T) — 8 outs, ~32% to hit on the river. Elon checked the turn, showing some caution/weakness. With decent equity and fold equity I should continue as a semi‑bluff: a bet pressures overcards and weak pairs while building the pot when I hit. Pot = 1,400; I’ll size to roughly half‑pot (700). This sizing keeps many worse hands (single overcards, small pairs) in a calling range while still folding enough air. If Elon check‑raises large I’ll respect it and fold (he likely has a made hand); I’ll call small raises given the pot odds and implied odds and proceed to the river if called.

Facing a second barrel of 700 into 2100 (~1/3 pot) on a turn 6h that doesn't improve my KcTd (king-high, no pair, missed gutshot from flop). Minni's button range is wide but her continued aggression after flop c-bet suggests polarization: strong value (Jx, 9x, sets, two-pair combos like J9/J3 rare) vs bluffs (missed broadways, Ax). King-high has showdown value vs pure air but poor equity (~20-30% max) vs value-heavy betting range, below needed ~25% for pot odds given reverse implied odds OOP (river bets likely if called, stacks deep). Preflop/flop check-call realized equity and controlled pot, but now folding to aggression exploits her lines, preserves 5525 stack for 79 hands left (tournament phase), avoids spew OOP with marginal hand. Consistent pot control strategy vs aggressive opponents; better spots await.

5-handed ranges widen, but we’re UTG (worst position) with a dominated, low-playability hand (K3o). Opening or limping invites tough spots versus players who have shown a willingness to raise/3-bet, and when called we’re frequently up against better Kx that dominates us. With ~48bb, preserving stack and waiting for higher EV opens is best here.

UTG+1 in a 5-handed game with K♦9♣. This is a marginal hand from early position, but in short-handed play I should be opening a bit wider. There’s only the blinds to act behind me (Button Elon, SB Flash, BB Claude), pot is 150 and the min‑raise is 200. I’ll open to a standard 3x the big blind (300) to get fold equity, avoid a limp-heavy multiway pot, and define the pot size. With ~97bb effective I have plenty of room to play postflop against one or two callers, but I’ll fold or proceed cautiously if I face a large 3‑bet from the button/SB. Plan: raise to 300; c‑bet favorable flops in position, check/controlled play OOP and fold to strong aggression if I don’t improve.

Qc7d is a marginal offsuit hand with poor playability, dominated by typical early-position opening ranges from Minni (UTG+1). On the button, position is an advantage, but SB and BB still to act, risking multiway pots where queen-high struggles. Pot odds to call 300 into 450 (~33%) are decent, but hand realizes equity poorly postflop without strong draws or pairs. My 55BB stack allows selectivity; no need to defend weakly. Minni's history shows aggression, but folding avoids tough spots. Standard fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Six offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Minni has opened for 300 from early position (UTG+1). Given my poor relative hand strength and the fact that I would have to play the rest of the hand out of position against a range that is likely much stronger than mine, the most prudent action is to fold. I have a significant chip lead in the tournament and there is no reason to risk chips in a marginal or negative expectation spot.

J7o is a weak hand to defend from the BB against an UTG+1 open. Minni raised from early position which typically indicates a stronger range. J7o plays poorly OOP and doesn't have good implied odds. The 200 to call for a pot of 450 (~2.25:1) isn't enough to justify defending with this marginal hand. Easy fold.

UTG with 8♠3♠ — essentially one of the weakest hands. In early position against four players left to act this is dominated by virtually every opening/defending range and has very poor post‑flop playability despite being suited. Limping (call 100) just invites raises and multiway pots where my backdoor equity is negligible; raising to 200 would be a reckless open with almost no fold equity and bad SPR postflop. I’ll conserve my ~99BB stack and fold to pick better spots later.

5-handed NLHE 50/100, UTG+1 position after UTG fold, unopened pot, facing blinds. Hand 8hTs offsuit is marginal at best: poor high card strength, limited playability without suitedness or connectivity payoff. Standard opening range from early position even short-handed excludes offsuit T8o (typically fold <15% of hands here). Limping invites multiway pots with dominated hand. Raising risks tough spots OOP postflop. BB has 47BB and may 3-bet light; others deep. Preserve stack for premium spots in tournament with 73/150 hands played, goal to maximize chips long-term. Clear fold.

I am on the Button with 5c 6h. The action has folded to me. While opening from the button is generally advised to steal the blinds, 56o is a very weak hand that lacks post-flop playability and showdown value. With Claude and FiveTwo still to act in the blinds, both of whom have shown willingness to defend or re-raise, I will opt for a disciplined fold to preserve my chip lead and wait for a better spot.

Folded to me in the SB with K8o. This is a decent hand heads-up against just the BB. I should raise to put pressure and potentially take the pot down or play post-flop with a reasonable holding. Standard raise to 250.

Claude is opening from the small blind into my big blind, which is typically a very wide range. I’m getting a good price (150 to win a pot that will be 500), so I only need about 30% equity and J5o clears that versus a wide SB open. It’s not strong enough to 3-bet for value and a 3-bet bluff here is too loose given domination/reverse implied odds, so the best line is a straightforward defend and play cautiously postflop.

Heads up against FiveTwo on Js7s4d. I have K8o - complete air with no pair and no draw. As the preflop aggressor, a continuation bet is standard here. The board is somewhat connected with a flush draw possible. A small c-bet around 1/3 pot (175) should fold out hands like A-high that missed. If I get called or raised, I can give up on later streets.

Claude’s small flop c-bet into a heads-up pot from the SB is typically very wide. On Js-7s-4d we have top pair but a very weak kicker, so we’re ahead of lots of floats/overcards/spade draws, but we’re also dominated by better Jx and behind sets/two pair. Given the excellent price (175 to win 675 now, and we realize decent equity/SD value), calling is best: we keep his bluffs in, avoid inflating a pot where reverse implied odds vs better Jx are real, and can make cleaner decisions on turns (especially on spade/overcard runouts).

Turn brought 9d. I have K-high with no pair, no real draws. FiveTwo called my c-bet, showing interest in the pot. Continuing to barrel here is risky - I have no equity and FiveTwo is a shorter stack who's already committed. Check and see a free river, folding if they bet.

Claude’s turn check after the small flop c-bet caps his range a bit and contains a lot of give-ups (overcards), medium pairs/7x, and spade draws that would like a free card. With top pair we can get thin value from worse pairs and charge draws while keeping the pot controlled by using a small sizing. If Claude springs a large check-raise, our J5 (very weak kicker) is a clear candidate to fold and avoid reverse implied odds versus better Jx/two pair/sets.

I have K-high with no pair and no meaningful draws. FiveTwo bet 300 into 850 (about 35% pot) after calling my c-bet - showing continued interest. I have essentially no equity here except catching a K (3 outs). Calling 300 to then have to either bluff the river or hope to hit is -EV. FiveTwo is short-stacked and already committed - not a good spot to bluff. Fold and save chips.

UTG position with 5 active players requires a tight opening range. 2s8s is a speculative suited hand but too weak and uncoordinated for early position play. Folding preserves stack for better spots, avoiding domination or multi-way pots where it plays poorly post-flop. Tournament stage favors discipline over marginal calls.

I am currently the chip leader with over 25k chips. I am in UTG+1 with K4 offsuit. While it is a 5-handed game, K4o is a very weak hand to open with from early position. I have no need to get involved in a pot with a marginal hand when I can wait for better opportunities to leverage my stack. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.

Pocket 3s on the button with just the blinds left to act. This is a standard raise spot - I have position and a playable pair. I'll raise to 2.5x to either take down the blinds or play postflop in position with a hand that can flop a set.

We’re in the SB facing a BTN open to 250, and Q2o is too weak to defend out of position with no ante. Calling realizes poorly (dominated by virtually all Qx in Claude’s range), and turning it into a 3-bet bluff is unnecessary at ~50bb while the BB can still wake up/squeeze. Best EV is to preserve stack and fold.

Claude opened to 250 from the button. I’m in the big blind with T♥7♣ and ~98 BB effective stacks. This is a marginal, offsuit hand and I’ll be out of position postflop, so I want to keep the pot controlled and see a cheap flop. Calling the extra 150 (total 250) gives me reasonable pot odds (~27% required equity after the call) versus a likely wide BTN opening range. A min 3‑bet is possible, but 3‑betting OOP with T7o commits me to difficult postflop decisions and risks a 4‑bet; I’ll flat-call and evaluate on the flop.

I have top pair (T♥) with a weak 7-kicker on Js Ts 2d. I'm out of position versus a BTN raiser and the pot is already a decent 550 with ~96 BB effective stacks — I want to keep the pot controlled. Claude's BTN open is likely wide (lots of Jx, Ax, broadways and some suited connectors), so many hands beat me (Jx, sets, two-pair combos) and there are straight/flush draw possibilities. Betting OOP here would risk being check‑raised and committing with a marginal kicker; checking lets me see how Claude proceeds, call a single bet to extract value from worse tens/draws, or fold to a strong line. If he checks back I get a free turn to improve or take a stab later; if he bets small I plan to call and re-evaluate on the turn.

I have pocket 3s on a Js-Ts-2d board. This is a draw-heavy texture with flush and straight draws possible. My hand has little showdown value - just an underpair. Minni checked to me, showing weakness. I should c-bet here to take down the pot since I won't win at showdown often. A ~half pot bet is appropriate.

I have top pair with a weak kicker (T♥7♣) on Js Ts 2d and I’m out of position versus a BTN opener. Claude’s range is wide and his half‑pot bet (275 into the ~550 pre‑flop pot, making it 825) will include many worse tens and draws but also Jx and the occasional set/two‑pair that dominate me. Raising OOP with this marginal kicker is unnecessary and can get me check‑raised or committed; folding surrenders value against bluffs/draws. With ~96BB effective I’ll call to keep the pot controlled, realize equity against draws and worse tens, and re‑evaluate on the turn — if Claude barrels big or the turn is a scary spade/J/T I’ll fold to strong aggression.

Claude c‑bet the flop and then checked the turn. I have T♥7♣ — top pair but a very weak kicker and I’m OOP. His turn check shows some weakness and gives me a chance to take the initiative. A small value/deny bet (~1/3 pot) folds out overcards and missed draws, extracts from worse tens and other calling hands, and keeps the pot controllable if I’m called. If Claude check‑raises I’ll respect it and fold this marginal kicker; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river. Betting a modest amount gives the right mix of fold equity and protection, so I’ll bet 400 into the 1100 pot.

Minni called my flop c-bet and is now donk-betting the turn for 400. This is a concerning line - donk bets often indicate strength or a draw wanting to set its own price. My pocket 3s have almost no equity here - I'm behind any pair, any draw, basically everything. The board is extremely draw-heavy with flush and straight possibilities. With only 2 outs to improve and facing aggression, continuing would be lighting money on fire. Time to fold and preserve my stack.

Holding King-Jack of Spades (KJs) in a 5-handed game is a premium starting hand. Being Under the Gun (UTG), I want to open the pot with a standard raise to exert my chip lead and define my hand's strength. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a solid opening sizing in this tournament structure.
2h3d is a very weak offsuit hand. Facing a raise from UTG, this is an easy fold. No reason to invest 300 chips with such poor cards and poor position.

We’re on the Button facing a 3bb open from Flash in a 5-handed spot (UTG is still an early position range). Th4o is too weak to call due to dominated ten-x, poor playability, and minimal equity realization, and it’s also not a good 3-bet bluff candidate (bad blockers, poor postflop coverage). With ~52bb effective, best EV is to preserve stack and wait for better steal/3-bet spots.

Fold. Facing an UTG raise to 300 (3x) from Flash while I’m in the small blind with 6s5s is too marginal. 6♠5♠ is a speculative suited connector but it's likely dominated by an UTG opening range and performs poorly out of position — especially if the big blind calls or 3-bets behind me. Calling 250 into ~450 (~2.8:1 pot odds, ~26% equity required) is not enough versus a typical UTG range, and squeezing/3-betting with this hand is not profitable. With ~104bb I’ll preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

Preflop in BB with AKo facing an UTG raise to 300 from Flash (deep stack). AKo is a premium hand that dominates much of an UTG opening range. Calling 200 into a 650 pot is loose; better to 3-bet for value, to isolate, and build the pot OOP. Standard sizing is 3x the open to 900. My stack of 5425 (54BB) supports this easily. Flash has been active from early position but shows strength; still, AK crushes his likely range. SB and others folded, so likely heads-up.

I opened UTG with KJs, a premium hand in a 5-handed game. Elon 3-bet from the Big Blind to 900. With Elon being short-stacked (starting the hand with 4625), this raise represents a strong range, but KJs is too strong to fold getting 2:1 odds while being in position for the rest of the hand. I will call the 600 difference to see a flop. Given the pot size will be 1850 and Elon will have 3725 remaining, the SPR will be low, meaning I am likely looking to commit if I hit top pair or a significant draw.

Heads-up on the flop with AKo OOP vs Flash who opened UTG and called my 3-bet. Flopped TPTK on a K-9-J rainbow-ish board (9c Jh Kd), which is very strong. My hand crushes Flash's calling range (JJ+, QQ+, KK, AA, AK, AQo/s), beating everything except AA (which is possible but I have 6 outs to the A), sets (unlikely), or unlikely straights like QT (weak preflop). Board has some straight draw potential (T10, Q10) and a club flush draw, so betting protects against those and extracts value from worse Kx, QJ, AJ, etc. Pot 1850, c-bet sizing ~2/3 pot (1200-1300) builds the pot while leaving fold equity and position leverage. My stack 4625 supports this; Flash deep at 24k but pays off. No check; always c-bet here for value/protection.

I have hit top two pair (Kings and Jacks) on a board that is quite coordinated (Kd-Jh-9c). Elon 3-bet from the Big Blind pre-flop, which usually indicates a strong range like AA, AK, KK, JJ, or QQ. I am currently ahead of AA, AK, and QQ. While KK and JJ beat me, I block both. There is a straight draw available (QT), but it is less likely for Elon to 3-bet pre-flop with that specific hand. Given the pot size and Elon's remaining stack of 2025 chips after his 1300 bet, he is effectively pot-committed. I should raise to put him all-in now to maximize value from hands like AK or AA and to protect my hand against any potential draws (like a Queen or Ten appearing on the turn).

Facing a large flop raise (to 3325, ~2.5x my 1300 c-bet) in a 3-bet pot OOP against an UTG opener/3-bet caller (Flash). Board K♦9♣J♥ is somewhat coordinated (gutshots, flush draw possible, straight draws like QT/QT). My AdKc is TPTK, very strong in general, crushing worse Ax/Kx draws/pairs and overpairs QQ/JJ (JJ now set). However, Flash's tight UTG calling range vs 3-bet (JJ+, QQ+, KK, AA, AK/AQ) combined with aggressive raise sizing reps monsters: KK (trips using board K), AA (overpair), JJ (set), 99 (unlikely but set), or rarely QT straight. Beats my AK frequently (esp. KK/AA/JJ), equity <35% vs this range. Calling 2025 (61% pot, leaving 1300 behind ~10BB) into 8500 pot commits me fully (turn shove likely), poor implied odds OOP vs deep stack. Raising all-in (3325 more to 4625 total) gets called by better too often, no fold equity vs nuts. Preserve ~33BB stack for better spots mid-tournament (hand 75/150); folding TPTK correct vs pro-level opponent strength rep. Opponent history shows aggression but strength here.
J4o from UTG is a clear fold. This is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability, and I'm in the earliest position with 4 players left to act behind me. No reason to open this hand.

We’re 5-handed but still first in from UTG+1 with 32o offsuit—bottom-tier equity and terrible playability. Opening (raise or limp) will put us in tough postflop spots versus three players behind, including a deep and active big blind (Flash). With ~52bb, preserving stack and waiting for better stealing candidates is clearly higher EV than entering with this hand.

Button with J6o is marginal but fine to open given position and folded front. Pot is 150 and to-call is only 100; calling invites a multiway pot where J6o is poor. Min-raising to 200 is too weak and will encourage the blinds to defend; I prefer a standard 3x BB open to apply pressure and isolate. Raise to 300 gives good fold equity against the SB and BB while leaving plenty of stack behind (I have ~104 BB). Plan: fold to a large 3-bet/shove, otherwise play postflop in position — c-bet favorable flops, control pot on bad boards and exploit position.

Pocket eights is a strong hand in 5-handed play, roughly top 12% of hands. Minni's button raise to 300 is likely wide, including many broadway cards, suited connectors, and weaker pairs that 88 dominates. From the small blind with ~33BB effective stack (my 3275), calling risks a multiway pot OOP, which is suboptimal for a medium pair/set mining. Folding is too tight. 3-betting isolates against Minni's wide range, applies pressure on BB Flash (who acts after), and has fold equity. Sizing to 900 (~3x the open) builds a pot while leaving room for further action. If called/4-bet, 88 has good equity. Shoving is overplay given deep BB stacks and ICM considerations mid-tournament.

I am the chip leader in the Big Blind holding 7s7d. Minni (Button) has raised to 300, and Elon (Small Blind) has 3-bet to 900. Elon is extremely short-stacked with only 1,525 chips remaining behind. While 4-betting could isolate Elon, it also risks a large confrontation with Minni, who still has 10k chips. Calling the 800 more (to make a total of 900) is the most prudent play; it allows me to set-mine or evaluate a flop in a potential 3-way pot while maintaining the flexibility to fold if Minni 4-bets or if the flop is dangerous. This strategy prioritizes maintaining my chip lead through controlled aggression and calculated risk-taking.

I opened to 300 on the button with J♠6♣ to steal, but Elon (SB) 3-bet to 900 and Flash (BB) cold‑called — now I face a 3‑bet plus a caller. J6o is a weak, often‑dominated holding and plays poorly multiway against a 3‑bet range. Calling 600 into a ~2700 pot requires ~22% equity, which J6o doesn’t reliably have vs these ranges; 4‑betting as a bluff risks being jammed by Elon (he’s ~24BB) or getting called by the big stack. With poor playability and bad odds, the correct play is to preserve chips and fold.

Pocket 88 on Ah Td 5s rainbow flop. As the pre-flop 3-bettor OOP facing a BB caller in a 2100 pot with 2425 behind, this board heavily favors Flash's flatting range, which includes Ax (AJo+, suited aces), broadway suited (JTs, QJs, KQs for wheel/T draw potential or Ax), and some medium pairs/traps. 88 blocks no strong hands but is a clean underpair with minimal showdown value OOP. Straight draws are limited (e.g., 76 for gutshot, J9), flush draws absent (rainbow). C-betting risks calls from Ax/top pair and raises from stronger, while fold equity is low vs. deep BB defender. Checking preserves stack, induces bluffs from missed draws/broadways, and allows cheap fold to bet (88 equity poor vs. his value range). Continuation aggressive sizing unnecessary; check-fold maximizes EV long-term in multi-hand tournament.

In this heads-up pot against Elon, who 3-bet pre-flop, the board has come Ace-high (Ah-Td-5s). Since Elon 3-bet from the Small Blind, his range is very strong and heavily weighted towards high cards like Ax and broadway pairs that have me beat. My pair of sevens is now an underpair to the board. When Elon checks, it could be a trap or a realization that the board missed his specific high cards (like KQ or JJ). However, there is no value in betting here; if I bet, I am likely only called by hands that beat me (Ax, Tx, or higher pairs). By checking behind, I take a free card to either hit a set or get to a cheap showdown while minimizing risk against a short-stacked opponent who might be looking to commit his remaining chips.

Turn 6h completes some gutshot straight draws (e.g., 4-7, 7-9) and adds a heart flush draw, increasing the board's drawiness in a 2100-chip pot with my 2425 stack (SPR ~1.15, pot-committed). As preflop 3-bettor OOP, my 88 has decent showdown value against Flash's BB flatting/check-back range, which is capped (weak Ax, broadways like KQ/KJ/JT, pocket pairs below A, missed draws) after checking flop—strong hands like sets/top pair likely bet flop for protection/value. Betting seizes initiative, denies equity to his improved draws/floats, extracts value from weaker pairs/stubborn Ax that call a pot bet, and has fold equity against air/bluffs. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or facing river bets OOP where 88 is often beat. With low SPR, pot-sized bet (2100) commits my stack effectively, maximizing EV vs. his polarized range in mid-tournament ICM where survival matters but aggression builds stack. Shoving slightly overbet unnecessary with his deep stack.

On the turn board of 5s-Ah-Td-6h, I am holding a pair of sevens, which is an underpair to the board. Elon, who 3-bet from the Small Blind pre-flop, has checked the flop and is now leading out for 2,100, which represents nearly his entire remaining stack (leaving him with only 325 chips). While his check on the flop could have been a trap with an Ace or a medium pair like Jacks or Queens, his turn lead into an Ace-high board is very strong given his pre-flop aggression. My sevens only beat total bluffs like KQ or KJ, and even some of those (like heart draws) have significant equity against me. Given that the Ace and Ten are heavily represented in a Small Blind 3-betting range, and that I am the chip leader with over 26,000 chips, there is no reason to risk a large portion of my stack on a hero call with a weak underpair. I will fold and maintain my dominant chip position.

5-handed UTG, A♣T♣ is comfortably within an opening range: strong high-card value, suited playability, and it benefits a lot from having initiative. Limping invites the blinds (especially the deep stacks) to realize equity cheaply and puts us in tougher multiway spots; folding is too tight at this depth (~52bb). I’ll open to a standard 2.5x size that has been common at this table, aiming to win the blinds or play a pot in position against narrower calling ranges.

UTG open to 250 (~2.5x BB) from FiveTwo and I'm UTG+1 with 3♠4♥ offsuit. This is a very weak, poorly coordinated hand with little equity vs an UTG opening range and I'd be out of position post-flop. There's no reason to call 250 or to 3-bet as a bluff here — I'd be playing a marginal spot for a small chance of reward. Preserve my 10,100 stack for better position and stronger holdings. Fold.

T5o is a marginal offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against an UTG raiser's strong range. FiveTwo's UTG open indicates strength, and while button position offers some flexibility, calling 250 into a 400 pot doesn't justify the risk given the hand's weakness and multiway potential with blinds behind. Folding preserves the stack for better opportunities in this tournament.

I am in the Small Blind holding Jack-Deuce offsuit, which is a very weak hand. FiveTwo has opened from UTG with a 2.5x raise. Given my position and the weakness of my hand, there is no strategic reason to call or raise. I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand against the opener, and the Big Blind still has to act. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

K6o is a weak hand to defend against a UTG raise. FiveTwo opened from early position which typically indicates a stronger range. K6o plays poorly postflop - often dominated by better kings and lacks connectivity. Even getting 150:400 pot odds, this is a clear fold.

I have A♦K♦ UTG in a five‑handed game — a premium that should be opened for value and fold equity. A standard 3x BB open (to 300) builds the pot, helps isolate the blinds, and takes the initiative post‑flop while avoiding the passivity/invites of a min‑raise. Size is in line with my table image and keeps the pot manageable against the deep/aggressive button (Flash) and the medium stacks in the blinds; I can re‑evaluate if I face a sizable 3‑bet (call or 4‑bet/shove depending on opponent and sizing).

Qc2h is a complete trash hand: offsuit, poor connectivity, dominated by virtually any part of Minni's UTG opening range (which includes strong hands like pairs, broadways, suited aces, etc., based on her history of selective aggression). In UTG+1 position with 45BB stack, calling a 3BB raise commits too much equity to a speculative hand with low implied odds potential. Raising is suicidal without premium strength. Folding preserves stack for superior spots in this tournament phase (hand 78/150), where patient play maximizes EV long-term.

I am currently the chip leader with over 26,000 chips. Holding KJo on the button is a standard calling or 3-betting hand in a 5-handed game. Minni raised from UTG (which is the first position in this setup), but looking at her previous hand history, she has been quite active, suggesting her range is wide. While I could 3-bet to take control of the pot immediately, calling allows me to play a pot in position against an aggressive opponent while keeping the pot size manageable and minimizing variance given my massive lead. I'll call and evaluate the flop.

8T offsuit in the SB facing a UTG raise and button call. Getting 3:1 odds to call 250, but I'll be out of position multiway with a marginal hand. 8To doesn't play well in multiway pots OOP - it's not strong enough to continue profitably here. Fold and wait for a better spot.

We’re in the big blind facing an UTG 3x open and a button flat, so ranges are relatively strong and we’ll be out of position against at least one opponent. T5o has very poor playability multiway, is frequently dominated, and doesn’t realize equity well from the BB versus tight/strong ranges. The pot odds (~21%) aren’t enough to justify defending this offsuit trash, and a squeeze bluff is too spewy here (no blockers, deep stacks behind, high chance of getting called and playing a big pot OOP). Best EV is to just release.

I opened to 300 preflop and got one caller on the button. Flop 9s 4d 2s is very dry with two spades — I have A♦K♦: two overcards and a backdoor diamond draw but only ace‑high right now. A standard continuation bet is +EV here: it pressures the button's wide calling range, charges spade draws, and preserves initiative while keeping the pot controllable. I size about half‑pot (≈375) to deny correct odds to spade draws and extract value from worse broadways/one‑pair hands. Plan: if called I will barrel on favorable turns (A/K/diamond) or pot‑control on blanks; if I face a large check‑raise I will give credit to a made hand and re‑evaluate (fold vs very large aggression).

On this dry flop of 9s-4d-2s, Minni's half-pot continuation bet of 375 is a standard move for an aggressive player who opened from UTG. I hold King-Jack offsuit with the Jack of spades, giving me two overcards and a backdoor spade flush draw. Given my significant chip lead and position on the button, calling here to float is a viable strategy. I am looking to see if the turn improves my hand (pairing a King or Jack) or provides more equity (a spade or a straight draw). If the turn is checked to me, I can potentially take the pot away, or I can re-evaluate if Minni continues her aggression.

I opened UTG with A♦K♦ and c‑bet the dry 9♠‑4♦‑2♠ flop (375) and was called. The turn 8♠ is a problem — it brings a third spade to the board and turns many of the caller’s flop hands into made or near-made spade flushes. My backdoor diamond route is dead (turn wasn’t a diamond), so I’m left with just two overcards (~6 clean outs to pair, roughly 6/46 ≈ 13%), which is poor equity against a range that can now include completed spade flushes. Betting here would be thin: I’d often get called by hands that already beat me and risk a check‑raise from a made flush. I’ll check to control the pot size, induce bluffs or small bets from weaker hands, and preserve the option to call a modest river bet if I pick up equity or fold to clear strength. Plan: check, call small/medium river bets to realize equity, fold to large aggression.

On the turn, the 8 of spades brings a third spade to the board, potentially completing a flush. Minni has checked after being the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop. I currently hold King-high with the Jack of spades, giving me a high-equity draw to a flush and two overcards. While I could bet to try and take the pot down immediately, checking back is a safer and more strategic play as the chip leader. It allows me to realize my equity and see the river for free. If the river is a spade, a King, or a Jack, I will likely have the best hand. If I miss, I still have some showdown value against her bluffs or can evaluate a potential bluff myself depending on the river card.

I check. Holding A♦K♦ on 9♠–4♦–2♠–8♠–8♥ I never improved — I’m just Ace‑high. The turn completed many spade flushes in the caller’s range (they called my flop bet and then checked the turn), so their continuing range heavily contains made spade flushes and small pairs that also beat me. A river bet would be a pure bluff with poor blockers (I don’t hold spades to block the flush), and most hands that will call a bluff (pairs, completed flushes) already beat me. Checking controls the pot size, preserves chips, and keeps the option to fold to aggression; if the button leads a small, cheap bluff I may consider a call as a bluff‑catcher, but I will fold to a standard/large river bet. Betting for value is not an option with no pair, so checking is the lowest‑variance +EV line here.

I hold King-high (Kh Js) on a board of 9s-4d-2s-8s-8h. Minni, who opened from UTG and c-bet the flop, has checked both the turn and the river. While my King-high has some showdown value, it is likely that Minni's UTG range contains many Ace-high (Ax) hands that currently beat me. However, as the massive chip leader, I prioritize minimizing variance. Checking back is the safest play; it allows me to see the showdown for free without risking chips on a bluff that might be called by a pair or a better high-card hand. I will take the free showdown and see if my King-high is sufficient to win the pot.

UTG in 5-handed play with T9o (offsuit). This is a marginal speculative hand with straight potential but no suitedness or high cards for top pair value. Early position makes it vulnerable to 3-bets and multiway pots. Opponents frequently raise preflop (2-3x BB common from history), and limping UTG invites trouble while open-raising is suboptimal with this hand. Stacks are deep except FiveTwo (51BB); better to wait for stronger spot to maximize stack growth. Standard fold.
J4o is a very weak hand in early/middle position. With a large chip lead, there is no strategic reason to play marginal hands from UTG+1. I will fold and wait for better spots.

On the button with 6d8d - a suited connector. Two players folded, only blinds remain. This is a standard opening spot with position. Raising to 250 (2.5x) to steal blinds or play in position if called.

Tc2c is a very weak suited hand and plays poorly out of position versus a Button open, with heavy domination issues (any Tx, many suited clubs). Although the direct price is tempting, equity realization from the small blind is bad and the big blind can come along, making us multiway and even more difficult to navigate. With ~52bb effective, this is a spot to avoid bleeding chips and wait for better defend/3-bet candidates.

Hand is 3♦5♣ — very weak offsuit. Claude opened to 250 from the button and it only costs me 150 more to see a flop; pot would be 550 after my call, so I need ~27% equity to justify calling. Heads-up vs a button open my hand has borderline equity but calling is cheap relative to my deep ~108BB stack and lets me realize equity or fold to aggression postflop. 3-betting is unnecessary and a poor bluff here (no meaningful blockers) and folding is too tight for the price. Plan: call to see the flop and proceed cautiously — continue only with a pair, strong draw, or clear postflop edge; otherwise fold to significant pressure.

I have 3♦5♣ on A♠ 9♥ 6♠ — no pair and no meaningful draws (only a very remote backdoor straight). Claude was the preflop raiser and has position, so he can c‑bet and apply pressure. Betting as a bluff here is a poor use of chips (I have no blockers to A or the spade suit) and check‑raising would be overly ambitious. Pot control is key: check and give up to significant aggression. Plan: check now; only continue if I pair or pick up a real draw, or if Claude makes a very small c‑bet (I may call a tiny bet to see a cheap turn), otherwise fold to standard/large c‑bets.

Flopped bottom pair (6s) on a A96 board with backdoor diamonds. Minni checked to me. With just bottom pair, I'm mostly getting called by better hands (any ace, any 9) and folding out worse. Best to check back for pot control and see a free turn card.

Check. I have 3♦5♣ — no pair and only a backdoor straight (a 4 gives 3-4-5-6-7; ~4 outs ≈ 8–9% to river). Pot is small (550) and Claude, the preflop raiser, has position and likely holds A‑x, broadways or a medium pair; he can apply comfortable pressure. Betting as a bluff here is unnecessary — I have no meaningful blockers and this spot is better played for pot control. Plan: check to avoid bloating the pot, fold to any substantial aggression, but consider calling a very small probe on the river to realize my 4‑out equity.

Turn brings the 7h. I now have bottom pair (6s) plus a gutshot straight draw (need a 5 for 5-6-7-8-9). Minni has checked twice, suggesting weakness or a trapping hand. With my marginal holding, betting risks getting called by better (any A, 9, or 7) while folding out worse. Checking maintains pot control and gives me a free river to potentially improve or show down cheaply.

The river 5d pairs my 5 giving me a weak, low pair. Claude opened preflop but has been passive — checking both the Ace‑high flop and the turn — so his range is wide and includes a lot of missed broadway hands and medium holdings, but it also contains many hands that still beat me (Ax, 9x, 7x, 6x, pocket pairs). Betting here is thin value: it will fold out a lot of worse hands and get called by most hands that beat me, so it’s negative EV to bluff/value-bet without blockers. With a small pot relative to effective stacks I prefer pot control and a cheap showdown. I’ll check and see if Claude fires a river bet; if he bets small I can call for pot odds, but I’ll fold to substantial sizing.

River 5d completes my straight! I have 5-6-7-8-9 (my 6d8d + board 5-7-9). Minni has checked three streets showing weakness. Time to value bet this made straight. Betting around 70% pot to extract value from any ace, pair, or weak hand that might call.

River paired my 5 — I now have a weak bottom pair (3♦5♣ on 6♠ 9♥ A♠ 7♥ 5♦). Claude was the preflop raiser and checked both flop and turn, then fires 400 into a 550 pot. Calling 400 into a 950 pot requires ~42.1% equity (400/950). Given Claude’s passive line earlier, his river bet is consistent with missed broadways / medium hands trying to steal or thin value from Ace-highs, rather than a large proportion of strong hands (Ax, 9x, sets) which would more often have bet earlier. That means a sizable chunk of his river-bet range is behind my pair. Raising with bottom pair is thin — it will fold out only the very weakest hands and will get called/raised by many better hands, so it’s unnecessary risk. Folding forfeits good pot odds and a hand that is often good. I’ll call 400 to realize equity and take the showdown; do not enlarge the pot with a raise.

I am UTG with Ace-Ten offsuit in a 5-handed game. This is a solid opening hand. I have a significant chip lead, so I want to continue playing aggressively and applying pressure, especially with the shorter stacks of FiveTwo and Elon behind me. A standard raise to 3 big blinds is appropriate here to take control of the pot.
A7o facing UTG raise. This hand is marginal - easily dominated by better aces in an UTG range. Out of position and the hand doesn't play well postflop. Clean fold.

Facing an UTG open to 3bb, 3c2h is far too weak to defend even on the button. It plays poorly postflop (dominated, low connectivity, weak equity realization) and there’s no profitable 3-bet bluff candidate here given stack depths and opener position. Best decision is to preserve stack and fold.

Flash opened UTG to 300 (3x BB) — a strong, early-position range. I'm in the small blind with Q♦6♥: a weak, often-dominated offsuit hand with poor post‑flop playability and I’ll be out of position versus the raiser (and likely the BB if he defends). Calling 250 to play a multiway pot OOP is marginal and 3‑betting light here is not profitable with this holding. With a deep stack and lots of better spots to pick, the correct fold is to conserve chips and avoid a difficult postflop situation.

Pocket 77 is a strong hand in the big blind facing an UTG raise from Flash. UTG opening ranges are generally tight, but 77 has excellent equity against it (dominates many Ax, pairs below, suited connectors), set-mining potential with great implied odds given Flash's deep stack, and blocks some of villain's premium hands. Folding is too tight, flat-calling invites multiway pots and difficult postflop spots OOP. 3-betting is standard to isolate, build the pot, and take control. Sizing to 900 (3x the raise) is appropriate for position and stack depths (my 44bb effective). Tournament stage (hand 80/150, 5 players) favors aggression to maximize stack growth.

I opened UTG with Ace-Ten offsuit, and Elon has 3-bet me from the Big Blind to 900. Elon is quite short-stacked (36.25 BB total), and this 900 bet represents about a quarter of his stack. I have a massive chip lead, and the price to call is 600 more to win a total pot of 1,850, giving me roughly 3:1 odds. While A-To is often dominated by a standard 3-betting range, Elon has shown a tendency to 3-bet to this specific sizing from the blinds before. Calling allows me to use my positional advantage post-flop and see if I hit top pair. Given the low SPR (Stack-to-Pot Ratio) of approximately 1.5, I expect the rest of the chips to go in if the flop is favorable or if Elon jams on a dry board where I feel my Ace-high might still be good, but for now, a call is the most prudent way to realize equity.

77 is an overpair to a dry 9-4-3 flop with two spades. This board misses most of Flash's UTG 3-bet calling range (QQ+, AK, AQ, maybe some suited broadways), providing excellent equity. Few sets possible, and draws are weak (spade flush draw unlikely in their range, gutshot straights). As preflop aggressor OOP, c-betting for value and protection against any draws or floats is standard. Villain deep, implied odds good if behind. Sizing 2/3 pot (1200) balances protection/value thin, denies equity to worse Ax/pairs/draws. Check-fold too passive, check-raise risks overplaying. Tournament mid-stage, aggression builds stack.

In Hand #80, I raised UTG with Ace-Ten offsuit and called a 3-bet from Elon in the Big Blind. The flop comes 9s-4s-3h. Elon, who is playing a very short stack (starting the hand with roughly 33 BB), leads out for 1200 into a pot of 1850. This bet represents nearly half of his remaining chips, effectively committing him to the hand. With a remaining stack of only 1225 behind, he is unlikely to fold to any further aggression. My Ace-high has some equity with two overcards and the Ten of spades (backdoor flush possibilities), but against a standard 3-betting range from the blinds, Ace-Ten is frequently dominated by hands like AK, AQ, or pocket pairs (TT-AA). Given my substantial chip lead, I choose to play conservatively and avoid doubling up a short stack on a marginal holding where I am likely behind. I previously successfully pressured Elon in Hand #75, but with his stack now critical and his flop lead being so large relative to his total chips, he is clearly prepared to go all the way. I will fold and preserve my stack.
JTo is a decent broadway hand in UTG. While not premium, it's playable at a 6-handed table. I'll open raise to 2.5x to build the pot and maintain aggression. Standard opening strategy.

5-handed UTG opens 2.5bb, so ranges are wider than full-ring, and Ts9s is a strong, playable suited connector with good implied odds and postflop maneuverability. Calling is profitable given the price (250 to win 400+250=650) and keeps the pot manageable versus a likely strong-but-not-nutted UTG range; a light 3-bet here doesn’t have great blocker value and risks getting 4-bet or forced to play a bloated pot with players still behind who can continue/squeeze. I’ll flat and realize equity in position versus UTG while keeping my range protected with suited connectors.

Button with 5♣7♣ — a suited, connected speculative hand that plays excellently in position. Claude opened to 250 and one early caller already; calling 250 keeps the pot manageable, gives great positional advantage against both raiser and caller, and offers backdoor flush/straight equity without overcommitting preflop. A small isolation raise is possible but invites 3-bets from big stacks (Flash/Elon) and is unnecessary with this holding. Plan: call to see a flop; continue aggressively on favourable boards (club flush/straight draws or a pair), but fold to heavy action on dry boards or to a large 3‑bet preflop.
