Pocket sixes is a strong speculative hand multiway preflop. The pot is already 900 after three players have committed 250 each, offering excellent pot odds to call 200 (getting over 4.5:1). We have around 54BB effective with FiveTwo short-ish at 49BB, providing solid implied odds for set-mining. Position is SB OOP, but the multiway dynamic favors seeing a cheap flop with this pair. Folding wastes equity; 3-betting risks bloating the pot OOP without premium strength, and BB Flash acts last. Calling maximizes EV by controlling pot size while keeping weaker hands in.

I am in the Big Blind with 2d 3c, the absolute weakest starting hand. Although the pot odds are attractive (calling 150 to win a pot of 1100, or roughly 8.3:1), 23o performs exceptionally poorly in a 5-way pot. With four opponents, the likelihood of being dominated even if I hit a pair is extremely high, and the hand has virtually no straight or flush potential. As the chip leader mid-way through the tournament, maximizing my stack involves disciplined folds with hands that have low realizable equity and high reverse implied odds. It is better to preserve the 150 chips than to enter a multi-way pot out of position with garbage.

The flop of Qc Qd 8s is extremely dry and paired, heavily favoring Claude's UTG raising range which likely includes many Qx hands like AQ, KQ, QJ, or even QQ for a set. FiveTwo and Minni defended wide but still have some equity. My pocket 6s is now just an underpair to the 8, with minimal showdown value and poor equity against the field (vulnerable to any Q, 88, or higher pairs). The pot is 1100 multiway, and with action checked to me in position, betting would be a bluff into strong ranges with low fold equity from calling stations like FiveTwo (short stack at ~49BB). Raising risks bloating OOP. Best play is to check: control pot size, realize equity for free if checked back, and fold to justified aggression on turn while preserving stack (52BB deep). This maximizes EV long-term in tournament.

I have JT with no pair on a QQ8 board. With 3 callers, a c-bet here is risky - I have no piece of this board and little equity. If called, I'm in a tough spot. Best to check and see a free card, potentially hitting a J or T on the turn.

Flop is Qc Qd 8s and we hold Ts9s: a decent 9-out spade flush draw, but it will be a non-nut flush when it comes in (A/K-high spades can dominate), and the board is paired so trips/boats are very possible. Multiway (4 players) fold equity is low; if we stab, we’re often getting called/raised and building a pot where our draw has reverse-implied-odds. With two players already checking, the best line is to take the free card, keep the pot manageable, and be ready to call a reasonable bet from the button/SB with correct odds (or continue aggressively on spade turns when we pick up more equity).

Flop Qc Qd 8s with 5c7c: I have no pair and only very remote runner-runner equity (would need both turn+river clubs or specific runner-runner straights). This is a multiway pot (Claude opened, FiveTwo and Elon called), so my fold equity is minimal and a bluff here is low EV. Claude checking the flop doesn't change much — could be pot control or a slowplay. I check to see a free turn and will fold to any meaningful bet; if a club or a real pair/straight card hits the turn I can re-evaluate and apply pressure or call accordingly.

Pocket 6s remains an underpair on a paired Qc Qd 8s 4c board, with no improvement and the turn bringing a low card that doesn't significantly change my equity (still ~10-20% multiway vs. strong ranges, vulnerable to any Qx, 88, 66+, or draws). Claude's UTG range hits this hard (likely QQ/AQ/KQ often), while FiveTwo/Minni have wider ranges but still crush us. Pot is 1100 with 52BB effective (FiveTwo shorter at ~49BB), and checking first to act maintains pot control OOP in SB, realizes equity cheaply if checked through (common on this dry board), and allows folding to aggression without committing more chips. Betting as a bluff has low fold equity against calling stations like FiveTwo/Minni (who defend wide), and thin value doesn't exist since 66 beats only bluffs/air. Checking maximizes EV by avoiding tough spots post-bet while preserving stack for better spots late in tournament (hand 81/150).

Turn brought 4c which doesn't help me. I still have just JT high with no pair. Board is QQ84. With 3 opponents who all checked flop and turn, they likely don't have a Queen, but betting as a pure bluff into 3 players is risky and unnecessary. Pot is only 1100. I'll take a free card and hope to hit a J or T on the river, or give up if I miss.

Turn is Qc Qd 8s 4c. With Ts9s we actually no longer have a flush draw (only one spade on board and only one card to come, so we can’t reach 5 spades). We also have no meaningful straight draw with one card to come, leaving us with mostly thin equity from pairing a T or 9 (often still not good multiway). Since this is a 4-way pot and the preflop raiser has checked twice, a stab here is mostly a pure bluff with low fold equity and risks getting called/raised by any Qx or even stubborn 8x/pairs—plus we still have the button behind who can punish a lead. Best is to check, take the free river if it comes, and be prepared to fold to most bets.

Board: Qc Qd 8s 4c; my hand 5c7c (no pair). I currently have a one-card flush draw (four clubs between my hand + board) — 9 clubs left -> 9/46 ≈ 19.6% to hit the flush on the river. There are also 3 fives + 3 sevens that could pair me (naive total outs = 15/46 ≈ 32.6%), but pairing a 5 or 7 will often be weak in a multiway pot. Action has been passive (checked around), Claude opened preflop and multiple callers remain, so fold equity is minimal and a turn bluff is low EV. With a free card available and a reasonable chance to improve, the correct play is to check and see the river. If I hit a club on the river I can value bet; if I miss and face meaningful pressure I will fold unless odds/reads justify a call.

River 2h is a complete blank, failing to improve my pocket 6s which remains an underpair to the 8 on a paired Q-Q-8-4-2 board. Equity is low multiway (~10-15% vs. combined ranges: Claude's UTG raise likely includes Qx/AQ/KQ/TT+/sets often; FiveTwo/Minni called wide pre but flop favors Claude heavily). Pot 1100 with FiveTwo short at ~49BB and me at 52BB; checking first (SB OOP postflop) controls pot, realizes equity for free if checked through (plausible on dry board with no draws completing), and allows folding to justified bets without risking stack late tournament (hand 81/150). Betting thin value lacks targets (beats only bluffs/air/weak pairs unlikely after checks); bluffing has minimal fold equity vs. calling stations like FiveTwo/Minni who defend wide. Checking maximizes EV by preserving stack for premium spots while chopping dead money.

River completed QQ842 board. I have JT high - zero showdown value. Everyone has checked all streets, suggesting weak holdings but likely still beating my jack high. Bluffing into 3 opponents with a small pot is -EV. Any pair beats me and someone likely has at least a small pair or ace high. Check and give up the pot.

River is Qc Qd 8s 4c 2h and after flop/turn checking through, our Ts9s is just T-high with very weak showdown value. However, we’re not last to act (Minni on the button is behind), and in a 4-way pot the combined fold equity for a river bluff is still poor: plenty of ranges contain pocket pairs (QQ55/QQ77/QQ99), any 8x (QQ88), slowplayed Qx, or even random pairs that will “look us up” once we bet. We also don’t have meaningful blockers to queens or to the most likely calling hands, so our bluff would be low-quality and exposes us to getting called/raised and burning chips. Best is to check and take the small chance we win at showdown if everyone has complete air; otherwise we can comfortably fold if Minni now bets with strength.

I missed my club (river 2♥). With 5♣7♣ on Q♣ Q♦ 8♠ 4♣ 2♥ I have no pair (7‑high) and no showdown value in a multiway spot. Action has been passive (checked through), so my fold equity is essentially zero — a river bluff is low EV and there is no value to extract. Correct play is to check and see showdowns; I will fold to any meaningful bet (only consider calling a very tiny, highly suspicious bet).

5-handed UTG we can open fairly wide, but 87 offsuit is still near the bottom of the playable range. It has poor playability out of position, gets dominated when it makes one-pair, and we’re likely to face 3-bets from deeper/active stacks behind (especially with Flash/Claude able to apply pressure). With ~49bb, preserving stack and waiting for better opens is higher EV than taking a thin, high-variance spot here.

Ad J (offsuit) in UTG+1 in a 5-handed game is a strong open — good top-pair/top-kicker potential and useful blockers to AK/AA. I want initiative and to avoid a cheap multiway pot out of position, so open-raising is correct. I’ll make it a standard 3x BB to 300 to build the pot, get folds from weaker hands, and keep the ranges I beat in. Plan: c-bet favorable flops; fold/defend cautiously to large 3-bets (fold to big shove 3-bets, consider calling small 3-bets depending on sizing and opponent).

K2o is a very weak offsuit hand from the button facing an early position raise from Minni to 300 into a 450 pot. While button position allows a wide calling range, K2o has poor playability, is easily dominated by better kings, and has low equity against a typical EP opening range which includes pairs, suited aces, broadways, etc. Minni has been active but this spot doesn't justify entering with trash. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities in a tournament setting with 68 hands left.

I am in the Small Blind with 7h 2h. While it is a suited hand, it is mathematically one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. Minni has raised from early position (UTG+1), which typically signals a strong range. Calling would put me out of position for the rest of the hand against a strong range with very poor equity and playability. Given the circumstances, folding is the most disciplined and standard play.

As9s is a solid hand in the BB. Getting good pot odds (200 to win 450) with a suited ace that has nut flush potential. Minni has been active with raises. Calling is standard here - 3-betting is also reasonable but calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me play position postflop.

Flopped the nut flush draw with As9s on 7s-Ks-5d. I have 9 clean outs to the nuts. Out of position, checking makes sense - if Minni bets I can call with excellent pot odds to hit my flush, or potentially check-raise as a semi-bluff. Checking to let the preflop aggressor continue is standard here.

I opened AdJh to 300 and got one caller from the BB. Flop K♠7♠5♦: I have only Ace-high but I am the preflop aggressor and my A♦ is a useful blocker to many Ax hands. Claude’s BB calling range is wide, so a standard continuation bet should fold many worse hands and charge spade draws. I’ll make a ~50% pot c-bet (650 pot → bet 325). If Claude thin-calls I’ll reevaluate on the turn; if he check-raises big I’ll assume strong Kx/sets and fold (I have no pair), while if he folds I take the pot.

Nut flush draw with As9s on 7s-Ks-5d. I have 9 outs to the nut flush. Pot is 1300 with 325 to call - getting 4:1 pot odds which is close to my ~19% turn equity. With implied odds when I hit the nuts, this is an easy call. Check-raising is possible as a semi-bluff but calling keeps things simple out of position.

Turn paired the board with Kc. I still have the nut flush draw (9 outs, ~18% to hit on river). The paired board is concerning since a flush would lose to a full house. Checking is standard here - if Minni bets, I'll evaluate pot odds. If checked through, I get a free card.

Turn pairs the board with K♣, which makes Claude’s calling range on the flop (Kx, pocket pairs, spade draws, some weak Ax/ broadways) much stronger. I hold A♦J♥ — only Ace-high with an Ace blocker but no spade and no pair. Betting the turn would be a pure double-barrel bluff that is unlikely to fold the Kx and will put me in a tough spot vs a check‑raise or a call; I don’t want to inflate a 1,300-chip pot with a marginal hand out of position. I check to control the pot and see a river. Plan: if Claude bets a small amount on the river I will call for pot odds or showdown value; if he fires a large river bet I will fold. If he checks the river I can consider a thin value bluff on certain runouts, but for now pot control is optimal.

Board is 7s-Ks-5d-Kc-Kd - three Kings! My As9s gives me trip Kings with Ace-high kicker. Only a King in Minni's hand beats me (quads). Minni bet flop but checked turn, suggesting likely no King. With Ace kicker, I have a strong hand and should bet for thin value - most hands without a King will call expecting a chop or hoping I'm bluffing. Small sizing to get called by worse kickers.

Final board: 7♠ K♠ 5♦ K♣ K♦ — trip Kings on board. I hold A♦J♥ (ace-high only). Pot = 1750, to call = 450 => pot odds = 450 / (1750+450) ≈ 20%. Claude’s small 450 river bet into this pot is consistent with thin value (trying to get called by Ax/A-high) or a blocker/size bet — but it can also include pure bluffs. My hand has very little showdown value vs a value-heavy river betting range (any King in hand is now quads, pairs like 7s/5s make full houses), but the price to see showdown is cheap and I block some Ax combos. Raising as a bluff is too risky (I have effectively no real fold equity vs many hands that beat me and would only get value-called by better hands); folding surrenders correct pot odds. Therefore I call the 450 to see showdown.

AKo UTG is a premium hand — I want to take the lead, build the pot with most worse hands, and reduce the chance of a multiway pot. Table is short-handed and many players open-raise to ~300, but I prefer a slightly larger isolation size to pressure the short-stacked BB and discourage limp-calling. Raising to 600 (6x BB) gives good fold equity while preserving plenty of stack depth to handle a 3-bet or play postflop; I can comfortably call a standard 3-bet or 4-bet shove if the dynamics demand it. Folding or flat-calling 100 is too passive with this hand, so I raise to seize initiative and extract value.

Pocket tens is a premium hand (TT), top ~6% of hands, strong enough to 3-bet facing an UTG open from Minni. Her UTG raise to 600 (6bb) indicates a strong range, but TT has excellent equity against it, dominates pairs below TT, and performs well multiway or heads-up. Calling OOP from early position risks difficult postflop spots with implied odds concerns given stacks. 3-betting isolates, builds the pot, denies equity to worse hands, and leverages fold equity. Sizing to 2100 (~3.5x her raise) is standard aggressive size into the 750 pot, leaving room for further action while pressuring her stack (105bb) and shorter BB (48bb). Effective stacks support this; my 63bb covers it well. History shows Minni raises wide UTG but folds to pressure often.

I am on the button with Pocket Jacks, a premium hand in a 5-handed game. Minni (UTG) opened for 6x, and Elon (UTG+1) 3-bet to 2,100. Elon is quite short with only 4,225 total. This 3-bet effectively commits half of his stack. I want to isolate Elon and put pressure on Minni, who has a 10,500 stack. By raising to 5,000, I put Elon all-in and force Minni to decide if she wants to play for a large portion of her stack. JJ is too strong to fold here, and flatting allows Minni to potentially see a cheap flop or 4-bet shove over me. I will raise to isolate.

Facing a 4-bet pot with A6o. Minni opened, Elon 3-bet, Flash 4-bet to 5000. A6 offsuit is a weak hand against a 4-bet range - dominated by AK/AQ/AJ and crushed by big pairs. Even with decent pot odds (~1.6:1), this is a clear fold as I'll be in terrible shape against Flash's 4-betting range.

We’re facing a UTG open, a 3-bet from Elon (who is effectively committed with only 4225 total), and then a cold 4-bet to 5000 from Flash. That action heavily concentrates ranges toward strong hands (big pairs, AK/AQ), and Kh8h is dominated very often and performs poorly multiway. Even though the pot is large, calling would be for our entire stack and would require roughly ~38% equity, which we won’t have against these ranges. Preserving our stack and avoiding a high-variance punt is best here.

Sequence: I opened to 600 UTG, Elon 3-bet to 2,100 and Flash 4-bet to 5,000. With AdKs (AKo) this is too strong to fold but I don’t want to commit the bulk of my stack blindly. A 5‑bet shove or a huge re‑raise (to 7,900) will either get called by only the absolute nuts (AA/KK) or induce a jam that I’m frequently flipping or behind in — that’s too high variance and likely lower EV long‑term. Calling to 5,000 keeps the pot under control, preserves post‑flop options, and extracts value when Flash’s 4‑bet range contains bluffs (button polarised 4‑bets are often mixed). Practical numbers: pot is 7,850; calling 4,400 more makes the pot ~12,250, so break‑even equity is ~36% — AKo usually has sufficient equity against a polarized 4‑bet range, and Elon is short enough he may just shove (creating a side pot) which I can handle. Plan: call, see the flop, and fold to overbearing action on very unfavorable boards; continue aggressively when I hit or get favorable runouts.

Pocket Tens was strong for the initial 3-bet over Minni's UTG open, but Flash's massive button 4-bet squeeze to 5000 (50bb) and Minni's flat call committing half her stack (now 6100 behind) drastically narrows their ranges to premiums like QQ+, KK, AA, AKo/s. TT has poor equity (~20-30% multiway) against this and plays terribly postflop OOP first to act (Minni acts before me), with only 1325 left (13bb) after calling 2900 more into a 15k pot. Shoving all-in (to 6325 total) risks calls from better hands with minimal fold equity given Minni's commitment; she likely snaps. Calling leaves a crippled stack in a bloated pot multiway. Folding preserves 42bb stack for better spots in a long tournament (67 hands left). Opponents' history shows Flash aggressive on button but Minni's call is too strong to continue profitably.

I opened preflop and called Flash’s 4‑bet with A♦K♠ to see a flop. The board T♦‑3♣‑9♣ leaves me with two overcards but no pair and no club draw — I have very little immediate equity against a polarized 4‑bet/call range that contains lots of pocket pairs and strong Ax/Kx. The pot is already large (~12,250) while my effective stack is relatively shallow (6,100), so leading or building the pot here risks committing a big portion of my stack when I’m usually behind. I’ll check to control the pot and realize equity: if Flash checks back I get a free turn to improve; if he makes a small c‑bet I can call and continue to the turn with the option to fold to heavy pressure; if he jams or posts a large bet I’ll fold this texture without improvement. Checking preserves stack, avoids marginal all‑in spots, and keeps later options when/if I pair up.

On a flop of Td-3c-9c, I have an overpair with Pocket Jacks. The pot is 12,250 and Minni only has 6,100 remaining, which makes the stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) approximately 0.5. Given the low SPR and the presence of two clubs on the board, I want to commit Minni's remaining chips now to maximize value and protect my hand from overcards or draws. Checking back would give her a free card that could easily beat me. I will bet enough to put her all-in.

I checked the T♦‑3♣‑9♣ flop with A♦K♠ to control the pot and realize equity. Flash (button) 4‑bet preflop and now bets 6,100 — exactly my remaining stack — so calling would commit me all‑in. Pot is 18,350, so a call requires ~25% equity (6,100 / (18,350+6,100) ≈ 25%). On this texture I have two overcards but no pair and no club draw; my equity versus a 4‑better’s likely, value‑heavy range (AA/KK/QQ, some JJ/TT, AK combos and occasional bluffs) is at or below that threshold. A shove/raise is just an all‑in, high variance, and unlikely to fold out enough of his value combos. Preserving a 6,100 stack in a five‑handed tournament gives more future leverage than making a marginal, likely negative‑EV call here. Per my preflop plan to fold to heavy pressure on unfavorable boards, I’ll fold and wait for a better spot.

Preflop UTG in 5-handed play with J9s suited connectors. This hand has good playability postflop due to suitedness and connectivity, capable of making straights, flushes, and two-pair. In short-handed tournament play (42BB effective stack), UTG opening range includes J9s as it's within the top ~20% of hands. No action before me, pot 150, blinds 50/100. Standard open-raise size around 2.5x BB. Raising to 250 builds the pot, takes initiative, and denies equity to weaker hands while folding out junk. Opponents behind include deep Flash but also shorter stacks; history shows frequent aggression but also folds to opens. Folding too tight misses value in dynamic spot. Calling limps UTG OOP is suboptimal. Raise for value/protection.

I am holding King-Seven offsuit (K7o) in UTG+1. Elon, the UTG player, has opened with a raise to 250. K7o is a weak hand that is easily dominated by Elon's UTG opening range (which typically includes stronger Kings and pocket pairs). Although I am the chip leader with over 300 big blinds, there is no strategic reason to call or 3-bet with a marginal hand against an early position raise, especially when the opener has a relatively short stack of ~40BB and might be looking for a spot to commit his chips. I will play it safe and fold.

As Tc on the button facing a UTG raise to 250. This is a solid hand in position - AT offsuit plays well. I'll 3-bet to around 750 to isolate Elon and put pressure on the blinds. With position and a decent holding, this is a standard play to either take it down preflop or build a pot with positional advantage.

We’re getting a reasonable price (700 to win 1150 now) and 66 plays well in a pot that can go multiway since Elon is likely continuing often after opening. A 4-bet with 66 is unattractive: we don’t block Claude’s strong continuing range, and when called we’re usually in rough shape OOP. Folding feels too tight given the immediate odds and the chance to realize equity by flopping a set or navigating low/connected boards at a manageable SPR.

Facing an UTG open to 250 and a button 3-bet to 750 with the small blind cold-calling, I'm out of position in a multiway pot holding Qs9c — a marginal, often-dominated offsuit hand. The pot is 1,850 and it costs me 650 to call (about 26% pot odds), but against two callers/raisers my equity is well below that and multiway play OOP is difficult to realize. A small 4-bet to 1,250 would be an ineffective bluff here and shoving is too polar with this holding, so conserving my 6,000 stack for better spots is the prudent choice. Fold and wait for a clearer opportunity.

Facing a button 3-bet to 750 after my UTG open to 250, with SB FiveTwo flat-calling 700 behind and BB folding. Pot is 1850, calling 500 OOP in a multiway pot. J9s suited connectors have playability but are marginal vs Claude's wide button 3-bet range (Ax, broadway, suited gappers, bluffs) and FiveTwo's flat-call suggesting medium strength (pairs, suited broadway). Postflop OOP multiway reduces realized equity significantly; J9s plays poorly without initiative. Stack ~40BB, no need to risk 12.5% of stack for ~21% pot odds needed, as equity likely <30% combined and high variance. Folding preserves stack for better spots; history shows Claude aggressive but this squeeze spot is standard fold for UTG range.

On A♠A♣4♠ in a 3-bet pot, our 66 has limited equity and almost no ability to improve, while Claude’s range contains a lot of Ax and higher pairs that can comfortably continue. Leading doesn’t achieve much (we rarely fold out better, and worse hands won’t pay often), so the best line is to keep the pot controlled, check, and then respond based on sizing—potentially defend versus a very small range-bet, but be ready to fold to meaningful pressure given the low SPR.

I flopped trip aces with As Tc on Ah 4s Ac board - a monster hand. FiveTwo checked to me. I need to bet for value here. With the pot at 1850, I'll bet around 40-50% pot to extract value from any pocket pairs, smaller aces, or draws while not scaring them away completely.

We actually have real showdown value here: on A♣A♥4♠ our 66 makes two pair (Aces and Sixes), which beats all of Claude’s non-A broadway cbets and any smaller pocket pairs he might range-bet. Claude’s 800 into 2650 is a small, likely range-oriented c-bet; folding would be too tight given price and how many hands he can be betting once (KQ/KJ/QJ, etc.). Raising is unattractive because his continue/jam range is heavily Ax and higher pairs (77+), where we’re in bad shape, and we’d just fold out the bluffs we want to keep. Best is to call and re-evaluate turns—comfortable checking down if he slows down, and willing to fold later to big polar pressure.

Turn SPR is < 1 (we have 3275 into a 3450 pot). Our 66 is now two pair (Aces and Sixes), which has decent showdown value versus Claude’s c-bet bluffs and some underpairs, but it’s in bad shape versus his value range (any Ax, 77+, 44/55). Leading risks value-owning ourselves and/or getting jammed off our equity by exactly the hands that beat us, while folding out the bluffs we want to keep. Best is to check, let him continue with bluffs at some frequency, and then make a price-sensitive decision versus his sizing (call small, fold to big/polar).

I have trip aces with As Tc on Ah 4s Ac 5d. FiveTwo called my 800 flop bet and checked the turn again. With 3450 in the pot and FiveTwo having only 3275 behind, I should size my bet to set up a river all-in. Betting around 1500 leaves them with ~1775 for the river, which I can extract if they're sticking around with a pocket pair or weaker ace. This sizing also gives me value while not overcommitting if they somehow have a boat or quads.

We have two pair (A♠A♣ + 6♠6♥ with 5 kicker), which is a reasonable bluff-catcher on this paired board. Claude’s flop sizing was very small and his turn bet (1500 into 4950) still gives us strong pot odds: we’re calling 1500 to win 6450 (~23%), and even if he’s value-heavy we retain a little equity plus we beat all his non-Ax double-barrel bluffs and any smaller pairs he’s using to apply pressure.

Raising is bad here because his continue range (any Ax, overpairs like 77+/big pairs, 44/55) crushes us, and we’d fold out exactly the hands we want to keep betting (KQ/KJ/QJ-type air). Folding is too tight given the price and the likelihood he can be barreling to push out our mid pairs. Call and re-evaluate river (often forced by price if he jams small, but we can still fold to certain runouts/lines).

River is a terrible spot to value-bet: our hand is AA66K (two pair via board aces + our sixes), which only really beats pure air and tiny underpairs. Any Ax, any Kx, and most pocket pairs 77+ beat us, and those are exactly the hands Claude can continue with after betting flop/turn. If we bet (or jam our 1775), we mostly get called by better and fold out the hands we want to keep in (bluffs), so we’d be value-owning ourselves.

Checking maximizes EV: we realize our showdown value when he checks back his give-ups/medium hands, and we keep the door open for him to bluff a very small river shove sizing (1775 into 6450) with missed broadways. We can then make a price-based bluff-catch decision if he bets.

I have trip aces with As Tc on Ah 4s Ac 5d Kh. FiveTwo has been check-calling my bets on flop and turn, and now checks river with only 1775 behind. The King is a great card as it could have improved some of their range (KX hands). They likely have a pocket pair or a weaker ace. I should bet to put them all-in and maximize value from my strong hand. Only quads or a full house beats me, and those are unlikely given the action.

River: Claude bets 1775 into a 6450 pot, putting us all-in. Calling 1775 to win a final 10,000 means we only need ~17.8% equity. Our hand (AA66K) is a pure bluff-catcher: we lose to any Ax, most Kx, and any pocket pair >66, but we beat all his unpaired broadway floats/misses (QJ/JT/QT-type) that c-bet small and can double/triple when we look capped after calling twice. Given the price and that this sizing is very attractive for him to bluff with (risk 1775 to win 6450), folding is too exploitable. We can’t raise for value, so we take the pot-odds call.
4c7c is a weak suited connector that plays poorly from UTG. Not a hand to open from early position - easy fold.

Button with T7o in a 4-handed game — marginal hand but strong positional advantage and good opportunity to steal the blinds. Pot is 150 and calling 100 is just calling into two players; better to take the initiative. Table open-sizing has often been ~300; a 3x BB open gives solid fold equity vs the blinds and leaves us plenty of postflop maneuvering room with 60BB effective. If Flash (deep) 3-bets big I'll fold or re-evaluate; vs a smaller defense from Elon (SB) I can proceed in position. Raise to a standard 300 to apply pressure and play the pot in position.

Pre-flop in SB with JTo offsuit facing a button raise to 300 (standard 3x open). Button ranges are wide in late position, especially with multiple folds before. JTo has good playability, decent equity (around 40-45% vs wide button opening range), and live cards. Pot odds are favorable: calling an additional 250 into a 450 pot offers about 2.8:1, and post-flop we have position on BB and can realize equity well despite SB position challenges. My stack of 3925 (~39BB) is not desperate, so calling keeps the pot small and allows seeing flop cheaply. Folding is too tight given the spot; raising risks commitment with offsuit broadway. Plan to play post-flop aggressively if we hit.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Ten offsuit. The Button (Minni) raised to 300 and the Small Blind (Elon) called. I only need to put in 200 more to call into a pot of 700, giving me 3.5:1 pot odds. KTo is well above the defending range in a 4-handed game against a button open. While both opponents are short-stacked, calling to see a flop is the most efficient play to leverage my massive chip lead without unnecessary pre-flop variance.

Flop is 4h-8c-6d, rainbow with some straight draw potential (5-7 open-ender, 7-9 gutshot). JTo offsuit hits nothing: no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw (overcards with backdoor potential). Pure air but live cards. As SB first to act OOP vs button raiser (wide range) and BB defender, betting risks raising villain's fold equity or getting called/floated easily. Checking allows pot control, realizes fold equity if villain cbets (Minni cbets frequently per history), and preserves stack (39BB effective). Plan: check, fold to bets unless small cbet with backdoor equity justifies float. Folding pre was tight; this keeps us in line with calling range.

I have King-high with two overcards on a low, somewhat connected board (4-8-6). Elon has checked from the Small Blind. Given that Minni was the pre-flop aggressor and is yet to act, checking is the most standard and disciplined play. My hand has some showdown value and potential to improve, but it is not strong enough to bet for value, and a bluff here is unlikely to be effective against two opponents on this texture. I will check and see how the Button reacts.

I was the preflop aggressor from the button with Tc7s and the flop is 4h-8c-6d. I have no pair but a double‐gutshot (5 or 9) — 8 outs, ≈31% to make a straight by the river — so this is a strong semi-bluffing candidate. Both opponents checked, showing weakness; with position and decent equity I should apply pressure to fold missed overcards and extract value from weaker draws/small pairs. Because it’s multiway I size down to ~1/3 pot to maximize fold equity while keeping worse hands and draws interested. Bet 300 into the 900 pot; if called I’ll continue with equity and re-evaluate on the turn. If I face a large raise (especially from the deep-stacked Flash) I will fold to avoid getting stacked with only a draw; vs a small raise from Elon I can consider calling to realize equity in position.

Flop is 4h-8c-6d, a coordinated board with straight draws (5-7 open-ender, 7-9/3-5/5-9 gutshots) and pair potential, favoring Minni's wide button continuation range. JTo completely misses: no pair, no straight draw (10-J needs 9-Q or Q-K), weak backdoor heart draw with Jh. Multiway pot (3 players) OOP as SB after checks, facing 300 (1/3 pot cbet into 900 side pot). Calling commits ~8% of stack for marginal equity vs Minni's polarized range (value: 66+, sets; bluffs: Ax suited, broadways), but poor implied odds OOP vs two opponents (Flash may float/call loosely per history). Folding preserves 37BB stack for better spots; consistent with pre-flop calling plan to fold to cbets without improvement. Raising is a bluff with no fold equity multiway.

I am heads-up against the Button (Minni) on a board of 4h-8c-6d. Minni bet 300 into a pot of 900 after I and Elon checked. Elon has folded. I am receiving 5:1 pot odds (1500 to call 300), which means I only need about 17% equity to continue. My King-high with a Ten kicker has overcard value and may even be the best hand currently if Minni is continuation betting with air or lower overcards like QJ. Given the price and the fact that I have a massive stack to absorb this small bet, calling to see a turn card is the most mathematically sound decision.

On the Turn, the board is 4h-8c-6d-9d. My King-high with a Ten kicker has not improved to a pair, but I have picked up a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7 to complete a 6-7-8-9-T straight). The 9 is a card that interacts heavily with a lot of middle-card ranges that the Button might hold. Given that Minni was the pre-flop raiser and the flop aggressor, I will check to her. Leading out here would likely only be called by better hands or semi-bluffs that have me in a difficult spot. By checking, I can see how she sizes her next bet and decide if the price is right to chase my gutshot or if I should surrender the hand.

The turn 9d completes a 6-7-8-9-10 straight for my T7. Flash called my flop c-bet and then checked the turn, which shows caution and leaves him with many one-pair hands (8x/6x/9x/4x), medium pockets, or speculative holdings that will happily call a value bet. This is a clear value-betting spot — I want to charge those hands without folding them out. Pot is 1,500, so I size to ~60% pot (900) to extract calls from pairs and worse made hands while keeping worse hands and some draws in. If Flash check-raises big it becomes a polarized line (likely a set or rare bluff) and I’ll re-evaluate — call small/medium raises, but treat an overbet shove as highly suspect for a set and fold to preserve my stack.

On the turn, the board reads 4h-8c-6d-9d. I am holding King-Ten offsuit, which gives me King-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7 to complete a 6-7-8-9-T straight). Minni, the Button, has bet 900 into a pot of 1500, which is a significant sizing (60% pot) and represents about 20% of her remaining stack. This level of aggression on a highly connected board suggests she has at least a pair or a strong draw. My King-high has very little showdown value here, and while I have 4 outs for a straight, 9% equity is not enough to justify a call of 900 to win a total pot of 3300 (requiring ~21% equity), especially since her short stack limits my implied odds if I do hit. Furthermore, on this texture, my overcard outs (King or Ten) are not 'clean' as they could easily give her two pair or a straight if she holds cards like 7-5 or 8-9. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my chip lead.

AdKs UTG in a 4-max game is a premium hand and worth opening. With a healthy 69BB stack I want to take the initiative and build the pot while retaining fold equity — I’ll open to a standard 3x (300) rather than the min-raise (200) which invites multiway action and gives less fold equity. 300 is consistent with table sizing, isolates the blinds, and gives a comfortable SPR to play post‑flop. If I face a 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate by sizing and opponent: I may 4-bet or call vs a light 3-bet from the shorter-stacked Button, and play more cautiously vs a large 3-bet from the very deep SB.

46d is a suited connector with straight and flush potential. Position on the button is excellent for realizing equity post-flop. Minni's UTG raise indicates strength, but getting 2.5:1 on the call with implied odds against Minni's 66BB stack. Multi-way pot likely with blinds, but position allows control. 37BB stack affords speculation without overcommitting. Raising is too strong; fold too tight given playability.

I am in the Small Blind with 2d 4c, which is an extremely weak hand. Minni has raised from UTG and Elon has called from the Button. Even with 4:1 pot odds to call, this hand has very poor playability post-flop and I would be playing out of position against two opponents. Folding is the most disciplined and correct play to preserve my large stack.

I have 5c3c suited in the BB. Getting 3.75:1 pot odds (200 to win 750) and closing the action. Suited connectors have decent implied odds if I hit a straight or flush. With only 200 more to call and good pot odds, this is a profitable call.

I completely missed this flop with 5c3c. Board is 8h 4s Kh - no club flush draw, and I only have a weak gutshot (3-4-5 connected, need 2+6 or A+2 for a straight). With two opponents and a K-high board that likely connects with someone's range, checking is the only reasonable play. No reason to bluff into a multiway pot with air.

I hold AdKs — top pair with the best kicker on K♣? (board K♥ 8♥ 4♠). This is a three-way pot (two callers), so I want value and protection: there are possible heart combos/draws and multiple opponents reduce my equity if I check. A half‑pot c‑bet charges draws and extracts from worse Kx and broadway/medium pairs while keeping the pot controllable against Claude’s deep stack. Bet ~½ pot to deny equity and build value; if I face a large raise I’ll re-evaluate (fold to a very strong raise, call smaller/short‑stack aggression and proceed cautiously on the turn).

Bottom pair with 4d6d on a Kh-8h-4s flop. Minni, the UTG preflop raiser, continuation bets into two opponents after BB checks, representing a strong range including top pair (Kx), overpairs, sets, and draws. My pair of 4s is the bottom pair, highly vulnerable to any king, 8, or better 4. No strong draw (gutshot unlikely with 6d, backdoor diamond flush). Pot odds ~3:1 (1425 pot + 475 call), but reverse implied odds are terrible—unlikely to get paid if I hit two pair or better, as board is drawy (hearts flush draw possible). Claude's check suggests weakness. With position, calling keeps pot small, but folding preserves 34BB stack (3375 chips) for better spots in tournament (hand 86/150). Minni's 6125 stack limits fold equity if raising, and my hand doesn't play well multiway. Standard fold to avoid difficult turn decisions.

I have 5c3c on 8h4sKh. I missed completely - no flush draw (wrong suit), only a weak gutshot (need a 2 or 6 for a straight). Pot is 1425, need 475 to call = 25% pot odds. A gutshot is only ~8% to hit on the turn, so I'm not getting the right price. Easy fold.

Q2s is a speculative suited hand but extremely weak from UTG in a 4-handed tournament with 33BB stack. Poor playability multiway, dominated by better Qx or suited hands frequently. No fold equity worth raising, limping invites trouble. Opponents have deep stacks except Minni at 74BB, but still, folding maximizes EV long-term by avoiding marginal spots early position.

I am on the button with Jh9c. This is a standard opening hand from the button in a 4-handed game, especially when the small blind and big blind are the most active players. Opening here allows me to take control of the pot and potentially take it down pre-flop. Given the stack sizes, I'll raise to 3x the big blind (300).

Q2s in the SB facing a button open of 3x. This is a marginal hand that doesn't play well postflop - dominated by better Qx hands and poor connectivity. Getting 1.8:1 odds but will be out of position against the button. Fold is the correct play here.

I’m BB with 3♣8♥ — a very poor, unplayable hand out of position. Flash opened to 300; pot is 450 and it costs me 200 to call (total commit would be 300). The break‑even equity for a call is 200/(450+200) ≈ 30.8%, and 3‑8o has well under that vs a normal button opening range and performs poorly OOP with no useful blockers. A min‑3bet to 500 is possible but thin and likely to be exploited by Flash’s large stack. Preserve the 7,450 chip stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am UTG in a 4-handed game with 8s 3s. While suited, this hand is far too weak to open from early position, especially with a short-stacked Big Blind (Elon) who might look for a spot to shove. I will fold and wait for a more profitable situation.

On the button with 76s - a decent speculative hand. Flash folded, so only the blinds remain. Good spot to raise and either steal blinds or see a flop in position with a hand that can make straights and two pairs. Standard 2.5x open to 250.

I’m in the small blind with K♠2♥ facing a BTN open to 250 (pot 400, I must call 200). K2o is a marginal hand — a lot of its equity is threatened by domination (Kx and Ax in opener’s range) and I’ll be out of position postflop versus the button. There’s also a 32BB big blind (Elon) who can call or shove, which would put me in uncomfortable spots with a weak kicker. 3-betting K2o is too thin — it’s a bluff that’s easy to exploit and could get me into a multiway pot or a shove I don’t want to call. With a healthy 74BB stack I can wait for a better spot or a hand that plays well OOP. Fold and preserve stack equity.

85o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability, especially out of position in the big blind against a button raise from Claude, who has been aggressive in recent hands. The pot odds are about 2.67:1 (150 to call for 550 total pot including call), but equity against Claude's wide button range is insufficient postflop OOP, where I can easily get dominated or outdrawn. My stack is 32.75BB, so preserving chips for better spots is key in this tournament stage (hand 88/150). Folding is standard and disciplined.

J8o from UTG in a 4-handed game is too weak to open. While I have a healthy stack, this hand plays poorly from early position against 3 opponents still to act, including the big stack Flash. Standard fold.

I’m on the button with 2♥6♥ (suited but very weak). Folds to me, pot 150, to-call 100. This is a standard steal spot — I want to leverage position and get the blinds to fold rather than limp/passively allow the small blind to isolate. Raising to 300 (≈3x BB) gives good fold equity while keeping my risk small; if I face a sizable 3-bet or shove from the SB (who has ~32bb) I’ll fold this marginal hand. If called I can proceed postflop in position and use the backdoor heart/straight potential.

Q6o is a marginal hand from the small blind facing a button raise to 3x. Button ranges are wide, but calling OOP with this hand is unprofitable long-term due to poor playability, reverse position, and lack of strong equity. Pot odds are about 1.8:1 (calling 250 to win 450), requiring ~35% equity, which Q6o doesn't consistently achieve against even a loose button range (~30-35% at best). My stack is 32BB, so preserving chips for better spots. Folding is standard and disciplined.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Trey offsuit. Minni, on the Button, has raised to 300. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 more to win a total pot of 450), K3o is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position. Minni is a competent player and although she is opening from the button, K3o is easily dominated by her calling and raising range. Given that there are 4 players remaining and I have a significant stack lead, I prefer to avoid marginal situations out of position with weak holdings. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

UTG with K♠4♠ (Ks4s) in a 4-handed game with stacks of ~75bb (me), ~32bb (BTN), ~316bb (SB) and ~174bb (BB). With only the blinds in and To Call = 100, K4s is a marginal UTG hand: often dominated by other Kx and has poor kicker/playability postflop. Opening invites squeezes and 3-bets from very deep players (Flash/Claude) and a possible shove/3-bet from the shorter button — all situations where K4s will be in trouble. Limping is passive and will get me into multiway pots out of position. Prefer to preserve my healthy stack and wait for a better spot in position, so fold here.

Folded to me on the button with 9h8h, a suited connector that plays very well postflop in position. Blinds are deep but I have 32BB effective. Standard RFI (raise first in) from button includes 98s for fold equity on blinds and initiative. Raise to 3x BB (300 total) is a standard sizing to build pot or take it down preflop. Avoid limping as it invites multiway pots OOP; raising maximizes value and fold equity.

I am in the Small Blind with T2o. The action has folded to Elon on the Button who has raised to 300. Elon has a very short stack of 2925 chips (less than 30 big blinds). T2o is one of the worst starting hands in poker and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. Given Elon's short stack, a 3-bet would likely commit me to a pot where he might shove, and calling would put me in a difficult spot out of position with a marginal hand. The standard and most profitable play here is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Holding 9c6c in the BB facing a button raise to 300. Suited connector with reasonable playability. Getting 2.25:1 pot odds (200 to win 450). Elon is short-stacked (2925), limiting implied odds but also limiting my risk. Price is reasonable to defend with this suited connector.
Completely whiffed this flop with 9c6c on Kd-Qh-2d. No pair, no flush draw, no straight draw. Best play is to check and likely fold to any bet from Elon.

Preflop raise with 98h suited from button was standard. Flop 2d Kh Qs completely misses my hand; no pair, no straight draw, no flush draw. However, heads-up vs BB caller in position on this dry, top-heavy rainbow board, continuation betting is highly profitable. My button opening range has good equity realization and includes many overcards, broadways, and Ax that hit this board (AK, KQ, AQ, KJs, etc.), while villain's BB calling range is capped somewhat (weaker Kx, Qx, pairs, suited connectors). C-bet to deny equity, charge draws/floats, or extract value from worse. Half-pot sizing (325 into 650) balances value and bluff, induces folds from medium strength, gets called by worse if he has it. Checking folds out too much equity OOP for him and gives free card. With 29BB effective, can't afford passive play.
Missed completely with 9c6c on K-Q-2 board. No pair, no draws. Elon is short-stacked (~2600 behind) making bluffs/floats pointless as he's nearly committed. Easy fold.

QTo offsuit is a marginal hand at best from UTG in a 4-handed game. While ranges widen short-handed, offsuit broadway without suited or connected value is not strong enough to open voluntarily preflop given the deep effective stacks behind (especially Flash on button with massive stack). Folding preserves stack for better spots; limping is exploitable, and raising risks playing out of position against strong calling ranges. Standard play is to fold and wait for premium hands.

I am currently the chip leader and holding Queen-Deuce offsuit on the button. While I am in a prime position to steal the blinds, Q2o is a very weak starting hand and does not play well post-flop if called by either the Small Blind (Claude) or the Big Blind (Minni). Since I am looking to preserve my stack and avoid unnecessary marginal spots, I will fold this hand and wait for a better opportunity.

6s7s is a decent suited connector heads up against the BB. With Elon and Flash folding, I should raise to put pressure on Minni and potentially take down the pot. Suited connectors have good playability postflop if called. Standard raise to 250.

I have J♣8♦ in the big blind facing Claude’s raise to 250 (pot 350); to call is 150. J8o is marginal but defendable — pot odds require ~30% equity (150/(350+150)=30%) and stacks are deep enough to realize implied odds. Claude opens a lot and often plays postflop aggressively, so I prefer to keep the pot manageable and see a flop rather than 3-bet out of position with a speculative hand. Plan: call 150, fold to a large 3-bet or shove, and proceed cautiously OOP on the flop (check/call small C-bets, look to continue only with real equity or strong reads).

I have 6s7s on a 5h Jh Th board - three hearts and I have no heart. I have no flush draw and would need runner-runner 8-9 for a straight. This is a terrible flop for my hand. C-betting as a pure bluff on a monotone board is risky since any opponent with a single heart will continue. Checking and giving up is the prudent play here.

I have top pair with a weak kicker (J♣8♦) on a very wet flop: 5♥ J♥ T♥. This board is dangerous — three hearts mean many made flush combos, Q9 type hands have straight outs, and tens or fives give two pair. Claude was the preflop raiser and checked the flop, which can be either weakness or a trap. Out of position and with a mediocre kicker, I don’t want to bloat the pot into someone’s flush or two‑pair/straight. Checking controls the pot, keeps his checking range wide, and lets me call reasonable bets as a showdown catcher while folding to strong aggression. Plan: check and evaluate the turn — call small-to-medium bets for pot control and fold to large pressure; only get aggressive if I improve or get a clear read.

The 9d gives me an open-ended straight draw - any 8 gives me a 6-7-8-9-T straight. I have 8 outs (~17% equity to hit on river). However, the board still has 3 hearts and Minni could have a flush. With position and a free card available, checking is optimal - I can see the river cheaply and bet if I hit, or fold to aggression if I miss.

I have top pair (J♣8♦) but a very weak kicker on a dangerous 5♥ J♥ T♥ 9♦ board — there are many 3‑heart flushes, two‑pair combos and straights (KQ/Q8 etc) that beat or heavily threaten me. Claude was the preflop raiser but checked both flop and turn, which keeps his range wide (can be weak showdown hands or slowplayed strong hands). Pot is small relative to our stacks, so I want pot control and to avoid bloating the pot OOP with a marginal hand. Checking keeps the pot manageable, lets me call reasonable river bets as a showdown catcher, and fold to strong pressure if Claude shows aggression. Action: check and re‑evaluate on the river (call small/medium bets, fold to large shove unless I improve or get a very specific read).

River brought the Ah completing a 4-heart board. I missed my straight draw (needed an 8) and have zero showdown value with 7-high. Bluffing is very risky - anyone with a single heart now has a flush, and Minni has been checking throughout which could indicate a medium-strength heart waiting to trap. The pot is only 500 chips - not worth risking chips on a bluff that's unlikely to succeed when the board screams flush.

I have top pair (J♣8♦) on a very dangerous board: 5♥ J♥ T♥ 9♦ A♥. With four hearts out there, any single heart in Claude’s hand is a made flush; KQ (and similar Broadway combos) makes straights; holding a T/9/J/A gives two‑pair or trips. Claude raised preflop but checked flop/turn/river — that’s ambiguous (weakness or slowplay). The pot is small (500) relative to our stacks, so I want pot control OOP with a marginal kicker rather than bloating the pot and risking check‑raises or losing big to a flush/two‑pair/straight. Checking preserves showdown value; if Claude bets a small/medium amount I’ll call as a showdown catcher and fold to a large shove. Therefore: check.

Holding Ace-Queen offsuit (Ah Qs) under-the-gun in a four-handed game is a premium starting hand. My stack is the largest at the table, while my opponents have varying shorter stacks, particularly Elon with only about 35 big blinds. I will make a standard raise to 300 to build the pot and exert pressure on the blinds and button, as this hand performs very well in short-handed play.

As3o on the button facing UTG raise of 300. A3o is marginal but I have position. Flash raised from UTG which shows strength, but my button position gives me good post-flop playability. Calling to see a flop in position is reasonable - hand isn't strong enough to 3-bet for value but too strong to fold on the button.

UTG (Flash) opened to 300 and the button (Claude) just flat-called. I’m in the small blind with 4♦2♣ — an extremely weak, dominated holding with almost no playability out of position. Calling 250 into a 750 pot to play multiway OOP is a losing proposition here: my equity is very poor versus UTG’s opening range and any button calling range, and the big blind (Elon) can still squeeze or isolate. A 3‑bet bluff would be too risky against deep and competent opponents and would commit a lot of chips with no fold equity guaranteed. I’ll conserve my 7,750 stack and fold to wait for a better spot.

Q9o is a marginal offsuit hand in the BB facing an UTG raise from Flash (3x) and a button call from Claude. UTG range is strong (pairs, AK, AQ+, broadway), and Claude's call widens it slightly but still favors folds. Pot odds are decent (200 to call into 750, ~3.75:1), but equity is low (~25-30% vs ranges), poor playability postflop with reverse domination risk (vs KQ, AQ). Stack 35BB, better to preserve for stronger spots in late tournament stage. Standard fold.

I am the pre-flop aggressor in a heads-up pot against Claude. The flop of K-high, 9, 5 is quite dry and generally favors my range, which contains more strong Kings (AK, KQ) and big pocket pairs than the caller's range. Although my Ace-Queen hasn't hit the board, it remains a strong hand with significant backdoor potential. I will fire a standard continuation bet of 300 (40% of the pot) to represent a King and force Claude to fold his weaker holdings or air. If he calls, I will have more information about the strength of his hand and can decide whether to check-down or continue the bluff on later streets.

Flopped nothing with As3d on K95 rainbow. Flash c-bet small (300 into 750) after raising UTG. I have just ace high with no draws on a K-high board. Against an UTG raiser's c-bet range, my hand has very little equity. No reason to continue with complete air.

K8s from UTG is a borderline open. The suited king gives me some playability with flush potential and high card value. With a healthy stack of 167 BB and only 3 opponents remaining, I can open this hand with a standard raise. My position isn't great, but K8s is strong enough to open in a 4-handed game.

Fold. Q♦5♠ is a weak offsuit queen with a bad kicker and will be dominated by a UTG open in a 4‑handed game. Calling 250 into a 400 pot requires ~38.5% equity — far more than this hand will have vs Claude’s likely raising range — and a tiny 3‑bet to 400 is a poor play here (it invites 4‑bets from deeper stacks and puts me in marginal spots). With 77+ BB I should preserve chips and wait for a better spot in position.

Hand #93 pre-flop in SB with Kh6h, a suited king but gapped and weak kicker. Facing an UTG raise to 250 from Claude, who has been aggressive in history (frequent raises and post-flop action). My stack is 3425 (~34BB), not desperate. UTG range is strong (top ~15%: pairs 66+, AJo+, KQo+, suited broadways/AKs). K6s is not strong enough to defend OOP from SB, where post-flop playability is poor with reverse position to BB and initiative lost to raiser. Pot odds 200 into 600 (after call) are ~3:1 but equity vs range ~30-35%, plus reverse implied odds. Folding preserves stack for better spots late in tournament (hand 93/150).

I am holding 82o, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Although the pot odds are somewhat favorable (150 to call into a 400 pot), 82o has extremely poor playability and realization of equity post-flop, especially out of position against an UTG raise. Given my significant chip lead, there is no reason to defend the big blind with garbage and potentially get involved in a difficult spot post-flop.

Hand: UTG with T9o in a 4-handed game and ~77BB effective. In 4-max you should be opening much wider than full-ring — T9o is a good open to take the initiative and deny good odds to the blinds. I’ll open to a standard 3x the big blind (300) to build the pot, get fold equity, and isolate the shorter button if he wants to shove. Plan postflop: c‑bet favorable, play cautiously vs heavy action and fold to large 3‑bets/shoves from the big stacks unless equity is strong. Raise to 300 keeps options open and avoids a cheap multiway pot.

Preflop on the button facing an UTG raise to 300 into a 450 pot from Minni with 7450 stack. My hand is 7c4h, a complete trash hand with no high card value, no suitedness, poor connectivity, and dominated against any reasonable UTG raising range (which includes pairs, broadways, suited aces, etc.). Pot odds are about 1.5:1 but post-call it's worse, and I have no fold equity with a call. Even on the button, speculative hands need better playability or multiway potential, which this lacks. With my stack at 3425 (~34BB), preserving chips for premium spots in a tournament with 56 hands left is key. Standard fold to avoid spew.

Minni raised to 300 from UTG in a 4-handed game. Holding 9-8 offsuit in the Small Blind is a marginal situation. While the hand has some connectivity, playing out of position against an UTG open is generally unprofitable in the long run. Additionally, Claude is still to act in the Big Blind and has shown significant aggression throughout the tournament. Folding here preserves my chip lead and avoids a potentially difficult post-flop scenario with a weak hand.

54 offsuit in the BB facing a UTG raise. Getting 2.25:1 pot odds (200 to win 450), but 54o is too weak. Limited implied odds with this hand - it rarely makes strong enough hands to justify calling. Fold.

Jh7d is a trash hand: offsuit, gapped, no high card strength. UTG in a 4-handed game, playing this risks difficult post-flop situations out of position. My stack is 34BB, sufficient to wait for better spots. Standard fold to preserve stack and maximize EV over remaining hands.

I am on the button with 6s 2c, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. While the button is an ideal position for stealing blinds, this hand has almost no post-flop equity and the blinds (Claude and Minni) have shown they are willing to defend and play back. With a significant chip lead and 55 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no reason to waste chips on such a low-probability hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
Premium hand QQ in the small blind with only Minni (BB) left to act. Standard raise to build the pot and get value. Raising to 250-300 is appropriate sizing heads-up.

Call 200 more (300 total). K4o is marginal but I should defend the big blind vs a frequent SB 3x open — Claude has been opening to ~250–300 a lot, so his range is fairly wide. Pot is 400; calling 200 to make it 600 requires ~33.3% equity, which is borderline but acceptable versus a loose SB range. I’ll avoid a 3-bet with K4o (poor post‑flop playability and unnecessary risk against Claude’s deep stack). Plan: see the flop, proceed only on a favorable king/draw or check‑fold to heavy aggression on dry boards.

I have QQ as an overpair on a 5s Tc 8d board - excellent spot. No overcards to worry about, but there are some straight draw possibilities (97, J9). I should c-bet for value against worse pairs, tens, eights, and draws. Betting around 60% pot.

Claude opened from the SB a lot this level and now makes a small c‑bet (375 into a 975 pot) on a dry, rainbow T‑8‑5 board. Calling 375 to a pot that will be ~1350 gives ~28% pot‑odds, which is reasonable with K♥4♦ vs a wide SB c‑betting range — my king is an overcard and has decent showdown equity against many missed c‑bet combos. A raise as a bluff OOP is too risky and likely to be called by hands that beat me. Plan: call to see the turn, continue only if I pair the K or pick up clear equity; fold to significant continued aggression when I miss.

I have QQ overpair on 5s Tc 8d 7h. The 7h completes 96 for a straight, but most of Minni's flop calling range is still behind me - Tx, 8x, pair+draw combos. The board is getting coordinated but I should continue betting for value. Sizing around 60% pot to charge draws while getting value from worse made hands.

I defended the BB with K4o and called the flop to see a dry T‑8‑5 board. On the turn Claude now puts in a sizable bet (850 into a 2200 pot). I didn’t pair my king and have only 7 clean outs (3 kings + 4 sixes) ≈ 15% to improve on the river. Calling 850 to win 3050 requires ~28% equity, so this is a clear negative‑EV call. Raising as a bluff OOP with K4o is too risky — Claude’s turn sizing looks value‑heavy and I’d be dominated by Tx, pairs, two‑pair, or better. Per my earlier plan to fold to significant aggression when I miss, I’ll let this one go and preserve my stack for better spots.

In a 4-handed game, 9-5 offsuit is too weak to open from Under the Gun. While the table is short-handed, this hand lacks connectivity, high card value, and suit potential. Elon is short-stacked in the Big Blind and might look to shove over any raise with a wide range, making a bluff opening with trash hands less profitable. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

2h3c is one of the weakest hands in poker. Even on the button with a steal opportunity, this hand has no playability - no high card value, not suited, and the worst possible straight draw potential. Elon is short-stacked at 33BB and could 3-bet shove. Easy fold.

Heads-up preflop from the small blind with 2♣4♠. This is a very weak, poorly playable hand out of position, but the cost to see a flop is tiny (call 50 into a 150 pot — good pot odds). I don't want to raise with this rag since Elon (BB, 33BB) can 3-bet or shove and I'd be forced into tough spots. So I will call the 50 more to make my total in the pot 100, see a flop cheaply, and then proceed cautiously: fold to significant aggression unless I pair or pick up a real draw.

Preflop in the big blind facing a small blind limp after UTG and button folds. Heads-up situation with TdJd, which is a strong suited connector with excellent playability, straight and flush potential. Minni has limped SB, showing weakness or speculative hand. Raising from BB vs SB limp is standard to build the pot, take initiative, and realize equity postflop where we have position. Raising to 3x the BB (300 total) is appropriately sized given pot of 200 and stacks. My stack of 3325 (33BB) supports aggression here without overcommitting.

I’m in the small blind with 2♣4♠ — a very weak, easily dominated hand and I’ll be out of position postflop. Pot is 400 and Elon’s raise to 300 means I must call 200 more. That requires ~33% equity to break even (200/(400+200)). 2‑4o has well under that vs any realistic opening/3x range and even if I pair I’ll often be behind or in a very difficult OOP spot. I also already planned to fold to significant aggression. With a healthy 71BB stack there’s no reason to invest in a marginal, low-ev spot — preserve chips and wait for a better hand/position. Fold.
T6 suited from UTG is too weak to open. It's a gapped suited hand without good playability, and UTG requires a tighter range even 4-handed. Folding is the standard play here.

Button with J♥T♥ is a firm open-raise candidate: suited connector with good playability and position. Stack ~7,125 (~71BB) — plenty of room to pressure the blinds. SB (Elon) is mid-stack (~34BB) and BB (Flash) is very deep, so I want a standard sized open that gives fold equity but doesn’t overcommit if I face strength. Raising to 3x BB (300 total) is a standard, balanced sizing: it maximizes steal equity, keeps weaker hands and callers in, and preserves maneuverability post‑flop. Plan: c-bet favorable flops, fold to large, committed 3-bets (re‑evaluate against reads and pot odds), and play postflop for position if called. Therefore I raise to 300 total.

A3o is a marginal hand at best, offsuit with a weak kicker. Facing a button raise from Minni, who often has a wide range in late position. I'm out of position in the SB, making post-flop play difficult with poor playability—no flush or straight potential. Pot odds are about 1.8:1 (250 to call into 700 post-call pot), but equity realization OOP is low against her continuing range. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots late in tournament. No value in calling or 3-betting profitably.

I am in the Big Blind with Qd 8d, a decent suited semi-connector. Minni, who is on the button, has raised to 300. The stack sizes are quite shallow for some players, particularly Elon (3.4k) and Minni (6.8k). Calling 200 more into a pot that will be 650 offers good pot odds (3.25:1). Given my large stack of nearly 32k, I can comfortably defend the big blind here. Minni's button raise range is typically wide, and my hand plays well post-flop, especially in position if the small blind folds (which Elon already has). I will call to see a flop.

I'm in the Big Blind with Qd 8d. The flop is Ac 5c Js, and the pot is 650. Minni, who raised pre-flop, has not yet acted on this street as they are in position. I have no piece of this board—no pair, no straight draw, and no flush draw. The Ace and Jack are both very much in the pre-flop raiser's range. My best strategy here is to check and see what Minni does. If they bet, I will likely fold unless the bet is extremely small, as I have very little equity.

Board: 5♣ A♥ J♠ — I hold J♥T♥ (top pair, decent kicker) in position. The Ace on the flop heavily favors the big blind’s defending range (they called my 3x open), so this is a board where my range is somewhat capped and a c-bet will be called by or dominated by many Ax hands. I want pot control and to realize equity from worse hands while keeping my options open: check keeps the pot manageable, induces bluffs, and lets me call reasonable bets or fire later if checked to. If Flash checks back I can take a free turn card and potentially barrel when advantageous; if he leads I’ll call small/medium bets and fold to large committed aggression if the action indicates a made Ace or better. Action: check.

On the turn, the board is Ah Js 5c 7d. I am in the Big Blind with Qd 8d, giving me Queen-high and no direct draw. The flop checked through, which suggests that Minni (the pre-flop raiser on the button) likely does not have a strong Ace or might be playing a marginal hand cautiously. While I could attempt a probe bet here to take the pot down, checking remains the most prudent play out of position with no showdown value and no significant equity. I will check and see if Minni checks behind again, which would give me a free look at the river, or if she finally decides to bet.

I hold J♥T♥ on a 5♣ A♥ J♠ 7♦ board — top pair with a decent kicker but heavily dominated by Ax combinations that the big blind likely defends preflop. Both players checked the flop and the big blind checked the turn, which signals a fairly wide, often capped range (weak A’s, pocket pairs, floats). With the pot already 650 and my stack deep but the effective stacks limited to my 6.8k, I prefer pot control and realizing equity rather than bloating the pot into an Ace-heavy range. Checking keeps the pot manageable, induces bluffs, and lets me call reasonable bets on the river or barrel if I pick up additional information. Therefore: check and reassess on the river; call small/medium bets but fold to large, committed aggression that strongly represents an Ace or better.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-high. The board is quite heavy with broadway cards (Ace, Jack, and now a King). After calling pre-flop, both Minni and I have checked on the flop and turn. This sequence suggests Minni, who raised from the button, likely doesn't have a strong Ace, but she could easily have a Jack, a small pair, or now a pair of Kings. My Queen-high has almost zero showdown value, but bluffing here is risky because even a small pair like 66 or a weak Jack might feel inclined to call given the passive action. Since the pot is small and my stack is large, I will check and concede the pot if she bets, rather than risking chips on a low-equity bluff.

I check. I have top pair (J♥T♥) but the flop Ace and the river King are both very worrying — the big blind defended preflop and has a range heavy in A‑x and K‑x that now beats me. Flash checked the flop, turn and river, which looks like pot‑control/showdown lines (weak A’s, Kx, medium pairs) rather than hands I can extract thin value from. A thin value bet here will mostly be called by better hands (A/K/two‑pair) and will fold out few worse hands, so betting is expected value negative. With the pot only 650 and large effective stacks, pot‑control to showdown is optimal: check and call a small river bet if shown, but fold to large, committed raises that clearly represent an Ace/King or better.

Fold. J♠4♣ UTG is a weak, unsuited, poorly-connected hand that will be dominated by typical opening/calling ranges from the Button and blinds. In 4‑handed play I’d be out of position versus Elon (button) and the blinds; calling 100 to see a flop creates multiway, OOP scenarios where this hand has very little equity and raising has little fold equity and risks a 3‑bet from deep stacks (Flash 31,600, Claude 17,300). With ~74BB effective and many better spots to pick, preserving my stack and waiting for a stronger holding is the right play.

7c2d is one of the worst possible starting hands in Hold'em, with extremely low equity against any calling range. Even on the button with no action ahead (only UTG Minni folded and blinds posted), entering the pot voluntarily is -EV due to poor playability postflop, dominated potential, and reverse implied odds. My stack is 34.75BB, solid but no need to risk chips on trash when better spots will arise in 52 remaining hands. Folding preserves stack for premium opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Trey offsuit. The action has folded to me, leaving only the Big Blind (Claude) to contend with. While this is a Blind vs Blind situation, Q3o is at the bottom of the range and playing it out of position against Claude—who is the second-largest stack and has shown significant aggression in previous hands—is unnecessarily risky. With a substantial chip lead, I prefer to fold this marginal hand and wait for better spots rather than inviting a confrontation with a competent opponent.

Pocket nines (99) is a strong premium hand from UTG in a 4-handed tournament spot (effective 6-max based on history). Standard play is to raise for value to build the pot, deny equity to weaker hands, and isolate against blinds. My stack is 3475 (~35BB), which is playable but not desperate, so a standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate rather than min-raise or limp. Opponents: Flash very deep (316BB), Claude deep (174BB), Minni shorter (74BB). Raising isolates potential weak calls from blinds and takes initiative. Folding or limping is suboptimal with this strength. Post-flop playability is good with set-mining potential and overpair strength.

In a 4-handed game, the UTG position is effectively the Cutoff. Elon, who is the table's short stack with roughly 32 big blinds, has opened for 3x. Queen-Nine offsuit (Q9o) is a marginal hand that performs poorly against a standard opening range from this position, especially when facing a raise. Calling would also offer the Small and Big Blinds (Claude and Minni) favorable pot odds to enter the hand or potentially squeeze. Given my significant stack lead, there is no need to commit chips with a dominated or weak hand against a short stack who is likely looking to commit. I will fold and wait for a higher-equity situation.

I have KK - a premium hand. Elon opened to 300 from UTG with only 3175 behind. This is an easy 3-bet for value. I'll raise to around 900 to put pressure on him. With his short stack, if he shoves I'm happy to call. KK is the 2nd best starting hand.

I have T♥8♦ in the BB facing an UTG open to 300 and a sizable reraise to 900 from Claude. The pot is 1300 and calling 800 would require ~38% equity to break even (800 / (1300+800) ≈ 38%). Versus an UTG open and an SB 3‑bet my hand is frequently dominated and has poor playability out of position in a multiway pot; Claude’s 3‑bet looks value/polarized and Elon (short stack) can still shove or call and create tough spots. 4‑betting or shoving with T8o is far too risky/loose here. Preserve my 7.3k stack and fold.

Pocket 9s remains very strong facing a SB 3-bet after my UTG open. Claude's SB 3-bet range vs UTG raise is polarized: premium value (QQ+, AK), some broadway bluffs/semi-bluffs, suited Ax, connectors. With my ~32BB stack, calling the 600 into 1900 pot commits me post-flop (SPR ~1.2 after), where 99 plays well but is vulnerable to overcards/AK. Folding is -EV as 99 crushes his wide range. 4-bet shoving maximizes fold equity against his bluffs/light 3-bets (high history of aggression from Claude) while getting value from worse pairs/broadways. If called, 99 has ~40-45% equity vs typical calling range (TT+/AQ+). Tournament stage favors stacking off with premiums to build stack. Deep stacks behind don't matter as Flash folded, Minni folded.

Elon 4-bet shoved with only 3175 total (300 behind). I have KK - the 2nd best hand in poker. This is a trivial call. I'm only behind AA, and even against a tight 4-bet range, KK is way too strong to fold. Easy call.

Flopped an overpair with KK on a Q62 board. Elon only has 300 chips left after the 4-bet. Perfect flop for me - no ace to worry about. I should bet to put him all-in and get his last chips in the pot. No reason to give free cards.

Pocket 9s is an overpair on a Q-6-2 board with two hearts, facing a small 300 donk-bet into a 6750 pot from Claude, who 3-bet pre-flop and called my large 4-bet shove. My remaining stack is exactly 300, making this a pure call for my tournament life. Pot odds are exceptional: calling 300 to win 7050 requires just ~4% equity, but 99 has strong equity (~35-45%) against Claude's polarized calling range (QQ+, AK, AQ, some bluffs/draws like suited broadways or hearts). The board is relatively dry despite the flush draw; I block sets of 9s/nines. Folding is massively -EV; raising isn't possible as stack equals the bet. Claude's small bet sizing suggests weakness (possible Qx weak kicker, FD, or trap with monster, but unlikely given history), but irrelevant with commitment. Standard play: commit with the overpair OOP.

J7o on the button with only 2 opponents in the blinds. This is a standard open situation - the hand is marginal but playable from the button. I have position and fold equity. Standard 2.5x raise to steal the blinds or play postflop with position.

I have 4♦2♦ in the small blind facing a standard button open to 2.5BB. This is a very weak, low-equity hand that will be dominated by most of the button's opening range and plays terribly out of position against both the opener and the big blind. Calling 200 to 250 total gives poor realization of equity and exposes me to c-bets and squeezes; a 3-bet bluff is also unattractive (no useful blockers, and the stacks are deep so I can be 4-bet or forced to play big pots out of position). With ~73 BBs I should preserve my stack and wait for a better spot, so I fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 5d 3c, which is an extremely weak hand. Claude has raised to 250 from the button, a standard 2.5x open. While I am getting a decent price to call (150 to win a pot of 400), this hand has very poor playability and realization of equity out of position. With my current chip lead and 50 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no need to defend with the bottom of my range against a button open. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I’m on the button with 8♠5♥ in a 3‑handed pot (pot 150, call 100). This is a weak, unsuited holding but in short‑handed play the button should open a wide range to seize initiative and use positional advantage. Limping/calling invites multi‑way pots where this hand has poor equity; folding is too passive. I’ll open‑raise to a standard size (300) — big enough to generate fold equity and consistent with table raising tendencies, small enough that I can comfortably fold to a sizeable 3‑bet from these deep stacks. If I get callers I’ll play postflop in position; if I face heavy aggression I can release the hand and preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with a very weak hand (4-5 offsuit). Minni, who is on the button, has raised to 300. Calling with such a weak hand out of position is generally a losing play, and 3-betting as a bluff against a relatively short stack (70BB) who might feel committed or play back is unnecessary when I currently hold the chip lead. The most disciplined action is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Holding 9h6h in the BB facing a button raise to 300. Getting 2.25:1 odds to call 200 into 450. This suited one-gapper has reasonable playability for a BB defense against a likely wide button opening range. Easy call.

I flopped middle pair (9s) with 9h6h on Kc7s9s. Board has two spades for a flush draw. With middle pair and weak kicker against a button raiser, I should check and play pot control. Betting here risks getting raised off my hand or building a pot I can't comfortably play. Checking lets me control the pot size and potentially catch bluffs or call small bets.

I opened preflop and Claude called. On Kc 7s 9s he checked to me. I have 8♠5♥ — no pair, only a 4‑out gutshot to a 6 and a weak backdoor spade possibility. Versus Claude’s check‑back range (wide: Kx, 9x, pocket pairs, overcards, spade draws), I want to seize the initiative as the preflop aggressor and apply pressure. A small-to-medium continuation bet will fold out a lot of his marginal hands and deny equity to overcards/draws while keeping my commitment manageable. I’ll bet about one‑third to two‑fifths of the pot to maintain fold equity but leave room to fold to strong resistance. Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll pot‑control on the turn and only continue aggressively if I hit (6 or strong spade action) or get a clear read; if he raises big I’ll fold given my poor equity.

Flopped middle pair (9s) with 9h6h. Minni bet 250 into 650 - a small c-bet sizing. Getting 3.6:1 pot odds to call 250. Middle pair has decent showdown value against a button opener's wide c-betting range. The small sizing often indicates either a weak continuation or trying to build a pot cheaply. Folding middle pair here is too weak, and raising for value isn't appropriate with this kicker. Call and re-evaluate on turn.

Turn brought the Js completing the spade flush draw. I have middle pair (9s) with no spade - can't improve to a flush. Board is now Kc7s9sJs - extremely wet with flush completed and straight possibilities (QT has the nuts). My 9h6h is in a tough spot - any spade holding has me crushed, plus K, J, or even 7 with a spade beats me. Best play is to check and see what Minni does. If they bet big, I should consider folding as this board smashes a lot of their range.

On the turn (Kc 7s 9s Js) with 8♠5♥ I have no pair but a strong semi‑bluff: I now have four spades (one in hand + three on board) and a gutshot to a 10.
