That’s 9 spade outs + the 3 non‑spade tens = 12 total outs (~12/46 ≈ 26% to improve on the river). I was the preflop aggressor and c‑betted the flop, so a turn bet continues a coherent line and applies pressure to many of Claude’s calling hands (Kx, 9x, single pairs, overcards) while still keeping my commitment controlled. Pot is 1,150 — I’ll make a modest/medium semi‑bluff sized bet (450, ~40% pot). This size preserves fold equity, doesn’t overcommit the stack, and leaves room to fold to a large raise. Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’m drawing with ~26% equity and will only invest further on the river with a completed hand or a clear read; if he check‑raises big I will release.

Board is now Kc7s9sJs with 3 spades. I have middle pair (9) with no spade and weak kicker. This turn card is terrible for me - it completes flush draws, adds another overcard (J), and creates more straight possibilities. Minni betting 450 into ~1150 on this wet board likely represents real strength - any spade, K, J, or straight beats me. Getting ~3.5:1 odds but I'm drawing essentially dead against most of their value range. Only 2 outs to improve. Fold and save chips.

I am on the button with 9-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Although I have a significant chip lead, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot with such a poor hand, especially since Minni is relatively short-stacked and might look for an opportunity to shove. Folding is the standard and most prudent play here.

Heads up against just the BB with 86o. This is a marginal hand but completing for 50 more to see a flop in position against one opponent is reasonable. The hand has some connectivity for straight draws. Folding is also fine but I'll take the cheap flop opportunity.

I have 3c Td in the big blind — a weak, marginal hand. Button folded and SB completed to 100, leaving the pot small (200) and me last to act. With no reason to inflate the pot out of position against Claude (who can be aggressive) and with a deep but relatively short stack, I’ll preserve chips and see a flop in position. I can proceed postflop based on texture (fold to big action on dry boards, continue when I hit or get good odds). Checking keeps control and is the lowest-variance play here.

Completely missed this flop with 8-high. No pair, no flush draw (no clubs), and no straight draw. The K-Q board hits many hands Minni would have. Betting here as a bluff would be risky with zero equity. Better to check and take a free card - might improve to a pair on the turn.

Board: K♥ Q♣ 4♣. I hold T♦3♣ — no pair, only a weak backdoor club draw and limited straight possibilities. Claude checked; his range (Kx/Qx, pocket pairs, clubs, floats) often dominates my ten. Pot is tiny (200) compared with both stacks, so the lowest-variance play is pot control: check and see a turn card. If Claude bets a small amount (cheap to call) I can continue because of backdoor equity and the chance to hit a ten, but I will fold to significant pressure — this hand has poor showdown value and I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position.

I have 8-high with no pair, no flush draw (only 6c), and no straight draw on K-Q-4-2. Completely whiffed. Minni checked the flop and turn came a brick 2s. With zero equity and no blockers to strong hands, bluffing makes no sense. Check and take the free river card - could hit an 8 or 6 for a small pair to potentially win at showdown.

I hold T♦3♣ on K♥ Q♣ 4♣ 2♠ — no pair and essentially no realistic draw. With one card to come I can only pair a T or a 3 (3 tens + 3 threes ≈ 6/46 ≈ 13% to improve), and even a small pair will often be behind Claude’s likely Kx/Qx/pair holdings. Claude checked flop and turn, showing weakness, but the pot is tiny (200) compared with my deep 7,825 stack. The lowest-variance, highest-expected-value line is pot control: check and see the river. If Claude bets a small amount on the river I can call for pot odds or fold to significant pressure; bluffing now would risk chips with limited fold equity against his range, so I’ll check.

River paired the Queens. I have 8-high with zero showdown value. Minni has checked every street showing weakness. However, the pot is only 200 chips - not worth bluffing for such a small pot when Minni could easily have any pair or weak K/Q that would call. Check and accept the loss if they have anything.

I have T♦3♣ on K♥ Q♣ 4♣ 2♠ Q♥ — no pair and no completed draw. Claude checked flop and turn, then checked the river as well; his line shows weakness but his range still contains Kx/Qx and pocket pairs that beat me. The pot is tiny (200) relative to stacks, so bluffing here has poor risk/reward and little fold equity against his likely calling range. Lowest-variance, highest EV line is to check to showdown and preserve chips; if Claude fires a small bet I can call for pot odds, fold to large pressure. I’ll check.
On the button with 65o against just the blinds. This is a standard steal spot - I have position and fold equity. 65o isn't strong but button opens should be wide. Raising to 250 to steal the blinds.

Holding A♦5♣ in the small blind facing Claude’s button raise to 250 (pot 400, I must put in 200 more). With ~80bb effective this is a standard defend: A5o has good showdown and top-pair potential and is worth seeing a flop. Calling 200 into a 400 pot gives attractive pot odds (required equity ~33%) and keeps the pot manageable out of position. A min-3bet to 400 is too small to generate reliable fold equity and a larger 3bet would commit too much with a marginal offsuit ace. Plan: call to 250 total, see the flop, and proceed cautiously — value-bet top pairs, fold to strong aggression on dry boards or big 3-bets.

With Ten-Three offsuit, I am looking at a very weak hand that performs poorly multi-way and out of position. Despite receiving 4:1 pot odds to call the 150 chips, T3o is near the bottom of any starting hand range and is easily dominated by the button's opening range and the small blind's calling range. In a three-handed game where Claude (the button) has shown significant aggression and loose tendencies, I prefer to fold this trash hand and maintain my chip lead rather than navigate a difficult post-flop scenario with very little equity.

I have A♦5♣ on Q♥ K♣ 7♥ — only ace-high with no heart and I'm out of position to Claude (button raiser). Claude's opening range is wide in 3-handed and will include many hands that continue (Kx, Qx, Ax, heart draws). I have little fold equity and no strong equity to build the pot now, so I should control the pot and realize equity. Plan: check and evaluate — call small continuation bets to see a turn but fold to big pressure unless I pair the A or the action clearly favors a bluff-catching line. If Claude checks back I can consider a delayed probe on a safe turn.

Missed flop completely with 65s on QK7 board. I have no pair, no flush draw (only one heart), and minimal straight potential. Minni checked to me. While I could c-bet as a bluff, this board hits calling ranges well (broadway cards) and I have almost no equity if called. Better to take the free card and see the turn.

I have A♦5♣ on Q♥ K♣ 7♥ T♥ — only ace-high, no heart and no pair. Claude opened pre and both of us checked the flop; his passive line could be a marginal K/Q, a slow-played strong hand, or a heart/ten — the board is now dangerous (three hearts and a ten that pairs). Betting out-of-position with essentially air has very low fold equity and needlessly inflates a pot I’m behind in many cases. With ~78bb effective and the pot only 600, I should control the pot and gather river information. Check the turn and plan to fold to meaningful river aggression (only consider calling very small bets for pot odds).

Turn brought Th, putting 3 hearts on board. I have 6h giving me a flush draw with 9 outs (~18% to hit). No straight draw as I'd need both 8 and 9. Minni checked again. I could semi-bluff but the board is scary with possible flush already out there. Better to take the free card and see if I can hit the flush on the river. If I hit, I can value bet; if I miss, I can give up cheaply.

I paired my ace on the river (Ad with As on board) so I have one pair, aces, with a 5 kicker — decent but vulnerable. Claude opened from the button in 3-handed (wide) and played very passively (checked flop and turn, checked the Ace on the river), so his range includes Kx/Qx/Tx, some Ax, KQ/KT/QT two-pair combos, and lots of Js and Jx-type hands that just became the Broadway straight (A-K-Q-J-T) when the river J wasn’t needed — any jack in his hand now makes a straight. Several common holdings (AK, AQ -> two pair; AJ or A with a better kicker; any J -> straight; KQ/KT/etc -> two pair) beat my hand, and a thin value bet is likely to get called by many hands that are ahead while folding out some K/Q/Tx that I might beat. With 78–80 bb effective and the pot only 600, there’s no reason to inflate the pot out of position with a marginal top pair. I check to go to showdown and reserve folding or calling decisions for any river aggression from Claude (call small bet sizes from obvious one-pair hands, fold to large raises that represent two-pair/straights).

Missed my flush draw completely - river brought As instead of a heart. I have 6-high which beats nothing. After checking back flop and turn, a river bluff tells no coherent story and won't fold out any pairs Minni might have. The board is extremely coordinated (broadway possible, 3 hearts). Just check and accept the loss - I can't win a showdown but bluffing here is burning money.

On the button with 9♦8♣ and ~83BB effective, this is a good spot to open a wide stealing range. I have position on both blinds and a speculative, playable hand with straight potential. A 3x BB open (to 300 total) gives solid fold equity without overcommitting the stack. Plan: take the initiative — if both fold I pick up the blinds; if called I play postflop in position, c-betting favorable textures, floating, and realizing equity with straights/paired boards. I will fold to a large 3‑bet/shove; only consider calling small 3‑bets where position and implied odds justify it.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Four suited. Minni, who is the shortest stack at the table with about 80 big blinds, has raised from the Button. While Ten-Four suited has some speculative value, calling out of position against a Button raiser is generally unfavorable, especially with an active player like Claude in the Big Blind who could squeeze or simply outplay me post-flop. Given my significant chip lead, there is no need to play a weak hand out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack advantage.

J5o in BB facing a 3x button open. Getting 2.25:1 pot odds (200 to win 450). In a 3-handed game, button opens very wide. J5o has enough equity to defend here - the jack gives some showdown potential and I'm getting reasonable odds. Calling to see a flop.

Completely missed this flop with J5o on A76. I have no pair, no draw - just jack high. The ace is bad for my range as Minni likely has plenty of aces in their button opening range. Standard check here and fold to any bet.

I have 9♦8♣ on A♥7♦6♠ — an open‑ended straight draw (need a 5 or 10, 8 outs → ~31–32% to hit by river) and I have position. Claude checked the flop, showing some weakness/elasticity in his range (could be missed broadways, small pairs, or some Ax that check behind). This is an excellent spot to semi‑bluff: bet for fold equity and to build the pot with a strong draw. I will make a half‑pot c‑bet (325). Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if called I still have decent equity to improve and will continue depending on the turn (value bet if I complete the straight, occasionally barreling on safe cards; fold to a large check‑raise or extreme aggression representing sets/top two).

Completely whiffed this flop with J5o. I have no pair, no flush draw, and only a gutshot (need 4-8 but I don't have the right cards). Ace-high board favors Minni's button range heavily. Getting 3:1 odds but my equity is too low to continue with just jack-high and no real draws. Easy fold.

I am on the button with Jack-Four offsuit. This is a very weak hand in a three-handed game. While I have the largest stack, there is no need to play junk hands and get involved in potentially difficult spots out of position against Claude or against a short-stacked Minni who might be looking to shove. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

9h3s is a very weak hand. Heads up against the BB, I'm out of position with poor cards. Even though it's only 50 more to call, this hand plays terribly postflop. Better to fold and wait for a better spot.
As6s on the button is a clear open with suited ace. Only 2 opponents left in blinds. Standard 2.5x raise to 250.

5♣3♠ is a very weak offsuit holding with poor playability and high chance of being dominated by a button open. Claude opened to 250 (2.5x BB) and Flash (BB) still acts — calling 200 to play out of position in a likely multiway pot is a losing long-term line. A light 3-bet bluff is possible with deeper stacks, but this specific holding has almost no equity and is a poor candidate to invest chips OOP. Preserve the 8,725 stack and wait for a better spot; fold.

QJs is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. Claude is raising from the button, which is usually a wide range. By 3-betting, I am building a pot with a strong hand and taking the initiative, even though I will be out of position post-flop. A raise to 850 is a standard sizing to put pressure on the button opener.

Flash 3-bet to 850 from BB after my button open. As6s is a decent hand with nut flush potential. Getting about 2:1 on a call (600 to win 1150). I have position and good stack depth to play postflop. Folding suited aces to a standard 3-bet is too tight, and 4-betting would bloat the pot unnecessarily. Call and play in position.

On a very wet and connected board of 9h-8h-7s, I have flopped an open-ended straight draw with two overcards. As the pre-flop 3-better, I have the initiative. While this board often favors a caller's range more than a 3-better's range, my specific hand has significant equity. I will lead with a continuation bet of approximately half the pot (800) to maintain aggression, protect my overcards, and build a pot in case I hit my straight on the turn or river.

Board is 9h8h7s - extremely coordinated with straight and flush draws. I have As6s - just ace high with no draw (backdoor spade flush is very weak). Flash 3-bet preflop and c-bet on this connected board. My hand has virtually no equity here - no pair, no real draw. Easy fold.

On the button with Q♠2♣ — a weak offsuit hand with poor playability but decent positional value. The pot is 150 and calling 100 is very cheap relative to my ~87BB stack, so I prefer to see a flop in position rather than open-raise a marginal holding that is likely to be dominated or induce 3-bets from Flash/Claude (both have shown aggression). Calling preserves stack, keeps ranges wide, and lets me use position postflop to realize equity or fold if the flop is bad. Plan: call 100; if I face a sizable raise I'll fold or re-evaluate based on sizing and who raised.

I have Ace-Ten offsuit in the small blind, which is a strong hand in a 3-handed game. The button limped, which often signifies a weak range. I will raise to 400 to isolate the button and take control of the pot before the flop.

Q4o is a weak holding in the BB facing a raise to 400. Getting 2:1 odds but this hand plays poorly postflop - dominated by better queens and has poor playability. Flash raised after a limp which could be wide but Q4o doesn't have the equity to profitably defend here. Fold.

I’m on the button with Q♠2♣ — a weak, marginal hand but in position. I’ve already invested 100 and Flash (SB) has raised to 400; calling the extra 300 is cheap relative to my ~86BB stack and gives very attractive pot odds (we need ~25% equity to break even). I don’t want to 3-bet this holding (dominated too often and Flash is deep/aggressive), and folding would be too tight given the price. Plan: call to see a flop in position; proceed cautiously postflop — fold to large aggression on dry boards, play trips/top pair or strong draws for value, and use position to control the pot size.

I am the pre-flop aggressor with Ace-Ten offsuit. The flop of 5c-7s-2s is relatively dry, although it may have connected with some of the button's limp-calling range like small pairs or suited connectors. However, as the raiser, I have a significant range advantage. I will place a standard continuation bet of 450 (half the pot) to maintain pressure, represent a strong overpair, and potentially take the pot down now while also having the best hand against most of my opponent's non-pairing cards.

I have Q♠2♣ on the button — one pair (deuces) with a decent queen kicker, in position. Flash opened and made a small flop bet (450 into a 1350 pot), which looks like a fairly wide continuation range; calling 450 gets ~25% pot odds and is only ~4.5 BB of my ~83 BB stack, so it’s cheap to continue and realize my equity in position. I won’t raise — my hand is too marginal and would be dominated too often — and folding would be too tight given the price. Plan: call to see the turn, use position to evaluate further; fold to large turn aggression or a raise on this street, continue for value if I improve (2/Q) or if action checks to me on a safe turn.

On this 2h turn, the board has paired. My Ace-high (Ace-Ten) is still the best hand reasonably often against a limp-calling range from the button, which might contain suited connectors, small pairs, or weak Broadway cards. However, checking is a superior play here for several reasons: it controls the pot size against a possible set or slow-played pocket pair, it induces bluffs from hands I beat, and it allows me to see a river card for free while maintaining showdown value. If Minni bets after I check, I can evaluate the sizing to decide on a call, but betting again into a board that hasn't improved my hand further risks being raised off the best hand or value-owning myself against a 2 or a slow-played overpair.

I have trip deuces (2c with 2s/2h on the board) with a queen kicker, in position against Flash who bet the flop small and then checked the turn. This is a very strong made hand but the turn completed some obvious draws (there are two spades on board), so slowplaying is unnecessary — I want value and to charge/deter spade draws and weaker pairs. Betting around 2/3 pot gives good value from one-pair hands (5x, 7x, A/K/Qx that called the flop) and spade semi-bluffs, while making drawing hands pay to see the river. If I get a large raise I’ll re-evaluate (likely call reasonable raises with trips and extract on the river); if called I can go for more value on many rivers or protect versus the flush. Bet 1,200 into a 1,800 pot to extract and protect.

I am currently the chip leader in this three-handed game. Pre-flop, I raised with Ace-Ten offsuit to isolate the button's limp, and I followed up with a continuation bet on a dry, low-card flop (5c-7s-2s). Minni called, which suggests she has a piece of the board (likely a 7 or a 5) or a spade flush draw. The turn 2h is largely a blank card, but it does pair the board. After I checked to control the pot, Minni lead out with a substantial bet of 1,200 into an 1,800 pot. While my Ace-high has some showdown value and could theoretically be ahead of a bluff or a draw, her line of limp-calling pre-flop and then calling a flop bet before leading the turn often indicates a marginal made hand like a 7. Given that I have a massive stack and there are still 43 hands remaining, I don't need to make a risky call with only Ace-high against a range that contains many 7x and 5x hands. I will fold and look for a more favorable opportunity to utilize my chip lead.

I am on the button with 82o, which is one of the weakest hands in the game. While the game is three-handed and aggression is important, 82o has very little playability post-flop and doesn't justify a raise or a call, even with a significant chip lead. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to avoid unnecessary losses with garbage hands.

Heads up vs BB with 9h5s. Getting 3:1 odds to complete (50 into 150). While 95o is weak, completing is fine in this spot - I can fold to aggression postflop if I miss. Folding loses the 50 already invested, raising with this weak holding is too loose. Complete and see a cheap flop.

I have 6♣2♣ — a very weak, low suited hand. SB (Claude) merely limped and the pot is small (200) with no cost to see the flop. Raising here with this holding is a thin bluff: it risks bloating the pot and plays poorly post‑flop against a caller who frequently continues. I’ll check and see a flop for free. Plan: fold to significant pressure on dry flops, but if I pair a 6/2 or pick up a strong club draw I can start folding equity or apply controlled aggression (small bets or raises) — otherwise keep the pot small and conserve chips for better spots.

Flopped two pair (9s and 5s) on a 5c 7d 9s board - this is a very strong hand. Heads up vs Minni in the BB. Board has some straight potential with 68 but is relatively dry. Need to bet for value to build the pot and charge draws. Betting around 60-70% pot (~140) is appropriate.

I have 6♣2♣ on a 5♣-7♦-9♠ flop facing a 140 bet into a ~340 pot (to call 140). This is a very weak hand, but I have a small backdoor club draw plus six outs (three 6s + three 2s) to make at least a pair by river. Pot odds: calling 140 to potentially win ~480 requires ≈29.2% equity. Probability to pair a 6 or 2 by the river ≈24.1%; probability to hit the two-card (backdoor) club flush by river ≈4.2%; combined ~28.3% — slightly below the raw break‑even point. Claude limped preflop and his flop raise is small, so his range is fairly wide and contains bluffs; plus the call is cheap relative to my 9.6k stack. I’ll call the 140 to see the turn, keep the pot small, and fold to significant turn aggression unless I improve (pair or pick up a strong club draw), in which case I can continue or raise selectively.

I have two pair (9s and 5s) but the turn 8s is dangerous - it completes a straight for any 6 (5-6-7-8-9). Also brings a spade flush draw. My hand is still strong but more vulnerable now. I should bet smaller for protection/thin value - around half pot. If Minni raises big, I may need to reconsider. Betting ~240 keeps the pot manageable while charging draws.

I fold. I have 6♣2♣ on 5♣-7♦-9♠-8♠ and did not improve. With only the river left I have 6 outs (three 6s + three 2s) → 6/46 ≈ 13% to pair or make the straight on a 6. Calling 240 into a 720 pot would make the pot ≈960, so I need ~25% equity to justify a call — my equity (~13%) is far below that. Claude limp-called preflop and barreled flop and turn, showing initiative; small sizing doesn’t mean he’s folding to further pressure and a bluff‑raise risks too much. My prior plan was to fold to significant turn aggression unless I improved, so I’ll preserve my stack and fold here.
J3s on the button with only the blinds left. This is a decent spot to steal with suited broadway. Standard 2.5x open to apply pressure on the blinds.

I’m in the small blind with A♦2♦ facing a button open to 250 (I’ve posted 50, to call 200). A2s is worth defending in this spot: suited ace with blocker value and decent postflop playability. A tiny min‑3bet to 400 is unattractive (invites callers/4-bets and leaves me OOP); a larger isolation 3‑bet is an option but unnecessary with a deep stack and a relatively standard open. I’ll call to keep the pot controlled, realize equity, and re-evaluate on the flop. Contingency: fold to a large 3‑bet; if we see a flop I’ll proceed cautiously OOP (small c-bet on dry boards, check back coordinated boards, value bet when I connect).

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace of Clubs and Eight of Spades. Claude raised to 250 from the Button, and Minni called from the Small Blind. With a pot of 600 and only 150 more to call, I am getting 4:1 odds to see a flop. While a squeeze raise is a viable option to take down the pot pre-flop, calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and realize the equity of a relatively strong 3-handed hand like A8o. I have a significant chip lead and don't need to force a large pot out of position against two opponents at this stage.

I’m OOP (SB) with A♦2♦ on Ts Kc Tc — no pair and only a backdoor diamond; this is weak showdown value. The flop is paired (two tens) so many hands in the opener/caller ranges (Kx, pocket pairs, tens) already beat me. Betting as a bluff into two opponents has very little fold equity and risks large action I’m unlikely to have equity against. I’ll check to control the pot, realize equity, and see how opponents proceed. Plan: call a small C‑bet (roughly ≤1/3 pot ≈250) to see a turn for an Ace or a diamond, but fold to significant aggression (>>1/2 pot). If checked to me on later streets I’ll consider betting only on a safe card (an Ace or diamond) or check back and re‑evaluate.

The flop of Ts-Kc-Tc is a paired board that generally favors the pre-flop raiser's range, as Claude could easily have a King or a Ten. Since I am out of position in a three-way pot and Minni has already checked, checking is the most standard and safest play. My hand, Ace of Clubs and Eight of Spades, provides me with Ace-high and the nut flush blocker (Ac), which might allow for some creative lines on later streets if a third club appears, but leading into the aggressor here is unnecessarily risky.

Flop is Ts Kc Tc - paired board with two clubs. I have J3s (jack-high with backdoor spade flush potential). Both opponents checked to me. While c-betting as a bluff could work on this board texture that favors the preflop raiser, being multiway reduces my fold equity significantly. With jack-high and minimal equity, I'd rather take a free card and potentially improve on the turn with running spades or hit a jack. Checking back preserves my stack and pot control makes sense here.

Board T♠ K♣ T♣ 5♠; I hold A♦2♦. I have only Ace‑high and no realistic draw — there are zero diamonds on the board and only one card left, so a flush is impossible. My only meaningful outs are three Aces (and three deuces that would make a weak pair) — roughly 6 outs (~13%), but pairing a deuce is often irrelevant versus typical opener ranges. Claude opened from the button so his range contains many Kx, pocket pairs and tens that already beat me; both opponents checked the flop which can be weakness but also slowplay. Leading OOP as a pure bluff into two players has very little fold equity and risks a costly raise/call. I’ll check to control the pot and realize equity. Plan: call a small turn bet to see the river (given my limited outs) but fold to large aggression; if checked through I’ll re‑evaluate the river and only bet for value (an Ace) or bluff selectively against one opponent.

On the turn card 5s, the board remains paired (Ts-Kc-Tc). Minni has checked for the second time. My Ace-high (Ac 8s) has some marginal showdown value, but it is not strong enough to bet for value into two opponents on this board. Claude, the pre-flop aggressor, checked behind on the flop, which could indicate a weak hand or a slow-play. By checking, I am looking to see a free or cheap river card. If the action remains checked around, I may be able to win at showdown with Ace-high or re-evaluate on the river.

Turn brought the 5s, giving me a flush draw with Js3s (two spades in hand, two on board). Both opponents checked again showing weakness. I could semi-bluff here, but taking the free card makes sense - I have 9 outs (~18% to hit) and checking preserves my stack while realizing equity. If I bet and get check-raised, I'm in an awkward spot. Free card is the play.

Check. The river paired my 2 (Ad2d → 2h) giving me a very weak, low pair that’s easy to beat but also easy to lose to. Claude (button opener) and Flash (BB) have ranges that contain many hands that already beat me — any Kx, any Ten, and essentially any pocket pair 3+ are ahead. Both opponents checked the flop and turn, which can be either genuine weakness or slowplay/trapping with strong holdings; with two opponents in the hand my fold equity on a river bluff or thin-value bet is extremely low. Betting a small amount would rarely get called by worse hands and will mainly create action from hands that already beat me (or induce a raise), so it’s leakier than checking. Pot is only 750 and my stack is healthy (9185) while opponents are deep enough to put me to a tough decision post-bet. I’ll check to get to showdown and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

The board has been checked through the flop and the turn, indicating a very weak range for all players involved. My Ace-high (Ac 8s) has significant showdown value in a pot that has seen zero aggressive action post-flop. Since Minni has checked on the river, betting now would likely only be called by a King, a Ten, or a medium pocket pair that was slow-playing. By checking, I allow for a free showdown to realize my equity. If Claude bets behind me, I can evaluate the sizing to see if he's making a late-position stab at a dead pot, but for now, checking is the most prudent way to handle a marginal hand in a three-way pot.

River completes with 2h - my flush draw missed. I have J-high with a busted spade draw. Both opponents checked all three postflop streets showing consistent weakness. However, betting here as a bluff multiway is risky - J-high actually has some showdown value against complete air, and a bet will only get called by better hands (any K, any T, pairs). Since it's checked to me with zero investment needed, checking back and hoping to win against two missed draws or lower high cards is the optimal play.

3-handed, on the button with Q2o I want to be aggressive and try to steal the blinds and play postflop in position. Q2o is marginal but acceptable as an opening hand from the button in short-handed play; it has a queen blocker and I can fold to heavy aggression. I’ll make a small standard open (min-raise to 200) to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable. If I face a big 3-bet I’ll fold; if called I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously when out of equity.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Six offsuit, which is a weak hand even in a three-handed game. Minni has raised from the Button, and while the price to call is small (150 chips), the hand plays poorly out of position and is easily dominated by Minni's opening range. With a significant chip lead and 40 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand and risk chips against the shorter stacks.

J8 suited in hearts in the BB facing a min-raise from button. Getting 3.5:1 on a call (100 into 350). J8s has good playability with flush and straight potential. Easy call with these odds - folding would be too tight and 3-betting isn't necessary with a hand that plays well postflop.

Flopped middle pair (8s) with J kicker on 8c5c4d. Board has club flush draws and straight draws. Against a button min-raiser, my hand has showdown value but is vulnerable. I should bet small for value/protection - around 1/3 pot to charge draws and get value from overcards or weaker pairs.

I opened preflop with Q♥2♣ and Claude called. The flop 8♣5♣4♦ misses my hand completely — no pair, no club — so I only have Q-high and a very weak backdoor straight/runner possibility. Claude’s bet is small (175 into a 625 pot ≈28%), so calling only requires ~22% equity; that’s a cheap price to see a turn in position. Folding here would be too passive given my preflop raise and the cheap price; raising to 350 would be a thin, unnecessary bluff (I have no club blocker and would bloat the pot with essentially no equity). I’ll call 175 to keep the pot manageable, use position to control future streets, and re-evaluate on the turn — fold to strong continued aggression unless I improve or pick up a clear double-barrel spot.

The Ace on the turn is a bad card for my middle pair of 8s. Minni min-raised preflop and called my flop bet - they could easily have an Ace in their range (Ax hands are common button opens). If I bet and get called/raised, I'm often behind. Checking to control pot size and potentially induce a bluff from busted draws or check behind from hands I beat. Can evaluate river if checked through.

I hold Q♥2♣ and completely missed both the flop (8♣5♣4♦) and the turn (A♠). I called the tiny flop c-bet to keep the pot manageable and use position — that was the right low-cost play. Claude checks the Ace on the turn, which looks like either weakness or pot-control with a hand that still beats Q-high (small pairs, 4s/5s, A-x, or a club draw that missed). I have no club blocker and no pair, so betting here would be a pure bluff with poor blockers and a reasonable chance of being called by better hands. Pot-control is the +EV line: check back the turn, see a free/cheap river to try to improve (or to use as a bluff spot if the river is favorable), and fold to meaningful river aggression when I haven’t improved. Action: check.

River brought Kd, completing a board of 8c5c4dAsKd. My middle pair of 8s with J kicker is now facing two overcards on board. Minni min-raised pre, called flop, checked turn - consistent with weak made hands, busted club draws, or Ax/Kx that's pot controlling. My pair of 8s has showdown value but isn't strong enough to value bet - if I bet and get called, I'm usually losing to Ax or Kx. Check to hopefully get to showdown.

I check. I hold Q♥2♣ and completely missed: no pair, no flush, no straight. I called a tiny flop stab to keep the pot manageable and use position; Claude’s small flop bet then checking the turn and river looks like weakness/pot-control (medium pair, a float, or a missed club draw). Bluffing the river without a meaningful blocker (my queen only blocks a few Qx hands) is thin — many hands that beat Q-high will still call a small river bet. The pot is small relative to my stack, so preserving chips and taking showdown is the +EV line here: check and fold to a sizable river bet, or see a check-down and show Q-high if called.

I am on the button in a three-handed game with a very weak hand (6h 4c). While the button usually raises with a wide range to steal the blinds, 64-offsuit is at the bottom of the range and generally not worth playing. With a significant chip lead and 40 hands remaining, I can afford to wait for a better spot rather than engaging with trash hands.

Td9d is a strong suited connector. Flash folded, leaving me heads up against the BB. This is a clear raise spot - suited connectors play well and I can take down the blinds or play a good hand postflop. Standard raise to 250.

I have pocket tens (Tc Th) in the big blind facing Claude’s raise to 250. With ~95 BB effective and the button folded, TT is a very strong heads-up hand and SB’s opening range is likely wide. I want value and isolation plus fold equity, so a standard 3-bet is appropriate rather than just calling (calling gives him cheap odds and surrenders initiative). Min-raise is 400, but I’ll size to about 3.5x the open to 900 — big enough to narrow his range and build the pot while leaving plenty of room to play postflop. If he 4-bets I’ll re-evaluate; if he calls I’ll proceed with position-aware C-betting.

Td9d suited is a decent hand that plays well postflop with good implied odds. Minni 3-bet to 900, I need to call 650 more into a pot of 1150 - getting about 1.77:1. While I'm OOP, suited connectors can flop well and I have good playability. Minni has ~8660 behind, so decent implied odds if I connect. Folding seems too tight, 4-betting is risky against a shorter stack who may just jam. Calling to see a flop is reasonable.

I have Td9d on 8h8dKc - completely whiffed this flop. I have no pair, only a backdoor diamond draw (8d on board). Minni 3-bet preflop and likely has strong holdings like AK, KQ, or pocket pairs. Betting as a bluff here is risky - they'll likely continue with any K or pocket pair. Best to check and potentially give up or catch a miracle runner-runner diamonds/straight cards.

I 3‑bet preflop with TT to isolate and build a pot against Claude’s wide SB open. Flop 8♥ 8♦ K♣ is a paired, relatively dry board. TT is an overpair to the 8s but can be behind any Kx or a set (88). Claude checked — his range includes missed broadways, medium pocket pairs and some Kx. A half‑pot c‑bet (~900 into 1800) is the optimal line: it gets value from worse pairs and other calling hands, folds out many unpaired hands/overcards, protects my equity on later streets, and keeps the pot manageable against a caller. Plan: if he folds I take it; if he calls I re‑evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously vs aggression (a large check‑raise usually represents 88/Kx and I will give it credit; I’ll call small raises but fold to strong reraises), if called and the turn is safe I’ll barreling for value or pot‑control depending on the card.

I have Td9d on 8h8dKc - complete miss. I have only a gutshot (need a 7 for open-ender or J for OESD) and backdoor diamond draw. Minni 3-bet pre and is c-betting 900 into 1800 on this paired K-high board. Getting 3:1 odds but my equity is likely ~12-15% against their 3-bet range (likely Kx, overpairs). Not enough equity to continue with T-high. Fold.
KJs on the button 3-handed is a premium hand. Standard open raise to build the pot with a strong suited broadway holding. I'll raise to 250 (2.5x BB).

Facing a standard button open to 2.5bb from the small blind with 5♣6♠ (offsuit), this is a marginal hand to defend out of position. Calling 200 more would put me OOP to both opponents and lead to many difficult, low-equity postflop spots; a minimal raise to 400 is a thin bluff that will either get called by better hands or face a 3-bet from the BB/button. 5-6 offsuit has poor playability and is often dominated by the opener's range, so folding to preserve my 104bb stack is the highest EV line here. I’ll fold and wait for a better spot in position or with a hand that plays well postflop.

I hold Ace-Queen offsuit in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. Claude raised from the Button, a position that typically opens a wide range of hands. With my significant stack advantage, I should three-bet for value and to capitalize on my positional strength if Claude calls. A standard 3-bet size out of position would be around 3.5x to 4x the initial raise. I will raise to 900 to build the pot and pressure Claude's opening range.

KJs is a strong suited broadway hand. Flash 3-bet from the BB to 900. Getting about 1.85:1 pot odds to call 650 more. KJs plays well postflop with its suited nature and straight potential. Too strong to fold, not strong enough to 4-bet. Standard call.

I have top pair with an Ace and a Queen kicker on a board of As-4h-8h. As the pre-flop 3-bettor, I have the range advantage on this Ace-high board. I will place a continuation bet of approximately 40% of the pot (750 chips) to extract value from Claude's potential weaker Aces, pocket pairs, or heart flush draws while maintaining control of the hand from out of position.

Missed the flop completely with KJs. Ace-high board heavily favors Flash's 3-betting range. I have just king-high with a weak backdoor spade draw (need runner-runner). Flash c-betting 750 into 1850 is standard with any Ax or even as a bluff, but I have no equity to continue. Folding is correct - calling with king-high and backdoor draws against a 3-bettor on an ace-high board is -EV.

I’m on the button with 8♦3♣ — a very weak, unsuited hand with almost no playability postflop. Pot = 150, call = 100, min-raise = 200. Both blinds are deep and have shown willingness to defend and raise; a raise here is high-variance and will often get called or 3‑bet, creating tough spots out of position postflop. Calling 100 to see a flop gives marginal pot odds for a hand that will be dominated and is unlikely to realize equity even in position. With a healthy stack (10,410) and many hands left, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is the best EV play. Action: fold.

In this three-handed situation, with the button folding, I am in the Small Blind holding King-Four offsuit. King-high is significantly stronger than a random hand in a heads-up battle against the Big Blind. Although I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand, an open-raise is standard here to exert pressure on Claude and potentially win the blinds immediately. Given the current stack depths and the stage of the tournament, a 3x raise to 300 is a standard opening size.

Holding 6d9c in BB facing a 300 raise from SB. This is a weak offsuit hand that plays poorly out of position. While I'm getting 2:1 pot odds (200 to win 400), 6-9 offsuit doesn't have good playability - no suited draws, poor straight potential, and will be dominated by most raising ranges. Flash has a big stack and has been active. Best to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with a significant chip lead. While Qd 2d is a weak hand, it is suited and in a 3-handed context, positional advantage is paramount. Opening from the button with a wide range (approximately 40-50% of hands) is standard to put pressure on the blinds, especially since both opponents have shorter stacks and may play more conservatively to avoid elimination. I will make a standard raise to 300.

J6 suited is a marginal hand. Facing a button raise, I'm out of position and need to call 250 into 450. While suited, J6 doesn't play well OOP against an aggressive opponent. The hand lacks connectivity and high card strength. Folding is the disciplined play here.

Facing a button open to 300 from Flash while I’m in the BB with 6♣2♥. Pot is 450 and I must call 200, which means I need ~200/(450+200) ≈ 30.8% equity to break even. 6-2 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in hold'em, is frequently dominated, and has very poor postflop playability out of position. Although Flash raises a lot (wide-ish button stealing frequency), this hand still performs poorly vs a normal raising range and is not worth defending with deep stacks — 3-betting as a bluff would be an aggressive, high-variance line I don't want to take here. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot. Action: fold.
6h8h is a suited connector on the button. This is a great spot to open raise - I have position against both blinds, and suited connectors play well postflop. Standard open to 250.

Facing a button open to 250 from the SB with 6♣5♣. I’ve already posted 50 so it’s 200 more to call; that makes the pot 600 after my call and I need ~33% equity to break even. 6♣5♣ is a playable suited connector against a wide 3-handed opening range — good flop equity and multiway potential. A min-raise to 400 is an unattractive 3-bet bluff out of position and could get me into a tough spot vs the deep-stacked BB or BTN. I’ll call 200 more (total committed 250) to see a flop, fold to a sizable 3‑bet, and proceed cautiously postflop: continue with strong pairs or good draws, otherwise check/fold to heavy aggression.

I am holding 3c 2h in the Big Blind. Claude raised from the Button and Minni called from the Small Blind. Although I am getting 4:1 pot odds to call (150 to call into a 600 pot), 32o is one of the worst hands in poker and realizes equity very poorly, especially when playing out of position against two opponents. I have a significant chip lead and there's no need to defend with such a weak hand.

Check. Out of position with 6♣5♣ on A♦ 7♣ 4♣ I have a huge drawing hand: a 4-card club flush draw plus an open-ended straight draw (4-5-6-7). Unique outs ≈ 15 (9 clubs + the non-club 3s and 8s) — roughly a 54% chance to hit by the river. Pot is only 600 and stacks are deep, so the +EV line is to realize equity cheaply by checking and calling a standard c-bet rather than bloating the pot OOP with a lead. Example: if Claude bets 300 I only need ~25% equity to call; I have ~54%, so a call is profitable. Plan: check now, call reasonable continuation bets (pot-sized or smaller) and re-evaluate on the turn — extract value if I hit a club/straight; fold/react to extreme over-aggression if the turn bricks and opponent barrels heavily.

I have 6h8h on a 4c Ad 7c board. I only have a gutshot straight draw (need a 5). The Ace-high board hits Minni's calling range well from the SB - they likely have some Ax combos. With just 4 outs and no backdoor flush draw since the clubs are on board, I should take a free card rather than c-bet into a board that favors their range.

I have 6♣5♣ on 4♣ A♦ 7♣ (turn A♠). On the flop I had a strong 2‑card club flush draw plus the open‑ended straight draw; after the turn I still have a four‑card club draw and the 3/8 straight outs. Unique outs ≈ 14, so my chance to improve on the river is ~30%. Pot is only 600 and both players checked the flop, so Claude’s range is capped/weak and he may often fold to a small amount of aggression — but betting out of position now bloats the pot with only one card to come and exposes me to raises from hands that now make trips (Ax) or better. I want to realize equity cheaply and avoid committing chips OOP with a one‑card draw. Plan: check the turn, call a reasonable small bet on the river (or call a small turn bet if he leads), but fold to large raises/overbets. If I hit my flush/straight on the river I will extract value; if I miss I will fold to heavy pressure.

Board is 4c Ad 7c As - paired aces with no flush possible yet. I have 6h8h giving me a gutshot (need a 5). Minni has checked both flop and turn, showing weakness. However, with just a gutshot (~8% equity) and a paired board that could be trapping an ace, betting is risky. If Minni has any ace, they're not folding. Taking a free card to potentially hit my straight on the river is the best play here.

I rivered a club flush (Jc gives me J‑7‑6‑5‑4 clubs with 6c5c). Claude was the preflop raiser but checked flop and turn, which makes two‑club, high‑club combos (the hands that beat me) less likely — many two‑club hands would have defended/bluffed the flop. The board has two aces, but trips lose to a flush, so most Ax holdings are behind me. With the pot only 600 and stacks deep, the +EV line is to extract thin value without bloating the pot against the rare better flush. A small value bet (~1/3 pot) will get called by worse (pairs, Jx, some K/Q hands, thin calls) while keeping losses limited if I’m facing a raise. If Claude raises big, I’ll assume a better flush and fold. Therefore I’ll make a modest value bet of 200.

I have 6h8h - complete air with 8-high. Board is 4c Ad 7c As Jc with paired aces and a flush possible. Minni's small 200 bet into 600 looks like thin value or a blocker bet. I can't beat anything - not even a bluff catcher. Easy fold.

Call 100. 4♦8♠ offsuit is a very marginal hand, but the price to see a flop is tiny (1 BB, ~0.9% of my stack) and I have position on both opponents. I don’t want to bloat the pot by open‑raising a hand that’s often dominated and plays poorly multiway — Flash is deep and Claude has shown willingness to defend/raise, so a raise here is likely to get action I don’t want. Calling preserves stack, realizes a little equity and lets me use position to fold to heavy aggression on most dry flops or continue when I pick up equity. If faced with a big 3‑bet I’ll fold; if checked to on a favorable flop I can probe or take the pot with a modest c‑bet.

In a 3-handed game, J5 suited is a reasonable hand to play, especially when getting 5-to-1 pot odds after a button limp. Minni's limp from the button suggests a marginal hand that doesn't want to face a large raise, but is happy to see a flop. By completing the small blind, I am seeing a flop cheaply with a hand that has good post-flop playability (flush and straight potential). If Claude (BB) checks, the pot remains small and manageable, allowing me to utilize my stack lead to outplay them on later streets if the board texture is favorable.

In BB with K4o facing two limps. This is a marginal hand - not strong enough to raise for value, but I get to see a free flop. Checking is the standard play here to see if I connect with the board before investing more chips.

In a 3-way limped pot with middle pair (Jacks) and a weak kicker (5), my hand has some showdown value but is vulnerable to any Ace or a better Jack. The board (As 9s Jh) is coordinated, meaning there are several straight draws and an overcard present. Leading out here often forces folds from hands I beat and gets called or raised by hands that have me crushed (like an Ace). Checking is the most prudent play to keep the pot small, allow opponents to bluff with weaker holdings, and realize my equity cheaply.

Completely missed this flop with K4o. Board is A-J-9, I have no pair and no draws. With two opponents and an ace on board, checking is the only reasonable option - no value in betting and bluffing into two players on this texture is -EV.

I have 8♠4♦ — no pair and no realistic draw on A♣ J♥ 9♠. I was a pre‑flop caller (no c‑bet credibility) and both opponents checked, but this is a three‑way pot so my fold equity is limited and both players have shown a tendency to call flop bets. With deep stacks and nothing to improve to, the lowest‑variance play is to check and see a turn card. I will give up to any meaningful bet and only consider a delayed bluff if the turn is a safe card and both check again.

On the turn, the board is 9s-Ac-Jh-6h. I have a middle pair of Jacks with a weak kicker. Everyone checked the flop, which suggests that an Ace is unlikely to be in anyone's hand, as a player with an Ace would typically bet for value or protection on a coordinated board. The 6h doesn't significantly change the board texture other than adding a heart flush draw. I will check again to control the pot size and see if I can get to a cheap showdown or induce a bluff from a weaker hand. There is no need to build a large pot with a marginal hand in a 3-way limped scenario.

K4o on A-J-9-6 board with two hearts. I have no pair, no flush draw, and only a gutshot (need a Q for broadway but that's weak). Both opponents checked flop and Flash checked turn. With K-high I have minimal showdown value but bluffing into two players with nothing is -EV. Check and see a free river.

I check. Holding 8♠4♦ on A♣ J♥ 9♠ 6♥ I have no pair and essentially no realistic draw (only weak pair outs). I was a preflop caller and both opponents checked the flop and turn, so my betting credibility and fold equity are low in this three‑way pot against deep, calling opponents. Betting here risks chips with little chance to fold out better hands and little equity to improve; the lowest‑variance line is to see the river for free. If either opponent fires a sizable bet on the river I will fold; I may consider a delayed bluff only if the river is a very safe card and both check again.

The river has brought a second nine, making the board 9s-Ac-Jh-6h-9c. I hold a pair of Jacks, which combined with the board gives me two pair (Jacks and Nines) with an Ace kicker. Given that this was a limped pot and has been checked through on the flop and turn by all three players, it is highly unlikely anyone is holding an Ace. While someone could have a nine for trips, they would likely have checked the flop and turn as well. My hand has significant showdown value in this specific line. I will check to see if I can get to a cheap showdown or induce a small bluff from a missed draw or a weaker pair. Betting here is unlikely to get called by many hands I beat, while a check-call or check-check allows me to realize my equity in a small pot.

River paired the 9s. I have K-high which is weak but has some showdown value. Everyone checked through all streets suggesting weakness, but bluffing into two opponents is risky and K-high might occasionally win if they both have complete air. Check and see showdown.

I have 8♠4♦ on 9♠ A♣ J♥ 6♥ 9♣ — no pair and no realistic showdown equity. This is a three‑way pot and both opponents checked flop and turn, so my fold equity is very low and both players have shown a tendency to call down. The pot is only 300 while both opponents are deep (Flash ~32k, Claude ~17k), so a river bluff would need to be large to have any chance of folding out better hands and would be high variance. The lowest‑variance, +EV line is to check and preserve my stack; I will fold to any meaningful bet.

I am on the button with 4d 2c, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With a significant chip lead and 33 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no strategic reason to play this hand or attempt a speculative steal against two active opponents. Folding preserves my stack and avoids unnecessary risk.

I have pocket Jacks in the small blind - a premium hand. Flash folded, so it's heads up against the BB (Minni). JJ is way too strong to just complete; I should raise for value. Standard raise to 3x the BB (300) to build the pot.

I have pocket 4s in the big blind facing a 300 open from the small blind (pot 400, 200 more to call). With ~104 BB effective stacks this is textbook set-mining: Claude’s 3x open from the SB is likely fairly wide, and calling keeps the pot controlled while preserving huge implied odds if I hit a set. 3-betting OOP with a small pair is unnecessary — it bloats the pot with a hand that wants to see a flop. Plan: call to see the flop; if I hit a set I’ll raise/commit, if I miss I’ll fold or proceed cautiously to significant aggression depending on board texture.

Flop is Ac Ah Ks - very scary for my pocket jacks. I'm essentially playing an underpair to the entire board. However, the paired aces reduce the likelihood Minni has an ace (only 2 remaining). Still, checking for pot control makes sense here. If I bet and get called or raised, I'm in a tough spot. Checking lets me get to showdown cheaply and possibly induce a bluff from worse hands.

Called preflop to set-mine. Flop Ac Ah Ks — I completely missed. My pocket 4s is an underpair on a paired-ace board and has very little showdown/value versus Claude’s SB opening range (which contains many Ax and Kx hands). Claude checked, which could be either weakness or pot‑control with an ace; betting here is a thin bluff that will be called by Kx and most Ax, and can get check‑raised into trouble. With deep stacks and a dry board, the prudent line is to keep the pot small and see a turn for free. Plan: check and re-evaluate on the turn; fold to substantial aggression unless I pick up direct equity or a clear read to bluff.

Turn 5s doesn't change much. Board is Ac Ah Ks 5s. My JJ is still an underpair. Minni checked behind on flop, suggesting they likely don't have a strong ace or king. The 5s adds a spade draw. Checking again for pot control is best - I can get to showdown cheaply with what's likely the best hand given Minni's passive play. Betting risks getting raised off my hand or only getting called by better.

I have 4♣4♥ on A♣ A♥ K♠ 5♠. Preflop Claude opened to 300 from the SB and I called to set‑mine. I completely missed — this is an underpair to a paired‑ace board with almost no showdown value vs his opening range (lots of Ax and Kx). Claude checked flop and turn; that could be weakness or a trap, but with deep stacks a lead here is a thin bluff that will be called by many Kx and worse and can be punished by a check‑raise from an Ace. The pot is only 600 relative to ~100+BB stacks, so the prudent, lowest‑variance line is to keep the pot small: check and re‑evaluate on the river. If Claude fires a substantial bet I’ll fold; if checked to the river I’ll consider a small bluff only if the river card helps my story or I pick up a clear read.

River Ace gives the board trip aces. My JJ now makes Aces full of Jacks (AAA-JJ). Only hands that beat me are Kx (Aces full of Kings), a pocket pair higher than JJ, or quad aces (extremely unlikely). Minni checked flop and turn, showing weakness - likely doesn't have a king or premium pair. I should bet small for thin value from worse pocket pairs or random holdings. A small bet around 250 extracts value while keeping the pot manageable.

River: A♣ A♥ K♠ 5♠ A♠ — my hand (4♣4♥) is now Aces full of fours (AAA44). Claude bets a small 250 into the ~600 pot (pot is 850 after the bet). Only hands that beat me are quads (Ad) or any hand that makes a better full house (any K or 5 in his hand, or a pocket pair 5+). Quick combinatorics: there are C(45,2)=990 possible two-card combos for him; roughly 329 of those combos produce a hand that beats my full house (~33%) and ~661 combos (~67%) I beat or tie. Calling 250 to win ~1,100 (post-call) requires ~22.7% equity to be break-even (250/1100 ≈ 22.7%), so my equity far exceeds the required threshold — a call is highly +EV. I won’t raise: a raise mostly charges worse hands (which I already beat) and will get called or shoved by many of the hands that actually beat me (Kx, 5x, pocket pairs, or Ad), so raising risks building the pot for the hands that dominate me. Plan: call the 250 and see showdown; fold only to a large raise/shove which would indicate quads/clear superiority.
A8o on the button is a solid opening hand, especially 3-handed. I'm in position against both blinds. Standard raise to 250-300 to pick up the blinds or play in position post-flop.

Facing a standard button open to 250 from Claude, I’m in the small blind with J♦2♠ — a weak, offsuit hand that’s frequently dominated by the button’s raising range. Calling 200 to defend would leave me out of position vs the raiser (and possibly the big blind), with poor post‑flop playability and little implied equity. 3‑betting is also unattractive: this hand has minimal fold equity and would be a losing line if called. With a healthy stack (~99bb) it’s better to conserve chips and wait for a better spot, so I fold here.

I am the chip leader with 32,250 chips. My current hand is 9c 4d, which is very weak. Claude raised to 250 from the button, and Minni folded. Although it's only 150 more to call, 9-4 offsuit plays very poorly out of position and is unlikely to be ahead of a standard button opening range. Given my stack advantage, I prefer to avoid marginal spots with trash hands and wait for better opportunities to utilize my leverage. I will fold this hand.

Button with K♥Q♣ in a three-handed pot — a very playable, high-equity hand that benefits hugely from position. I want to open and either take the blinds now or play postflop in position, so I’ll raise to a standard 3x BB (300). Size is big enough to get fold equity vs the blinds but not so large that I commit my stack unnecessarily. If I face a small/standard 3-bet I’ll often call and use position to play postflop; versus a large 3-bet (very big sizing) I’ll fold or re-evaluate depending on opponent and sizing. Postflop plan: C-bet on favorable K/Q/broadway flops, control the pot on wet boards, and extract value when I improve.

I am in the Small Blind with 6s 7d. Minni (Button) has raised to 300. While I have a significant chip lead over the table, 67-offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position, especially against a button open where the ranges are wide but my hand realization will be poor. Folding here preserves my stack and avoids a difficult post-flop situation against either the button or the big blind.

Holding 78o in the BB facing a button raise of 300. Need to call 200 into a pot of 450, getting 3.25:1 odds. 78o is a decent connected hand to defend against a likely wide button opening range. This is a standard BB defense spot - calling to see a flop in position with a playable hand.

I have 78o on a 9s6d3s flop - giving me an open-ended straight draw (need 5 or T). The pot is 650 and I'm first to act. With just a draw and no made hand, checking makes sense to see a free card or evaluate Minni's action. If they check back, I get a free turn card. If they bet, I can decide whether to call or check-raise based on sizing. No need to bet and bloat the pot with air when I can control pot size.

I opened to 300 from the button and Claude (BB) just called. Flop 9♠-6♦-3♠ is very dry and highly favorable for a continuation bet — I have two overcards (K♥Q♣), position and the range advantage as the preflop aggressor. A medium c‑bet around half the pot (~325 into 650) puts pressure on Claude’s calling range (many unpaired hands, small pairs and marginal holdings) and folds out a lot of equity while keeping the pot manageable if I get called. Plan: take the pot down now; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and continue only on favourable cards (K/Q or blank turns that allow a credible second barrel) and fold to significant flop/turn aggression since I currently have no pair.

I have 78o on 9s6d3s - an open-ended straight draw needing a 5 or T. Getting 3:1 odds (325 into 975) with ~32% equity to hit by river. This is a standard call with 8 clean outs. The spade draw on board doesn't help me but my straight outs are live.

The 5d on the turn completes my straight! I now have 5-6-7-8-9, which is the nuts on this board. No higher straight is possible. With two flush draws on board (spades and diamonds), I need to bet for value and protection. I'll bet around 65% pot to extract value from Minni's likely holdings (pairs, draws) while making draws pay incorrectly to continue.

Claude check-called my flop c-bet and now puts in a sizable turn bet (850 into a ~2150 pot). I have K♣Q♥ (two overcards) and position but no pair or real draw. Folding here is too nitty given the price and my positional advantage; raising is thin and would build a bigger pot with a hand that has poor equity. Calling 850 is the best option: it’s a modest portion of my stack (~9%), gives correct pot odds to see a river, keeps weaker hands and bluffs in Claude’s range, and preserves my ability to control/resize the pot on the river. Plan: call to see the river; fold to large river aggression if I don’t improve, and extract value or raise small if I pair up or Claude shows weakness.

I have a straight (5-6-7-8-9) on this river. The 7s completed a potential spade flush (9s, 3s, 7s on board). My straight is strong but loses to any flush. Minni called my turn bet - they could have a flush draw that just got there, or a pair/weaker draw. I should value bet thinly since I beat most of their calling range (pairs, weaker straights with a 4), but keep it small enough to fold to a raise. Around 1/3 pot seems right for thin value while controlling my losses if they show up with a flush.

I have K♥Q♣ on 9♠-6♦-3♠-5♦-7♠. Claude bet 1,000 into the ~3,000 pot (pot is 4,000 after his bet); calling 1,000 to win a 5,000 final pot requires ~20% equity. My hand missed — no pair, no spade — so I only beat pure bluffs or extremely thin value bets. Claude’s line (check-call on the flop, a big turn bet ≈850, then another river bet after a third spade appears) is much more consistent with value: pockets/single pairs, sets, or two-spade combos that just made a flush on the river. The bluff frequency I’d need (>20%) is higher than I assign here. Raising would be thin and would bloat the pot with very poor equity. With stack preservation and pot odds in mind I fold.

On the button in a 3-handed game, Jack-Ten offsuit (JTo) is a strong opening hand. I have the chip lead and the best position at the table. Raising to 300 is a standard move to take control of the pot, apply pressure to the blinds, and capitalize on my positional advantage. If either the small blind or big blind calls, JTo has decent post-flop playability.

Q7o in SB facing a button raise. This is a weak offsuit hand that plays poorly out of position. Not strong enough to 3-bet and calling will lead to difficult postflop situations. With a healthy stack of 19k and 30 hands remaining, no need to defend marginal hands OOP. Easy fold.

Facing a button open to 300 from the big blind with Q♠2♦. Pot is 450 and it costs me 200 to call (total 300 committed) — break-even equity ≈ 31% (pot odds ~3.25:1). Q2o is weak and often dominated, but against a wide BTN opening range it has enough raw equity and there are deep stacks (≈84bb) so implied odds justify a defend. A small reraise to 500 is unnecessary with this marginal hand and would put me OOP into a deeper-stacked opponent. Plan: call to see a flop, fold to heavy action unless I pair the Q, improve to two pair/straights/flushes or pick up clear equity; otherwise keep pot control.

I have Q♠2♦ on A♣ T♥ 9♦ — I completely missed. No pair, no meaningful straight or flush draw (only runner-runner outs), and I’m out of position vs the BTN who opened. Pot is small relative to stacks, so I don’t want to inflate it with air. Check for pot control and to see a free turn; plan is to fold to a standard continuation bet and only continue to the turn with a cheap probe or if I pair/meaningfully improve.

On this Ace-high flop, I have middle pair (Tens) and a gutshot straight draw. While I was the pre-flop aggressor and have a range advantage on this board, my specific hand has significant showdown value and doesn't necessarily need to turn into a bluff or a thin value bet immediately. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and potentially induce bluffs from Minni on later streets or see a free turn card to improve to a straight or two pair.

Board A♣ T♥ 9♦ 6♣ with Q♠2♦ in the big blind — I completely missed. I’m out of position against the BTN who opened preflop and, although both streets checked, his range still contains many A/T/9 combos that beat me. The pot (650) is small relative to my stack (8185), and betting the turn with pure air buys little fold equity and can get me called by better hands. Best line is pot control: check and see a river for free. If the river pairs my Q or brings a favorable card I can consider bluffing or a thin value attempt; otherwise I’ll fold to meaningful river aggression.

I have middle pair (Tens) with a decent kicker and a gutshot straight draw. After checking back the flop to control the pot, the turn 6c is a relatively blank card. Minni's second check suggests she might be in check-call mode with a weaker hand or is playing a marginal hand passively. Since my hand has good showdown value but is vulnerable to an Ace or a better Ten, checking back again allows me to realize my equity and keep the pot small, ensuring I see the river and potentially reach a cheap showdown.

I have Q♠2♦ on A♣ T♥ 9♦ 6♣ A♠. I never improved and have essentially no showdown value. Both streets were checked, so the button's range still contains a lot of Ax, Tx/9x, and broadway hands that will either beat me or call a bluff. The river pairing an Ace actually reduces my fold equity — many hands that beat me will happily call, and the hands I might fold out are unlikely to pay off enough to make a bluff +EV. The pot is small (~650) relative to stacks, so risking chips with air is unnecessary. Best line is pot control: check and fold to meaningful aggression. If the opponent checks back I keep my stack intact; if they bet, I’ll fold unless it’s a very tiny probe.

I have middle pair (Tens) with an Ace on the board, and a second Ace appeared on the river. The board is now Ac-Th-9d-6c-As. Minni has checked all three streets (Flop, Turn, and River). My hand, J-10, has significant showdown value as two pair (Aces and Tens). While I could potentially bet for thin value, hoping to be called by a 9 or a small pocket pair, checking back is the most prudent play. It ensures I reach showdown without risking a check-raise in a small pot. Given the passive action, I am very likely ahead, and checking back guarantees I realize that equity.
On the button with T7o against blinds. Marginal hand but position is great for a steal attempt. Standard 2.5x raise to put pressure on the blinds.

I’m in the small blind with 9♠5♦ facing a BTN open to 250 (pot 400) and must call 200. 9-5 offsuit is a weak, poorly-connected hand that will be dominated by the button’s raising range and plays very badly out of position. With the big blind still to act it’s likely to become a multiway or a tough heads-up OOP pot. A thin 3-bet bluff is an option occasionally, but against two deep stacks here it’s not a +EV spot — I don’t want to get committed out of position with such a marginal holding. I fold and wait for a better spot to invest chips.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Eight offsuit. Claude has raised from the Button to 2.5bb (250). With the Small Blind folding, I only need to call 150 more into a pot of 400. This gives me excellent pot odds of approximately 2.67-to-1. In a three-handed game, Q8o is well above the equity threshold needed to defend against a standard Button opening range. Given my large stack, I can comfortably call to see a flop out of position and look to realize my equity.

I have Queen-high on a Jack-high board after defending my Big Blind. This board texture (J-3-4) doesn't offer much for my specific hand, as I have no pairs or draws. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to him and see how he reacts. I have no reason to lead out here with a marginal hand.

Dry board with Jd 3d 4s. I have T7h with no pair and minimal equity (just a gutshot needing an 8). Flash checked to me after calling pre-flop. This is a standard c-bet spot - I raised pre-flop, have position, and the board favors my perceived range. A small c-bet of ~35% pot should take this down often enough to be profitable.

I defended the Big Blind with Q8o and completely missed the flop of J-3-4. Claude has continued with a bet of 200 into a 550 pot. While the pot odds are relatively good (calling 200 to win 750), I have no pair, no draws, and no backdoor equity. Queen-high has very little showdown value on this board and I am out of position. Continuing here without a plan to bluff is a leak, and since I have no equity to fall back on, the most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

On the button with J♣8♣ (suited, decent playability). It's 3-handed and both blinds are deep, so opening here to take the initiative and pick up the blinds is profitable. A standard 3x open (300 total) applies pressure, avoids being cheaply called by the big blind, and leaves plenty of stack behind to play postflop. If called I'll c-bet favorable flops and use position to realize equity; vs a small 3-bet I may call for implied odds, but will fold to large 3-bets/4-bets. Overall an aggressive open from the button maximizes expected value here.

In a 3-handed game, Ace-Eight offsuit is a relatively strong hand. Minni is raising from the button, which is a wide range. I have the chip lead and am getting good odds to see a flop. While a 3-bet is an option to isolate, calling allows me to control the pot size out of position and evaluate the flop. I will call the additional 250 chips.

JQo in the BB facing a min-raise and a call. Getting 3.5:1 pot odds (200 to win 700). JQo plays well in multiway pots and I close the action. Easy call here - hand is too strong to fold and not quite strong enough to 3-bet squeeze against two players.

I have top pair with a moderate kicker on an Ace-high board. Checking to the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) is the standard line here. My hand is strong enough to check-call most bets, but I want to keep the pot manageable and see how the other two players react to this board.

I flopped second pair (queens) with jack kicker on A-Q-4 two-heart board. Flash checked to me. In a 3-way pot with an ace on board, my middle pair has showdown value but isn't strong enough to bet for value - I'd only get called by better hands (aces) or draws. Checking controls the pot and lets me see a cheap turn. If Minni bets, I can evaluate whether to continue based on sizing.

Three-way flop with A♣? (A♥) Q♠ 4♥ — I have J♣8♣: no pair, no meaningful draw and very little equity. Both opponents checked to me after my preflop open; multiway pots massively reduce the profitability of a bluff c-bet on an Ace-high board. With deep stacks and weak equity I want to control the pot and see a cheap turn in position. Plan: check back, fold to sizeable aggression on the turn unless I improve (club or pair), and look to take the pot on later streets if the board runs out favorably or opponents continue to show weakness.

I have top pair (Aces) with an 8 kicker. The flop went check-check-check, which indicates that neither Minni (the pre-flop raiser) nor Claude likely has a dominant hand like AK, AQ, or a set. Since I most likely have the best hand, I should bet for value and to protect against potential draws (hearts or gutshots). A bet of half the pot is appropriate to extract value from weaker pairs or draws.

I have second pair (queens) with a jack kicker. Flash checked flop and now bets 450 into 900 on the turn. Getting 3:1 pot odds (450 to win 1350), I need ~25% equity. Flash's delayed bet could be an ace, a queen, a heart draw, or air. My QJ beats draws and bluffs, though an ace is certainly possible. With these pot odds and still having Minni behind, calling is reasonable - folding is too tight and raising overplays my hand against Flash's range which likely includes aces.

Three‑way pot, I opened preflop with J♣8♣ and checked the Ace‑high Q♠4♥ flop. The turn 5♠ is not an improvement for me and I have no meaningful draw (no backdoor spade/heart/straight). Flash leads for 450 and Claude has called — so I’m effectively facing aggression from two players. My only realistic outs are three Jacks + three Eights = 6 outs (~13% to pair on the river). Even if the pot is ~2,700 after the bet/call, calling 450 would require roughly 14–15% equity to be break‑even; my raw equity is slightly below that and will be worse versus callers holding Ax/Qx or middle pairs. Raising into two callers with one pair boards is spewy and will commit chips with almost no fold equity. With deep stacks I can preserve 7.8k and wait for a better spot. Fold.

On the river 9s, the board is Ah-Qs-4h-5s-9s. I have top pair with an 8 kicker. The flop checked around, and I led the turn for half pot (450), which Claude called. This suggests Claude has a marginal hand, likely a weaker Ace or a Queen. The 9s is relatively safe, though it could have improved a hand like A9 to two pair. However, given Claude's passive play, I believe I still have the best hand most of the time. I will place a value bet of 900 (half pot) to extract value from his marginal holdings like KQ, QJ, or lower Aces that are likely to call a single bet on the river but won't bet themselves if checked to.

River decision with second pair queens, jack kicker. Flash bet 900 into 2700 pot (~1/3 pot sizing). Getting 4:1 odds (900 to win 3600), needing ~20% equity. Flash's line (check flop, bet turn small, bet river small) could be thin value with an ace, a worse queen, or a missed heart draw bluffing. The small sizing suggests either thin value or blocking. My QJ beats all queens except QA and QK, and beats all bluffs. At these odds, folding second pair with good kicker is too tight - I only need to be good 20% of the time. Call.

I am currently the chip leader with a significant margin. I am on the button with Jack-Trey offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. While stealing blinds is a viable strategy 3-handed, the Big Blind (Minni) is relatively short-stacked (77 BBs) and might look to shove over a loose open. There is no reason to risk chips with such a low-equity hand when I can wait for better spots to maintain my lead.

Button folded, leaving me heads up against the BB. Q4 suited is playable in this spot. I should raise to put pressure on Minni who has a shorter stack (7735). A standard raise to 250 is appropriate here.

I’m in the big blind with 3♣9♠ — a very weak, poorly playable offsuit hand out of position. Claude opened to 250 from the small blind; I must call 150 into a 350 pot, which gives ~30% immediate pot odds. 3‑9o has well below that equity versus a normal SB opening range and will be hard to realize OOP (often dominated, few profitable post‑flop lines). A 3‑bet or shove here is unnecessary and speculative. Preserve chips for better spots and fold.

3-handed on the button with T5o. While this is a weak hand, button position in 3-handed play warrants a wide opening range. I should raise to steal the blinds - if I get 3-bet I can easily fold. Standard 2.5x open to 250.

Claude opened to 250 from the button; I’m in the small blind with Q♣?Q♥J♦ (QJ offsuit). Calling the extra 200 (250 total in the pot) is the right play: QJoff has decent heads-up equity and it’s cheap relative to my 7,685 stack, while a 3-bet from the SB out of position with a marginal hand is overly committal. Calling keeps the pot manageable, lets me see a flop and re-evaluate (I’ll fold to a large 3-bet, or continue cautiously post-flop — c-bet or check/float based on texture).

I am in the Big Blind with King-Five offsuit. Claude raised from the button and Minni called from the small blind. I am getting 4:1 pot odds (150 to call into a 600 pot) to see a flop. Given my significant chip lead and the favorable price, I will call to close the action and see a flop. While K5o is a marginal hand, it has reasonable equity against wide button opening ranges and small blind flatting ranges in a three-handed game.

Flop: 3♣ 8♠ 9♠. I have Q♥ J♦ — two overcards and a gutshot to a 10 (4 outs), but no spade flush draw. There are two opponents (BB and button) and I’m out of position, so c-betting with only overcards + a gutshot into two players is thin and will often get called by spade draws/pairs. Pot is 750 and my stack is 7,485 so I can cheaply see a turn — I want to control the pot and gather information. Plan: check — call a small/standard c-bet (≈≤1/3 pot) to realize equity and fold to big overbets; if both check through I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and look to value/continue only if I improve (pair, T for the straight) or get a favorable texture to bluff one player.

I have King-high on a board of 3-8-9 with two spades. I missed the flop completely and am currently out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Claude). Minni has already checked in the small blind. Given that I have no pair and no draw, there is no reason to bet here. I will check to see how the pre-flop aggressor acts. My goal is to play cautiously with marginal hands and protect my significant chip lead as the tournament nears its end.

Flop is 3c 8s 9s. I have T5o - complete air with no pair, no flush draw, and only a weak gutshot (need a 7 for T-9-8-7-6 but I don't have the 6). Both opponents checked to me showing weakness. While this could be a c-bet spot, I have very little equity and betting just burns chips. Better to check behind and take a free card - if a T or 5 comes I'll have a pair.

Turn card (Jh) pairs my Jack: I now have top pair with a Queen kicker. The flop checked through with three players, so opponents’ ranges are wide (weak pairs, spade/straight draws, broadways). Betting for value and protection is correct — I want to charge the T-gutshots and spade draws and extract from worse Jx, 9x/8x and small pocket pairs while keeping the pot controllable. I choose a ~0.5–0.6 pot bet (400 into 750) to accomplish that. If I face a large raise (polarized/committing), I’ll fold; I’ll call a small single raise and re-evaluate on the river based on the river card and opponent sizing.

On a board of 3c 8s 9s Jh, I have King-high with no pair and no real draw. Minni has led out for 400 into a 750 pot after the flop was checked around.
