While I have a massive chip lead, there is no reason to continue with a hand that has almost zero equity against a range that is betting into two players. Claude is also still to act behind me. Conserving chips and avoiding unnecessary risks with marginal hands is the optimal strategy at this stage of the tournament.

I have T5o on a 3c 8s 9s Jh board. Minni bet 400 into 750 pot. I have a gutshot straight draw - need a Q to make Q-J-T-9-8. That's 4 outs, roughly 8% equity on the river. Pot odds require 400/1550 = 26% equity, so pure pot odds don't support a call. Even with implied odds against Minni's ~7k stack, calling a gutshot here is -EV. Easy fold.

6♣3♥ is a very weak, easily dominated hand. Pot is 150 and I must call 100 (or raise to 200) — calling 100 into this spot with two deep, competent opponents will put me in tough post‑flop spots with minimal equity. A min‑raise to attempt a steal is risky versus deep stacks who defend/3‑bet frequently. Preserve my 8,235 chip stack for better spots; fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace of Clubs, Eight of Clubs, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. Since the Button folded, it is down to me and the Big Blind (Claude). Raising is the standard play here to put pressure on the Big Blind and build a pot with a hand that has high equity. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate to challenge Claude's wide defending range.

I have pocket tens in the BB - a premium hand 3-handed. Flash raised to 300 from SB. With TT, I should 3-bet for value. A standard sizing would be around 900-1000. This builds the pot with a strong hand and defines opponent's range.

I have Ace of Clubs, Eight of Clubs in a Small Blind vs. Big Blind battle. After my initial open to 300, Claude 3-bet to 950. In a 3-handed game, A8s is a very strong hand with significant equity. Claude's 3-bet is relatively small (a 'click-back'), providing me with excellent pot odds (650 to call into a pot of 1,250). While I will be out of position post-flop, folding is out of the question given my hand strength and stack lead. Calling is preferable to 4-betting here as it allows me to keep Claude's bluffs and weaker Ax hands in the pot while realizing my equity on the flop without over-inflating the pot prematurely.

I have flopped middle pair (8s) with an Ace kicker in a 3-bet pot. On a relatively dry board of J-8-4, my hand is strong enough to continue but not strong enough to lead out into the pre-flop aggressor. By checking, I control the size of the pot and allow Claude to continuation bet with his entire range, including his air and overcards. I plan to check-call a standard-sized bet to see a turn and evaluate.

I have TT on an 8s 4c Jd board. Flash called my 3-bet and checked. The Jack is the only overcard to my tens. I have an overpair to the 8 and 4, which is a strong hand. Flash's checking range includes draws, weak pairs, and some Jx hands. I should bet for value and protection against overcards. Around 60% pot (~1100-1200) is a good sizing to get value from weaker pairs and draws while not bloating the pot too much against better hands.

I have middle pair (8s) with the best possible kicker (Ace). On a board of J-8-4 rainbow, this is a very strong hand to defend in a 3-bet pot, especially in 3-handed play where ranges are wider. Claude's bet of 1150 into a pot of approximately 2000 (roughly 57% pot) is a standard continuation bet that he could be making with a wide variety of hands, including overcards like AK, AQ, and KQ, or smaller pairs. I don't want to raise here because I would likely only be called by a Jack or better, effectively turning my hand into a bluff. By calling, I keep Claude's bluffs in his range and can re-evaluate on the turn.

I have middle pair with an Ace kicker on a relatively dry board of Jack-Eight-Four-Three. In this 3-bet pot where Claude was the pre-flop and flop aggressor, my hand functions best as a bluff catcher. The 3s on the turn is a blank that doesn't significantly alter the board texture. Leading into the aggressor here would likely only result in being called by better hands (Jack-x, overpairs, or sets) and folding out his bluffs. By checking, I control the pot size and allow Claude to continue with his bluffs or check back with marginal hands that I currently beat. I will check and re-evaluate based on his action.

Turn 3s is a blank that changes nothing. I still have an overpair to most of the board. Flash check-called flop which could be Jx, medium pairs, or now a spade draw with the second spade on board. I should continue betting for value against worse pairs and to charge draws. Betting around 60-65% pot (~2600-2800) is appropriate.

I am holding middle pair (eights) with an Ace kicker on a relatively dry board of Jack-Eight-Four-Three. In three-handed play, especially in a Small Blind vs. Big Blind dynamic, this hand is a very strong candidate for a bluff-catch. Claude's 3-bet range pre-flop and his subsequent double-barrel on the flop and turn could represent a Jack or an overpair, but it also contains many semi-bluffs or air such as Ace-King, Ace-Queen, or King-Queen. The pot odds of roughly 28% (calling 2700 into a 9600 pot) make this a clear call. I will call to see the river and evaluate whether to call a potential final shove from Claude.

On this river, I have second pair (Eights) with the best possible kicker (Ace). The board of J-8-4-3-9 is relatively disconnected, with no potential straights or flushes. Since I am first to act on the river and have a hand with significant showdown value, checking is the most prudent play. If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I would be forced to fold if Claude raises. By checking, I can potentially induce a bluff from missed overcards like AK, AQ, or KQ, or simply reach a cheap showdown against other marginal hands. If Claude bets, I will evaluate the size and the likelihood that he is value-betting a Jack or an overpair versus bluffing.

River 9c completes some straights (QT, T7) and gives 99 trips. Flash has check-called two decent-sized bets, suggesting they have something - likely Jx, a set, or medium pair. My TT is only an overpair to 8-4-3-9 but loses to any Jack. Value betting here is thin - worse hands fold (missed draws, small pairs) while better hands (Jx, sets, 99 now) call or raise. Best play is to check back and showdown. If Flash has Jx I lose, but if they have a missed draw or small pair I win.

I am on the button with Ten-Six offsuit. While 3-handed play warrants a wide opening range from the button, T6o is at the very bottom of that range. Given that Claude (SB) has been playing very aggressively and Minni (BB) has a relatively short stack (about 81 big blinds) which might provoke a shove, I choose to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better opportunity to utilize my chip lead.

Flash folded, leaving me heads up against Minni in the BB. Q3s is a reasonable hand to raise from the SB heads up - I have a suited queen and significant stack advantage (22k vs 8k). Standard raise to 250 to put pressure on Minni.

I'm in the big blind with A♦7♣ facing a standard SB open to 250. Pot is 350; calling 150 (250 total) gives me good pot odds — I need roughly 30% equity to break even. Claude's frequent 250 opens indicate a relatively wide range from the SB, so A7o has decent equity and good post-flop playability. A min 3-bet to 400 is too small to fold out many of his hands and would leave me out of position post-flop; with ~81BB effective it's better to keep the pot controllable and realize equity. Plan: call, evaluate the flop (value bet/raise when I pair the Ace, proceed cautiously or fold to heavy aggression on dry boards).

Flop is Kh 6d 4d - I completely whiffed with Q3s. No pair, no draw (my spades don't help with the diamond board). Against Minni who called preflop, they could easily have a king, pocket pair, or diamond draw. With no equity and pure air, I'll check and give up rather than bluff into a board that likely connects with their calling range.

I have A♦7♣ on K♥6♦4♦. I called preflop and Claude — the preflop opener — checked the flop, which looks like a fairly dry K‑high board that misses my hand. I only have Ace‑high and a very remote two‑card backdoor diamond possibility; nothing worth building a big pot OOP with. With ~80BB effective I should pot‑control and realize equity rather than bluffing into a wide but often better raiser. Plan: check and re‑evaluate on the turn. If Claude fires a small c‑bet I can call to see a turn (looking to hit an A/7 or pick up additional information); if he bets a large sized c‑bet I will fold — I don’t want to commit with just Ace‑high. If checked through I’ll look to take a stab on safe turns or value bet only when I pair the Ace or improve.

Turn 7h doesn't help me at all - still have queen high with no pair or draw. Minni checked behind on flop which could be weakness or trapping. With zero equity (no pair, no straight draw, wrong suit for flush draw), I should check and try to see a free river. Bluffing here makes no sense with pure air.

Turn brought the 7h, pairing my 7 — I now have second pair (A♦7♣) with an Ace kicker. Claude opened wide from the SB preflop and has checked both flop and turn, which often indicates a missed or marginal holding rather than a strong King or a slowplayed set. With ~80BB effective and a 500-chip pot, a half‑pot probe (250) is the best play: it extracts value from worse hands (A‑high, weaker pairs, floats), folds pure air, and keeps the pot manageable out of position. If Claude calls I’ll re-evaluate on the river; if he raises big I’ll fold this vulnerable second pair to avoid committing with a likely behind hand.

Minni checked flop and now leads 250 into 500 on the turn. I have Q3s - complete air with no pair, no draw (diamonds on board, I have spades). Options: Fold loses 250 already invested. Calling to bluff river is speculative with zero equity. Raising as a bluff is risky since Minni's small bet could easily be a weak made hand that won't fold. With no equity and facing a bet, the disciplined play is to fold and preserve chips.

I have 65s on the button in a 3-handed game. This is a solid hand to open from the button - suited connectors play well in position. I should raise to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take the pot down preflop, or build a pot where I can outplay them postflop with position.

I have K♠T♦ in the small blind facing a button open to 250 (pot 400, I need +200). KTs is a playable hand with good equity and postflop playability, and the call is cheap relative to my stack. From the SB I’ll be out of position vs the button on all postflop streets, so 3-betting light here surrenders a lot of postflop leverage and can get me involved OOP in a bloated pot or face a 4-bet. I’ll call 200 more (total commitment 250) to see a flop and proceed based on texture: continue aggressively with top pair or strong draws, and fold to heavy action on dry, unfavorable boards. This keeps the pot manageable and preserves stack equity while realizing the hand’s postflop potential.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Trey offsuit. Claude raised from the button and Minni called from the Small Blind. Although I am getting 4-to-1 pot odds to call (150 to call into a 600 pot), this hand is extremely weak and plays poorly out of position, especially in a multi-way pot. With a significant chip lead and approaching the end of the tournament (hand 127 of 150), I prefer to wait for better spots rather than defending with junk hands that often lead to difficult post-flop decisions.

I called preflop with K♠T♦ to see a flop. The board K♦ A♥ 6♠ gives me top pair with a mediocre Ten kicker and I’m out of position vs Claude (he opened from the button; Flash folded preflop). Claude’s button raising range contains a lot of A‑x that dominate me, so betting OOP here would bloat the pot when I’m frequently behind. Checking controls the pot, keeps his bluffs and thin value bets in play, and lets me react to his flop action and to turn cards. Plan: check and evaluate — call a small/medium c‑bet and re‑assess on the turn; fold or proceed cautiously to large aggression showing strong strength. Checking preserves stack equity and maximizes EV with this marginal top pair OOP.

Flopped bottom pair with 65cc on an AK6 board. This is a very unfavorable texture for my hand - Minni likely has ace-high or king-high hands in their calling range from the SB. My bottom pair has showdown value but if I bet, I only get called by hands that beat me. Checking back to control pot size and potentially improve on later streets is the best play here.

I hold K♠T♦ — top pair but a weak ten‑kicker and I'm out of position vs Claude's button open. His raising range contains a lot of A‑x that dominate me, and both players checked the flop so there’s a reasonable chance he has either an Ace, a draw, or a hand he’s happy to check. The 8♦ on the turn is a largely non‑threatening card that rarely turns my hand into a clear favorite. Betting now would bloat the pot and get called mostly by hands that already beat me (A‑x) or by worse kings; checking controls the pot, keeps bluffs and worse hands in, preserves stack equity, and lets me re‑evaluate based on his turn/river action. Plan: check the turn, call small/medium river bets for showdown value, and fold to large aggression.

I have bottom pair (6s) on a Kd Ah 6s 8d board. Minni has checked twice now. Betting here is risky - I'll only get called by hands that beat me (any A, K, or 8). My pair of sixes has some showdown value but isn't strong enough to bet for value. Checking to see a free river is optimal - I can potentially improve or check down and win at showdown against missed draws or weaker hands.

Board is K♦ A♥ 6♠ 8♦ 2♣ and I hold K♠ T♦ — top pair with a weak kicker. Both players checked flop and turn and there has been no bet on the river; Claude’s button open and passive line are consistent with many A‑x hands that beat me or medium Kx hands that I can only get thin value from. Betting here would mainly fold out worse kings/bluffs while getting called by the hands that already beat me (and it risks a raise OOP). Pot is small (600) relative to stacks, so the lowest-variance +EV line is to check and take my showdown rather than bluff/value-bet into a range that often dominates me.

River completes with 2c. I have bottom pair (6s) on Kd Ah 6s 8d 2c. Minni has checked all three streets. My pair of sixes is a marginal showdown hand. Betting for value is risky - I only get called by better hands (Ax, Kx, 88, 8x). Checking to show down is optimal here to realize whatever equity my bottom pair has against Minni's weak checking range.

I have Q♦5♥ on the button with deep stacks (≈88BB). In 3‑handed play the button should open a wide range — Q5o is marginal but playable with position. A min‑raise to 200 is too small and will invite too many callers; I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x BB) to generate fold equity and possibly isolate one blind. Plan: fold to a sizable 3‑bet, and if called proceed cautiously — c‑bet on favorable/dry flops and give up to heavy action when I’m likely dominated.

In a 3-handed game, Jack-Ten of Spades is a premium starting hand. When the Button (Minni) opens to 300, we are in the Small Blind. This hand has excellent playability and realizes its equity well post-flop. While a 3-bet is a viable aggressive option to take the initiative, calling allows us to see a flop with a strong speculative hand while keeping the pot manageable against the short-stacked Button. It also invites the Big Blind (Claude) into a potential three-way pot where JTs performs well due to its straight and flush potential.

4-2 offsuit is one of the worst starting hands in poker. While I'm getting 3.5:1 on a call, this hand has terrible playability and will rarely make a strong hand. With 22 hands left in the tournament and a healthy stack of 21,965, there's no reason to defend trash here. Easy fold.

On this flop of Ks 3d Tc, I have second pair with Jack-Ten of Spades. While this is a decent hand in a three-handed game, the presence of an overcard (King) makes it vulnerable. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Minni), checking is the standard play to control the pot and evaluate the strength of her continuation bet. My hand has enough value to call a standard bet, but leading out would likely only get called by better hands or folds from worse ones. I will check and see how the action develops.

I’m the preflop raiser (button) with Q♦5♥ on a dry K♠‑3♦‑T♣ flop and the SB checked. As the aggressor I have a range advantage and should c‑bet to fold out hands that missed and extract value from weaker holdings. A half‑pot stab (≈350 into 700) gives good fold equity while keeping the pot manageable if called. Plan: take it down now if he folds; if he calls I proceed cautiously on the turn (check most blank turns, continue only with a Queen/5 or a clear read); fold to a significant check‑raise since Q5o has little showdown equity vs real hands that would raise.

I have middle pair (Tens) with a Jack kicker on a King-high board. In a heads-up pot against the pre-flop raiser (Minni), middle pair is a strong enough hand to continue against a standard half-pot continuation bet. I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call, and my hand still has potential to improve to two pair or trips on the turn. Raising would likely only get called by a King or better, so calling is the most effective way to keep her bluffs in and see how the turn develops.

On the turn 6s, I have improved my hand significantly. While I still hold second pair (Tens), I have now picked up a flush draw to go with my gutshot straight draw (Queen). Since I am out of position against Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the most sound strategy. It allows her to continue bluffing if she missed or to value bet a King, which I can comfortably call given my current pair and extensive draws. Leading out would likely only be called by better hands, whereas checking keeps my range protected and allows me to see a river efficiently.

I c-bet the flop but was called; the 6♠ on the turn is a blank for my Q♦5♥. I have no pair and only ~6 outs (3 queens, 3 fives) — very poor equity against a caller’s likely range (Kx, Tx, middle pairs, small pocket pairs or draws). With the pot at 1,400 and both stacks deep, I want to control the size of the pot and avoid turning a marginal hand into a large losing spot. Checking is the prudent play: it keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize my (small) showdown equity, and induces bluffs I can potentially call on the river. I’ll fold to any large turn shove or overbet; I’ll re-evaluate on the river if checked to or facing a small bet.

On the river, the board has paired with sixes, and I hold Tens and Sixes (two pair) with a King kicker from the board. Looking back at the action, Minni (the pre-flop aggressor) bet the flop but checked the turn when the flush draw and straight possibilities increased. This check on the turn often indicates a marginal hand like a weak King, a Ten, or a missed draw. By checking the river, I am playing for a safe showdown. My hand has significant showdown value in a three-handed environment, and checking allows Minni to bluff with her missed draws (like QJ or Q9) which I can then consider calling. Betting myself is unlikely to get called by many worse hands and might only force folds from hands I already beat.

Board K♠‑3♦‑T♣‑6♠‑6♦ and I hold Q♦5♥ — I missed entirely and have no pair. Villain called my flop c‑bet and then checked the turn and river, which suggests a wide range of medium/strong hands (Kx, Tx, small pairs, any 6) that beat me, or a very weak hand that will also fold to a small bet. With the pot at 1,400 and deep stacks, a river bluff has poor expected value (many hands that beat me won’t fold; I’d be risking too much for little fold equity). I’ll check and take my showdown (and fold to any significant bet on the river).

I am on the button with Queen-Nine offsuit in a three-handed game. Q9o is a strong enough hand to open for a raise from the button to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take down the pot uncontested. A standard 3x open (300 chips) is consistent with my previous play and appropriate given the stack depths, particularly as Minni is the shorter stack at approximately 80 big blinds.

Pocket 5s in the SB facing a button raise of 300. This is a standard set-mining spot. I have good implied odds with deep stacks (219bb effective vs Flash). Calling 250 more into a pot that will be ~600-900 gives me reasonable odds to see a flop and potentially stack someone if I hit a set. 3-betting isn't as appealing since 55 plays poorly against a 4-bet range.

I have A♣3♥ in the big blind facing a button open to 300 with the small blind calling. This is a multiway pot and I'm out of position versus both players, so a raise (min to 500) is thin — A3o is easily dominated by the button's raising range and Claude's calling range and I don't want to inflate the pot OOP with a marginal hand. Calling costs 200 more into a 700 pot (pot will be ~900 after my call), so I'm getting roughly 22% pot odds to see a flop. With deep stacks it's correct to realize equity cheaply: call to see a flop and proceed only if I hit an ace/3 or get a favorable draw; otherwise play cautiously and fold to heavy action.

Missed my set on A-T-3 board with two spades. My pocket 5s have almost no showdown value here - any Ace, Ten, or overpair beats me. No reason to bet into two opponents on this board texture. Checking and likely folding to any bet is the best line.

I have A♣3♥ on 3♠ T♠ A♦ — two pair (Aces and Threes). This is a multiway pot and I'm out of position to both opponents. Two pair is strong but vulnerable to sets/trips and to spade turn/river draws, so I want to extract value and charge draws rather than slow-play. A bet around 2/3 pot will get value from worse A-x and T-x hands and force equity decisions for two‑spade draws while keeping worse hands in. If I face a raise I can re-evaluate (raise would often represent trips/sets). Therefore I bet 600 into the 900 pot.

On a flop of 3s-Ts-Ad, I have completely missed with my Q9o. Minni has led out for 600 into a 900 pot (a 2/3 pot bet) from the big blind after I was the pre-flop aggressor. This 'donk bet' often signifies a hand that wants to protect against draws or a made hand like an Ace or a Ten. With no pair and no real draw, there is no reason to continue. Claude is also still to act behind me, making a bluff or a float even less profitable. Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

Facing 600 bet from Minni on A-T-3 two-spade board with pocket 5s. I'm a massive underdog here - any ace, ten, or overpair beats me. I need ~29% equity to call but have maybe 8% if I'm drawing to a set. No flush draw, no real equity. Easy fold.
J7 suited on the button is a decent stealing hand. With only the blinds left to act, I should open raise to try to take down the blinds or play in position. Standard button open to 2.5x.

Call to 250 total. 10♣7♣ is a suited, playable hand in a 3‑handed pot with deep stacks: it has decent equity and good implied odds vs a button open. Calling costs 200 into a 400 pot (pot after my call ~600), so I need roughly 33% equity — reachable with this hand against a wide opening range and with flush/straight potential. A 3‑bet here is marginal (hand not strong enough to build a large pot OOP, and Flash in the BB can squeeze or call), and a min‑raise to 400 is too small to earn meaningful fold equity. Plan: see the flop and play straightforwardly — continue aggressively with top pair/strong draws, check‑call or fold to heavy pressure on dry boards, and use implied odds when I hit hidden flushes/straights.

I am currently the chip leader with 29,450 chips. In this hand, the button (Claude) raised to 250 and the small blind (Minni) called. I am in the big blind with Ten of Hearts and Trey of Diamonds (T3o). While the cost to call is only 150 chips into a 600-chip pot (giving me 4:1 pot odds), T3o is an extremely weak hand that performs poorly in multi-way pots, especially when out of position post-flop. Given that I have a substantial lead and we are nearing the end of the tournament (Hand 130 of 150), there is no need to risk additional chips or get into a complicated post-flop situation with a junk hand. I will choose to fold and wait for better opportunities to leverage my stack.

Flop K♠ 4♣ K♦. I hold T♣7♣ — no pair, no immediate straight, only a runner‑runner club possibility (very weak equity). Claude was the preflop raiser so this paired king flop heavily favors his range (many Kx and strong hands). I’m out of position and have almost no fold‑equity or realistic outs vs a King. Leading or bluffing here is high risk and -EV. I will check to control the pot and to let Claude show aggression; plan is to fold to any meaningful c‑bet and only continue if he checks back (free card) or a very favorable turn (a club giving a real draw) changes the situation.

Paired king board (Ks 4c Kd) and Minni checked. I have nothing but jack high with backdoor diamond draw. This is a good spot for a c-bet since the paired board makes it unlikely either of us connected, and Minni's check shows weakness. A small bet around 40% pot should fold out most of their range that missed.

Board K♠ 4♣ K♦; I hold T♣7♣. Claude was the pre‑flop raiser so his range is heavily weighted to Kx and other strong hands that flop well on a paired King board. I have no pair and only a backdoor flush possibility (need both turn and river to be clubs).

Pot math: pot = 850; Claude bets 250 → pot becomes 1100. Calling 250 requires ~250/1100 ≈ 22.7% equity to break even. My realistic equity here is tiny (runner‑runner club flush ≈ (10/47)*(9/46) ≈ 4.2%, plus negligible extra chances), well below the needed ~23% versus a typical c‑bet range.

Raising as a bluff OOP is high risk with minimal fold equity against a single deep‑stack opponent who opened preflop. Preserving chips for better spots and stronger equity is the correct long‑term play. Therefore I fold.

I’m on the button with T♣2♦ (Ten–Deuce offsuit) — a very weak, poorly connected hand that’s frequently dominated. There are 3 players and both opponents are deep-stacked, so min-raising to try a steal could easily be met with calls or 3-bets and put me in difficult multi-way pots. Calling 100 to see a flop is cheap, but it will often leave me with little equity and uncomfortable post‑flop decisions despite position. With ~85 BB I don’t need to gamble with marginal holdings; better to wait for a hand with real playability or a clearer steal spot. Fold and conserve the stack for a higher EV opportunity.

With Minni folding on the button, it's down to a blind versus blind battle. J8o is a strong enough hand to open for a raise in this situation, especially as I am the chip leader. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take down the pot immediately.

6h8s is a marginal hand but I'm getting 2:1 pot odds to call 200 into a 400 pot. This is a connected hand that can flop straights and two pairs. Against a SB raise heads up, I have position post-flop. At this price, defending makes sense.

I have top pair (Jacks) with an 8 kicker on a board of Jd 9c 2d. I was the pre-flop aggressor and should lead out with a continuation bet for value and to protect my hand against various straight and flush draws. Claude's range as the Big Blind is wide, and I likely have the best hand here. A standard bet of half the pot (300) is appropriate to charge drawing hands while still getting value from 9x or smaller pocket pairs.
I have 6h8s on a 2d Jd 9c board - completely missed. No pair, no flush draw, and only a very weak backdoor straight possibility. Flash bet half pot and I have nothing to continue with. Easy fold.

With only 18 hands remaining in the tournament and a substantial chip lead, my primary goal is to maintain my stack and avoid unnecessary risks with marginal hands. Hand 132, 3-handed, on the Button with Queen-Five offsuit is typically considered a fold in most standard opening ranges. While the Button can open wide, Q5o is weak and does not play well post-flop, especially against Claude and Minni who have shown they are willing to defend or 3-bet. Folding preserves my chips for better spots where I can apply pressure with more equity.

J8 suited in the small blind with just Minni in the BB after Flash folded. This is a playable hand heads-up. I should raise to put pressure on Minni and try to take the pot preflop or build a pot with a connected suited hand that has decent playability postflop. Standard raise to 250.

Flash folded so this is effectively heads-up: Claude opened to 250 from the small blind and I’m in the big blind with 9♠3♥ — a very weak, unsuited hand with almost no post‑flop playability. Calling 150 to play out of position against an opener who often raises from the SB will put me in marginal spots with little equity; a small 3‑bet to 400 as a bluff is too thin with this holding (it will be called or dominated too often). Stack depth is healthy, so preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is the +EV line. I fold.
9s4c is a weak offsuit hand. While I have position on the button, this hand is too weak to open-raise profitably. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I’m in the small blind with 2♠9♥ heads-up vs the big blind. This is one of the worst starting hands (very low absolute strength and often dominated), and I’ll be out of position post‑flop. Calling 50 to see a flop is paper‑thin EV — I rarely improve and will be playing difficult pots out of position — and raising with 9‑2o is a pure leak. With healthy stacks and only three players left I want to pick better spots to build my stack rather than invest here. Fold and wait for a stronger hand or a better positional spot.

On the button with Kc Js and ~84 BB effective, this is a standard open-raise spot. KJo is strong and plays well in position, so I want to take the initiative and shrink the field. I’ll raise to 3x the big blind (300 total) to deny the blinds proper odds and give myself postflop leverage. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold; versus a small 3-bet I’ll consider calling and using position to play postflop, and if called I’ll c-bet favorable flops and control the pot on dry/dominated boards.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten of Diamonds and Four of Hearts, which is a very weak hand. Minni raised from the button to 300. Calling out of position with this hand is not profitable, and given my current stack lead and the stage of the tournament (hand 134 of 150), there is no reason to take unnecessary risks with junk cards. I will fold.

Q3o in the BB facing a button min-raise. Getting 2.25:1 pot odds but Q3o plays poorly out of position - easily dominated by better queens and lacks connectivity. This is below my defending threshold against a button open. Fold.

I am on the button with 8s 4d, which is a very weak hand. The pot is currently 150, and I have to put in 100 to call the big blind of 100. However, with three players remaining and 84 off-suit, this is generally a fold from the button as it lacks post-flop playability and strength, especially when facing potential action from the blinds.
AJo is a premium hand heads up against the big blind. Flash folded, so it's just me vs Minni. I should raise for value - standard 3x raise to 300.

Facing Claude's open to 300 from the small blind; pot is 400 and I must call 200. My hand J♥3♣ is weak and largely dominated by the raiser’s typical opening range (many better Js, Broadway, pairs). Calling 200 into 400 gives only 2:1 pot odds (need ~33% equity), and J3o’s equity vs a standard SB open is well below that once position and post‑flop playability are considered. A light 3‑bet to 500 is also unattractive — I have little fold equity and don’t want to build a multi‑street pot out of position with a marginal hand. With 84bb and plenty of hands left, preserving chips and folding is the best EV play here.

52 offsuit is one of the worst starting hands in poker. Even on the button with only 3 players, this hand has very little value. With a healthy stack of 21815 and only 15 hands left in the tournament, there's no need to risk chips with such a weak holding. Fold and wait for better spots.

Heads-up after Claude folded I’m facing only the big blind. 5♦6♣ is a speculative but playable hand in heads-up — connected, decent equity, and I’ll have position postflop (I’m the button/small blind). Opening a standard 2x BB (200 total) seizes the initiative, creates fold equity, and keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks (~84bb). Calling would be passive and folding is too tight in heads-up, so I’ll raise to 200 total.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Eight suited (A8d), which is a very strong hand in a 3-handed game. Minni, the short stack, has min-raised to 200 from the Small Blind. I have a significant stack lead and position on Minni for the rest of the hand. I will 3-bet to 600 to exert pressure and extract value from her likely wide opening range. This also gives me the opportunity to win the pot pre-flop or go to the flop as the aggressor in position.

Facing a 3-bet to 600 after I opened to 200 with 5♦6♣ (offsuit connector). Pot is 800 and I must call 400 more (600 total) — about 3:1 immediate pot odds. Heads-up with position and deep stacks (~82bb) favors seeing a flop with a speculative hand that plays well postflop (straight/two-pair potential) against what is likely a fairly wide 3-bet range from the big blind. Folding is too tight in heads-up; 4-betting would be too committal or too small to credibly fold him. I’ll call 400 more (600 total) to use position and implied odds to realize equity.

Check. I hold 5♦6♣ on T♦ J♣ Q♦ — no pair and only remote runner-runner equity (very unlikely). Flash 3‑bet preflop, so his continuing range is heavy in broadways and hands that connect with this coordinated, high flop (Q/J/T, sets, two‑pair, top pair combos). A c‑bet bluff here would be thin and likely to be called or have to face a raise; folding to a bet keeps risk minimal. Checking uses my position to see his action, controls the pot (pot is 1,200 vs my ~7,835 stack) and lets me fold to real aggression. If he checks back I can take the initiative on safe turn cards; if he bets I’ll usually fold and preserve stack for better spots.

On a board of Td-Jc-Qd, I have the nut flush draw and an inside straight draw to the broadway straight (needing a King). This board is extremely connected and likely hits a large portion of Minni's calling range from the small blind (pairs, suited connectors, and broadway cards). As the pre-flop aggressor, while a continuation bet is an option, checking back here allows me to realize my high equity for free and avoids a potential check-raise from a short-stacked opponent on a board that favors her range. I will check to see the turn and re-evaluate based on the next card.

Turn decision: I have 5♦6♣ on T♦ J♣ Q♦ 7♦. On the flop I checked and Flash checked back after a preflop 3‑bet — that line includes a lot of broadway/overcard hands (AK/AQ/ KQ etc.) and some stronger made hands, but also some air. The 7♦ on the turn actually helps me: with my 5♦ I now have a one‑card flush draw (3 board diamonds + my ♦ = 4 diamonds total), about 9 outs (~19% to hit the river). Given heads‑up dynamics and position, a semi‑bluff here is ideal — I can represent a Q/J/T and apply pressure to hands like AK, Kx, or unpaired broadways that checked the flop. I’ll bet a half‑pot-sized amount (600 into a 1,200 pot). That size balances fold equity and keeps the pot manageable if called. Plan if action changes: fold to significant raise (my equity and hand are not strong enough to continue vs big aggression); if called I proceed to the river knowing I have ~19% to make my flush and have already taken initiative.

I have turned the nut flush with Ad 8d on a board of Td Jc Qd 7d. Since the board is not paired and a straight flush is impossible given the current board textures and my blockers, I hold the absolute best possible hand. Minni, who is the short stack, has led out for 600 into a 1200 pot. I want to maximize value from her likely range of straights (AK, K9, 98), sets, or lower flushes. Raising now builds the pot and sets up a potential shove on the river to commit her remaining stack.

I opened preflop and called a 3‑bet; on the flop both checked and I turned my hand into a semi‑bluff by betting 600. Flash then check‑raised me to 1,800. Pot is 3,600 and I must call 1,200 more. Calling requires ~25% equity (1200/(3600+1200)). I only have a one‑card flush draw (9 diamonds left) — 9/46 ≈ 19.6% — so my raw draw equity is well below the break‑even. Small additional chances (pairing a 5 or 6) are unreliable against the range that would check‑raise here. 

Range read: a preflop 3‑bettor who checks this coordinated T‑J‑Q flop and then check‑raises the turn is heavily weighted to value (sets, two‑pair, KQ/QJ/JT, sometimes strong single pairs); while some bluffs are possible, I don’t have the pot odds or fold equity to continue profitably. 3‑betting/raising as a bluff now would commit a lot more with only 9 clean outs and exposes me to a big 4‑bet or river shove. 

Decision: fold and preserve ~6.0k+ effective stack for better spots. This was a correct semi‑bluff attempt, but the opponent’s check‑raise shows too much strength for my draw to continue profitably.

With three players and me on the button I have position on both blinds. 5♠3♦ is a weak hand but opening wide on the button is standard short-handed — limping/calling is passive and lets the blinds see cheap flops where my equity is poor. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3×BB): big enough to apply pressure and pick up the blinds reasonably often but small enough to keep the pot controllable if called. Plan: fold to a meaningful 3‑bet, and if called proceed cautiously postflop — c‑bet selectively on favorable textures and check/settle for pot control on dry boards.

I am in the Small Blind with Kd 7s. Minni, the short stack at the table, has raised to 300 from the Button. While Kd 7s is a decent hand in three-handed play, it is difficult to play out of position against a Button raise, especially when the Big Blind (Claude) is also a deep stack who could squeeze. Given my significant chip lead and the fact that we are nearing the end of the tournament (Hand 137 of 150), I prefer to avoid marginal spots and preserve my stack rather than calling or 3-betting with a mediocre King-high hand. I will fold and wait for better opportunities.

T5 suited in the BB facing a button raise. Getting 2.25:1 pot odds (200 to win 450). This is a marginal hand but has some playability with suited cards and flush potential. Against a likely button steal from Minni, I can defend occasionally with this hand. Calling to see a flop.

Flopped middle pair (tens) with Th5h on Kh Tc Qs board. Very connected, dangerous board with straight possibilities. My hand has showdown value but is vulnerable - any K, Q, or J likely beats me, and Minni raised preflop so likely has overcards. Best to check and see what Minni does. Can call a reasonable c-bet or fold to aggression.

I missed the flop with 5♠3♦ — no pair and no meaningful draws. The K♥ T♣ Q♠ flop is highly connected and hits both my perceived raising range and Claude’s defending range, and Claude has shown a tendency to be sticky and call down. Bluffing here is low-expected-value, so I’ll exercise pot control with position: check, evaluate Claude’s action, check‑call small bets and only continue on favorable turn cards or fold to strong aggression.

Turn 5c gave me two pair (tens and fives)! Big improvement. Minni checked back the flop, likely indicating weakness or a draw. I should bet for value now - my two pair is ahead of most hands that would check the flop. Board is still dangerous (any J makes a straight), so I want to charge draws and get value from Kx, Qx, or weaker Tx hands. Betting around 65% pot.

Turn paired my 5 (5♠) on K♥ T♣ Q♠ 5♣. Claude leads for 425 into 1,075 (~40% pot). I have a very weak pair with a bad kicker — many hands (Kx/Qx/Tx, AJ or J9 straights) beat me — but Claude’s bet is small and he’s shown a tendency to be sticky and to check‑call down rather than polarize huge bluffs. Calling 425 gives ~3.5:1 pot odds (need ~22% equity) and keeps the pot manageable with position. Raising here would be thin and easily dominated/called by better hands or folded by worse hands that I’d rather let bluff later. Plan: call now, see the river, then fold to large value raises or bluff-catch small bets on safe rivers; consider a thin value bet on a blank river if checked to and the card doesn’t complete obvious straights.

River 6c completes the club flush draw. I have two pair (tens and fives) which was good on the turn, but this is a very dangerous river. Board now has three clubs, and Minni called my turn bet - could easily have been on a club draw that just got there. Also any J makes a straight (AJ specifically). My two pair has showdown value but isn't strong enough to value bet into this completed draw-heavy board. Best to check and call a small bet or check it down.

Board K♥ T♣ Q♠ 5♣ 6♣, I hold 5♠3♦ — a very weak pair (pair of fives with no kicker value). Claude led the turn for 425 and I called; he now checks the river. My hand is behind any Kx/Qx/Tx and also behind straights (A‑J, J‑9) and many one‑pair/overpair hands. Claude has shown a tendency to be sticky and call down rather than polarize with big river bluffs, so a river bluff is unlikely to fold enough better hands to be +EV, and a thin value bet would get called by virtually all better hands. Pot is only 1,500 and preserving stack while getting to showdown for free is the higher EV line here. I’ll check and take the showdown rather than bluffing into a calling station.

I am on the button with pocket eights, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. My goal is to maximize my stack while maintaining my lead. With Minni as the short stack (64 BB) and Claude having a healthy stack (225 BB), a standard 3x opening raise is effective for building the pot and applying pressure. If either opponent jams, especially Minni, pocket eights are strong enough to consider calling a shove.
T3o is a weak hand to defend from the small blind against a button raise. Out of position with poor playability, this is a clear fold.

Facing a 3x open to 300 from the button with J♣5♣ in the big blind. SB folded, pot is 450 and I must call 200 more (total commit 300). My stack is ~64BB so stacks are deep enough to realize equity. J5s is marginal but suited J has decent equity vs a wide button opening range and the pot odds are attractive (450:200 ≈ 2.25:1 → need ≈31% equity). I won’t 3-bet here — J5s isn’t a strong 3-bet bluff or value hand and a raise invites 4-bets or a bigger postflop fight out of position. Plan: call to see a flop, proceed cautiously OOP — continue with pairs, strong club draws or favorable textures; fold to heavy pressure when unimproved. Therefore I will call (total to 300).

I have J♣5♣ on K♠7♠4♣ — no pair and only a very weak backdoor club possibility. Flash opened a wide button range pre and will c-bet many K/x and pairs here. Out of position with ~62BB effective, this is a classic pot-control spot: I don’t want to bluff into a deep, well-capitalized opponent with nearly no equity. Check to keep the pot small, gather information, and only continue if I pick up real equity on the turn (club or a pair) or face a tiny probe bet that gives correct odds. Fold to sizable c-bets; call very small bets cautiously for the turn.

On a flop of K-7-4 with two spades, my pocket eights represent a marginal made hand with significant showdown value but vulnerability to overcards. Minni's check is standard for the big blind in a raised pot. I will place a continuation bet of approximately half the pot to extract value from weaker hands like 7x, 4x, or spade draws, and to deny equity to overcards like AQ or QJ. If I face a raise, I can comfortably fold as I'm likely beaten by a King. If called, I intend to check back on most turns to control the pot size.

I checked the K♠7♠4♣ flop with J♣5♣ to keep the pot small. Flash's 300 into the ~650 pot means I must call 300 to win ~1,250, so I need roughly 24% equity. My hand has only a weak backdoor club runout (~4.2% to make a flush by the river) and 6 outs to pair (≈24% to pair by the river), but many of those paired outcomes will still be behind typical continuation ranges (Kx, 7x, pocket pairs). I'm out of position against a deep, aggressive button; raising would be a thin bluff that won't fold enough hands and would put me in tough spots post-turn. Given marginal real equity, destructive positional dynamics, and the desire to control pot size, folding and preserving my ~62BB stack is the +EV play here.
A6o on the button is a solid hand to open with in a 3-handed game. Standard play is to raise here for value and position. I'll raise to 2.5x to put pressure on the blinds.

Claude opened to 250 from the button and I'm in the small blind with 5♠2♦ — effectively the worst hand. Calling 200 into a 400 pot gives me ~33% break‑even equity, but 5‑2o has very poor equity vs a button opening range and is unplayable out of position. The big blind still acts (possible call or squeeze), and with ~61 BB effective I can wait for much better spots. Folding preserves my stack and avoids difficult post‑flop situations, so I fold.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Trey offsuit. Claude (Button) has raised to 2.5bb (250 total). Minni has folded from the Small Blind. To call, I only need to put in 150 more into a pot that will be 550. This gives me pot odds of approximately 3.6:1 (requiring about 21.5% equity). In a 3-handed game, a Button's opening range is typically very wide, and King-high holds reasonable equity against that range. Given the stage of the tournament and my chip lead, calling to see a flop is a standard defensive play.

I am in the Big Blind with King-high on an Ace-high flop (Ad-6h-4h). Claude, the pre-flop aggressor on the button, has a significant range advantage on this board as his opening range includes many Ax combinations. With no pair and no draws, I have very little equity in this pot. My strategy here is to check and likely fold to a continuation bet, as I do not want to waste chips bluffing into a board that hits the aggressor's range so well.

I flopped top two pair (Aces and Sixes) on a 6h-Ad-4h board. This is an excellent result. There's a flush draw on board, so I need to bet for value and protection. Flash checked to me. I'll bet around 65-70% pot to charge draws and build the pot with my strong hand.

I have King-high on an Ace-high flop (Ad-6h-4h). Claude, as the pre-flop aggressor on the button, has a significant range advantage on this board since it hits most of his opening range. I have no pair, no draws, and very little equity to continue. The pot odds of roughly 2.5:1 are not enough to justify a call with only King-high. Therefore, I will fold and conserve my stack.

Button vs blinds with Jh7d: marginal but acceptable as a steal on the button. Pot is 150, to call 100 (min-raise to 200 available). I want respectable fold equity and to avoid inviting a multiway limp-pot with a hand that plays better in position, so I’ll open to a standard 3x BB sizing (300). That sizing makes opponents pay to see flops, reduces multiway callers, and still preserves plenty of stack (61 BB effective). Plan: fold to a large 3-bet that commits significant stack; if called, play postflop in position — c-bet favorable textures, and give up vs heavy resistance on dry boards. Goal is to pick up the blinds or play small pots with position advantage.

I am holding King of Hearts and Seven of Hearts in the Small Blind. In a three-handed game, K7s is a strong hand, well above the average starting hand. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. She is the short stack with about 58 BBs. While I could 3-bet to isolate her and put pressure on Claude in the Big Blind, calling allows me to see a flop with a disguised hand that plays well post-flop. Given the stack depths and the stage of the tournament, I prefer to keep the pot manageable and use my chip lead to outplay them on later streets if the board texture allows.

I have pocket Jacks in the BB - a premium hand. Minni raised to 300 and Flash called. With JJ, I should 3-bet for value to build the pot and thin the field. A raise to around 1100-1200 is appropriate here - roughly 3.5-4x the original raise with callers. This extracts value from weaker hands while defining my opponents' ranges.

I opened to 300 as a button steal and intended to fold to a large 3‑bet. Claude’s reraise to 1100 is a sizable 3‑bet and Flash (SB) has already called, which increases the chance of a multiway pot. Calling 800 into a 1700 pot requires ~32% equity; J♥7♦ has very poor equity and playability versus a typical BB 3‑bet range and performs worse multiway. A light 4‑bet bluff is possible but risky here — Claude and Flash are deep and Claude’s line looks strong. Preserving ~58BB and waiting for a better spot is higher EV. Fold.

K7s is a strong enough hand to defend against a squeeze from the Big Blind in a 3-handed game. I have favorable pot odds (roughly 3:1) and significant implied odds if I hit a strong board. Although I am out of position, my chip lead allows me to see the flop and play reactively to Claude's post-flop aggression. The call is 800 chips, which is a small portion of my stack.

I am out of position against Claude, who was the pre-flop 3-better. The board is wet and coordinated (J-9-8), which likely connects with a 3-betting range or at least warrants respect. I have a gutshot draw to a straight (needing a Ten) and one overcard (the King), along with a backdoor flush draw. Given I am first to act on the flop, checking to the aggressor is the most prudent play to see how he reacts to this texture. I want to avoid building a large pot with only King-high on a dangerous board while remaining flexible enough to see a turn if the price is right.

I flopped top set on Js-9d-8h! This is an excellent result but the board is coordinated with straight draws (QT, T7, 76). Flash called my 3-bet and checked. I need to bet for value and charge draws. A bet around 65-70% pot (~1700) will build the pot while getting calls from top pair hands, overpairs, and draws.

I am holding King-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten for a Seven-through-Jack straight) on a very coordinated board of Jack-Nine-Eight. Claude, the pre-flop 3-better, has followed up with a significant continuation bet of 1,700 into a 2,500 pot. This board texture hits many hands in a 3-betting range, such as overpairs, sets, or high-equity draws like Queen-Ten. My King is an overcard but may not be clean if Claude has AJ, KJ, or a higher pocket pair. Out of position and with only a marginal draw, the risk of calling outweighs the potential reward, especially given my current chip lead. I will choose the disciplined path and fold.

I am on the button with Ace-Seven offsuit (A7o), which is a strong hand in three-handed play. My goal is to maximize my stack as the tournament nears its end (Hand 141 of 150). Minni is relatively short-stacked at roughly 57BB, while Claude has a healthy stack. A7o is a standard opening hand from this position to put pressure on the blinds and potentially pick up the pot pre-flop or play in position. I will make a standard 3x raise to 300.

7c6d is a weak offsuit hand facing a button raise. I'm in the small blind so I'll be out of position postflop against the raiser. With 9 hands left and a comfortable stack of 24k, there's no need to defend marginal holdings OOP. Folding is the disciplined play here.

Flash opened to 300 from the button; pot is 450 and I need to call 200 (total 300). I have 8c7d (connected but offsuit) and ~57BB effective. Calling gives ~30.8% pot odds which is reasonable to defend heads-up against a likely wide button opening range. A minimum reraise to 500 would be a weak 3-bet and a larger 3-bet with this marginal hand is committing too much with mediocre equity. Plan: call to see the flop and play postflop based on texture — continue aggressively on favorable paired/connected/drawy boards, otherwise check-fold to significant pressure out of position.

I check. With 8♣7♦ on Q♣J♣6♥ I have no pair — only costly backdoor outs (a two-card club runner-runner flush ≈4%, backdoor 9+10 straight ≈1.5%) and ~24% to pair by the river. I’m out of position vs a wide button opening range that will c-bet a high frequency, so I want to keep the pot small and preserve fold equity. Checking lets me fold to significant pressure, call small continuation bets to realize backdoor equity or float to a favorable turn (club, 8 or 7), and avoid committing chips with a largely air hand. Plan: fold to a large c-bet, call a small c-bet (≈≤1/3 pot) to see the turn, and only barrel/raise if the turn gives clear equity or a good bluff story.

On a wet, coordinated flop of Qc-Jc-6h, my Ace-high (A7o) has some showdown value but very little equity against a calling range from the Big Blind. Minni's defending range includes many hands that connect with this board (Qx, Jx, straight draws, and club draws). Betting here might lead to a check-raise from a short-stacked opponent, forcing me to fold. By checking back, I control the pot size, realize my equity, and keep the pot manageable while playing in position on the turn.

Turn analysis: board Qc Jc 6h 3c, I hold 8c7d (single club -> a 4-flush on board). I have 9 club outs to make a flush on the river (~9/46 ≈ 19.6%) plus the non-club 7s/8s for pair-ups (total unique outs ≈14/46 ≈ 30%). Flash opened wide preflop but checked both flop and turn, so his range is broad and contains many hands I can fold out with pressure (overcards, Kx, Ax, occasional small pairs, missed broadways). Betting gives me fold equity and still keeps the pot manageable if called.
Plan: make a half-pot semi-bluff to take the initiative — it folds out a lot of his missed hands and gets value when I hit on the river. If he raises large I can release and preserve my stack; if he calls I still have ~30% to improve on the river and can re-evaluate then. Bet size chosen: ~1/2 pot (~325).

Ace-high on a three-club, coordinated board (Qc-Jc-6h-3c) has very little value. Minni's lead on the turn after the flop went check-check suggests she has either improved to a pair, a flush, or is taking a stab at a pot she thinks I am weak in. Given the board texture and my lack of a club (Ah7s), calling would put me in a difficult position on the river. I'll fold to preserve my significant chip lead with only 10 hands remaining in the tournament.

On the button with T8o in a 3-handed game. This is a standard open from the button - T8o is definitely within my opening range in this position. I'll make a standard raise to 250 (2.5x) to apply pressure on the blinds.

Claude raised to 250 (2.5x) from the button in a 3-handed game. I’m in the small blind with K♠6♦ — a weak, often-dominated offsuit King. Calling 200 to play a multiway pot out of position vs the raiser and the big blind is unattractive (I’d need roughly ~33% equity preflop and I’m behind much of his opening range). A light 3‑bet to 400 is also poor here: this hand has negligible 3‑bet/fold equity and is not worth bluffing out of position. I have ~61BB, so there’s no need to gamble — preserve the stack and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with 6h 4s. Claude (Button) has raised to 250, and Minni has folded. To call, I need to put in 150 more into a pot that will be 550. While the pot odds are roughly 27%, 64o is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position. We are approaching the end of the tournament (Hand 142 of 150), and I currently hold the chip lead. There is no strategic reason to defend such a weak range against a 2.5x raise from the button at this stage. I will fold and wait for better spots to protect my stack.

On the button with Qs8c in a 3-handed game and ~61BB effective. Q8o is marginal but position is powerful — I want to take the initiative, apply pressure to the blinds, and avoid giving a cheap multiway flop. I open to a standard 3x BB (300 total) to get fold equity and play postflop with positional advantage. If I get one caller I'll C-bet favorable textures and control the pot on dangerous boards; if I face a large 3-bet I'll fold and preserve my stack rather than bloat the pot with a dominated hand.

I am holding Jack-Trey offsuit in the Small Blind. The Button (Minni) has opened with a raise to 300. J3o is a very weak hand to play out of position, even against a Button open. Given my current chip lead and the proximity to the end of the tournament (hand 143 of 150), there is no strategic benefit to defending this hand. Preserving my stack and avoiding unnecessary confrontations with a marginal hand is the best course of action.

J2o is a weak hand. While I'm getting 2.25:1 pot odds to call 200, J2o plays poorly postflop and doesn't have good equity against a button raising range. With only 7 hands left in the tournament and a healthy stack of 24240, no need to defend with such a marginal hand. Easy fold.

I am currently the chip leader with 29,450 chips. We are at hand 144 of 150, meaning there are only 7 hands left in the tournament. My hand, 4-2 offsuit, is extremely weak. While I am on the button and could attempt a steal, Claude (SB) and Minni (BB) are both capable of defending. Minni is relatively short-stacked and might look for a spot to shove. Given the tournament context and the weakness of my hand, there is no reason to risk chips or get involved in a pot with garbage. Folding is the safest way to preserve my lead.

Hand 144/150, holding 74o in the SB. Flash folded, heads up vs Minni in BB. While I'm getting 3:1 pot odds to complete, 74o is very weak. With my comfortable stack (24k) vs Minni's short stack (6k), no need to play marginal hands. Folding saves me from tough postflop spots with a trash hand.

Button with J6o. Marginal hand but great position with only blinds to act. Standard steal attempt makes sense here with 6 hands left in the tournament. Stack is healthy at 24k. Raising to 2.5x to steal blinds.

I’m in the small blind with 5♦4♥ — a weak, low, offsuit connector — facing a Button open to 250. The pot is 400 and it costs me 200 more to continue, giving only ~33% immediate equity required. Even if I call, I’ll be out of position postflop vs the opener (and likely vs the BB if they defend), and 5-4o plays poorly OOP with limited fold equity or implied odds. With a healthy 63 BBs I can wait for much better spots in this 3-handed endgame; a flat-call or a light 3-bet here is unnecessarily spewy. Fold and preserve the stack for a higher-expected-value situation.

I am currently the chip leader with 29,350 chips and only 5 hands remaining in the tournament. Claude, the second-largest stack, has raised from the button. I am holding 4d 6s in the big blind, which is a very weak hand. While the price to call is relatively small (150 more), I would be playing out of position against a strong opponent with a marginal hand. Given my goal of maximizing my stack over the final hands and maintaining my lead, there is no need to take unnecessary risks or defend with junk cards. I will fold and wait for better opportunities or simply run out the clock.

I'm on the button with J♥4♥ and a healthy stack (~63bb). Pot is 150, call is 100 and min-raise is 200. In 3-handed play the button can open a wide range — J4s is marginal but has suit and position, so it's worth taking the initiative. I want fold equity and to avoid playing a weak hand passively out of position, so I open here. I choose a standard-sized steal/attack (300 total = ~3x BB) to apply pressure on the blinds while keeping the sizing reasonable vs these deep stacks. Plan: fold to a sizable 3‑bet (hand is too weak to defend vs a strong reraise), but if called I’ll use position — c-bet favorable flops, exercise pot control on dry/overcard boards, and be cautious multiway. This balances aggression and pot-control to maximize expected chips over the remaining hands.

With only five hands remaining in the tournament and me currently holding the chip lead, my primary goal is to avoid unnecessary risks that could jeopardize my position. Minni, the short stack, has raised from the button. While T8o is a marginal defending hand in a 3-handed game, playing it out of position against both the short stack (who may be looking to commit) and the second-largest stack (Claude) in the big blind is suboptimal. Folding here preserves my stack and maintains my lead going into the final hands.

I have QQ in the big blind - a premium hand. Minni opened to 300 from the button. This is a clear 3-bet for value situation. With only 5 hands left and Minni having about 6000 chips, I want to build the pot with my strong holding. A 3-bet to around 950-1000 is appropriate sizing - roughly 3x their raise.

I opened to 300 from the button with J♥4♥ as a standard steal. Claude’s 3‑bet to 950 is a sizable isolation — Flash folded so it’s heads‑up. The pot is 1,300 and I must call 650, which requires ~33% equity (650/(1300+650) ≈ 33%). J4s is heavily dominated by a typical 3‑bet range (pocket pairs, broadways, Axs) and won’t have that equity; a call only works vs a very wide bluffing 3‑bet range for which I have no evidence. A 4‑bet bluff is thin here — with deep stacks he can call or jam and I’d be committing chips with a poor hand. Preserve the ~6k stack, fold and wait for a better, clearer spot in position.

I am currently the chip leader with 293 hands played out of 150. Being on the button in a three-handed game allows me to open a wide range of hands to steal the blinds and maintain my lead. 9d 5d is a suited hand that has some playability post-flop, and a 3x raise is a standard opening size to put pressure on the short stack (Minni) and the second-largest stack (Claude).

2d6c is one of the worst hands in poker - very low cards, offsuit, no connectivity. Flash raised from the button and while I'm getting decent pot odds, this hand has almost no playability post-flop. With 4 hands left and a healthy 24640 stack, no need to defend with garbage. Easy fold.

Facing a button open to 300 from the big blind with T♠6♦. Pot is 450 and I must call 200 more; calling 200 into 450 gives ~30.8% break-even equity (200 / (450+200)). With stacks ~59bb I can realize implied odds when I connect and this hand has enough equity vs a wide BTN open to justify a flat call. 3-betting with T6o is too thin and folding here would be too tight given the pot odds. Plan: call and evaluate the flop — proceed with pairs or strong draws, use pot control on dry boards, and fold to heavy turn/river aggression when I miss.

Check. I have top pair (Ten) with a weak 6-kicker on a K-T-3 flop — a relatively dry board with an overcard (K) that beats me. Flash (BTN) opened wide preflop and will c-bet this texture frequently, but many of those c-bets are bluffs or worse pairs that I beat. I’m out of position and don’t want to build a big pot OOP with a marginal kicker, so I’ll keep pot control and let the button show aggression first. Plan: check-call standard c-bets (~1/3–1/2 pot) and re-evaluate on the turn; fold to very large turn overbets/shoves if I don’t improve; if he checks back I’ll take a free card and consider a small value bet on safe turns.

On a flop of K-T-3 rainbow, I have a significant range advantage as the pre-flop raiser from the button. My 9d 5d has virtually no showdown value or draws, so my best path to winning the pot is a continuation bet. Minni's check indicates weakness, and this board is favorable for representing a King or a Ten. A bet of around half the pot should be sufficient to fold out most of her non-pairing hands and garbage that defended from the big blind.

Call 325. I have top pair (Ten) with a weak 6-kicker out of position vs a wide BTN opening range. The K‑T‑3 flop is fairly dry; Flash will c-bet a lot of hands that I beat (missed broadways, smaller pairs, pure bluffs). Pot is 975 and calling 325 requires ~25% equity (325 / (975+325)) — my hand has better than that vs a wide c-bet range. 3‑betting OOP with T6o is too thin and folding is too nitty given the pot odds and implied fold equity. Plan: keep pot control by calling, re-evaluate on the turn — if I improve or villain shows weakness I can get value, but fold to very large turn/river overbets/shoves if I miss and face heavy aggression.

Check. I have top pair (T) with a weak 6 kicker and I’m out of position vs the BTN’s wide opening/c-betting range. The turn Qc is a dangerous card—it gives a lot of the villain’s c-bet range (Qx, KQ, some Kx combos) a better hand and doesn’t change my relative hand strength in a way that justifies building the pot OOP. There are few meaningful draws to fold out (only two clubs on board), so a lead here would mainly fold worse hands and get value from better hands that already beat me. I’ll pot‑control: check and call small–medium bets (roughly ≤ ~1/2 pot, ~650) to see a river, but fold to large turn overbets or shove. If Villain checks back I’ll re-evaluate the river and consider a small value bet on safe cards.

On the turn, the Queen of clubs is a very dangerous card that connects heavily with the board (3s Th Kc Qc). Since Minni called my flop continuation bet, her range likely includes Kings, Tens, or straight draws (like J9 or QJ). The Queen of clubs completes several straights (AJ, J9) and improves many of her calling hands. As I have no pair and no draw with 9d 5d, and considering Minni's relatively short stack, a second barrel is unlikely to generate enough folds to be profitable. I will check behind to see a free river and preserve my chip lead with only a few hands remaining in the tournament.

I rivered two pair (Tens and Sixes). Flash (BTN) opened wide preflop, c‑bet small on the flop (I called) and then checked the Q on the turn — a line consistent with a lot of marginal one‑pair hands and missed broadways. The river 6 turns my marginal top‑pair into a value hand that is ahead of most of Flash’s flop‑cbet / turn‑check range (single Kx/Qx, A‑x/J‑x, other worn bluffs). With the pot at 1300 I want a thin value bet to extract calls from those worse single‑pair hands while keeping sizing small enough to get called rather than fold them out. I’ll bet ~500 (~38% pot). If Flash raises big it’s likely a value raise (KT/QT/sets) and I will respect that; I’ll call small/medium raises but fold to large overbets. Action: bet 500 to extract value from worse pairs and missed bluffs.

I have 9-high with no pair or draw on a highly coordinated board (Kc Qc Th 3s 6h). Minni defended her big blind, called a continuation bet on the flop, and after both of us checked the turn, she has led out for 500 on the river. I have zero showdown value, so calling is not an option. While a bluff-raise is a possibility, Minni is relatively short-stacked and likely has at least a pair of Tens or better given her line, making her less likely to fold to a raise. With only three hands remaining in the tournament and a healthy chip lead over Claude, it is most prudent to fold and preserve my stack rather than risk chips on a high-variance bluff.

Hand 148/150, on the button with 9c5d - a weak offsuit hand. While button position is favorable for stealing, 95o plays poorly postflop and both opponents could defend. With a healthy stack of 24640, no need to take unnecessary risks with marginal holdings. Folding is the disciplined play here.

Holding 2♦8♣ — essentially the worst hand. Claude folded, so it’s heads-up vs the big blind (Flash). The extra cost to continue is tiny (50 more chips), and heads-up the small blind acts last postflop (positional advantage). Raising to 200 with this junk hand is unnecessary risk; folding is too tight for such a small price. I’ll call the 50 (100 total committed) to see a flop cheaply, keep the pot small, and fold to strong aggression on the flop if I don’t improve.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Seven of Spades. Claude (Button) folded, and Minni (Small Blind) completed the blind. I have position on Minni for the rest of the hand and a hand that plays well post-flop. Given that I am the tournament chip leader and there are only three hands remaining, I will check to see a free flop and maintain my lead without unnecessary risk.

On the flop I have 2♦8♣ vs board A♦ 3♦ 7♣. I have no pair and only a very weak backdoor flush (need two diamonds on turn+river — ~4% chance) and 6 outs to make a single pair by river (~24% chance), which is often not good enough if my opponent has an Ace or a better draw. Pot is small (200) and there's currently no bet. Best play is pot control and to see a turn for free — check and evaluate. If Flash leads with a small probe bet I can consider a call for the price, but I will fold to sizable aggression to protect my 6.6k stack and wait for a better spot.

In Hand 148, I am the Big Blind with Ten-Seven of Spades. After Claude folded and Minni completed from the Small Blind, the flop came Ace-Trey-Seven. I have middle pair. Minni checked to me. In a limped pot, my middle pair has significant showdown value, but I don't want to bloat the pot on an Ace-high board where the opponent's range can easily contain an Ace. Checking behind allows me to see a turn for free, maintain control over the pot size, and keep my tournament lead secure as we approach the final hands.

Turn is 5♠ — my 2♦8♣ has no pair and the flop backdoor diamond draw is gone (turn is not a diamond). I have only 6 outs to pair by the river (~13% equity). Pot is 200 and there’s no bet to call; bluff-betting here is unnecessary and thin. To break even on a call I would need a bet ≤ ~30 chips (B/(200+B) ≤ 13%), but the table min is 100, so any realistic bet is unprofitable to call. I’ll check to control the pot, see the river for free, and fold to any meaningful bet on the turn.

I have middle pair (sevens) on an Ace-high board in a limped pot. Minni has checked to me on both the flop and the turn, suggesting she likely doesn't have a strong Ace or has a very weak range. Since I am the tournament chip leader with only three hands remaining, my primary goal is to minimize risk and protect my stack. There is no need to bet and potentially face a check-raise or bloat the pot when I have decent showdown value. Checking allows me to reach the river for free and control the size of the pot.

Board is 3♦ A♦ 7♣ 5♠ 7♦ and I hold 2♦8♣ — no pair, no meaningful showdown equity. Pot is only 200 and Flash checked flop and turn, so either weak or slowplaying. Betting as a bluff here has very limited fold equity (min bet 100 is a large price relative to my chance of winning) and is an unnecessary risk late in the tournament. I’ll take the pot to showdown for free: check. If Flash leads out, I’ll fold to any realistic bet.

I have trip sevens on a board of 3-A-7-5-7. Minni has checked on every street (flop, turn, and river), which suggests she has a weak range or a marginal hand. Given the strength of my hand in a limped pot, I am almost certainly ahead. I will place a small value bet of 150 chips into the 200-chip pot, hoping to get a call from an Ace-high, a small pair, or even a high-card hand, while still protecting my lead as the tournament draws to a close.

Pot is 350 and Flash bet 150 on the river — calling 150 into a 350 pot requires ~30% equity (150/(350+150)=30%). I hold 2♦8♣ on 3♦ A♦ 7♣ 5♠ 7♦: no pair, only one diamond, so I have essentially no realistic showdown equity vs a value-heavy river range (any Ace, any 7, or any two-diamond combo that makes a flush). Flash checked flop and turn and then fires a smallish river bet — a line consistent with thin value (A, a diamond flush, or a turned/set/full house) and not a high enough bluff frequency to justify a call. My single diamond slightly blocks some flush combos but it’s not sufficient. With poor equity, insufficient pot odds, and no meaningful fold equity, the correct play is to fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

I’m on the button with A♣Q♠ (AQo) in a three-handed pot — this is a strong, positional hand. Blinds are 50/100 with a 150 pot; calling 100 is too passive. Open-raising to ~3x the BB (300 total) is the standard sizing here: it takes the initiative, creates fold equity against the blinds, and isolates heads-up while leaving a comfortable stack behind (I still have plenty to play post-flop). If I face a small/medium 3-bet I’ll consider calling and playing in position; if I face a very large 4-bet/all-in I’ll likely fold AQo out of caution.

I am currently the chip leader with only two hands remaining in the tournament. I am holding 5-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Minni has raised from the button, and I am in the small blind, meaning I would be out of position for the remainder of the hand. Given my stack advantage and the proximity to the end of the tournament, there is no strategic reason to commit chips with a trash hand. Folding preserves my lead and minimizes unnecessary risk.

I have 8h9d in the BB facing a button open from Minni. This is a decent speculative hand with straight potential. Getting 2.25:1 odds (200 to win 450), 89o is a standard defend from the BB against a button raise. Easy call.

Flopped an open-ended straight draw (6 or J completes) on Ts4s7h. Standard to check to the preflop aggressor. If Minni c-bets, I can call with my 8 outs (~31% equity). If they check, I get a free card to improve.

I opened to 300 from the button and Claude (BB) just flatted. The flop (T♠ 4♠ 7♥) is fairly dry and he checked — a great spot to take the initiative. I have A♣Q♠: two overcards and a backdoor spade (small chance), so my hand has decent equity and a lot of fold equity as the aggressor. Claude’s calling range from the BB will include many medium pairs and broadway combos that will often fold to a continuation bet on this texture. I’ll make a small-to-medium c-bet (~1/3–40% pot) to pressure those hands while keeping the pot manageable and leaving plenty of room to maneuver on later streets. If he folds I take it down; if he calls I’ll re-assess on the turn and continue only if I pair or improve or get a favourable runout; if he check-raises big I’ll give him credit and likely fold since I don’t have a made hand.
I have an open-ended straight draw (6 or J completes). Minni bet 250 into 600 pot, giving me 3.4:1 odds (need ~23%). With 8 outs I have ~17% immediate equity plus implied odds if I hit. Easy call.

The 6d completes my straight! I now have 6-7-8-9-T for the nuts. Minni has been aggressive so far. I should lead out for value rather than risk a check-through. Betting around 65-70% pot (~750-800) should get called by Tx, flush draws, and overpairs. Don't want to bet too big and scare them off.

I have A♣Q♠. I opened preflop and c‑bet the dry T♠4♠7♥ flop; Claude flat‑called. The turn (6♦) killed my small backdoor spade possibility and I no longer have any meaningful draw — only six outs (3 Aces + 3 Queens) to make a pair on the river (~6/46 ≈ 13%). Claude now bets 750 into a 1150 pot (pot will be 1900), so calling requires ~29% equity. His line (flat call of a small flop c‑bet, then a sizeable turn lead) is consistent with a value‑heavy range (T‑x, pocket pairs, maybe sets) with some bluffs. My raw equity to improve is well below the required pot odds and I have little fold equity or leverage here. With no made hand or realistic draw, the correct risk‑adjusted play is to fold and preserve chips for the final hand rather than call a large bet with only two overcards.

This is the 150th and final hand of the tournament. I am currently the chip leader with 28,725 chips, followed by Claude with 25,190 and Minni with 5,935. The goal is to maximize the final stack size. Queen-Deuce of Hearts (Q2h) is a very weak hand, even on the button in a three-handed game. In the context of maximizing my final stack, choosing the zero-variance path with a marginal-to-poor hand on the final deal is a sound strategic decision to avoid unnecessary losses.
